After completing my first play through (approximately 30 hours) I would give this game a 7.5 out of 10, and here's why:

Graphics: Decently good. The game looks good in MY books, but objectively if we compare it to other modern AAA games, its not quite at the top. Stilll, its not ugly, and the art style do compliment the graphical fidelity, so that's always a plus.

Performance: Questionable. Unreal Engine is a relatively terrible engine in the modern era when it comes to performance. Don't get me wrong, the game does run decently (at least 60fps consistently) at max graphics without ray-tracing, but yet it cannot hit 165 fps consistently without DLSS (or the equivalent), which would have been normal if the game had "next-gen" level of graphics... but it don't... so... where are my frames at? So if you're someone who's okay with 60fps, or okay with DLSS turned on, the performance is great, otherwise, its kind of mediocre.

Gameplay (RPG context): Good. The game's skills and perks systems definitely do feel nice to play with. These skills and perks not only give noteable changes to how you fight enemies, but also to the choices you can make when speaking with NPCs.

The game also really do go quite elaborate in terms of the choices available, with choices being affected by your skills, the knowledge your character has come across, the items your characters have found, and with all these varying choices affecting the story (though to what extent, idk, since its only my first play through). 

Gameplay (FPS Context): Questionable. Its like what you would expect from similar games (like fallout for example). Although there is a wide variety of guns, the primary difference I feel between them is just their damage and rate of fire, which is fine i guess. But the problem is the enemies. Enemy AI are more A than I, their fighting style sometimes feel like something is broken, and feel like sitting ducks rather than engaging/smart/dangerous enemies. 

World: It's good, there's like 4 major planets and a bunch of other stations, and each of these zones where you can visit is certainly well crafted. Be it the story telling elements, or things you can do, it feels sufficiently dense that the worlds arent just "nothing-burgers" as compared to another similar title (S*** F****).

Enemy variety: what variety. Expect to be fighting the same ♥♥♥♥ (in varying context but still the same ♥♥♥♥) over and over again. That's not a good thing. However, enemies don't respawn once defeated (even for those common mobs), which to me is a good thing, cause it makes my fights feel impactful despite the repetitiveness.

STORY: Its okay. The thing about the story is, it does have sufficient "oomph" and impact to it, in terms of the overall threat at hand, the justifications of the characters, the various possible dilemmas at hand etc. All that is the positive. However, beyond that, the OVERALL story writing isn't exactly considered "unique". The saving grace is the level of writing involved, which I find to be more complex than other titles. But in terms of the OVERALL CONCEPT, it feels rather generic imo, since its a typical (mild spoilers) [spoiler] "save the world from dangerous threat with the power of friendship" kind of trope [/spoiler]. 

Overall: The game is fun. Worth playing. BUT, maybe not worth buying at full price, so... buy only if u're rich, otherwise, wait for sale.

Other side notes: 
TURN OFF RAY TRACING. that ♥♥♥♥ has minimal benefits graphically in this game (especially with this game's unique and beautiful art style) yet is so detrimental to performance.

Obsidian really needed this W. I haven't gotten deep into the story, but there are a lot of strong signs pointing to this being their best RPG in ages. 

Avowed didn't cut it, let's be real. 

Out of the gate, the graphics are much better than the first game. But graphics don't make a good game (especially when it comes to RPGs). Fortunately, the elements that do make a great RPG are here. The character creator is good. It doesn't break the mold, but it's serviceable. Where this game shines is the background, traits, and skills systems. Despite having just started, I've seen my background and skills come into play, seamlessly, more than once. I mean, you start on your ship and your cabin door is jammed and there are like three different ways to get in; if that small scenario and the choices offered to solve it, is representative of the variety in the rest of the game, I feel I'm in for a great time. 

I've also seen myself locked out of multiple options due to character build and with no way to respec (a positive, if you ask me--I like games that make you stick with and think about your choices) I can see new playthroughs in the future. I heard someone say that this feels a lot like New Vegas, and I can see that. If you're a fan of older Obsidian titles and have been waiting for a return to form, this looks like it may be the ticket. I'll update this review and give a final analysis once I finish the game, but as of now, I reccomend it.

****Just beat the game tonight on Hard Difficulty (I would suggest playing on this one) and it took me just over 50 hours, I had a blast on this game and as I mentioned below, Outer Worlds 2 is my #2 game of 2025, just slightly behind Kingdom Come Deliverance 2 for GOTY for 2025. ****

This game is bloody Brilliant, it honestly makes the first Outer Worlds game feel like a test run. This second game is improved in literally every single possible way.

From the improved combat, to the improved audio, to the improvements to the roleplaying stuff, to how many more options you now have to tackle missions, to the improved dialogue, to the different character builds you can now do, to the improved writing, to the improved graphics, to the addition of a proper stealth system,etc.. this game has improved 10 fold from the first game.

I thought the first game was not to bad, but I was missing the variety that something like New Vegas had for how to build up your character and different ways that you can tackle missions.

Do you feel like going stealthy for this quest? Do you feel like using speech? Do you feel like shooting your way through? Do you feel like using Engineering to get Mechs Robots or Turrets on your team? Do you feel like using Locksmithy or hacking to get past stuff? Or do you feel like doing a bunch of each? The choice if yours.

It feels like Obsidian upped the choices and roleplaying to 11 for this sequel and I LOVE It. So much more depth to this game than the first one.

This game is like a mix of elements from the first Outer Worlds, but mixed with stealth of say Dishonered, the atmosphere of Bioshock and the choices,roleplaying and options of New Vegas. I honestly couldn't ask for a better sequel to The Outer Worlds 1, I was expecting 1 or 2 things slightly improved but literally everything is improved a ton here.

The performance is also very good, played 53 hours and not had any crashes or fps problems at all. I noticed only 1 bug the whole game and it was fixed in one of the patches that has come out, it also runs pretty well to, very polished game!

I am already thinking of my second playthrough because there are so many options for character builds and certain things in the game world you can only get to or do depending on your build, just like in New Vegas, you might need a certain skill to do something. This allows for great replay value as you don't see every single thing in the game during your first go around. I will play this game at least 3 time over again do to how things are set up with character builds, ways to tackle missions and requirements to do certain things.

So far the story and writing is also much darker and the tone is darker and I am all for it. The only thing that I think could be better so far is the AI of the enemis could use some improvement (it is not the worst but also not the best), but everything else is a vast, huge improvement from the first Outer Worlds game. And as much as I loved Pentiment & Grounded 2 recently, this is my favorite Obsidian game since Pillars Of Eternity 2.

This game also has one of the best soundtracks and radio stations I have heard in awhile, old school jazzy vibes and there are a bunch of them to. Brings me back to the music and radio stations from Fallout New Vegas and also the music in Bioshock.

KCD2 has been my favorite game for most of this year and Outer Worlds 2 is the only game to match that joy that KCD2 gave me early in this year. Def my Two favorite games of 2025 so far, I get that same feeling of "Just another hour more!" that I had while playing KCD2.

If the Outer Worlds was a 7.5 or 8/10, the Outer Worlds 2 is a 9/10 for me. I am already thinking about what I want to do next with my next playthrough, where I want to go explore, what character build I want to do next and the other faction that I will help out at the end of the game this time. It is a wonderful game and I cannot wait to play some more.

Did I mention the radio stations and the Soundtrack is fantastic? So dang good!! Who ever was in charge of getting all this soundtrack and radio stations together is a GENIUS, this is the best soundtrack and radio stations I have ever heard in a game and I have been gaming since the 80s. 

Lastly, this game REALLY rewards you exploring and doing side quests. I have gotten some VERY awesome items from just doing side quests, for example some boots that let you glide/double jump like BL4 and another item that lets  you see the answers to puzzles easier and also lets you see enemies that are invisible. All from doing side quests! Same goes for Companions, half the companions I have found so far have been from me doing side quests! Wonderful stuff!

This really is New Vegas in Space and a huge improvement over The Outer Worlds 1. Now excuse me as I go play some more.

Purple Berry Tequila song from the first Biomes Radio Station is so dang catchy, I just love it... I am still singing that song after beating the game

Four hours in, pretty good so far.
A definite improvement over the first one in terms of map size, dialogue animations, the graphical look (since that's oh so important). Guns seem to be completely new, nothing thus far from TOW1 & the RPG elements are definitely there, with NPCs remembering your dialogue choices.
All good stuff put, there is one thing that irks me, the perk system.

TOW1, we have a good base of everything from the start, dialogue skills, lockpicking, hacking, we were sort of a jack of all trades - though I've seen this was something players were unhappy about. Even so, starting with 0 in everything besides what you pick in the character creator & only receiving two skill points every level up feels a little unbalanced & unfair. Then again, this is so you pick your character & you stick with them, no option to respec. It does sorta force you to think what skills you need & what skills you don't need, but at the same time, it feels like a lot of things are blocked off to you which, in any other open-world RPG, you would be able to access them.

That aside, I am enjoying it. Good open-world, good RPG elements, Outer Worlds 2 is proof Obsidian's still got passion.

[b]Not Recommended[/b]

[b]TL;DR[/b]
Crashes, brutal stutter/frametimes, shallow RP that railroads to combat, and clumsy UI. On ultra‑high‑end hardware it still tanks. Hard pass.

[b]Specs[/b]
• i9‑13900K • RTX 5090 • 128 GB RAM • 4K/240 Hz
• Windows 11

[b]Stability[/b]
• Repeated crash: "[i]The application has hung and will now close. We apologize for the inconvenience.[/i]"
• Happens in multiple areas. [url=[https://www.twitch.tv/spizypie/clip/HealthyBlushingPeafowlTwitchRPG-gJRyOGZW-4HgZW_o]Repro](https://www.twitch.tv/spizypie/clip/HealthyBlushingPeafowlTwitchRPG-gJRyOGZW-4HgZW_o]Repro) clip[/url].

[b]Performance (4K)[/b]
• Many spots: [b]18–30 fps[/b] with RT on.
• Upscaling Balanced + Frame‑Gen x2: ~[b]120 fps[/b]; without Frame‑Gen: ~[b]60 fps[/b].
• Main menu is [b]uncapped (~1500 fps)[/b] → pointless power/thermals.
• Comparison: [i]Dying Light: The Beast[/i] holds ~[b]120 fps[/b] 4K on the same rig.

[b]Optimization Red Flags[/b]
• Severe 1%/0.1% lows (micro‑stutter).
• RT cost is disproportionate to image gain.

[b]Design & Writing[/b]
• Character creator uses [b]“Body Type 1/2”[/b] instead of clear labels; reads ideology‑first, clarity‑second. Give explicit labels/toggles.
• Companions constantly guilt‑prod; barks feel manipulative rather than reactive.
• Dialog trees frequently collapse into combat; even “smart” foes insist on dying.

[b]UI/UX[/b]
• Feels untested: tiny click targets, awkward inventory flow, inconsistent Back/ESC behavior, weak info density.

[b]Visuals[/b]
• For UE5, many materials read [b]plasticky[/b]; lighting doesn’t sell depth. RT doesn’t fix the flatness.

[b]Settings Tested[/b]
• RT on/off; TSR/FSR Balanced; Frame‑Gen on/off; native 4K.

[b]Verdict[/b]
In this state, not worth the time or money. Will not revisit even after patches.

This is a review on the performance of the game and overall graphics presentation. I'll start with saying I think this is a pretty good game, I'm definitely having fun despite my gripes with UE5.  

My system: 3080, i7-12700k and 16GB DDR5
Settings: All high, DLSS Quality, RTX OFF, 1440p

FPS
The game will fool you the first 20-30 mins with the intro area. With this setup and settings I was hitting 120 with no real issues. Once you make it to the first area after the intro however is where it falls apart. Whether my GPU is nearing 100% used or 50-60% the game in this first area doesn't seem to want to go over the 50-70 mark. Setting the game to low or high seems to make next to no difference. This wouldn't be too bad but the constant stuttering (which I assume is UE5 Shader Compilation Stutter) every 10 seconds is really wearing on me. My current setup for this game includes Lossless Scaling using LSFG 3.1 with fixed mode and 2x multiplier for a fake 120. This is my first time using Lossless Scaling and it's actually really smooth and nearly perfect. The only thing holding it back is the UE5 shader stutter which hopefully can be resolved by the devs.

TLDR on FPS, if you don't have a PC at or above mine don't expect good frames. Lossless scaling is amazing for this game.

Graphics presentation
I just wanted to put a note in here for those like me who thought on twitch and youtube vidoes the game looked pretty bad. In game however the game looks far better, the textures and models are mostly great and they just don't come across well on compressed video I guess. The lighting also is really good most of the time, sometimes it can make the game look very flat but overall it's a pretty good looking game. 

TLDR on Graphics, game looks way better than what comes across on youtube or twitch.

Just under 5 hours in and the game feels bigger, they took a lot of what made The Outer Worlds exceptional and cranked it to 11. Story seems paced but hard to tell as I spent a great deal of time wandering around Paradise, deliberately doing side quests rather than advancing the plot. Skill points upon leveling seem exceptionally valuable and force you to plan early on what type of character you will be playing. For me this is a bit of a drawback as I like to create massively OP characters and save what needs saving however I damn well please. That being said, I expect that most will like the true RPG feel of how a character develops; and in fairness it does seem to reward thoughtful planning.  Companions seem well developed with their own motives and affinity for the player.  Obsidian's talent for fun and quirky writing paired with morally ambiguous choices is on display, much as in the first game. Very much recommend. Also...grenades...just sayin'.
if you liked the first Outer Worlds, you'll love the sequel. Reviews are great and all, but as gamers, we should experience games first-hand. I hope you enjoy as much as I am.

Obsidian has finally stopped trying to break their own action RPG mold and accepted their place in the shadow of New Vegas. They've added the few ingredients they missed with the first Outer Worlds: narrative reactivity, meaningful skills and loot, interesting perks and traits. Remarkably little was needed to complete the formula, but what a difference it makes. The result may not truly "excel" at anything -- the dialogue, combat, characters are good, not phenomenal -- but, impressively, I don't care. Somehow, the itch is scratched. This plays like a worthy spiritual successor.

For anyone wondering if the game is good, in my personal opinion it is! It is very much an upgrade from the 1st game and I have found myself really enjoying the combat, character builds and weapon/armor variety. I said this before and I will always say it a game that doesn't have a 3rd person camera should never have character creation, it is dumb. Thankfully, Outer Worlds 2 has 3rd person camera perspective which makes the game such a joy to play. I have only played the tutorial plus a bit of the first world the game takes place on but I will say the exploration is fun just don't expect Fallout New Vegas levels of size. The opening tutorial really grabs you vs the 1st game and I found the story to be somewhat interesting. Just go out and explore and the game will reward you. I have also found that some things require you using a brain cell to get extra goodies and that makes traversing the map more engaging for me. 

I have a few gripes but nothing incredibly negative:
1. Seems to be an occassional bug where facial hair disappears when wearing a helmet/head armor. Another bug is where your skin at the back of your head with poke through on the beanie but havent seen anything else.
2. I really, really want weapons to be visible on your character. Not all weapons you have equipped (you can have a total of 4 weapon slots if you have perk/ armor mod) but at least whichever one you have currently equipped. I think that should be something that gets added for a better feel when walking around the world. 
3. 3rd person animations are so nice to have, but I feel like there are some missing. For example when switching shoulder view from right --> left, it is very abrupt and could use a little more animation to make it feel better. Same thing for drawing a weapon, you will just spawn it out of nowhere with very little animation. Lastly on this, when holstering your weapon I feel like holding the R button has to be held a second or two longer than it should. Adjusting that would be nice.
4. Radio will just not work randomly and this can be fixed by reloading a save. 
5. I have seen some armors that straight up look heavy to wear but its medium then I see it the other way around. Minor gripe.
6. A lot of notes all of over the place, as well as many terminal entries to read. Can feel very info dumpy.

All in all, I am extremely happy with the game so far and if you liked Outer Worlds 1 you will enjoy this much more. I can't speak on the companions just yet, but so far it is all running smoothly. Hope this review helps!

If you love TOW 1, you'll definitely enjoy the sequel as it improve every aspect from gameplay, dialogues, consequences of your choices, some meaningful skills and perks & most important of all the radio original soundtracks are top notch; worth the full price for it.

One thing to note, it is not loot heavy as a Bethesda game nor even the first game as they mostly drop ammo, healing, and crafting items and rarely weapons & armor. It is definitely more challenging than the first game; it is necessary to invest your skill point carefully as being an overpower Jack-of-all-Trade character is no longer an option (unless they increase the level limit) as if you don't put in specific amount on that one specific skill you will miss out on some loot or locked out of an area or you have to find alternative ways to solve the quest.

I feel like, upon starting this game, this developer has done the nearly impossible; they've made a concept of near infinite iterative potential. The way they've caught an entirely new dystopia with this already (only like an hour or so in to the pre-release), compared to the one of the first (The Outer Worlds was, for lack of a better term, a Corporatocracy, The Outer Worlds 2 seems so far to be a Theocracy, though that is up for change depending on how the rest of the game goes), proves that they can explore different kinds of dystopian leadership structures in different solar systems throughout the galaxy, and potentially without making any of it seem boring or repetitive. They also have a relatively unique design language to their game that manages to encapsulate Art Deco, Art Nouveau, Steampunk, Solar Punk, Cyberpunk, and Spacepunk all in one grand amalgamation that is both extremely pleasurable to view and also somehow exceedingly discomforting XD

[h1] Obsidian Listened to Fans [/h1]

Having way more fun than expected so far.

The main issue for me so far is the price.

It could've been better if it's not on UE5, but it runs well anyway.

But the gameplay and RPG mechanics are what make this game, they're the "bread and butter," so to speak.
The story so far is hilarious yet serious.
I love the fact that they added themes of religion and politics.
I love the fact that they added LOTS of sarcasm, be it in dialogues, notes, or simply the loading screen messages.
These make the game even more immersive and somehow realistic despite the art style and overall graphics.

I'm still early in the story, but the overall experience is exhilirating.
I'm always excited to see what's next, objective to objective.
The game also rewards you for exploring, even in the simplest of detours.

What I didn't like:
A bit long loading times in some instances
No blood splatters on enemies
Not much gore

I can't even begin to express how utterly disappointed I am with The Outer Worlds 2, it's a lifeless, disgusting pile of garbage that's somehow even worse than the first game. I went into this game ,thinking it had this promise of deep RPG elements, you know? Like, it gave off that vibe of meaningful choices and immersive worlds, but nope, it was all smoke and mirrors, just shallow mechanics dressed up to look profound.

The whole game feels dead inside, no passion, no edge, just a bland, preachy mess that's so in your face with its forced inclusivity and girlboss nonsense that it made me want to hurl. Representation? Sure, I'm all for it when it's organic, but this? It's insulting, like they think I'm too dumb to notice the agenda being shoved down my throat.

Combat? Don't get me started. I felt nothing. Zero weight, no blood, no grit—just these pathetic blasters and enemies flopping over like they're taking a nap. Where's the intensity? The stakes that make you sweat? This is supposed to be an RPG, not some goofy cartoon for the fainthearted. My choices meant jack squat; everything was so fluffy and consequencefree that I was bored out of my mind within 40 minutes of playtime. The writing is also HORRIBLE.


Look, people need to wake up and realize Obsidian isn't the studio it used to be back in the Bethesda days, when they dropped Fallout: New Vegas. That was peak gaming,brutal, thoughtful, with real depth that left you thinking for days. Now? The whole place is a woke loser cult, churning out this midtier Xbox slop that's more about virtuesignaling than making games that actually matter. The visuals are so obnoxiously bright and colorful, it's like they're screaming "look how GAY we are!" while forgetting to put any soul into the damn thing.
I'm so done with this industry and its obsession with politics over fun. I want games that rattle me, that make me feel alive with their grit and complexity,not this generic, agendradriven trash that's killing everything I loved about gaming. Devs, if you're reading this, snap out of it and remember what made you great, because right now, you're just embarrassing yourselves.

In the last couple of years, I have found a bulletproof way to test if a game is in a good state or not: just shoot into a pool of water. If there will be ripples and splashes, the game is good. If there are not, the game will be lackluster in many more departments. 

Two examples: 
Cyberpunk 2077 - no reaction whatsoever when shooting into water
Starfield - cheap ripples and no splashes when shooting into water

Guess what happens in this game which is 100€ for Early Access, reeling me in with the promise to play over the weekend instead of having to wait for the release in the middle of a workweek?

And yet, at first this game suprised me with its quality. I liked to read the lore pieces in the first level, I sometimes even had to laugh. The graphics are stunning. Level design is intriguing. Shooting is pretty darn good for a sci-fi RPG. But the obvious lack of polish got noticeable very soon: 

1. Stuttering and noise and flickering with or without DLSS4 and RT turned on and no matter on which setting the shadow quality is set. This gets worse AFTER you finished the first level which might be exactly after refund time. 

2. Overlapping dialogues. I hate this so much and I just can't understand how this slips through QA so many times (rarely happens in lower budget games). So I start walking towards an objective and someone is telling me something over the radio. But then I pass a trigger after 5 seconds and another dialogue begins, disrupting the earlier one which never gets finished. Which results in a feeling of having missed out and being overwhelmed with dialogue. Hated it ever since devs don't care about stuff like that anymore and I hate it in this game as well. 

3. While the lore pieces are written surprisingly well, the actual dialogues are the the opposite of subtle. "Bottom line is that we are at the bottom of the line" is just one of the many 'witty' remarks. 

4. Graphics, especially textures, get noticeably worse in the first open area. 

5. This is more of an RPG than the cutscene and dialogue simulator called Cyberpunk 2077. However, this is still nowhere close to the depth of an Owlcat or even earlier Obsidian game. You only have 3 negative traits to choose from, perks are less imaginative than even Fallout 4, it's just a damage modifier here and a health buff there, no actual skills. 

So while I intended to keep the game, I just can't justify the expense due to the discrepancy between the asking price and the actual quality once you start to look closer. More and more people start gaming (bigger market), yet games get more and more expensive (remember: this was supposed to be the first 80€ PC game published by Xbox) and for some reason the equation is that we get less and less product. And THEN the companies wonder why no one buys their games anymore and they make the games even more expensive and rush them out even quicker with shifty marketing strategies. 

I truly think that doing Advanced Access on a weekend just feels morally wrong. With so many upcoming blockbuster games and with a cramped workweek, I felt basically forced to buy the premium fee (yes, I also get DLCs but Obsidian is not really known for doing banger DLCs) to have some role-playing fun this weekend. Alas, it isn't so much fun after all because I feel kinda short-changed.
Interesting so far, but easy even on very hard. Something like survival/hardcore mode woould be awesome.

There are tons of HUD optimizations ond options so you can play the way you like it (arcady, full immersion or in-between). I wish all games gave me that many options.

Voice acting is great, dialogues are snappy and dark humoured satire doesn't fall short.

Exploration is actually rewarded (Yes, I'm looking at you, Starfield!). 90% chance that there is some good weapon, recipe or armor waiting for you when you stray off the path. Side-quests are relevant and motivating, you'll miss some of the best items or even a companion if you skip them (Yes, I'm looking at you, UBISOFT!).
This is the first game in a very long time in which I am willing to search every location from top to bottom without getting bored.

There are already 50+ great mods for all aspects of gameplay on nexus.

And...

There are the whiny little b**ches trying to spoil the fun for the others, e.g.  "The game is "woke"; There are too many women in the game; The characters are ugly; There is no breast jiggling; Oh, no, there is a Black companion as your first choice. Booo f**king hooo.
 

I'm not saying you should spend 30 more bucks for 5 days of early access and DLC that isn't even announced yet, but the regular version is completely worth it.

Outer Worlds 2 is a 9 out of 10 
for me
and this kinda comes as a surprise since the first game wasn't that great tbh.
as an (oldschool and old) Obsidian-Fan (Pillars of Eternity and especialy Tyranny) i liked the game, but i could clearly see all the issues and frankly didn't every feel the need or ability to re-play it

so i started 2 with low expectations and i chose a more combat-focused build, cause combat and the moment-to-moment gameplay of the first game was realy not that great
and dear god is it fun
it is not Borderlands 4 levels of FPS (or fully developed 3rd person) greatness, but for a very indepth/deep rpg it is fantastic
they definitly looked at the critic(s) of the first game and what they learned/used with Avowed and upped their game by a lot...and i do mean a lot. 
combat and interacting with the game-world feels tight, responsive, fluid and is just plain fun. i am kinda glad i went out on combat for my first playthrough cause every encounter (game starts after the titel-card) is just fun

then there is their doubling down on the rpg-aspect
pretty much every attribute of the rpg-formula has seen extensive QA, improvement and just more of everything
perks come usualy with multiple effects, some for combat, some for exploration or conversation, etc. 
they feed into nigh every game-aspect and mechanic
and the same goes for skills
"obeservation" (think perception) as a skill for example grants you more weakspot damage, but will also allow you to find hidden-stuff via kinda a "mini-game", but it also comes into play during conversation (think skill-check) and will unlock perks that fit the topic (like finding more loot, getting better/more crits, etc.)

and this extends to the quest design. there might be a place with a locked door, now you could have lockpicking, etc. to just open the door, or you could have some speech-skill to convince someone else to open it or find a terminal that might open it via hacking, but you might also find a guide-book for doors and now you can open it (or you find some complete alternate path)

then there is the work they put into macking the 3D-rpg-experience better. there is a seemingly none-existant transition between ingame, pre-rendered cutscenes and actual gameplay; or npcs will walk in on conversations (or out)
random npc-chatter feels a lot more natural and the gameworld at it's whole doesn't feel so "blocky" anymore

the writing is not just great as is kinda trait when it comes to Obsidian, but they respect you as a player (and your IQ). there is no hours of explanation for everything, no mountains of tutorials for the most basic things.
much of the writing also works (even more) on sub-text, or subtle hints. less in your face cause our places are braindead.
they realy pushed the "look at an ideology or problem/fact" from all sides, which are usualy more than just 2
kinda the gamefication of "you can have more than two thoughts active at the same time"

running on a i9, Win11 (sadly), a 4080 OC and 32GB i had no issues running at 1080p (without DLSS) or higher resolutions with DLSS, but i also skip RTX (the benefits rarely offset the cost for me personaly). it is an Unreal game so keep that in mind; i now the the dreaded crash-cycle from Borderlands 3 all to well. 
they definitly went all in on the art; armors look a lot more detailed, almost filigran (like Cleo's heavyier armors), the gameworld has tons of enviromental detail (heck even the skybox) and lots of quite high-quality art (like advertisements, ingame paintings, etc.) and has thankfully turned out to be less styelized than in the first game. the general art-design and color-work in the first game actualy gave me headaches from time to time.
it looks quite pretty and the same goes for the effects; the first time i used electric ammunition to fry mutated crabs i was quite pleasantly surprised about the work they put into improving the effects

even the U.I. has seen extensive work done. is it perfect? certainly not, but the map, etc. is actualy quite usable this time around

about the only nitpick i have is a) some minor glitches (food items in your inventory for example) and b) the companion A.I. in Avowed felt kinda "better" but was also pretty standout in general

i would probably give the game a 10/10 but then it will have to keep up for multiple playthroughs, which is to early to say

The Outer Worlds 2 is definitely an improvement over TOW1. Where TOW1 tried too hard to be funny and make tongue in cheek jabs at every opportunity got became old fast, TOW2 dials that back a lot (while still retaining plenty of humor and absurd moments) for a more serious, grounded experience. I have played 8 hours of it so far, and am enjoying it a lot. The RPG mechanics feel like they have significantly more depth to them, and the game feels tighter and more polished.

The gunplay is satisfying and feels good, and so far the enemies have been fun to fight. I find the 'hard' mode to actually *be* challenging, and require at least a semblance of basic tactics and remembering to use your companion abilities. 

It feels like skills matter more, there *are* places you simply can't access and dialogue options you won't be able to pick without putting points into engineering/lockpicking/whatever. You *will* miss content because there simply aren't enough skill points to build a character that can do everything, and I appreciate that the devs are willing to let players miss things. That said, The devs have put a lot of work into ensuring that you have workarounds for many things. A check in dialogue can often be bypassed if you've found information on terminals or notes or by speaking to people.

The story is a bit cliche for sure, but the writing so far has been enjoyable and I never felt like there was anything I outright rolled my eyes at for being dumb or anything.

The game runs very well in 1440p on my 7800x3d and 4070 super system, I hit a stable 100 fps in the open world with ray tracing off, DLSS quality, and no frame gen, on mix of high + very high settings. I have run into a couple bugs, but no softlocks or anything that actually affected progression or gameplay.

Overall, game good.

This game is a mixed bag, half empty.

For a Sci-fi FPS RPG in the realm of Bethesda type games, Outerworlds 2 checks most of the qualifying boxes on a base level.  It runs well. It's still quirky. Plenty of weapons, armor, and ammunition with mods and crafting recipes to go with them. No carry weight makes exploring and collecting effortless. A pro for those more focused on the story but a con for those looking for more immersive game play. Companions who may fall flat in a conversation but have effective combat abilities. Multiple planets that offer replay ability throughout not just the main story but faction and side quests. A wide selection of perks to build off of your skills which harmonize into your late game build. You still have a handful of ways to beat the game with different endings so you get the sense that you're crafting your story and not just following it.

This game is not the "new New Vegas." The story itself is not long or unique. Very basic tropes, and the ones copied from the first game were made shallow in comparison. The game tries to take itself more serious but as a result ends up being pretty mellow during a space-war between multiple factions while the world is ending. Enemies copied and pasted from the first game with no sign of anything new until the later half of the game. Inventory UI is abhorrent. 3rd person doesn't feel great-- from either distance. Not supporting PS5 controller hotkey notifications in 2025 is pretty stupid. Especially after Xbox just allowed PS access to GoW and Halo. Armor designs extremely bland (until the very end game where you don't care anymore) and no real reason to use any of the armor that doesn't give you a health bonus if you're picking up flaws. The game moves too slow without flaws, and not in the direction you want it to with them. The fact that there's no tree to manipulate the flaws since the first game seems a little silly. 

I tried to have hope. I loved the first game and was severely disappointed after influencers kept barking that this is "better than the first." While it did have it's moments, the story fell flat more often than not being predictable. Outside of the weapons (which I genuinely loved) it doesn't feel like anything was built upon. And you could argue they didn't even build on weapons because they were great in the first game.
I don't think it was awful-- I would've quit sooner if that was the case. Would wait til it's on sale to try it. Experiencing stories like this makes me look back at games like Starfield/Borderlands 4 and appreciate them much more.

Camera and character controls feel under cooked. Moving around in the game is stiff and unnatural. Poorly implemented stealth mechanics. The game has an Interesting story and great world building but the game play feels miserable, like its lacking depth and makes it difficult to enjoy.  Dev team should have taken an extra 6-12 months grounding the player character in the world and adding basic QL features to movement & camera controls. 

With the flop of Avowed it feels like Obsidian is really good at writing great stories but struggles with creating interesting gameplay.

Combat feels more solid and smooth, and the extra gadgets make a nice bonus, healings handled different so you cant just spam heal all the time, or it gets blocked out for a while.

Update, haven't noticed any issues since turning ray tracing back off. That may have been the culprit for the limited performance stutters I was experiencing on occasion. 

I really enjoyed The Outer Worlds and was very excited for a sequel after completing it. Then Obsidian released some other games that made me lose confidence in their ability to make The Outer Worlds 2 not suck. Thankfully, six hours in, the humor still seems to be there and I've been having a lot of fun. I mean, any game that roasts me for buying their premium edition by suggesting the flaw of "consumerism" is going to be my cup of tea. The RPG elements seem strong, gun play is ok (though I really wish this game had a cover system), and dialogue is long winded, but often funny, especially if you make your Captain a smart-ass. 

One negative is that there seems to be an issue with ray tracing and I'm not sure if it's the games fault or a Nvidia driver issue, but I primarily noticed it in the opening  area, where their was a heavy grain type appearance on a lot of surfaces. I haven't really notice it much since leaving the first area, but I may have kept ray tracing off when fiddling with settings  to see what was causing it. 

It also seems to have other negatives that come with Unreal Engine games. There are severe frame drops at times, I think due to shaders continuing to compile when first launching into an area, but I'm not a game dev, so could be completely wrong. Overall performance doesn't seem bad, but there seems to be headroom for more optimization. I'm playing on a 5090 and up-scaling to 4k still seems to be a necessity. So mileage may vary. 

So far, I'm enjoying it, but will update if that changes for any reason.

I was so hyped when The Outer Worlds was released back in 2019. A true RPG set in space with the feel to New Vegas. I loved absolutely everything about the first game, be it the characters, the weapons, the writing, the universe and lore, and the tone and atmosphere. When I think of the RPGs I played growing up, that one in particular, stands at the top of the pillars with its cult classic predecessors.

When the sequel was announced, I was super excited to set foot back in the stars again. I didn't follow the marketing as close as I had the first game, just the big trailers, but I knew that I needed it. 

I have only put around 6 hours into the game, including a restart after mere moments of stepping down onto Paradise, but the little I have played has made me incredibly happy to be a RPG fan. I can feel the New Vegas inspiration, the same dark humor and the beautiful atmosphere both from the first installment.

And it's absolutely GORGEOUS and runs like a champ!

If you loved the first game I highly recommend you pick this up, and If you love RPGs then you should jump in and explore a beautifully crafted world.

TLDR: If you are looking for what a game from Obsidian should be, this is a miss. Otherwise if you really don't care about the rich history and fall from grace this company has suffered, it's fine. 

First of all this is a game I really wanted to like and there is a lot here to like. However it falls short in a lot of ways. Firstly, and probably the worst issue, Unreal Engine 5. Everything wrong with UE5 rears its ugly head in this game. If you hate how UE5 makes games look, you'll hate it here. If you hate the stutters that come with UE5, you'll hate this game. It's done better than some, but I still truely believe any other engine looks better than UE5. Shadows and shimmering is as bad as any other pick of UE5 games but is still better than borderlands 4. However I will say the stuttering in this game is far worse than average. I have been having multiple multi second long hitches so far. Hoping that at least can be fixed in patches, although so many of the other issues are just because of UE5 that I am sick and ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ tired of this piece of ♥♥♥♥ engine ruining games.

Also, I unfortunately had my game bug out pretty early on where after first encountering Milverstreet. I didn't think this was a big deal but apparently talking to him is how you recruit one of the first findable companions, Inez. I didn't know this at the time and continued playing as normal since there was a second way to progress the story. After some later events I am now completely unable to recruit Inez because I was never able to talk to Milverstreet any of the number of times I tried to... Really sucks to see such major bugs that early in the game. I mean it is literally like 40 minutes after the tutorial and that just ruined getting a companion for the whole game.

Now on to the game more as designed than technical issues. I honestly can't believe thy took the lessons they learned from fan feedback on the first game and implemented for the first games DLCs and threw that out the window. Armor stats? Meaningless besides just using what has the best armor value. There are no pieces of armor that improve stats outside of menial combat stats. Not only that, chems have been overhauled so much that calling them "Chems" Plural is being extraordinarily generous. Gone are chems or food that have any impact on stats. Now it's just chems that all get combined into one item that gets used in combat, or food that can be quick ate out of combat and has absolutely no effects other than to heal you. What an unimaginably stupid step backwards in gameplay. 

The reason all this really sucks is that they had some of this stuff right in the first game where it felt really good even if some of the rpg elements were a bit lacking. Now it feels like they took the slider all the way to the other side and forgot about combat and equipment status effects in rpgs. It really sucks because the shooting is good, the gun diversity as vastly improved compared to the first game. Dialogue and situations rely much more on your character's skills and perks than in the first game. This game feels less like "upgrade everything to open up the whole game" and more "chose the skills for the experience you want, while other stuff will be missed" which I GREATLY appreciate in an RPG. It's more open, even if UE5 really holds back performance as usual. The game is, much like the first game, just fine, albeit for completely different reasons. I however don't recommend it for 2 main reasons, the use of UE5 making every game look like ass, and the regression in gameplay mechanics that the first game had right. It boggles the mind that Microsoft initially wanted to charge $80 for this game. Even walked back to $70 I don't think it's ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ worth it. This is at best a $50 game with worse performance than most $50 games thanks to the use of UE5.

I for the life of me can not understand that logic or decision to have that feature in a game like this. Sure making named enemies and special encountered enemies not respawn can make decision making more impactful and meaning full because said character it is part of a story. But to have even the alien wild life or random human bandit/soldiers disappear forever just makes the game become a giant empty world that leaves very little reason to go back to areas and explore outside of main and side missions. Might as well have ditched the open world idea all together. Didn't finish and I don't recommend.

Many graphical and engine side issues. 

-Mouse sensitivity tied to FPS, lower FPS=lower mouse sens, higher FPS=higher mouse sens, changing frequently

-Shadows are splotchy and will not look good unless they are on very high and using DLSS on DLAA

-HDR does not work correctly

-Lack of settings like you would see in a Bethesda title, no way to directly turn off Depth of Field for example

-Lens flares are blinding and pixelated, can not be turned off

-Every issue with Unreal Engine 5 applies here

-Ray tracing does nothing but lower FPS

-Built in mouse smoothing and mouse acceleration with no way of turning it off

-HUD elements that are unnecessary take up the screen

-Hit registration issues

-Overall poor performance

I needed to rewrite this review as I saw more in the game than on the first hour of play.

The Outer Worlds 2 has expanded on the first title. This is where the developers have listen to the community and their issues on the first game and decided to try to fix it here. So far I think they have done a lot of good fixes and personally I don't see much that is a huge drain to me. Let me explain a bit more.

One of the changes they did was making secondary non-combat skills be more desirable. If you put something in Science for example then you'll have certain things unlocked for you in dialog and what is around the world. Your background is more visible as well. Combat isn't the main dish in this title, consider combat as the side dish with playing your role (AKA RPG) being the main dish. Do you want to role play as an engineer that got sucked into the role of captain? Go for it, there are plenty of engineering tasks you can do. Want to role play as an idiot who just happen to get the job by accident, go for it as you try to get more lucky in your adventure.

The flaw system is now, in my opinion, better because it is a give and punish system. You can build your character around the flaws you do pick and unlike the first title you don't just get a free perk. More balance I say, unless you pick the flaw that forces you to accept all flaws you get here on out (Hint: you don't have to accept that flaw). And a bonus, some flaws gives you dialog options as well so there is flavor to be had.

The backgrounds are more sounded than ever. Remember how Starfield backgrounds were pointless and were never brought up? Well in this game they can be brought up a lot and you don't have to pick it, the game lets you decide if you want to use that special dialog. The game also from the start shows you how people may talk to you differently based on which background you have went with, some funny, some minmax, and all of them towards role play.

The combat in the game isn't the main focus but it is still a challenge. If you went with hard difficulty then you can get KO'd pretty quick if you're reckless. Melee is pretty hard but it can work if you spend your points into Melee and Engineer with Medic on the side. Remember to use block and take cover. The combat isn't a "let me face tank everything until they die" as it does take some thought into how you want to handle the encounters. Do you want to grab some and pull them to another area with more cover? Do you want to go after the smaller weaker enemies first or last? Do you want to focus on the range enemies? A lot of choices and your allies abilities do matter and can make the combat easier.

The story. I would say the story is better than the first game as your character has a reason to follow through. I am not going to give any spoilers, I'll let someone else do that, but the writing is done pretty well and has made me thought more of who should be given the pass or just destroyed outright. Thought provoking choices that are gray instead of this is good and that is evil is something I enjoy. Makes me, personally, decide how I would really do things in this world.

I still recommend the game because it does have a lot to offer. If you ignore the people who want to see the studio burn to ash on the discussions and give it a try then maybe you'll like it too. If not, then refund it. There is no way to "take advantage" of a freedom you already have on this platform.

After 18 hours of gameplay, I think this game needs a fix—especially for the FSR and Nvidia DLSS settings. I believe there’s an issue with those two options that causes the game to crash. Once I turned them off, the game ran smoothly. That’s the only major issue that really needs to be fixed, and I hope they patch it on day one.

Gameplay, animation, dialogue, and the world design are all really well done—there’s a lot of improvement compared to The Outer Worlds. On the other hand, the companion characters are very interesting to follow, though it’s a bit disappointing that you can’t romance them; it would’ve been a nice addition.

Aside from that, the gunplay is excellent, and there are plenty of cool elements in the game. Each planet has its own unique vibe, which I really liked. I’m just a bit confused why so many people complain that the game feels similar to Avowed or Vailguard. For me, the companion character models might look a bit rough, but still acceptable. The story is amazing, and you can even play as a complete psychopath if you want.

Anyway, for me, I’d rate it an 8.5/10. It’s a good game, but I’d suggest waiting for a discount—it’s still too expensive for the current price.

[h1]The Outer Worlds 2 does not start with its best foot forward.[/h1]

Just to be clear on where I was approaching this game from, I'm something of an Outer Worlds 1 defender. I honestly enjoyed the game quite a bit and think it was treated a little harshly by some critics in retrospect. 

The opening hour/s of TOW2 has not sold me, and I just don't have the time to persevere to see if it improves 10 hours later, especially for Obsidian who have not exactly been 'killing it' recently, particularly in the RPG department.

The game's visuals look wonderful. It looks to have much the same humour and charm of the first game, so if that's what drew you to the first game then this might work for you too.
Unfortunately the areas in which the game is immediately dropping the ball, are those areas core to creating a reactive RPG world which responds to and rewards your leaning into a role.

For starters, straight off the bat the first in-game character you talk to, Niles, immediately presents some questionable voice acting. Both the audio quality of the voice and the direction for the actor sound second-rate. Everyone else I interacted with throughout the opening seemed to sound fine, so that isn't necessarily a death blow, but it was still not a good leading moment.

Where I stopped playing however, was the opening mission. To summarise the series of events that led to me exiting and requesting a refund:

[*][h3]Cannot kill or even injure your own squadmates[/h3] 
Yes, kill everyone/psychopath runs in RPG's are stupid quirk chungus ♥♥♥♥ and ruin the experience. No, I don't need it to be possible to be satisfied. It is, however, a good early game litmus test for the freedom you're going to have. Lest we forget, one of the most popular points of contrast people will bring up in the New Vegas vs FO3 debate is the lack of essential NPC's in NV. Here, the first 5 characters you're forced to play with straight away have practically no reaction to you dumping mags in their mouth.

[*][h3]Stealth/hacking punished[/h3]
One of the first issues you come across is an active security system. Turrets and drones block the path forward. I had just so happened to spec early into hacking and stealth, so hoped that I'd be rewarded for sneaking the far way around the arena to disable the machines. Surely it would at least be an equivalent outcome, if not better.
Nope. 
Instead, I received 20% of the xp and none of the loot I would've gotten for just destroying the bots. Not to mention the fact that it actually [b]cost[/b] me in the form of the expended lockpick and hacking tool.
Now, I don't need every option to be rewarded exactly equally. Prey is one of my all-time favourite games, and it rewards different approaches extremely disparately. However, when the approach requiring the most skill and resource investment is actively detrimental to you, something's really wrong.

[*][h3]No reaction to my chosen method[/h3]
After reloading to try all-out combat, stealth and hacking, and non-lethal stealth to get past the security and being disappointed by the results, I then found that the following cutscene had absolutely no regard for the decision you had made. You're suddenly on the other side of the door with your whole team in tow. I guess, even if you sneak past and leave all the turrets active, your squad just Solid Snake that ♥♥♥♥ with a cardboard box right behind you. No comments made. No acknowledgement of your agency. No recognition of a silent approach, nor ramifications for going guns blazing.

[*][h3]Immediate hand-holding[/h3]
In the next conversation, I elected to split from my team for a solo stealth endeavour. "Great," they said. "Let's split up. You take the sneaky job," they said.
The second the conversation ended, one of those very same characters was immediately speaking to me over the radio, explaining in-depth how I should approach the stealth mission.
That was it for me. I'd closed the game before the needless, condescending tutorialisation was over.

I hope this gives some idea of why I just don't care to see whether it gets better later. You lead with your best foot forward, and there are too many other great games out there right now vieing for attention. If deep, responsive RPG is what you're after, I can't imagine The Outer Worlds 2 is going to fill that gap.

This game is a colossal mixed bag with, thankfully, potential for improvement in almost all negative aspects of it. On one hand, this is a clear upgrade over the original Outer Worlds which felt more like a prototype or a demo of a much bigger and better game. The RPG system is much more robust now and provides a lot of meaningful (and meaningless as well) choices, the plot (and location) progression makes more sense, the combat is… well, it's alright — and actually pretty challenging on the highest difficulty. I also want to commend the voice acting, it's pretty good. Even the least important characters sound with conviction and effort, excellent job on that part.

Unfortunately, all this goodness is absolutely swarming with bugs (this is a game by Obsidian, after all), which range from benign, such as subtitles disappearing mid-sentence, to frustrating (distraction grenades have tendency to fall through the floor or straight up not working at all, and your character keeps getting stuck in tight corners, especially if elevations are involved), to game-breaking (if you notice that other characters talk like they've never seen you before, just restart the entire game. Your story progression is broken now and it's just a matter of time before you run into a dead end. And what sucks is that it works backwards as well, your entire character is busted). Day one (or, I guess, day minus four) patch was, I think, six gigabytes, and surely a lot more coming. Better wait a while.

But on top of that, the game is also ugly as all hell. And I'm not even talking about basic fidelity, although noisy shadows and whatnot are very distracting — and that's on highest settings. No, I'm talking about the visual impression as a whole, because it feels like no one was in charge of the outdoor areas. It's like there's no lighting at all, and distant objects lack any post-effects that would give them a sense of scale. Traversing this game feels like you're back at CTF_Face, and you're always one step away from falling into the muddy space texture. Ground looks very rough and has strange green-ish outline around it (especially visible opposite the skybox), it all just looks weird and unfinished. Oh, and have I mentioned screen tearing? Don't play this game without vsync. On the bright side, performance-wise it's not as bad as I expected, and the slowdowns are much less noticeable compared to, say, Borderlands 4 (which just stops dead at points), so there's that.

So, I don't know. I want to play this game more but I have to start it all over with no guarantee that my new playthrough wouldn't get wasted. The fact that I spent seventeen hours on my no longer usable character fills me with contempt and anger. And because of that, I can't recommend OW2 in its current state.

First couple trailers don't accurately represent how good this game is.

To those saying "Everyone is a WAMEN" or spamming "WOKE" in the reviews and Discussions, the reality is pretty close to a 50/50 split in my experience, (With actually great story arcs for male characters, so I don't know what they're on about).

For Companions:
As I only have 15 hours in the game I can't speak to the whole thing, but the starting companion Niles that you get is great, has depth, and it's interesting to try to mold his character an underling or coach him as a new agent. I've found a lot of depth, and the dialogue choices I've made (Wayyy back at the first 5 mins) have resurfaced in conversations so what you say DOES matter. I passed on the second companion because she didn't fit the vibe of my playthrough, but so far I'm very impressed with how your choices and how you treat people matter, and change outcomes.

Gameplay:
So far I'm loving exploring all the tiny details of the handmade maps. Reading interesting tidbits in terminals actually gives you hidden details for where you can find special loot or secret areas, and opens up special dialogue or interaction options elsewhere in your playthrough. Even seemingly random encounters with wild animals and a couple of bodies allowed me to let a military commander know his scouts hadn't made it where they needed to go. So random exploring and non quest events have meaning too, which is lovely. Looting, sneaking, and trying to weave your way through different dialogue options is fun, and crafting is actually useful (though you will want to visit shops to buy new recipes asap to widen your options).

Combat:
The only slightly meh or troublesome part of this game. Gun play is alright, and can be fun if you have the right weapon or effect. I'm not sure if it's just a lack of oomph behind your shots, or what but it doesn't feel quite as satisfying as other games. Melee is okay, but with the healing system in this I would recommend keeping your distance and letting your followers take the up close and personal action.

Summary:
They've definitely put in the work, and done their due diligence in writing out branching paths.
Also... with those 15 hours, I've only just gone to the second planet cause there's so much to explore and do. Seeing TONS of replayability here as well. Even if you save and choose different dialogue, the actions you do have so much weight across the whole game that you'll want another run to hit the alternate paths for sure.

Despite having just started, I've seen my background and skills come into play, seamlessly, more than once. I mean, you start on your ship and your cabin door is jammed and there are like three different ways to get in; if that small scenario and the choices offered to solve it, is representative of the variety in the rest of the game, I feel I'm in for a great time.

I've also seen myself locked out of multiple options due to character build and with no way to respec (a positive, if you ask me--I like games that make you stick with and think about your choices) I can see new playthroughs in the future. I heard someone say that this feels a lot like New Vegas, and I can see that. If you're a fan of older Obsidian titles and have been waiting for a return to form, this looks like it may be the ticket. I'll update this review and give a final analysis once I finish the game, but as of now, I reccomend it.

[b] Swapped my review from "Recommended" to "Not Recommended". Lost 20+ hours worth of save after Hotfix 1.0.4.1 Patch was fixed. [/b]

[i]Don't mind my playtime too much. I "YoHo" just about every big release because I'm not getting burned again. It's how I dodged that absolute F-Tier dumpster fire Avowed and its cringefest dialogue. It's also how I dodged the first Outer Worlds which felt like a crappy James Gunn movie where every single line had to be a "gag".

Glad to report OW2 fixes this. It knows when to be serious and throws in jokes at the right times. I tried this one and it was so good I actually bought it.[/i]

[hr][/hr]

[h1]MASSIVE WARNING ON PERFORMANCE[/h1]

Be warned: this game runs [b]HEAVY[/b]. This is the average UE5 crap that's killing the entire gaming industry. I'm on a 9800x3D, 128GB of RAM, a Gen 4 NVME and a [b]5090 RTX[/b] on an Ultrawide 1440p monitor. Even with settings at Very High, DLSS on High and [u]RTX turned OFF[/u], my rig is still seeing drops below 90 FPS.

I however do not get the stuttering that other reviews report.

If my machine is struggling, your low-end or even mid-range PC is going to get [b]pretty cooked[/b]. Beating this UE5 jank with good hardware is ludicrously expensive. 

I had to turn Ray Tracing off and it's one of those rare games where RTX actually makes a [i]huge[/i] difference, especially in dark stealth sections. It makes me think the base lighting is just horrible and RTX is the band-aid. It's honestly a shame. So you're either getting a slideshow or a much worse-looking game. Consider yourself warned.

Please be aware the first area with your Earth Directorate crew is not representative of the average performanc in the game! I lost around 40 FPS from a constant frame-limited 144 FPS to ~105 FPS as the game goes "Open World" mode.

[hr][/hr]

[h1]It's Outer Worlds++ and That's Exactly What We Needed[/h1]

Haven't rolled credits yet but I've played enough to know the deal.
This isn't some mind-blowing revolution. It's Outer Worlds++ but that's exactly what makes it so good.

If you liked the old one, you'll absolutely love this one. If you liked the vibe of the old ones but were disappointed with the choices, freedom, exploration and combat, give this a try. "YoHo" it, there's no Denuvo. 

Obsidian took a hard look at [b]EVERYTHING[/b] that was janky, boring or just plain mediocre about the first game and fixed it. They turned that AA proof-of-concept into the EXTREMELY solid package it was always meant to be.

It feels like a 2.5A game. Not AAA, not AA.

[h1]A Proper RPG This Time[/h1]

OW1 felt like a linear jankfest pretending to be an RPG. That's gone. Thank god.

[list]
[*]This ACTUALLY feels like an RPG. The writing is sharper, choices have way more weight and you're not just walking down a series of hallways. You have freedom. 
[*]Your build matters. Every skill feels like it opens up a real playstyle. Speech and Engineering do open up qutie a decent amount of non-combat paths.
[*]They fixed that [i]insane[/i] head-scratcher from OW1 where companion abilities stopped combat cold. Gunplay is fast, has decent weight and you never lose the flow. It's still not Battlefield 6 or CoD but it's very serviceable.
[*]Companions are interesting (except Val - she's still a dud and Inez is kind of an unlikable b*tch). They feel like they belong in the world but I don't find that there is an equivalent to Pavarti or Vicar Max. So on average they are better, but the best of the best is worst than the old best of the best.
[/list]

[h1]Builds, Flaws and Balance[/h1]

The character system is where this game goes S-Tier on. Well that and the dialogue. The Flaws system is fantastic. They're not just minor debuffs! They are game-changing and novel trade-offs.

[b]WORD OF WARNING:[/b] Do [b]NOT[/b] take "Flawed" or "Foot-in-mouth syndrome" on your first run. They are absolutely brutal. You were warned.

That said, it's not perfectly balanced (but what RPG is?):

[list]
[*] [b]Traits:[/b] [b]Lucky[/b] and [b]Brainy[/b] are S-Tier, heads above the rest. On the flip side, [b]Sickly[/b] and [b]Dumb[/b] are manageable but [b]Abrasive[/b] is just crippling. Avoid unless 2nd playthrough.
[*] [b]Skills:[/b] The holy trinity of [b]Guns, Lockpick and Speech[/b] still runs the show. No surprise there.
[*] [b]Weaknesses:[/b] The game still has a clear bias against melee. Which really sucks. There's just not enough weapon variety to make it fun compared to the huge arsenal of guns. Sneak REQUIRES a Sneak Specialization and a Weapon Specialization in what you're sneaking in for 1-shot kills. Gadgets are also pretty mediocre which makes sinking points into [b]Science[/b] feel like a waste.
[/list]

Is it Fallout: New Vegas caliber? No. But it's its own thing and that thing is a massive improvement that finally hits the right targets. It's a fantastic RPG and an easy recommend... [b]if you have the hardware for it and I MUST stress that part again.[/b]

[h1]Final Rating & Recommendation[/h1]
My final score breaks down based on value:
[list]
[*] [b]8/10 at Full Price[/b]
[*] [b]8.5/10 at 30% Off[/b]
[*] [b]9/10 at 50% Off[/b]
[/list]

I can actually recommend this at [b]full price if you're an RPG Addict that likes Obsidian.[/b]
For the [b]average gamer, I can only recommend it at the very least at 30% off.[/b]
At [b]50% off, this game becomes a "no-brainer"[/b] purchase.

The flaws system is a ton of fun. The game will track how you're playing and will occasionally introduce optional, but permanent, "flaws" you can apply to your character to either encourage or discourage a mixup in your playstyle. For example, I don't use my medkit all that often as I prefer using companion skills or food outside of battle to heal up. The game popped up a flaw that was like "you don't use your medkit all that much. Do you want to take a flaw that gives you +25% medkit healing, but also +10% more medkit toxicity?" I took it because when I do use my medkit, I get a bunch of extra healing. Toxicity doesn't really apply to me since I don't spam heal.

This game improves upon every mechanic the first introduced, at the cost of performance, optimization, and some bugs. I will first list my pros followed by my cons.

--------------------

[b]Pros:[/b]

It has satisfying gunplay, interesting exploration including platforming, and interesting new factions that aren't just a rehash of old ones. The maps are large, allow for significant freedom of where to go next, and I like the area design.

The leveling system has mostly been improved - stats are gone, while skills have been redone to be less number-crunchy and there are a ton more perks to choose.

Weapon variety is much more interesting and weapon level has mostly gone away. There are still superior weapons that you will find, but they are simply different weapons and not a "Mk2" of a previous one. Grenades are incredible fun, and science gadgets are now a separate mechanic entirely. There are many seriously satisfying weapons, especially the unique weapons.

All of the companions are really fun and interesting to have around. They have deeply personal quests of their own that allow you to shape them based on how you respond to their issues and concerns, allowing for substantial character growth. I also enjoy the simplified companion leveling system and the mechanic for upgrading their gear (they now have predefined weapons and armor).

[b]Cons:[/b]

The most immediate con is an engine issue - UE5 is just incredibly unstable at this level of fidelity. You will need a very top-of-the-line machine to play at max settings at high resolutions, while utilizing DLSS or other upscaling options.

[strike]There are numerous bugs that begin to pop up as you proceed in the game. Others have pointed out the weird bugs with dialogue options refreshing as if you had never spoken to NPCs before, however I have not experienced any issues with soft locking or otherwise negative effects beyond the minor inconvenience of not knowing which dialogue I hadn't selected before. I have also noticed issues with shadows in the game often appearing as specks of black that flicker in and out, rather than solid shadows. This seems to happen randomly, but infrequently.[/strike] These bugs appear to be resolved, at least in my game.

[strike]My last con is that a couple of the side quests are deeply infuriating to complete. One I did was an overly long fetch quest, with too-complicated methods for completing objectives, while another railroaded me into a single outcome despite glaring opportunity for my character to take a different route - if only the writers had allowed it. This leads me to not want to finish that side content, which is incredibly disappointing. I hope this gets better as I go to other areas and will update this to reflect it if so. This is probably my biggest complaint right now.[/strike] This turned out to only be an issue with a couple quests, which were both part of a chain that had alternate avenues for progressing - so I consider this a non-issue.

--------------------

All told, I am extremely pleased with how much of an improvement this is over the 1st game. They have found great ways to fix a lot of the issues I had with the first and have made it a really fun time.

While the story isn't that interesting the way your stats and perks come into play in and out of conversations is the best it's been in any RPG in a long while.

The Outer Worlds 2 is very much if The Outer Worlds and Fallout: New Vegas had a baby.
Long story short if you liked both The Outer Worlds and FNV, you should give The Outer Worlds 2 a shot.

Your words, actions, and choices, even seemingly insignificant ones, do alter not only gameplay options available to you, but also who might come around to help you down the line. One seemingly small act of kindness in the very first mission comes back later in the main quest to help you out. The game is also not shy to remind you "This will be remembered" when you hit those dialogue choices.

Speaking of companions there are some pro and con changes from the first game::
Pros: companions can heal you when you drop to 0HP (w/ the proper item in your inventory), companions seem to deal more damage, and some can provide helpful skills like crafting while in your party. It's kind of nice to have a companion keeping track of your ammo and reminding you they are available to help craft more munitions when you are low. Companions also have a threshold point where they will leave if they lose trust in you so mind what you say and do (or don't do even)!
Cons: you cannot change their equipment (defaults suit each character well and can be upgraded), acquisition of companions is also slower/more spread out (25hrs+ and barely getting my 4th companion.), companions no longer skill buff your PC for skill checks out in the world (though they do bolster in other ways).

Combat is improved from the previous entry. Grenades and distraction devices can shift the tide and you have some new fun toys to play with other than a TTD. Stealth kills + dissolving bodies makes for prime stealth missions! It is also nice to be able to revive companions however many times you need and are able.

It's been loads of fun thus far and performs well overall!

This is after the tutorial and exploring the massive amount of territory of the first mission. 14 hours.  There's little collection quests, interesting side plots to get some of your starter tech and even little bits to help you understand some of the gameplay additions like Decryption Keys.

Like the original Outer Worlds this is from the people who made the original Fallout, even if Cain only worked with Obsidian as a creative consultant as he retired from gaming in 2020, and it has all of the hallmarks of the writing of Cain and Boyarsky.  The bitter cynicism mixed with gallows humor, the commentary on how social frameworks have shown to develop into and that feeling of being a person who is on the outside looking into the monstrosity of civilization that a lot of people often have before hunching their shoulders and pushing through.

Right off the bat it's hard to determine if you really are 'the good guy' by the opening footage. It suggests you are. Perhaps I'll remain hopeful of such, but some of the lines in trailers years back during the development hint at the underlying 'problems' of massive governing bodies.  But also you are faced with the normative situation of deciding which polished turd is the better for the people caught in the middle.  
The corporations created in the original Outer Worlds who drive their people into a loop of maximizing efficiency, adopting the darker portions of what is morally gray, and cutthroat business practice that is day today life.

On the other? The Protectorate, a seeming monarchy that does not follow the real life 'Dei Gratia' (Chosen by God) of classically recognized western monarchies, or the Arahitogami/Devaraja/Tianzi/Pharaoh of Near East/Eastern monarchies but have essentially placed themselves as gods without using the term and subjugate their people into accepting or even welcoming that they are the soil (literally called dirt) the foundation is built on and that they are cogs in a machine that needs them to survive (though it is clear to an outsider that is just a platitude that the brainwashed populace accept and believe by and large).
 
There is the evil we're familiar with from the Corporatists. There's new layers of evil that we see in the Protectorate. And like in the original Fallout 1/2 and New Vegas - what is the most survivable evil in your opinion?

Companions are more well rounded, and they were already amazing (except Felix, screw you Felix. You shallow, 2D, Anarchist wannabe) in the original Outer Worlds. Even the robot feels fleshed out.  My only gripe is I haven't found a command to have companions hold position like you could in the original and often they seem dead set on charging in and the conflict becomes you wishing you could get them back on their feet but to do so means charging into a description of a WWI No-Man's-Land, making them less useful for the MAJOR battles. But that's a pretty small gripe when you look at the overall scope.

Gameplay is fun, there are multiple routes to solve conundrums or if there's only one route there are sprinklings of little extras one can do based on their skills to accomplish their goals that can't be done if you don't have the talent for it. Some areas feel a little on rails, but when you step back you realize that there are parts of one's day to day that can feel like that. So even when forced a certain way, it doesn't feel bad.

[h1]The Ghost of Obsidian[/h1]

[b]TL;DR: A technically unstable, structurally constrained RPG that falls short of the studio’s legacy.[/b]

Obsidian once set the standard for narrative-driven RPGs, but this latest effort feels like a diminished echo of that legacy. While the studio’s signature writing remains competent, the overall experience is hampered by uneven balance, restrictive progression, and technical instability.

The early game highlights these issues immediately. The first post-tutorial planet introduces overly difficult enemies that enforce a rigid, linear quest order and discourage organic exploration — a stark contrast to the open-ended freedom found in titles like Fallout: New Vegas or even Fallout 1 and 2. The skill system compounds the problem: its opaque design demands heavy investment in single skills for them to be meaningful, and with no respec option beyond the initial transition out of the tutorial, early choices can feel punishing rather than empowering.

Companion AI is another recurring frustration. Allies frequently walk into gunfire, get themselves killed, and obstruct the player’s line of sight, issues made worse by the game’s numerous narrow and highly linear environments. These problems are exacerbated by bugs — including, in my case, a corrupted save that required rolling back nearly ten hours of progress.

For context, I have played and completed Fallout 1–4, Cyberpunk 2077, and Fallout: New Vegas at release. All of them had rough edges, but each remained fundamentally playable from start to finish. This title, however, is the first in decades where technical and structural issues actively interfered with my ability to complete a straightforward initial run.

Ultimately, despite flashes of the Obsidian of old, the game struggles to deliver a cohesive or compelling experience.

The world building and world design is stellar. LOTS of things to find, explore and discover. The sound design is BETTER this time around with voice acting really great.Some of the conversations actually had me LOLing for real. Funny stuff. 

I'm not going to get into the nitty gritty of the game, you'll have to discover that for yourself.
