Fantastic! Possibly Obsidian's best game to date. It's bigger and better than the previous game in every way and feels like a return-to-form like New Vegas.

The combat feels great, the weapon variety is large, with lots of Unique variants that have different looks, stats and gameplay mechanics. The weapon viewmodel animations are very nice and detailed, and the way you interact with skill-checks in the world is cool, with the character actually doing engineering and pulling gears and fuses out, hacking makes you plug in cartridges and download money, chemistry actually mixes things in a beaker, etc. Makes it feel really polished and immersive as you explore and interact with the world.

The humour and writing is great, and has made me laugh quite a lot. Though it's like every character in the game, including the lowly farmers, have a thesaurus stuck in their throat; constantly spouting the most eloquent conversationables repeatedly. Feels weird and makes it hard to relate to any characters when they all sound like a robotic Shakespeare. Nothing ever really sounds natural, though I guess that's part of the exaggerated satire in some way.

Ammo can be surprisingly hard to come by, which forces you to rotate through different weapon types or buy ammo at vendors before you head out to do a quest. It's refreshing to have resources that matter in an open-world RPG like this, and I get a little excited whenever I find a box of ammo for a weapon I like to use. The mod system is also nice, being able to attach suppressors and different scopes to weapons, which visibly change the appearance and stats. Good stuff.

Every quest so far has had 4-5 ways to approach it, with every faction / character offering their own opinion on what to do. It feels really open and lets you change the outcomes in a lot of ways. 

Graphically, it uses all the Unreal Engine 5 bells and whistles, with hardware ray tracing for accurate reflections, shadows and global illumination. It looks fantastic if you have the hardware to run it. On a 4080 and i7 14700K, with everything maxed at 2560x1440, it's running at 120-160fps 90% of the time and feels great. The model and texture quality on all the armour, weapons and objects in the world is beautiful. The varied environments / planets are also very nice to look at and explore.

If you enjoyed the first game but found it a bit shallow or repetitive, this has a lot more to offer.

Honestly, the improved combat alone makes this an improvement over the first game. I liked OW1, but combat was definitely its weakest link; not so in OW2. Weapons actually feel satisfying to use, and the way you build your character has more of an effect on combat. I'm finding it fun.

If you liked OW1, then OW2 is more of the same but better.

EDIT: Now that I've finished the game, I do have some criticisms.

- They hyped up factions prior to the game release, but there are only two factions you can pick from; The Order of the Ascendant and Auntie's Choice. You can also opt to have them work together, although I didn't find the ending slide results of that choice as satisfying as I'd like. You cannot side with the Protectorate, nor go independent like New Vegas.

- The level cap is 30, and you only get 62 skill points (64 if you pick Brilliant as a trait); skills max out at 20. The skill checks later in the game mean you can't afford to do jack-of-all-trades, so you're better off maxing three skills. I found this rather annoying, because I've always liked being able to lockpick in these kinds of games, and I couldn't do that at all.

After 25 hours of dedicated play, I can confidently recommend The Outer Worlds 2. It masterfully maintains much of the charm that made the original so beloved, while simultaneously introducing a narrative that feels significantly more serious and impactful.

I can already tell this is a game I'll be playing multiple times. The revamped skill and perk systems are phenomenal, allowing for completely different approaches to almost every in-game challenge. I'm genuinely excited to see just how drastically these varied builds affect different story outcomes.

Performance on a Modest Rig
For those concerned with hardware: I run a modern, yet modest, system—a 9600x and a 9060xt—and the game runs wonderfully. The one major exception is Hardware Raytracing, which is unfortunately completely unusable. Attempting to enable it causes the game to crash consistently upon entering the inventory. However, outside of those raytracing-induced failures, I have experienced zero other crashes or significant performance dips.

Final Verdict (and a Friendly Threat)
If you disagree with my review, then perhaps you need to be sent to... Mental Refreshment to rid yourself of these... troubling thoughts...

In all seriousness, if you liked the first one, you will absolutely love this one. If you didn't, don't dismiss it outright! I would recommend watching a more comprehensive review than mine. Depending on what you took issue with in the original, this sequel has very likely addressed and fixed it.

I'm liking it so far. I like how reactive the world is to what you do. There are speech options for what seems to be every perk, trait (positive and negative), skill, and even flaw at some point in the game. 

I want to like this game more, but there are a few things that are very frustrating:

You get a whopping 2 skill points per level and are locked out a ton of skill checks early on. If you want to complete everything then you just have to come back after leveling up a ton.
In the first game your skill checks pulled from your companion's skills. For example a companion with high engineering would help you pass an engineering check. That doesn't exist anymore. There are some moments where companions can have some input, but they can't help in raw skill checks because companions don't have skills. In the first game companions felt like useful members of the party. Please Obsidian, if there is a way to bring back the original companion system in a patch that would be amazing.

I also wish enemies would have friendly fire turned on for each other. Remember in New Vegas when Powder Gangers would hilariously blow themselves up on accident? That doesn't exist here. There are points in the game where there is nonstop grenade spam that instantly kills you in hard mode, but when they accidentally throw it at a buddy it doesn't hurt them at all. 

I still recommend the game, but some changes made through patches would be ideal. I'll update my review as I play more.

Despite on some strange hate towards Obsidian in a gaming community I like all their recent games.  Avowed was good (I really like Pillars universe). OW 1 was crazy and fun (like if New Vegas and Mass Effect had a child). 
So I enjoyed OW 2 as well. Not a masterpiece but pretty decent game whatsoever. 6.5-7 out of 10. 

Pros: 
That's basically the same Outer Worlds 1 but with polished and extended gameplay mechanics.  
Technical state. OW2 is working and looking flawlessly (definitely not the worst implementation of UE5)
Stats, skills and background affect the gameplay and dialogues more than it was in OW1  
Reworked flaw system: quite often the game suggests a flaw based on your gameplay style and even based on your interaction with companions, that's cool. The flaws themselves affect your playstyle much stronger than it was before.  
Polished gameplay: science weapons, gadgets, interactions with companions - this game shows how it should have been done in OW 1. Companion abilities and progression reminded me of Mass Effect 2, which is a good thing IMO.  
Still good humour and dialogues (I liked the ones in OW 1 more though).
Easy and fun exploration, the locations are more vertical now, feels interesting after OW 1. 
Stealth is much better than in OW 1. 
The game has optimal length for people with a life outside of games. I hate this modern tendency to make action RPG game enormously big. You can finish the game and, not all but many, side quests in around 50-70 hours or even less. 

Cons:
That's basically the same Outer Worlds 1 but with polished and extended gameplay mechanics. 
Main fractions and their views are too simplified and caricatured. OW 1 was more subtle in that way, even with amount of absurd there. 
Maybe it's just me, but I liked OW 1 companions more. The companions in OW 2 seem too mediocre, they are not bad, but could certainly be better. And their quests are less touching than the quests of companions from OW 1. 
There are a lot of roleplay and dialogue options, but in major part of cases you cannot negotiate a peaceful solution without a Speech skill even if you checked every corner/all terminals and found all pieces of information possible. Not a bad thing, but I don't want to be ability-locked with so many different skills to develop. 
IMO narrative, atmosphere and GG motivation, in general,  seem a bit weaker if we compare with the original. OW 1 speaks about serious and even terrible topics in an absurd & humorous way. OW 2 has more serious tone, but its writing and ideas are simpler. There is a bit less absurd and humorous situations (they still present though and sometimes are pretty good) and, unfortunately, more mediocre writing. 
Locations could be a bit better. Also vertical levels make exploration a bit more tedious.  

To sum up:
Many aspects of the first game were improved or extended. If you enjoyed the first game, most likely, you will enjoy the OW2 as well. If you didn't enjoy OW 1 this game won't convert you to the fan of the series. We also have a paradox here-OW 2 is certainly by far better than the OW 1 "as a game" aka gameplay-wise, but IMO it is worse as the "conception/idea" aka story/narrative-wise.

Note: Revised review from "Not Recommended due to unplayability"

Technical: After some hardware adjustments and a fresh install of OS and wipe of ALL hdds I was finally able to load the game. It even played relatively well at medium to high settings at 1440p on a 3080 ti.

Gameplay: In terms of gameplay recommendation- I would say that if there was a neutral response to recommending that would be my pick. I simply chose the thumbs up due to the fact that it is entertaining.  It doesn't really feel like I ever said to myself "WoW that's new!" from my memories of TOW 1. It seemed to just carry on from there, with some new skills, perks etc. Slight changes to companion development here and there.

Game world feels a bit small and congested at times. Companion depth is interesting at times, but can get very predictable. It felt like there were some inconsistencies with influencing your companions ideologies.  The good news is, on a bad day TOW 2 writing is better than most AAA games today, and it definitely carries on an interesting story. 

Hardest part for me was pacing: Both story and player progression. Story: It just felt like a yo-yo at times where you'd start to really get hooked in, just to need to make a pit stop to feed a turtle. Player progression was a bit difficult (I played on the Hard setting), in that I felt once I crested a certain plateau any meaningful power increases were negligible.

If you really liked TOW 1 or Fallout/RPG Quester/explorers it's a no-brainer.
If you play them occasionally - wait for a sale.

To start - I'm lucky, I haven't any technical problems 14 hours in, I know some people have so YMMV. 
TLDR: If you're a fan of Fallout with a side order of Bioshock aesthetics in a planet hopping pulp adventure with meaningful choices and good RPG character building elements, you've got a good one here. 

What a breath of fresh air. I moderately enjoyed some of Avowed and Outer Worlds so I wasn't expecting much except another partially cooked RPG with some interesting elements and enough frustrations to turn some people off. 
I was wrong. 
Choices really matter in a Fallout New Vegas kind of way (so far) and you can have a massive impact on the world very early on. 
Combat feels chonky without feeling slow and the weapons have excellent personality. 
Your character build can be unique to your preferred play style and what you choose directly impacts gameplay. 
The world refines the fun critiques of capitalism and socialism that it first built in the Outer Worlds in a way that's enjoyable without being preachy in either direction. 
Stuff that might turn you off: 
If you don't like comedy in your games or get cringey easily you won't like this. The comedy is good, but not everyone wants that. 
The graphics aren't top tier. 
Inventory management isn't great
It's still not clear what the gear score number in the top right had of gear means as compared to the actual stats on the items. It's possible I missed a tool tip, but without googling (which I had to do) I wasn't able to find a clear explanation.

Pros
- Lots of improvements when looking back to OW1
- Companions are all nice and well done
- Gunplay is nice
- Like the atmosphere and tone of the game, the whole ambience is very good
- Radio is pretty gud
- No carry weight
- Lots of nice goodies to collect (Weapons, Armor, Cards, Items...)
- Funny flaws
- Cutscenes are nicely done
- The level design of the worlds, stations and ships are better than in OW1
- Missions and items decorate your ship
- Lots of things to do with your abilities
- You can spec into the way you want to play
- Humor and dialog was better fleshed out (also lots of dialog choices)
- References to Secret Level and also OW1 (not that many, but some)
- Day & Night cycle was a nice touch for the ambience

Mids
- Exploration of the locations is nice, but still does not satisfy the itch I've had in other sandbox games
- No durability for weapons and armor
- Kinda wished for a better looking ship you have as a home and also to have more things you could do with it
- Armor is there and nice for some protection values, but else it seems to be not fleshed out well, but maybe it was also just there to have this in it
- Modifications of armor and weapons are okay (when you find the set of mods you will always use it for the weapons)
- Not sure how it exactly connects to the story of OW1, if there even was stuff like that, it is fine if there should be none
- Endslides, it feels nostalgic, but somehow also a bit out of time

Cons/Bugs
- Some quests were bugged in regards of progressing them (like triggers in levels not working, e.g. The Final Lesson)
- The maps still not feel the same like it does in some other open world games where there was a more sandbox like behavior and less just a random blob of things to shoot at
- Flaws no longer give perk points
- Sounds like music from the radio was bugged? and would carry over into several screens where it should be off
- Sneak DMG is just to overpowered
- Many weapons are made obsolete with very strong uniques (Used the same weapons until endgame after getting them early to mid)
- Had a sensory overload in one late game area where I was bombarded with sounds overlayed like 3-4 times, lights and some other things 
- The bounty system is not really working well in the favor of the player, as you need to go to a terminal inside enemy controlled zones to get rid of it, only to shoot more of them. (Yes sneaking is an option, but does not help sometimes)
- Loading Times were rather long for some mid and late game areas
- Not enough things that can be done in your ship or to change it interior
- No respecc
- Still missing lifecycles for NPCs where they actually have to do something, change their location etc.
- Lots of porting around companions and enemies in regards of where they need to be to trigger dialog, cutscenes etc
- Also sometimes even the porting did not work and they where like somewhere else and still talking, but almost out of hearing range
- Companions are far too weak in combat and die like flies

Also read about performance problems. I had none of such, for what ever reason I do not know. Using Windows 11, Ryzen R9 7900X and a RTX 4070. Also had DLSS active in balanced mode and graphics set to high and playing in 1440p.

I've grown used to seeing plain looking companions as a red flag. The lack of huge graphical leap from 1 to 2 took adjustment. I played 30 hours of the first and never finished it (I consider a game worth playing +8~ hours worth the price) . Still preordered this one.
Three hours in and I'm glad I did. The writing is excellent. No modern slang, quite the opposite. Very refreshing. I haven't skipped a single line of dialogue, which is rare for me.
The gunplay feels good. Animations are varied and well done. The character models are basic and occasionally break immersion when their plain faces move with the voice acting. Small gripe. I realize resources went elsewhere, and in the actual cutscenes, the animation quality is excellent.
The choices are genuinely unpredictable. What you expect to succeed doesn't always work. Leaning into your stats or the "right" option may surprise you. I love that.

Playing on Very Hard points out many of the flaws of their new approach to how combat works compared to TOW1. You're introduced to sneak attacking during the tutorial mission but immediately run into the issue of never having to use it ever again since every enemy is counted as being a strong one regardless of the level you're at. The AI for companions are extremely lackluster and lacking innovation. Compared to TOW1, they removed commanding your companions and even giving them gear to make them more durable/capable of not just being a useless target dummy for enemies. Besides hitting 2 buttons to occasionally tell them to do something, on the occasion I found that one companion you get early on doesn't always use their ability based on where you are but instead uses it out in the open which 10/10 times gets you and them killed from one bullet. I think combat would be more interesting with the new movement etc if they kept the charm of what makes their gunplay standout. It's incredibly frustrating sneaking up to an enemy (with 1,000-2,000hp) and dumping 80 critical hits from a gun on them just to have them 180 you and kill you instantly, it feels like you have to stunlock every single enemy in order to do the most basic things. To this day, when you ask people “did you enjoy [i][url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/578650]The Outer Worlds[/url][/i]?”, they’ll likely respond: “Yes! I loved that 20-minute time loop idea and… uh, [url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/753640]hold on a second[/url]…” After all, [i]The Outer Worlds[/i] was just an entertaining but otherwise pretty unremarkable open-world RPG that did nothing to innovate on the basic gameplay concepts popularised by the likes of [i][url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/22330]Oblivion[/url][/i] in the early 2000s, and also failed to live up to the standard of writing and storytelling set by Obsidian’s own [i][url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/22380]Fallout: New Vegas[/url][/i]. So if you’re reading reviews of the sequel to check if anything’s changed about that state of affairs, let me assure you: [b]The Outer Worlds 2 is pretty much more of the same, except with more dynamic movement, better gunplay and a greater focus on scripted gameplay situations in which different builds can utilise different approaches[/b].

Though if you’re still interested in The Outer Worlds 2 after having experienced the first game, I assume you don’t expect more from the sequel than the same 'comfort food' gameplay we all know and love from these 'entertainment park' style open-world RPGs. In that case, [b]there’s a lot to like about The Outer Worlds 2[/b]. Personally, I really enjoyed the darkly humorous dystopian theme of humanity being crushed between multiple totalitarian systems, with this underlying Orwellian feeling of 'there’s nowhere to escape to, because this is all there is now'. I also really like the way that NPCs are occasionally shown struggling with the worldviews these systems impose on their subjects, but it’s a shame that [b]any satire The Outer Worlds 2 can muster from that premise is pretty on-the-nose (and rather surface-level as well)[/b]. I guess it’s better than not having the game tackle such interesting themes at all. Though you’d be forgiven to think a game that features hyper-capitalism as one of its core worldbuilding pillars would be at least half as thoughtful about it as [i]Fallout: New Vegas[/i], with its three currencies illustrating the real-world impact of competing economic and political systems, to give just one example.

[b]The main problem with The Outer Worlds 2 then, is that it’s a 70 bucks RPG that's good fun, but doesn’t significantly innovate or even just improve on the 'comfort food' open-world RPG formula[/b]. It's nice that the devs went back to the [i]Fallout: New Vegas[/i] model of offering different gameplay approaches based on your skills, but keep in mind that this is the year where [i][url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/1771300]Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2[/url][/i] had the guts to double down on all the systemic world simulation and dynamic reactivity we should really expect from a proper role-playing game these days—and it’s 10 bucks cheaper, to boot. The Outer Worlds 2 has none of the simulationist depth that makes [i]Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2[/i]’s world so believable, such as NPCs having daily routines instead of standing in the exact same spot day and night. And that's just kind of a letdown for a full-priced RPG in 2025, considering that games like [i]Oblivion[/i], [i][url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/65540]Gothic[/url][/i] and [i][url=https://www.gog.com/game/ultima_7_complete]Ultima 7[/url][/i] already used such systems for immersive worldbuilding back in 2006, 2001 and 1992, respectively. Though I guess the [b]absence of simulationist depth in The Outer Worlds 2[/b] fits well with the 'entertainment park' nature of its gameplay. After all, if you’re just here to run around the open world and shoot alien critters, you don’t want to have to wait for the shops to open up again in the morning before you can sell your loot. But it does mean that [b]if you're hoping your 70 bucks will buy you a truly immersive RPG set in a mechanically believable world, you'll be sorely disappointed[/b].

[b]If what you're looking for in The Outer Worlds 2 is your annual [i]Oblivion[/i] or[/b] [i][url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/22370]Fallout 3[/url][/i] [b]fix, though, then you’re gonna have fun with the game. But you should seriously ask yourself if 'more of the same' is worth the inflated 2025 'AAA' price point to you[/b]. Much like the first game in the series, The Outer Worlds 2 is very enjoyable, but it's by no means a game any RPG fan desperately needs to experience, so you’re probably [b]better off waiting a year or two until it’s available at a discount[/b].

I’m well past the 40-hour mark (don’t trust my Steam clock—some hours were on a Steam Deck in offline purgatory), and I’m happy to report that Outer Worlds 2 feels like a smarter, sharper sequel in almost every way. The writing still slices like a boardroom memo written with a bayonet, the buildcrafting is gloriously abusable, and combat finally has the oomph to back up the jokes.

Let’s start with the laughs: the comedy remains some of the best in games. It’s not just punchlines; it’s worldbuilding that weaponizes corporate doublespeak until you can practically hear a “limited-liability waiver” every time you reload. Side quests detour into delightfully petty squabbles, contracts, and “ethically sourced” loopholes that make even the straight path feel crooked—in the best way. A few times I had to pause, not for Tactical Time Dilation, but because the dialogue hit me with a clean, regulation-compliant spit take. (“It’s not the best choice…”—yeah, you know the rest.)

Choice is where OW2 truly levels up. Character creation encourages deliciously weird archetypes—laser-eyed diplomat? Hammer-happy pacifist?—and the quest design actually accommodates them. Major beats bend without breaking, and consequences don’t scream “YOU PICKED THE WRONG DIALOGUE,” they just… happen. It’s the rare RPG where my second playthrough ideas started crowding my first.

Combat’s the other big win. Encounters flow better, guns feel punchier, and the expanded arsenal turns firefights into little laboratories of mayhem.

On the hardware front, Steam Deck folks are in good shape: the game runs fully on Deck. On the lowest settings I’m getting about 30–40 FPS—playable and consistent enough for questing and smaller areas, with the usual dips when firefights get busy. Controls and UI behave fine in handheld, and with just a little more optimization this feels on the verge of snagging that Valve badge.

Now, the gripes. You can’t change companion clothing or weapons—an immersion pothole in a world so obsessed with personal branding that even my sandwich had a slogan. And there are no romance options. I don’t need cinematic sex scenes, but romance is one more lever for roleplaying, especially in a game where choices are king. Without it, party dynamics sometimes plateau at “witty coworker energy.” Auntie Cleo says that’s healthy boundaries; my roleplayer heart says “missed opportunity.”

Bottom line: Outer Worlds 2 is a confident, funnier, and mechanically tighter follow-up that embraces player expression and makes multiple playthroughs feel mandatory in the best possible way. If the companion customization and romance options ever get the green light, we’re flirting with all-time status. As it stands, consider this a premium, Board-compliant recommendation—with only a small surcharge for “emotional damages.”

Loved the original game which I bought on the Epic Games Store so was very much looking forward to the sequel, especially after the massive disappointment that was Starfield.

I am happy to report that I am not disappointed so far. While the game itself is more of a refinement over the original - running on Unreal Engine 5 instead of 4 - it feels more polished and the environments are much larger than the first game. The dialogue is well-written and the game seems to offer a lot of choices for how I want to play from the skills and perk system to the equipment you pick up along the way. Combat is fun and the difficulty (on Normal) feels about right but the game allows you to lower the difficulty without penalising you at any time so that is very welcome. Nothing more frustrating than getting stuck on an annoying bullet-spongy boss!

Companions are perhaps the only aspect of the game that I feel is a downgrade over the original, only because I haven't found the three I've met so far to be as interesting or as likeable as the ones in the first game. Maybe it will improve the further I get into the game?

I did encounter an extremely annoying save bug where the game stopped autosaving checkpoints and I was unable to quicksave or overwrite existing saves (thankfully the game has 100 manual slots so I was able to get around it by saving over a new one every 5-10 minutes). That issue was fixed by signing into and out of my Xbox account a few times so hopefully it will stay that way! So nice to be able to quicksave and also not see the "Save Unsuccessful" messages constantly when the game failed to save a checkpoint.

I also briefly experienced a glitch where I was unable to equip and use a weapon but apparently this issue is now resolved in the first hotfix (I was able to load a 20 minute earlier save to "fix" it). 

Pleasant visuals, nice art style, although nothing spectacular, and radio music that reminds me of Fallout 3. It's a nice package in my opinion bar the bugs but it wouldn't be an Obsidian release if there wasn't some jank and bugs. I'd definitely recommend this if you enjoyed the first game or are looking for something to make up for the lacklustre Starfield.

As someone that loved the Outer Worlds 1, this sequel lives up to my expectations and then some! There are much deeper RPG elements, the worlds are fuller, the graphics are impressive, the VO work is stellar, and the leveling system has been completely redone. I would absolutely suggest this game if you want to have an RPG to really sink your teeth into!

Soft not recommend for right now due to bugs. I used an engine tweaks mod which fixed performance/crash issues so those were less of an issue, but still happened a few times. I really enjoyed everything up until the quest bugs. The mechanics DO make you feel rewarded for your skill/perk choices, but I feel the points are a little too few to flesh out a character without spoiling things for yourself. 

By this I mean: Maybe you don't NEED to max three skills to accomplish all the role-playing options the game provides for that skill, but how do you know without spoiling it for yourself - "What two skills could I leave at 10 and still get an experience I feel satisfied with?" - for instance. I feel that Armors/Mods/Companions/Items providing buffs to these would make that aspect of the game feel a little better/smoother. 

Not so much that they guarantee you can do 100% of the content, but allow you to feel less bad about missing something. Like when you have a level 30 combat medic companion with you and are gated off from flavor/role-playing content by a level 7 medical requirement, as a literal example.

I get the intent, I just feel it's a little too restrictive and makes it feel like you need to have perfect knowledge before creating your character if you don't want to feel bad a bout missing something you want your character to be able to experience.

Bug issues -

I hit multiple bugs that stopped quests from progressing after being given the freedom to pick sides ([spoiler] After unlocking Undisputed Claim and The Cloister [/spoiler]). Certain parts of the game have overlapping quest zones and I completed some things for quests I hadn't officially started yet through exploration. The dialog accounted for this, but the quest was just stuck. 

It kind of spoiled the last 1/3 or so of the game for me because, while I accepted I can't see everything in a single playthrough with how the skills work, I missed out on choosing a faction to side with or trying to see if I was able to unite them. So, I sped through the companion quests and then finished the game. 

I enjoyed it up until I hit that point and was excited for a 2nd playthrough, but after having to finish that playthrough like that, I just kinda closed the game and came here.

Some tips: I think the "jack of all trades"-esque flaw is a trap overall due to it forcing you to spec into the lowest skills. Maybe it did allow me to access more content in my playthrough in the mid-game, but early I found some skills too low and  in the endgame when you need 18s and 20s, you can't access anything. So I guess think about how many playthroughs you realistically expect to do. 

It's hard to give direction as to what opens up the most. 

Hacking gives you tons of information for dialogs that you'll want for peaceful resolutions and opens up a lot of areas, but it can't fully replace speech. 

Engineering I didn't use but it seemed less useful in dialog (though had its place) and gave less useful access to blocked doors (looked to be small areas with junk loot, but I can't say 100%) but I've seen mentioned that the damage reduction it gives is huge for higher difficulties.

Lockpicking gives great access to side areas and loot, but I really don't recall seeing any dialog for it. I would recommend a value point in it early (level 2) for pickpocketing, since I believe you can max 3 skills and have 4 points left over.

I'd say for at least normal, grab Lockpicking or Hacking as your "Utility" skill - based on whatever kind of character you're going for. Then whatever combat and role-playing skills fit what you like to do. 

It might not be necessary to take certain skills to 20 to get the optimal speech options for it. With the two points per level (again, the three point's per level flaw locks what you can put points into) it's hard to say how you should spend points. If you hit a medical check you can't do early on and you're role-playing a doctor, that kinda stinks but short of spoiling everything, idk how you'd manage that.

♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Where to start with this game.

I've always been a fan of the predecessor -- The Outer Worlds was my first "real" RPG and as such it holds a special place in my heart despite being pretty mediocre in comparison to many of the RPGs I have played since (obligatory Pentiment shilling here), so getting a sequel that (to me at least) is pretty on-par with New Vegas in terms of quality and features is spectacular. I fell in love with New Vegas and I am one of those people who heralds it as one of the greatest Western RPGs ever made. It sits firmly in my top five games, and once I mull it over more I don't doubt this game will also make it into that prestigious top five position.

The pros:
- visually stunning in both graphic fidelity and art direction (turn on ray tracing!!)
- well optimised (all bugs encountered were extremely minor or patched out before the full release, most being funny visual goofs)
- charming cast of characters with excellent voice performances all around, from your ever important companions to minor, one-off NPCs
- very fun quests
- amazing reactivity. an action i took seemingly arbitrarily came back to bite me in the ass ten hours later, it's that good
- a return to form for obsidian in the world of "consumer-friendly" rpgs (if you've been around to play their isometric or point and click games you'd know they never left)
- really utilises its AAA budget to the fullest it can, making up for the first game's issues that stemmed from financial trouble
- shooting mechanics that are the best i've handled in years outside of dedicated fps games

The cons:
- you cannot side with the protectorate, at all. you can play missions into their favour, but i have no idea what outcome this provides as it isn't the route i took.
- builds are very very specialised, which isn't an issue for me, but being a jack of all trades is not viable if you want to get a good ending. again, i think this is good, but i know many wouldn't hence its placement here
- no romance. not an issue for me personally because i don't care but i know many of you who would be better off playing dating sims or otome games play rpgs to fill that gap instead so rest assured you guys will not enjoy this one. no smooching at all, not even a little handholding. it is scandalous in the eyes of the sovereign, or something.
- companions are really weirdly balanced but this may be an issue that gets resolved in later patches

Who I would recommend this to:
- fans of the first game
- fans of Bioshock (much of its satire feels akin to Bioshock's but with some Futurama style silliness)
- fans of Fallout (from any point in the series, it'll hit the same itch for whatever you're looking for)
- people who want a fun narrative shooter

Who I would not recommend this to:
- people who disliked the first game (unless you really want to, this one is a huge improvement)
- people who play rpgs instead of dating sims
- people who won't let obsidian escape new vegas' shadow -- lower your expectations to a healthy minimum first
- disco elysium fans expecting this game to be anywhere near as profound in its commentary on capitalism (i saw an actual journalist do this)

Overall, solid 9/10 for me. I hope the DLCs can flesh out the companions a bit more like they did with the first game, but other than that? Solid. Minimal complaints here that aren't just outright nitpicky. At this point though I'd advise getting just the base game and buying the DLCs when they actually have a release date -- I got premium for the early access and so I didn't have to worry about paying for the DLC pass next year. (That being said CDKeys should have discounted codes for Steam by now, so if you want to you should check it out for premium! The base game is definitely well worth its offering price.)

If you loved and I mean really loved the first part and couldn't get enough of it, you will have some fun with this one.

Pro:
- longer then the first part
- more locations and clear indication what skill is used for what in dialogue / exploration etc
- graphics somewhat better than TOW1
- if you enjoy listening to people that love to hear themselves speak, that game will be a treat for you
- there is a bit of silly humour in this game, that might be enjoyable to some

Cons:
- shooter part is still weak, basically no encounter design (no tatics, no cover system, almost no skill combos with companions, enemies jumping up walls they shouldn't be able to, other times enemy just stare/freeze, only a few boss fights with only one having some sort of what is supposed to be a boss mechanic... I'm not joking, sadly) 
- companions borderline useless (little control over them, barely customizable, no inventory on them, barely level up mechanics)
- stealth part is.. meh. Stealth+melee is somewhat fun in the beginning and some indoor locations. Stealth+gun in the more open areas pretty much useless, just unload your MP or whatever on each mob and move on... why sneak around and position carefully to take out one of the ten mobs that you could have all killed in the same time with less hassle without sneaking? 
- no lone-wolf mode (was possible in TOW1 and somewhat fun/challenging)
- crafting is worse than in TOW1, less mods, less upgrades, less everything but 50 different food items that all do the same
- I switched weapons like three times, put one mod on it and used the same armor and hat for one playthrough start to finish.. let that sink in
- exploration is little fun as there is basically nothing to find besides food and crafting items that you don't need (see above)
- you can't pick locks unless you constantly put points in it, which means 50% of your skill points. Wow. Stupid. Worst lock pick system I've seen in ages - TOW1 allowed at least to open locks above your skill level and sacrifce picks - you can't do that here; then again those locked boxes only contain trash (food, ammo, junk) 
- in-game lore texts are bad, most of them read like ChatGPT generated filler texts with no relevant information or interesting story telling, just bland (e.g. no comparison to texts on vault terminals you may remember from Fallout 3/4)
- most dialogue is of the category "long and rambunctious sentences with little real value to your current mission", you could go with skipping all dialogue and just read up the journal, much less hassle
- character progression in TOW1 was limited and simple, but in TOW2 it's even more simplified and runs out of steam quickly, e.g. playing a gunslinger there are not even enough good gun perks to pick up to level 30 - just lazy design
- loot progression does not exists, not even near the simple tier 1 to tier 3 system that was in TOW1 - as I said I used a pistol at start then switched over to the unique sniper rifle like 1/3 in the story. That is all the loot progression there was. What a step back from TOW1. Wow. I was shocked, given that the game is like 3x longer
- companions are mostly bland and one-dimensional, Marisol was somewhat better written and a little more deep, but if you expect anything near Mass Effect 1/2 companions you wont find it here, I'm afraid (a 2007 game with waaaaaay smaller dev team, mind you) but much much better writers it seems
- in-game puzzles are repetitions of "pick that fuse from over there and put it here"
- "open worldish maps" can only be traversed on the very paths the map designer intended you to, meaning it's usually a bunch of locations you need to see for the main quest stitched together with long tubes you need to walk. But then again, nothing much to see outside those locations. The important locations look nice, though. Everything in between not so much.
- touch water, die instantly
- step over a cliff deeper then 5m die instantly
- step to close to a gas cloud and die
- make every location as vertical as possible so players can constantly die when "exploring"

Really "fun" map design. I would fire those level designers instantly. But I'm afraid they are only interns that use ChatGPT. 
And I mean, look at TOW1 map design. It was not great, but it was okay. Way better than.. whatever this is.

The game clearly aims on replayability (stealth vs gun vs leadership etc) but there is not enough "meat on the bone". Honestly, the game would have been better with less filler content and more actual (good) gameplay. It excels in not a single category.

I hope I'm wrong. Maybe The Outer Worlds 3 in a few years will be great. Or maybe just another run-of-the-mill microsoft game pass experience.

I did not play the first game but I'm enjoying the hell out of this one. For those completely unfamiliar, this game is a sci-fi satirical narrative driven action looter shooter role playing game that takes place in another solar system where you engage several factions that are in active war with one another, each supporting a different type of economic/political system with conflicting ideologies. Each of your companions is part of one of the factions and the game offers you so much choice in how to handle everything. 

The skill system is phenomenal, an absolute masterpiece. Instead of traditional stats you have skills, perks, flaws, traits, backgrounds that all add something to the way you play the game. You can improve both combat and non-combat ways you approach every problem. The progression is fantastic. For example, not only can you upgrade your traditional stealth capabilities like weapon dmg, weakpoint dmg or stealth dmg, but putting points into sneak will reduce enemy detection rate, you can unlock upgrades to how fast they lose track of you, your crouch speed, you weapon noise, various cloaking procs... Every single level into my stealth build felt like an upgrade not just a basic chance to be detected or you can one shot them even harder approach of other games.

The flaw system is genius, if you over do one activity a lot the game offers to buff that activity at the cost of reducing the effectiveness of another. You can choose to opt in or out per flaw choice. If you craft more than you buy stuff the game will make buying things harder but crafting easier, if you spam reload a lot you get a bonus to that, if you don't use your heal a lot you get a bonus to its heal but less frequent usage of it, if you do the opposite you get less heal but more usages. Not just practically but also in humor, like if you buy too much the game hampers your spending until you're old enough to be responsible with your money while giving you interest or if you look at the sun too long you might go blind; skip dialogue too much and all dialogue choices are put on a timer that you have to race through or a random choice is made on your behalf, these also come with benefits like bonus exp for the latter.

There are several ways to reach quest objectives, depending on your build. You can brute force a door open, engineer it open, hack your way in, lockpick it, talk your way in or sneak your way in through a hidden path you can observe. Not all options are present at every turn but every turn has multiple options you can take. The same can be true for conversation, as most skill points or perks can have a check that makes things interesting. I love how combat skills can influence conversation options this way, as well as the opposite. I particularly love that passive skills like speech have multiple combat benefits so no matter what skills you master you get both combat and rp elements out of it.

The story is good, the lore is great, major story beats are fantastic, the way companion storylines bleed into the main story is amazing and the humor is subjective but just perfect for my taste.

This is a hard maybe of a game. It polished down a lot of the bad parts of OW1 and left not much to be inspired or interested in. The problem, ultimately, with the game is that everyone is a cartoon character in terms of their motivations and beliefs. 

"I AM HYPER CONSUMERIST!"
"I AM HYPER CONFORMIST"
"(WE ARE SOMEHOW THE SAME BUT DIFFERENT)"

The tone of OW1 was even more of this but OW2 never really strayed from this, just more muted. Interesting characters are those that are flawed, working for the wrong side, yet have some amount of care of depth to them. Every side character you meet is insane. Scientists that want to turn crabs into futuristic fusion reactors, an entrepreneur that is handed jump boots that he can fully patent and sell unhindered throwing it away cause it sounds stupid, and a janitor who proceeds to go on terrible team building exercise after another, getting his friends and colleagues killed, seemingly for no reason.

There is NO ONE to hope for. No one is carrying the banner. We are introduced to world where we play tin-foil cops in a sector of the galaxy that largely has never heard of us and decidedly doesn't care. Every faction mistreats and punishes people for doing normal things, just like OW1. In a world that broken, what is there to fix?

Also, Very Hard isn't when enemies are tethered to their locations. There is an invisible line that if the enemies cross, they will turn around and reset. Not their health, though, so you can cheese almost every encounter until you have enough of a grasp on mechanics and weapons to kill most things in the game. Hint: slowing time opens the window of stealth kills for the entire time of the charge, making 1 or 2 shots with a repeating rifle silenced rifle into 5, killing most things in the starting area.
I actually like it.

I like some things about the game, so I will temporarily give it a thumbs down due to a few specific problems, and I'll be happy to change it to positive if they're fixed.

I had a save related bug that prevented autosaves, quicksaves, and manual saves. It didn't notify me this happened however, so I had been hitting the save button with no notification it wasn't working, until I had to quit and discovered the next time I logged in that nothing saved, and I lost a few hours of progress. After I was on the lookout for it, it did happen a couple more times. Exiting to the main menu or relaunching the game did fix it temporarily, until it happened again the next day.

As for gameplay, most things are improved from the first game, but bullet sponges are a problem already by level 9. The boss in the Vox Relay for example has 4.4 thousand hp, and even melting his armor with acid and getting headshots, the sheer amount of bullets doing only 20-40 damage each makes it tedious. Longer fights are fine, but not if it's just a bullet sponge.

As for some positives, I especially like that not every dialogue option that is unique/unlocked by background or skills is automatically right. In most games, if you see a skillcheck or unique character related dialogue option, you automatically select it because it solves or bypasses the situation, but I've had several instances where a skill or background related option was still rejected, and it made sense that it was rejected when I thought about the situation.

So far, I also like that things are mostly toned down from the first game. This one feels a bit more realistic, and the player character has a better motivation this time.

I think in most ways it's an improvement over the first game, but there's just a few things holding it back. Most of them fixable. If the problems are fixed, I would say it's fun.

First and foremost, the game has been entirely enjoyable up to the point I've completed. It is clear they took what worked in the first gamed and successfully modernized its mechanics, particularly in the movement system. This is 100% one of the best RPGs on the market. You can murder everyone as a psychopath or you can be a benevolent helping hand, or something in between. The dialogue has, on a multitude of occasions, gotten a laugh out of me, It is witty, sometimes stupid, but all around good writing. 

I have plenty more of the game to complete as I write this, but can wholeheartedly reccomend the experience to anyone who tends to enjoy these types of games.

However, the only gripe I have with this game is that the Premium Edition is practically useless. The benefits/buffs associated with them very quickly gets outshined as you progress and I only found myself using them at the beginning. Though they aren't shabby to look at, you truly wouldn't be missing out on much.

That being said, I was gifted the Premium Edition so no harm done - and it was mostly for the early access anyways. The price is questionable, but sadly nothing unique in today's market. If you can justify a recycled COD game for the same price, this is 1000 times more justifiable a purchase.

only played for 4 hours. i wondered why my "save game" button had been greyed out for so long, but figured because i was still in the prologue, it was auto saving and would probably let me manual save later. got a little annoyed about how the skill points work, only gaining 2 points per level means you straight up can't play a jack-of-all-trades type character and you pretty much have to specialize. i don't like this, i want to be able to AT LEAST put a point into a third skill (THERE ARE SO MANY SKILLS, ONLY GETTING 2 POINTS PER LEVEL IS CRAZY) so i decided to install a mod that doubles skill points. exited the game, again, assuming it had been making auto saves and that it would make an exit save as well because it's a game made in 2025, not 1995. installed a mod to give me 4 points per level. boot game. continue game. it loads, and i'm back on the ship during the prologue. the game simply...stopped saving 17 minutes into my game. i lost 4 and a half hours of progress because the game simply...stopped saving, nor did it let me manually save. the manual save option was still greyed out when i exited the game, but again, auto saves and an exit save are a perfectly reasonable assumption in a modern action-RPG.

at least wait to pick this up until it gets a patch for this bug, because this is a completely unacceptable bug to ship with, especially when a quick google shows it's been there since day 1 of the early launch, which means they've had 5 days to figure out what's causing this progress stopping bug, plus, y'know, the years of dev time. just ~10 years ago, if a game launched with a progress halting bug like this that's as common as my googling seems to suggest that it is, it would get destroyed online. remember when Watch Dogs got completely eviscerated for the crime of...not being as pretty as the trailers and gameplay previews? imagine if that game sometimes also just...stopped saving and didn't let you manually save.

beyond that, i was having fun despite it having a lot of the same problems as Avowed. just some...strange and not very good game design decisions. if you liked Outer Worlds 1, like i did, and didn't completely hate Avowed, also like me, then you'll at the very least have fun. but gone are the days when Obsidian made stuff like Pillars 1&2, New Vegas, Tyranny, and KOTOR2. i swear, whoever is running the company now should retire and hand the reins to Josh Sawyer so he can just make games like Pentiment over and over for all eternity. Put his brain in a jar so the brain jar can keep churning out tight, incredible indie games and incredible RPGs until the end of time.

Pros
The flaw system is fun and unique, it really changed the way I played and it was fun to discover new flaws based on my play style.
Large selection of skills and traits that have a meaningful impact on gameplay.
Great level design with multiple paths to any objective.


Cons
Mostly average writing, though there was definitely some solid quest design to keep it going.
Item and Crafting system is shallow and half-baked, you will spend most of the game using the same weapon or armor based on your build.

Overall, if you like the genre its a decent entry and a distinct improvement over the first one. I spent the entire game thinking how much better than Starfield it was.

Your character build actually affects the gameplay now, which is a nice improvement over the first game,  but it seems like melee builds have been tossed to the wayside. I'm on normal difficulty and it is largely foolish to engage any enemies in melee combat unless they are the last opponent. Other than opening up a fight with a stealth attack, there are no times where melee is viable. The new inhaler and companion systems are also major downgrades from the previous title. You have limited uses of your inhaler, there are almost never enough supplies to rely on it, and you can no longer equip companions with gear. 

    Aside from the main quest, none of the quests or factions really have any emotional impact. In fact, more often than not I have completely forgotten why I'm even doing what I'm doing, who I'm doing it for, and why they even want it done in the first place. Outer Worlds 1 was more interesting on average, and to be perfectly honest I found the first one largely boring. I feel almost forced to restart this game and hope it's more enjoyable with a gun build, but levelling your character is so streamlined that it feels like you're forced to choose between either being viable in combat, or being good at engineering, speech, or whatever skill you pick. It feels as if you are punished by trying to be even just a tiny bit versatile. 

   I really hope they change some of these issues, but with the positive reception the game has gotten, I'm doubtful they'll bother to right any of these wrongs. After Avowed, and Outer Worlds 1, this very well might be my last Obsidian game. I have no idea what happened to the people that made New Vegas and Pillars of Eternity, but they are clearly long gone. And this dev studio is clearly not interested in making sprawling RPGs anymore, just colorful and half baked action games

The game isn't bad exactly, but not particularly good either. If mixed was an option I'd pick that. I'm primarily saying no, because I don't think the game is really worth $70, let alone the $100 some people are paying. 

There were a couple moments that I thought were okay, once or twice even nose exhaled a bit, but overall pretty meh on the story, characters, and dialogue. The combat is serviceable, but has its issues. Sneak attacks are very weird, basically having you stab people in the back of the knees to 1 shot them. The shooting feels off, sometimes shots that seem like they should hit miss and vise versa. Also, aim assist is on by default for PC, which I disabled. 

I've seen some reviews mentioning things that are factually incorrect, such as that the game has no FOV slider, it does. As for other settings its mostly what you'd expect, with the notable absence of framegen, which is suspicious to me, because I've been playing on native (upscaling off), and have had noticeable artifacting, as if framegen is on in the background and there is no option to disable it. I have no proof of this, but I'm pretty sensitive to artifacts from upscaling/framegen, and the game definitely has some artifacting in the areas you would expect (fine details, hair, grates, etc.).

The character creator is pretty underwhelming, but equally feels almost too in depth for a game that never shows your character in cut scenes or dialogue. You can swap to third person mode, but it felt pretty jank to me. I don't super care about the whole body type 1 and 2 thing, but I do wish it had some variety, because the female body type is the exact same build as the male with (very) slightly narrower waist and breasts. There are 0 sliders for height or weight or anything like that, and all of the characters appear to be created using the same creator, resulting in every human character in the game looking identical to each other aside from skin tone and face.

As for the RPG mechanics, so far they seem underwhelming as well. I chose lock picking and hacking when creating my character and both actions are just press button then your character uses a tool to do it. There's no mini-game or anything. Doesn't really feel like you have any expertise or skill. Hacking especially, you just slam a rod into the side of the computers and it does it, how is this a skill?

I ended up liking the first game alright, so I'm going to keep playing and give this game a chance, but I'd recommend people wait for a sale.

I can't recommend the game. Everything feels quite dated. It does most things OK, but other games do it better. The combat is improved from the first game, but still very basic. The gameplay feels quite linear and pales in comparison to other open ended RPGs. I only played for under 2 hours before refunding the game, but even after leaving the tutorial area, I got a pretty strong sense that dialogue choices and gameplay approaches could vary (e.g. sneak in using stealth or go in blasting), but I'd essentially be funneled along a generally linear pathway. I'd be prepared to bet money this would indeed be the case if I continued playing beyond the refund window.

Probably the biggest hurdle for me, which was also an issue with the first game, is how unimmersive the environments look. It seems like the developers didn't give much attention to how the game would look from a first person perspective. There are no interesting vistas, and everything just seems very flat. The lighting is also very unimpressive, unless you have a new machine that can allow for enabling ray-tracing.

If the game was $40, it would seem like a good value and something I could get my money's worth with, however, for $70, I can't justify purchasing it.

A sequel that improves on everything from the previous game. 

The gun play feels much smoother and the larger array of weaponry with different damage types allows you to make more unique builds. The armour has also been expanded to include more variety so that you can equip gear for your build or for fashion (which you'll be able to see with a well implemented 3rd person camera). Both weapons and armour include mods that can further specialise builds.

One downside, for me personally, is the skill and stat allocations. The max level is 30 and the low amount of skill points per level locks you into a very specific build quite early. If you're aware of the low skill count, if not you can find your skill build being less than ideal when you hit the max level. This makes a first playthrough of a jack of all trades difficult and it sort of forces players to play through multiple saves in order to experience all dialogue options.

Dialogue choices are much better balanced between black and white choices with good and bad outcomes depending on the situation. My only downside again, similar to the skill tree, is that certain choices are locked in most cases (from what I've played) behind two skill checks and it limits the player experiencing certain outcomes or experiencing an outcome altogether  unless they build specifically for certain situations.

The exploration is also very well done in The Outer Worlds 2. The maps feel and look more interesting to explore. Some locations can get repetitive such as the houses and drone/electrical facilities but there are little secrets here and there that can give you some good loot.

One major complaint is the AI for the companions. They do deal decent damage to help you in fights but they are really squishy and constantly walking in front and through you causing collision which can mess up your movement pathing in a fight. The companions also have a very basic skill tree and aren't customisable.

Overall, I highly recommend this game for FPS RPG lovers. Lovers of the Fallout franchise,  people who just enjoy a solid semi open world game with some good writing and interesting loot and skill mechanics, and interesting and well designed locations to explore.

*Do take into account that the game isn't as in depth as a game like the Fallout series when it comes to the immersion. Things like stealth and stealing feel half baked, with NPC reactions being straight to combat with no way to calm them down. Although exploration is fun, it almost always ends up being running around and looking for things with a white outline so you know what can be interacted with. And combat most of the time will end up with you backstepping constantly as the enemy don't seem to have any combat AI to make them engaging, they just run at you.

Handwritten Review: The Outer Worlds 2

I’ve currently played The Outer Worlds 2 for around 30 hours, not including idle time.
I don’t really know how to judge what makes an FPS-RPG game truly “deep,” since I rarely play RPGs myself.
So here I’ll just write down the aspects of this game that stood out to me personally, hoping they can help you form an effective sense of judgment.

===

Part One: This game suits players who enjoy the society, weapons, and armor styles of the 18th–20th centuries.
You could say this game carries a very romantic quality — that is, within such a modern space sci-fi setting, humanity’s technology and weapons still feel distinctly retro.
In this game, you’ll see many old-fashioned, high-tech weapons inspired by real-world prototypes such as the Mauser pistol, STG44 assault rifle, Lewis gun, Czech-style LMG, and Enfield rifle, all faithfully recreated.
The game also features armor designs with a slightly punk-retro style, while the entire world carries a light “space opera” aesthetic.
If you like this kind of world-building, then this game may be worth your attention.

===

Part Two: The game’s storyline is fairly tight, coherent, and well-connected.
Main and side quests are closely intertwined, so you don’t need to worry too much about loose narrative threads, excessive bugs, or logical inconsistencies.
The story writing and text presentation are quite delicate — it may not be the most profound or famous narrative, but both the main and side stories are interesting, and the level of interactivity is strong.
NPC performances and dialogues are engaging, and the immersion is good.

However, there’s one part that bothers me. We all know that story-driven games tend to have certain ideological leanings.
Although this game offers a wide range of dialogue choices, fully allowing the player to decide, there are certain main quests where the direction feels somewhat one-sided.
For example, you can choose to kill someone, but once you do, a large number of side quests and story branches disappear.
It seems as though the game itself is nudging players toward forgiveness rather than violence.

Even though it offers freedom of choice, I personally think that if a game truly wants to deliver deep, interactive storytelling, both paths — killing or sparing — should have equally complete narrative content.
That is to say: the world should not be sucked into a black hole just because one person disappears.
Of course, Obsidian didn’t achieve this, likely because it would require a massive amount of development work.
But one thing you should know is that companion missions and interactions are very well made and genuinely enjoyable.

===

Part Three: About exploration and the world: I personally call this game an “open linear” game.
Except for special locations, every planet serves as a new stage.
The map size is much larger than in the previous game, but that doesn’t necessarily mean every planet is rich in exploration content.
It’s a mid-sized open world, but I find it rather static.

It’s a one-time experience — like a linear game, every place you’ve explored will forever become part of the past.
There won’t be any new life or refreshed resources.
So, the design feels quite static; there’s no truly dynamic world, nor any living ecosystem or variation.
Once you’ve finished exploring a planet, you’ll never really have a reason to return, except for certain special dialogue events.
Otherwise, you simply won’t come back, because there’s nothing left.

It’s fine that resources don’t respawn, but enemies don’t respawn either, and there’s no dynamic difficulty system.
As an RPG, I consider this a major flaw.
I don’t quite understand why Obsidian insists on keeping it this way, but personally, I’m not a fan of such design.

===

Part Four: The game’s skill and perk system has been significantly expanded.
Skills now include: guns, melee, sneak, lockpicking, engineering, explosives, hacking, medicine, science, perception, persuasion, and leadership.
If you want to take a non-direct approach — sneaky or tactical — you’ll need to invest in support-oriented skills first.
Although the entry cost is high, they aren’t mandatory for the main story.

The perk system includes as many as 92 perks, and they not only interact well with combat and stealth skills but also provide helpful support bonuses.
They’re very practical.
However, you must be careful when spending points, because you cannot respec.

I assume many players dislike this, saying it’s Obsidian forcing players to play a certain way — that it’s bad design.
Not being able to respec isn’t ideal, but I think Obsidian wanted to convey a sense of choice:
you should treat your playstyle and decisions seriously.
It’s a way to make players more attentive and thoughtful — not perfect, but understandable.

Also, I want to clarify something: some players keep complaining about the negative trait system, but in reality, Obsidian never forces players to accept them.
You can freely choose to reject or accept those traits.
If you don’t want them, just refuse — it won’t affect your main experience or progress.
So stop overblowing that issue.
The system is there for freedom of choice, not as a punishment.

===

Part Five: The game offers a third-person view, but switching to it reduces the synergy between aiming and movement.
However, the weapon shooting feel and sound design are excellent.
Gunfire sounds heavy and impactful, and hit feedback is clearly conveyed through distinct sound cues.
Each weapon’s recoil and firing animation are handled with detail and precision.

There’s a wide range of weapon types — pistols, revolvers, SMGs, assault rifles, shotguns, sniper rifles, LMGs, rocket launchers, energy weapons, melee weapons — basically all kinds of stuff.
It also includes unique and rare weapons that can be modified.
However, unlike Borderlands 4, this isn’t a looter-shooter.
In games like Borderlands, weapons of the same type drop with different random stats, but in The Outer Worlds 2, weapons and armor of the same type and rarity share identical attributes.
So there’s no point grinding endlessly.

Besides, there aren’t that many enemies to grind anyway, because everything is one-time only — once dead, always dead.

===

Part Six: The game includes tactical gadgets such as double jump, corrosive disassembly, time-slowing, shields, scanners, etc.
These tools are not only useful in combat but also very practical during exploration, so make sure to use them frequently.

Lastly, I think the game’s optimization isn’t very good.
The frame rate isn’t necessarily low, but it’s unstable — you’ll notice stutters, drops, or delays after dialogues, trading, or fast travel.
Not a huge deal, but it’s noticeable.
We’ll have to see how this improves later.

That’s all for now — these are the main aspects I’ve summed up about the game.
I believe I haven’t missed anything crucial, though I may add more later if needed.
My recommendation: if you plan to buy it now, just get the Standard Edition, since it probably doesn’t have much replay value — it’s more like a one-time experience.

===

Good game overall, but not without flaws. Context : I finished the game in around 55hours while exploring everything i could on very hard difficulty. I played a Sneak based gunner. 

Pros : 

- Story is nice and choices matter a lot, so there is potential for multiple run to experience everything the game has to offer.
- Factions are nice in the sense that this isn't some good/evil choices, and you could choose to help any of those factions without feeling like you're playing a villain. You could also play a full blown villain tho, as no npc are essentials afaik.
- Voice acting is good overall.

Mid : 
- Gameplay is allright, but nothing that we didn't see done better in other games.
- Quite a bit of uniques stuff (well, mostly uniques "modifier" on stuff and some really uniques weapons), but most of them aren't that usefull, so it's cool to find but you won't use most of it except for trying purpose.
- Side quests are decent, but there's a few "collect those stuff" meaning exploring aimlessly, and almost none of the side quest really got me interested past the reward and wanting to complete everything i could.
- performance wise, it's allright. I had to use framegen to get to high fps tho on a 2k resolution with a 165hz screen, but that's usually the case. Even without, the game was running around 60 to 80 fps so it's ok. 

Flaws : 
- Gear variety isn't great. Since build tend to favor some weapons over others, limiting even more what is efficient to play with depending of your skills choices. For reference, i played a sneak ranged build, so my main weapon of choice was a pistol with a silencer. Well, there is like 3/4 type of pistol in the game, and once you get the last base (got one around mid game i think, but you'll loot them everywhere past that point), i moded it and didn't find anything to replace it with for the next 30 hours.
- This impact directly the quality of exploration. Since gear isn't exciting, that mean most of what you find during exploration isn't usefull at all. Some random grenades, some money, weapons you'll not use 90% of the time, and junk to sell or dismantle. This make exploring the world somewhat of a chore especially past mid game since you'll already have a decent loadout based on your build.
- Most companions are sadly boring. I didn't have charisma skills, so most of my companion where the same at the start and at the end of the adventure, and tbh, i was bored out of all of them quite fast. Still, i did like one of them a lot, and missed another so i don't know about this one. 

Overall, nice game, but not gripping enough gameplay wise to make me do another run after the first to experience other choices.

I honestly wish though that... at character creation you were allowed to Tag 3 skills by default and not 2. Its incredibly limiting when you pick one non-combat stat and one combat stat and realize 90% of interactions of locked off from you until you start leveling. Im not sure a combat stat is needed tbh as we dont have an egregious level scaling, but it feels like players should have 3 by default with the brilliant trait pushing you up to 4. Aside from that the game is captivating and great for anyone old and new to Obsidian RPGs

This game is self-aware--but NOT in the overplayed way a lot of media has become. It's honestly pretty refreshing. While the Deadpool movies may hit you over the head with with their jokes, TOW2's writers have subtly woven references and gags into writing in a way that's natural and does more to immerse you than break the 4th wall.

One of the bits I enjoyed the most was a running gag where ex-dictatorship citizens are having trouble adjusting to life as corporate wage slaves. There's layers of trauma played upon in a way that makes you chuckle darkly. One such interaction is had at a fishing pier outside Fairfield--one new corpo rants about the joys of earning a wage, while the other has trouble remembering to refer to people as 'co-workers.' I am not doing it justice here--it's good.

Everything else is solid. Combat is fun and inventive, landing somewhere between Dishonored and Bioshock, and the RPG choices are well thought out. It may not be as big or ambitious as a huge The Elder Scrolls game, but so far I am having a ton of fun and find myself laughing in places I would have never expected it.

Oh and everywhere I get sidetracked to go winds up uncovering some cool NPC or other encounter. Even when the game is 'wasting my time,' none of my time is wasted.

I've been waiting for this game for 5 years, and it absolutely lived up to expectations.

I really liked The Outer Worlds 1, and to see the sequel to this game be this good is very nice to see. The one thing I knew going into it is that they spent a lot of time improving the combat/gameplay from the 1st one, which was the weakest part of that game, and it really shows here. I was worried that was going to come at the cost of other aspects of the game, but it didn't. The plot, characters, world-building, locations, and dialogue are on par or better than the 1st game as well. There seems to be a new engine, and with the combat being modernized and of course the graphics getting an upgrade I was worried the performance would take a hit, but somehow it runs better than the 1st one. I have almost 60 fps in most situations of the game; 0 stuttering, which is an insane accomplishment compared to a lot of AAA games coming out today (Borderlands 4, Oblivion Remaster). I do not have a NASA supercomputer also FYI.

Your background, skills, and perks really matter here, even more than the 1st one. If you like skill checks, you will love this game. Every skill either unlocks dialogue options, bolsters your playstyle, or allows you to interact with the world to open doors, repair power panels, unlock secret passageways, etc, and these are fully animated too. You can see your character open up power panels and replace broken components, or use magpicks to open doors, or using your technical knowledge to repair doors, etc. These seem like minor things, but it is really rewarding to see your character interact with the world like that. This game is loaded with RPG elements, even more than the 1st one.

This may be a controversial decision to some, but all drugs are now consumed on pickup to grant you percentages of inhaler charges, which has it's own toxicity meter, meaning you can't spam it in combat. I think it's a great change; drugs in Fallout and The Outer Worlds 1 were always way too overpowered and the whole game needed to be balanced with them in mind, so removing those and limiting how many heals you have per combat is a really great change in my opinion. You can build into your medical skill to give you more uses of your inhaler before crashing, and can find different equipment to slot into your inhaler to give different health effects, varying in HOT, instant heal, and toxicity amount. You don't go into your inventory before every fight to ingest 12 different drugs, you just play the game. There is a lot of other things to replace that aspect of the game, including multiple gadgets you can switch between, lots of throwables, interesting companion abilities, and movement abilities. They spent time streamlining parts from the 1st game that were clunky and adding new stuff that gels really well with the existing elements.

With the combat feeling really good, and the other elements of the game also excelling, I think it's very important to note that this does not rely on "memberberries" from the first game. Some factions return in altered ways, but its an entirely new solar system with (at least to the point I am at) completely new characters. It truly makes this universe feel huge, and that there is massive potential moving forward.

If you like Fallout or any 1st Person/3rd Person Shooter/RPG then please give this game a shot, Obsidian makes nothing but bangers.

64 hours on the clock, real life on pause, my friends probably worried, and I loved every second of it. This game completely sucked me in. The characters, the choices, the humor, the design, everything feels like Obsidian just said, “You wanted Fallout in space? Fine, let’s do it properly.”

What really surprised me was how often I stopped to think. Not just about skills or dialogue options, but about what kind of person (or corporate drone) I wanted to be in this messed up universe. The writing is classic Obsidian: clever, sharp, and perfectly fits the chaos.

At its core, The Outer Worlds 2 gives us exactly what we wanted from the first game and Fallout: more weirdness, better systems, and a much bigger world to explore. You can tell Obsidian actually listened this time.

Yeah, I ran into a few small bugs (after about 64 hours), but nothing major. Just the usual stuff you laugh off because the rest is so damn good.

Gameplay? Smooth. Story? Excellent. Characters? So well written you actually want to listen to their nonsense conversations. Not a single minute feels like a grind  it’s just pure adventure.

If you love RPGs, play this game. No “maybe later,” no “I’ll wait for a sale.” Just do it.

A true role playing game. You have a decent character creator, many options for skills, perks and gear to suit your play-style, and companions from each of the main factions. When adventuring, you can bring two companions with you, for a party of three. Melee, guns, and explosives are all fun, but the gadgets are where it gets whacky. You have the option of first or third person, with accessibility options to turn off motion blur, change the field of view, and disable the camera bobbing. 

If you liked Outer Worlds, this is a great sequel, with a new star system, and new factions, but the same colorful art style, satirical writing, and firefly-esque space colonies to explore. The setting is my favorite part of the game, followed by the companions and developing my own version of the commander and seeing how the world reacts to my choices. There are three main types of enemies, humans of various factions and roles, creatures, which vary planet to planet, and auto-mechanicals, this includes both small drones, and large fighting mecha dragoons. At launch the base game feels a little short, but I suspect that the post launch DLC will fill in the gaps, giving a longer play time. Obsidian remains one of my favorite developers, and while I liked Fallout New Vegas, Knights of the Old Republic II, and Tyranny better, that is a very high bar, and Outer Worlds 2 stands tall, easily as good as the first, and for me, slightly better than either Avowed, or Pillars of Eternity, though Deadfire was really good.
So much for this studio being the "King of Sequels"... more like the King of bad design choices.

Pros:

- Clear improvements over the first game with visuals and skill systems.
- Companions have more utility.
- Despite all the cons listed below, the game is fun and a good successor to the original, which I enjoyed. If you did not enjoy the first, you will not enjoy the second 

Cons:
- Performance was ok on my lower end gaming laptop (5 years old, GTX 1660TI) but I ended up refunding and repurchasing on PS5 instead. Performance mode is smooth as butter on there but quality mode isn't the best.

The gun play is fantastic as well. Very improved. 

Now, performance: I suggest turning off hardware ray tracing and just using the software raytracing via global illumination. Shadows is also worth turning down to high. Now I get around 100 FPS. Hardware raytracing really causes this game to stutter and suffer in performance on a 5070ti. I expect this will likely see improvements overtime. Now, people are trying to paint this game as being as unoptimized as Borderlands 4, but it is NOT that bad, honestly ya'll.
