A solid follow up to The Outer Worlds and worth full price.

I completed a melee play-through in the hardest difficulty. I used VALERIE and Marisol in my party for most of the game, but I completed all companion quests. The story kept my interest.

The Good: It works. Everything works--combat, the story, the personalities, the graphics. It's just solid. The setting is adapted from great sci-fi. All the things are here, and they are either fun or silly fun. This universe is fantastic to run around in. The story-telling got a lot of polish from the previous game and is where they made great improvements.

The Bad: The only thing that shines like a diamond is the setting. And there's nothing new to the mechanics, nothing visionary. I really wanted to see something I hadn't before. It seemed like there was room to improve. No one got around to it. And that's just too bad, because it's a good game that could have been great.

10 hours in and I can't help but feel like this isn't actually a role-playing game. 

As of now, I haven't been able to make any choices that meaningfully change the outcome of quests, the lore of the world is an inch thick - and this extends to the people inhabiting it. Skills amount to shallow stat-ups and skill-checks amount to locking you out of content- essentially punishing exploration. 

Just a few examples to demonstrate how anti role-playing this game is (spoilers):
- [spoiler]You meet a defector to a group of religious zealots while in their stronghold. She is trying to sneak out, but a cave of monsters is in her way. In dialogue you can either lie and to send her into the monsters to her death, tell her to return to her order of zealots, or just leave. Playing a good character, i went and cleared out the monsters then returned to her, only to find she was now a basic zealot NPC and was hostile.[/spoiler]
- [spoiler]A quest involves a character that is framed for a crime. You conduct an "investigation" which hides clues behind skill-checks. In the end, whether you had sufficient skills for your character to interpret the obvious clues or not, the ending is binary - you side with the obvious framee and catch the framer or you kill the aforementioned framee. There is no punishment for not finding the clues and the determinant for finding those useless clues is having specialized in one or more of 12 skills - not your own deductive power as a player. You either kill the wrong person or, with or without the right clues, kill the right person. And yes, you can't even choose whether to spare the perpetrator or not[/spoiler]
- [spoiler]As the conclusion to the games first planet, the game, with no player input, crashes a space station onto a nearby town owned by one of the game's main factions - killing everyone in it. As a result of this you receive a minor decrease in reputation with that faction (not even enough to push you into a lower category with them) and nothing else. You aren't made to crash the station into one faction or another, aren't made to trade a prize or an important npc to prevent it, and aren't able to maximize the carnage for an evil playthrough - and for crashing a space station into a town you receive no punishment.[/spoiler]

Those are just a few - but they're a microchasm of the [i]entire[/i] experience.

Pros: graphics are great and the vistas are quite beautiful. 
Voice acting is top notch.
Multiple ways to resolve situations.  Skill choices are rather meaningful, characters remember your choices, but the overarching story never really changes, which frankly is normal. There really isn't a game where you can "go off the grid" so to speak.

Cons: I have a top of the line rig with a 5080OC, the performance is atrocious. While you can lower and tune all the settings to get high fps, if you want to run ray tracing and to play on 4k on the highest setting, the game will run fine for a bit, spike, run fine, spike randomly. I would rather it just ran poorly but consistently. Instead, it's all over the place. 

The game is slow to start and does not hook you. The first main villian isn't very interesting.  For over half of the game, you are just chasing her, following a breadcrumb trail of audiologs. Its boring, but gets far more interesting after events FINALLY get into motion halway through.

Compare that to BG3: you awaken on a ship under assault by Dragons after Squidward puts a mind control slug into your head. There's intrigue, mystery, a ticking clock (even if nothing ever happens if you long rest 100 times) its far more captivating. 

In this game, they try to setup a ticking clock solar threat, but it never really amounts to anything until you are more than 50% into the game.

Also build variety isn't as interesting as it first seems. You quickly realize that many skills arent really worth it like hacking, lockpicking and leadership. The rewards arent worth the investment because the game caps at 30 and it caps EARLY. 

All in all, still alot of fun. Lightweight and relaxing game that doesn't test you or have you think too much. There's violence, but its so goofy and colorful, that frankly this could be a PG rated game if they took out all the blood and dead bodies everywhere. 

Worth a buy on sale.

After reading all the folks saying Fallout NV is back I was very excited for this game. But this is nothing like FO NV and most certainly not a role playing game.  There are no skill checks or rolls of the dice its all binary. Do you have this skill?  Is it a high enough level? Yes? Get something if no fail or get nothing. Take lock picking it is a number. If your skill meets or beats your character just does it no input from you except a single button push same with hacking.  No choice seems to matter nor do they seem to change anything. The story seems fine but that's it, fine. And  in 18 plus hours I can't remember a single characters name. Guns have no real weight and every enemy seems like the most damage spongy slog of a fight. Get the drop on a group? Great, stealth breaks after the first shot and everyone knows where you are. Also allies love to continue shooting while following you through the doors you closed and leading enemies directly to you. Not that the combat is hard it's just mostly boring.  Long and short of it is if you are interest in a mediocre story and very very light rpg mechanics this might be for you. But if you are looking for NV 2 keep looking this ain't it. ANd if you find it let me know.

While playing through the first planet, I made friends with Auntie's Choice, saw my rep with them go up, and heard talk of the Protectorate. Eventually, I figured, I'd need to work on my rep with them, and talk with them, and then ultimately decide who I'd want to help.

But when I ran into the Protectorate, they were immediately aggressive toward me. I was shoot-on-sight, as it were. I wondered what I had ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up. I wondered why I didn't even have a reputation bar with them. Had I done something to make them hate me forever? 

Then I realized that I can't befriend them. The only faction that matters is Auntie's Choice. I don't have a choice. :P 

And, admittedly, I don't even like the Protectorate. But I don't like AC, either. But I had assumed going into this that this would be a game about choices and supporting a faction and investigating their beliefs. You know, sort of like New Vegas.

This isn't like New Vegas, though. 

And when I realized this, I realized why I was kind of "meh" (or 6-7, as the kids say) about the game. 

I suppose it's my fault for trying to draw comparisons between TOW2 and NV--but, at the same time, the devs themselves have definitely drawn comparisons between TOW2 and NV. And, frustratingly, those comparisons are coming up short. 

It's not a bad game. I'm certainly having what could be described as "fun." I had a few "laugh" moments. But, for the most part, nothing's really jumped out at me. Nothing's really memorable. It's just a bunch of wacky events and someone says something wacky and then they get space-blasted or something. 

It's not a game that should be invoking NV. They did, though. And I dumbly bought it on that invocation.

I will play more to see how this evolves but as of right now I would say that this game is a 6/10, very mediocre. Why? I find that there's a distinct lack of narrative RPG elements. Sure all quests usually have one skill check, that can change the narrative in a minor way, but nothing that leave a impact for the rest of the game. 

Most side quest usually has two options, either the regular way of solving it through the basic dialogue choices or if you're lucky and have spent your skill points in the right skill, the other way. Sometimes but certainly not a majority there's a way to find an alternative solution through reading computer logs or finding something, but these are not as common as I'd like. 

Then comes my other issue, I never feel immersed in the world. There's no stakes and lives are worth as much as a spilled milkshake, I know that's what they're going for with the late-stage capitalism narrative, but why should I care if I save a village or not? I never see any moral consequence for it, nothing challenges my sense of what is right or not. 

So would I recommend it, yes? I don't think it deserves to be hated on and I think there is some joy to be had from it, but I would choose a lot of other RPGs before this.

The Outer Worlds 2 – A Familiar Yet Fresh Journey Through the Stars

The Outer Worlds 2 manages to capture much of what made the original game memorable while refining several aspects for a smoother, more engaging experience. Even though the story stands on its own, newcomers might miss a few subtle references and contextual details from the first game. That said, it’s still perfectly playable as a standalone title and an enjoyable one at that.

The Good

The storyline is intriguing and well-paced, filled with moral choices and witty dialogue that define Obsidian’s style. Character creation is impressively detailed, offering a variety of background stories, perks, and skills that let players truly shape their own spacefaring persona. The perk and skill systems are noticeably more streamlined than in the first game, allowing for flexible playstyles and encouraging multiple playthroughs without feeling repetitive or stale.

Exploration is another highlight. Each planet feels distinct and alive, showcasing a wide range of environments that keep the experience fresh. The reduction in area transitions and loading screens is also a huge improvement—exploring the galaxy feels more seamless and less interrupted. As always, the humor shines through in the dialogue and narrative interactions. The writing is sharp, witty, and full of the darkly comedic tone that fans of the series will appreciate (though that’s always subjective, of course).

The Not-So-Good

Unfortunately, The Outer Worlds 2 isn’t without its flaws. Companion characters, while diverse in personality, don’t feel meaningfully different in combat performance. Whether this stems from AI behavior or balancing issues, it makes their presence in battles somewhat underwhelming. Their personal quests are decent, but none particularly stand out or leave a lasting impression.

Combat balance is another area that needs attention. On higher difficulties, the game feels punishing—not because of smarter enemies, but because of how quickly the player can be eliminated in just a few hits. This issue is compounded by the lackluster companion AI, making some encounters feel frustrating rather than challenging. Investing heavily in Leadership perks becomes almost mandatory just to keep companions useful, which in turn skews overall balance and limits build diversity. I enjoy a good challenge, but the current difficulty curve feels uneven and, at times, unfair.

Final Thoughts

The Outer Worlds 2 delivers a rich, witty, and immersive space RPG that refines much of what worked in the original while introducing new systems and better world design. However, its balancing issues, inconsistent companion mechanics, and uneven combat keep it from reaching its full potential. Still, if you loved the tone and storytelling of the first game, this sequel is absolutely worth experiencing.

Alright. So I've finished my first playthrough of this doing as much side content as possible that I could.

To the Pros of this game!
Very fun to play, truthfully kept me in the game whenever i launched it for the entire day almost.
Lots of fun weaponry to mess with and some unique builds to go with
Love the armour designs in the game they feel quite the breath of fresh air.
Gameplay was super fun, fast movement, weapons didn't need updated every level, neither was armour
Fun perks but I prefered the first games leveling system, could've done some kinda mix of perks and stats but thats me.

Sadly now the long list of Cons imo
Story is very straight roaded. even with all the side content that i found
Maps have a lot of tall terrain so whenever looking at the map it honestly looks like I just followed quest markers and I didn't feel like i explored the world as much as the first game because of this
There was a very large lack of other factions, one of the best things about the first game was there was a lot of random factions around even minor ones that didn't have huge impacts but they still existed making the world feel alive.
The story was fun however it felt very bland at the same time, i have maybe 2 or 3 memorable quests in my head and the rest kinda blurred together of how little they mattered to me
The companions are great all unique, HOWEVER only TWO feel like they have any real meat on them and are interesting which is Niles and Tristin which ended up causing me to only use them as well. They were the only two that would have interactions with eachother, randomly just chat with eachother where everyone else was always dead silent, Aza had some interest at the start but as the game went on she fell into the dead quiet and quite boring catagory. VAL gets a pass cuz shes very much like SAM and has only a couple lines and doesn't really have major things to do. Grandma (i will always call her this) was neat but her introduction of being this helpless old lady then being like this hardcore killer was cool 100% cool. but after that, she's just..there like everyone mentioned how dangerous she is but there was virtually nothing involved with that. Tristin was mentioned more when he was around than she was 100%. Inez...oh Inez. potential to being really cool, her questline was a nice dilemma but without her questline i could have easily forgotten she was a companion at all. Mind you i finished everyones stories cuz i wanted to see how much I could enjoy more.
The factions themselves. their writing Honestly felt kinda like spicy water. they had some neat interactions and could be very interesting and have a ton involved with them. The order was super cool when I met them, a new faction all about Logic. super neat! but of course it was sadly not as interesting as I hoped. Auntie Cleo was boring and I hated her faction every second of the way. even with getting High relations I did what I wanted to do which instantly made the faction my enemy and i felt honestly happier with them as an enemy than an ally and even then I hoped for SOME interaction
Sub Rosa. I forgot was even there truthfully. I couldn't find really any quests that involved them and even with the ones i did i sped through and forgot about almost fully because unlike the last game they really had almost no impact at all.
Protectorate. simple. should have been their own faction and similar to the board or even a faction that starts off negative but you could potentially build relations with them. I mean the first games main badguy (even if not actually the bad guy) was the Board and you could be on their side instead of Welles. which had a lot of interest!

Now I haven't even gotten into some of the more spoiler cons of the game but I do love this game. I'm going to play it again. and I want to let you guys know I do reccomend it. I just think the first game honestly had much more love than this one. I will be doing another playthrough and possibly update this again, to let everyone see how I feel. overall I would give the game a 7.5/10
Loving the game thus far. No complaints.

If there was ever a need for a neutral recommendation in Steam, this game is it because I keep changing my midn between Yes and No. The game is too flawed and not enjoyable enough to recommend, but it has some strong hints of greatness that make it feel bad to just reject it outright. Too bad the good is barely enough to outweight the bad, or even more, the mediocre.

It's a good enough game but be warned that it is also UE5 slop with bad performance and the mod to have 3 skill points per level is bascially required. Combat starts really rough and limiting but balance improves as you level up.

Companion writing is meh and they are completely useless in both dialog and combat, again a shocking regression from the original. They cannot help with skill checks and their only usability in combat is serving as decoys for the around 2 seconds it takes for them to be downed. As you cannot change their gear, there is not much that you can do, other than dump points into the Leadership skill to reduce the insane cooldowns of their already weak abilities (which you don't want to do as the game only gives you 2 skill points per level at a max of 30 levels, meaning only 3 skills can be maxed at cap out of around 10-15, and there is no way to reset without mods -- another symptom of the game's hollow attempts to mimic depth with restriction and punishment).

it's not a bad game but it it's by far not a good one either. Obsidian needs to do better for a full-priced game, because this is definitely not it, nor is it a move in the right direction in most parts. It's not a deep RPG, it's a restrictive RPG that pretends restrictions = meaningful choice. And the downgrade in character writing, despite an overall better plot (especially on terms of factions/companions) and truly bad combat, combined with the awful performance, are just not something that can pass in 2025.

7.5/10 - given the overall constraints it displays and the obvious low ambition and budget.

I didn’t quite enjoy The Outer Worlds 1 — for some reason, it just never clicked with me. Still, I wanted to give the sequel a chance, and I have to say, I’m loving it so far. The universe feels rich and alive, and the story has been genuinely engaging up to this point.

What really stands out is how your character progression and choices actually matter. Decisions carry real weight, and you have to live with the consequences — for better or worse. It gives the game a nice sense of permanence and depth that I really appreciate.

It also reminds me a bit of KOTOR in the way the world opens up to you. The pacing feels natural — it nudges you forward without ever feeling forced.

Overall, it’s a great game and, in my opinion, much better balanced than Avowed. I was a bit worried they’d feel too similar, but I’m glad The Outer Worlds 2 found its own solid identity.

My only feedback: it would be great if you could swap companions either in the main town or right at the ship’s door, instead of having to go back inside the ship every time. It’s a small thing, but it would make party management much smoother.

I would recommend this game for those who enjoyed what Outer Worlds 1 provided, with a little more depth. I do wish that many things were locked behind the need to heavily spec into certain skills if you wanted other options in dialogue and open world interactions. With only 60 skill points to spend, you do have to narrow your focus (vs Skyrim where you literally can just keep leveling up and get it all). That is not to say it isnt a good mechanic, I think it forces to some degree the "Role" in RPG. Gunplay is fun, I do agree with other reviews were enemies are definitely spongy in early game, but it evens out by the end of the 1st/2nd world as you level up (and do side missions to scale your character accordingly). 

Storyline wise, it could have been longer and I feel like my actions could have had more consequences for the overall world.  I did enjoy trying to discover every route to a main mission and seeing which route would have the best results, and I did like how detailed the end title card is when explaining the world after the final mission. I always enjoyed seeing how my actions end up influencing the world (and not a short blurb like Starfield). Also, I do wish the companions you work with had a bit more life to them, maybe it was just my overall playtime but I did not see much of my companions interacting with each other when out in the world. 

If you enjoyed Outer Worlds 1, FO, and ES, this is pretty much another chapter in exploring your moral/immoral tendencies and seeing how you influence the world around you.

I came into this game very excited as I loved The Outer Worlds 1. However, I have to say I'm extremely disappointed in the final product here. They give you the illusion of choice with perks, strengths, weaknesses but it always seems you're under leveled for every encounter or skill check no matter what. It feels like I'm being railroaded towards a certain outcome. Progressing any storyline feels like a grind, you're always out of ammo, money and crafting supplies. The damage dealt by enemies is high yet the guns feel weak and powerless, everything feels like it shoots nerf darts. Over all this game feels frustrating to play. The real sin here is the writing and overall vibes of the storyline, gone are the witty, fun, tongue in cheek dialogue. Gone is the sense of wonder and adventure the first game built. It's just too serious in tone, just feels like a dystopian hellscape. 

It feels like this game is actively trying to punish me for playing it. I don't recommend it and I wish I never bought it. I'm disappointed to say I will not be finishing this title. At this point anything that comes from an Xbox owned studio, stay away from it. t least it's better than Starfield. 

TL;DR: feels like a chore, game punishes creativity, poor balance, and lacks the soul that made the first game so damn good. Don't bother, save your money like I wish I could.

First off I do want to say that I have been loving this game and it builds off of everything I like from the first one. Unfortunately, I’ve encountered a late game bug that completely soft locks you from finishing the game with both main factions united. Non spoiler version: If you go to the archive early to scope it out, do not fix the lift right away until it’s patched. Also: make sure you save right before you do in case you encounter any bugs that I outline under spoilers:

[spoiler] During my first iteration, I decided I wanted to do the archive mission before finishing wrapping up the side mission’s aunties choice and the order give you. After getting the lift parts form that one guy in the cave I fixed the lift, and decided to leave and do side missions / companions questions, etc. Since I didn’t talk to the Bishop Ruth before this I was unable to ask her about forming an alliance with aunties choice *the dialog was completely unavailable, even though I was reminded there would only be one time to propose it). It also seems to lock out any dialog with aunties choice after completing the refreshment mission – I also noticed the VP’s recite the same dialog as if you’re starting the mission. TL/DR of that instance: by completing the archive solo for the achievement, you’re soft locked out of negotiating an alliance with no dialog options available. 

So I wasn’t sure what happened, and decided to roll back before getting the lift parts. Did both missions and secured the alliance, but somewhere along the way I enabled the lift again. The Outer Worlds 2 is a "yes but" in my book. It's greatest fault is trying to turn a AA game into a AAA experience, falling into every trap it could fall into. While the first game was short and sweet with good ideas but mediocre execution, this game lacks the impact of the first while focusing on how to make everything bigger and longer and grander without any significant justification

The story for once is borderline nonsensical. Sure you have all the faction and choices that is Obsidian's playground, but it's just more factions, more dialogue, more intrigue, and NONE of it is memorable. The protectorate, the directorate, auntie's choice, the order, the golden dawn, are just names at the end of the day. None of the companions is memorable let's say like Parvati of the first game. None of the storylines offer a punch to make it stay and resonate. The villains are vague and their motives are lost into endless lines of dialogue OR into optional information that you may never find. 

The gameplay and combat is good, for a time. After a while, and especially towards the end, it will spiral into an endless "fast travel marathon". Yes, it has all the strengths of an Obsidian game - and it is main redeeming quality. But more weapons, more damage types, more type enemies, more abilities do not necessarily mean better experience. Weapons have damage type AND ammo type, making picking the optimal choice of weapon and gear into a drag. Mods are joke, period. 

And then you have the errors, (I'm plagued by the UE5 errors and the constant loading earlier files) the UE5 optimization that drags the fun even lower on low-end machines. And the 70 euro pricetag

So why the positive recommend? Because at its core is not the AAA Microsoft wants it to be. It's a AA obsidian game that has the same heart at least as the first. I'm positive - although its a theory and I don't have any proof - that Obsidian wanted to make a small AA game, but the execs from Xbox pushed them into making it more big and more intricate to justify the price.

🧊 The Outer Worlds 2 – “A Galaxy Rendered Twice”
⭐ 5.5 / 10 — When beauty meets bloat and the engine forgets to breathe

⚙️ System Setup
Played entirely under Ubuntu Server 25.4, self-compiled kernel and Wine environment.
No Proton layers, no prebuilt packages — I ran this raw through Wine-Staging 10.16 using VKD3D-Proton 3.0 for DirectX 12 translation.
MangoHUD + Gamemode active throughout testing.

Hardware Den (The Den of Ice & Circuits):
🧠 CPU: Intel Core i7-3820 (4 cores / 8 threads, 3.6 GHz base, ~3.8 GHz boost)
🧊 GPU: NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 (8 GB VRAM, Pascal generation)
🪵 Motherboard: Rampage IV Extreme
💾 Memory: 16 GB DDR3 (dual-channel, 1600 MHz)
🧱 Storage: SATA SSD (OS + Game)
📺 Display: 1920×1080 @ 60 Hz
🐧 OS: Ubuntu Server 25.4 (X11 + GNOME desktop environment)

Graphics Stack:
NVIDIA 550.xx drivers (Vulkan backend active)
DX12 handled through VKD3D-Proton
GStreamer codecs installed for in-engine videos
DXVK and NVIDIA shader caches enabled:
DXVK_SHADER_CACHE=1
__GL_SHADER_DISK_CACHE=1

💻 Performance Overview
Let’s get this straight — this game is absurdly GPU heavy.
The Outer Worlds 1 ran 60–90 FPS easily on this same setup, but the sequel barely managed 25–35 FPS on High settings, even after multiple patches.
It’s not due to VKD3D or Linux overhead — it’s just badly optimized, both on Windows and Linux.

VKD3D handled translation beautifully; I had zero crashes, stable frametimes after caching, and consistent input response.
But frame pacing was all over the place when the game insisted on rebuilding shaders from scratch every single startup.

🧩 The Shader Cache Nightmare
This is where things went from annoying to ridiculous.
Even with both DXVK and NVIDIA shader caches enabled — which normally precompiles shaders and prevents stutter —
The Outer Worlds 2 completely ignores them.

Every single launch starts with the same dreaded screen:

“GENERATING SHADERS – This process converts shader code into a format tailored to your hardware.”

That message appears every time you boot the game.
Doesn’t matter if you played yesterday, or an hour ago — it recompiles the entire shader library from scratch,
using its own internal cache system separate from DXVK and NVIDIA’s Vulkan disk cache.

The process takes 20–30 minutes, pegging the CPU between 70–85 %, while the GPU idles.
You literally watch your hardware burn electricity doing work that’s already been done — twice.
And this behavior is identical on Windows, which means this isn’t a Wine issue — it’s the engine itself.

For a game using Unreal Engine 5-based tech, it feels like the caching pipeline is half-baked.
No precompiled shaders, no reuse, no validation against driver-level caches — just blind recompilation every launch.

If you’re on Linux, that means your DXVK and NVIDIA caches — the same ones that make DOOM Eternal or RE4 Remake butter smooth —
are completely ignored.

It’s honestly one of the worst “modern game optimization” practices I’ve seen in years.

🎮 Gameplay Experience
Once you finally get past the shader purgatory and into the actual game, the first few hours are solid.
It has the charm, sarcasm, and quirky corporate dystopia of the first Outer Worlds.
You wake up, you talk, you loot, you shoot — all the usual Obsidian rhythm.

But after the intro, the pacing collapses.

The first game had a tight focus and witty character dynamics.
This one spreads itself too thin — it wants to be Fallout, Mass Effect, Dishonored, and Bioshock Infinite all at once.
You get stealth mechanics that feel designed for small, dense maps… but then you’re thrown into open landscapes that don’t support them.

At around 60 % progress, I caught myself thinking:

“I just want this over.”
And that’s never a good sign.

⚔️ Combat & AI Behavior
* Gunplay feels heavier and slower than the first game.
* Melee combat still feels floaty and under-tuned.
* Time-dilation skills are back, but effects are over-filtered and obscure vision.
* AI pathfinding is inconsistent — enemies forget you exist after 10 seconds.
* Companions constantly interrupt with voice lines about morality but never actually leave.
* One companion threatened me three times over my choices,
and every time I said, “Sorry, won’t happen again,” — then did it again —
and nothing happened. No departure, no consequence.

That lack of consequence sums up the game perfectly.

🧠 Writing, Tone & Representation
Let’s talk story.
It starts strong, like The Outer Worlds 1, with corporate satire and philosophical undertones about freedom, morality, and manipulation.
But the midsection drags and loses identity.

Characters like Niles (voiced by Eugene Byrd) feel added for marketing appeal more than narrative purpose.
And before anyone screams “woke,” let’s be clear:
the first game already had diversity — Parvati was openly lesbian and wonderfully written.
The difference is that in the first game, representation felt organic.
Here, it feels mandated.

Diversity in storytelling is great — but it should serve the narrative, not checklist quotas.

🌆 Graphics & Engine Behavior (DX12 via VKD3D)
Visually, it’s gorgeous.
Lighting, texture detail, and color depth are all excellent.
But it’s drenched in post-processing filters: chromatic aberration, heavy film grain, dynamic bloom, and motion blur stacked three layers deep.
Even when disabled in menus, the filters still partially render.

The result?
A GPU furnace.

My GTX 1080 ran between 80–83 % load the entire time, yet CPU threads barely fed it fast enough.
The problem isn’t Linux or VKD3D — it’s the game’s overdraw.
This is a title that renders 5 layers of lighting where one would do.

🧩 Hotfix 1.0.4.1 and Ongoing Support

Two consecutive updates (3 GB + 2 GB) arrived quickly.
They fixed major crashes, quest progression locks, and save corruption — all good moves.
But nothing touched the root problems:
shader compilation times, performance scaling, or the heavy filtering system.

I appreciate fast patch response, but the core performance issues remain exactly as before.

💡 Linux Performance Summary
Aspect	                Status	        Notes
Stability	               ✅ Excellent	Zero crashes under VKD3D
Performance	       ⚠️ Poor	        25–35 FPS on High settings
Shader Handling    ❌ Broken	Rebuilds internal shaders every launch
Visuals	               ✅ Beautiful	Dense, cinematic lighting
Optimization	       ❌ Terrible	High filter cost, poor threading
Gameplay Flow      ⚠️ Mixed  	Great intro & finale, dull middle

🧊 Verdict

The Outer Worlds 2 is the definition of wasted potential.
It looks incredible, has flashes of brilliance in writing and art direction,
but its pacing, mechanics, and technical execution drag it down.

It’s not unplayable — it’s just exhausting.
You spend more time compiling shaders and waiting for smooth frames than actually exploring its world.
That’s not innovation — that’s negligence disguised as fidelity.

It feels like a studio torn between wanting to make art and pleasing shareholders.
The result is a confused sequel with good intentions, bad code, and an engine that hates efficiency.

🧊 Final Thoughts
I finished it, but only because I’m stubborn.
It’s not a bad game — it’s just wasteful.
It wastes power, time, and narrative momentum.
And while it technically runs on Linux better than many Windows builds,
it still serves as a perfect reminder that beautiful doesn’t always mean optimized.

❄️ Final Rating
⭐ 5.5 / 10 — A cold masterpiece wrapped in warm incompetence.
System: Ubuntu Server 25.4 • Wine-Staging 10.16 • VKD3D-Proton 3.0 • GTX 1080 • 25–35 FPS (High)
Reviewed by: 🧊 Kiba Snowpaw – Alpha Ice Wolf of HowlStrom 🐾

Some spoilers ahead!

I enjoyed the game but the lack of content hit me like a brick as I neared the end and realized "It's ending already? Where's the rest of the Sub Rosa content? Why do the conversations with Ruth or Auntie Cleo both not include the option to negotiate the peace deal, is it because I took over the Archive the hard way? My reputation with Auntie's is higher than my reputation with the order, so why does Auntie just suddenly hang up and not give me the option to side with Auntie's? Maybe that one was a bug idk, but either way, why after that conversation with Auntie led to nothing positive or negative, does continuing the Order's quest lead to a kill on sight reputation with Auntie's? The delegates survived, and they were so close to having peace, and I had a good reputation with Auntie's, and now it all just means nothing? And like was someone outside the door on the phone with Auntie while I was talking to Ruth to tell her my decision immediately? (That last one's not really a big deal just regular video game logic but still lol)" I decided to keep my reputation with Auntie's by siding against the Order and soloing the ending, I would've chosen to side with Auntie's if I could, maybe if I had first went to Ruth and sided against the Order then I could continue Auntie's quest line, I'm not sure.


There's 3 or 5 (or 6?) factions, I'll talk first about the Protectorate. The Protectorate have a large presence in the game but you don't have the ability to side with them officially, the only times you really interact with them as anything other than "the baddies" are if you choose to help Westport rather than "convincing" (sabotaging) the folks to defect (I didn't do this one I "convinced them" to defect, so can't really comment on it), and the companion quest line for Tristan has you working to investigate the death of a protectorate official but still even in that quest line there are times you're forced to kill several protectorate soldiers.

Then there's the Earth Directorate who are basically your bosses but they're outside of the solar system the game takes place in, so they're barely talked about past the intro/prologue.

There's also the rift cult but again they're mostly just enemies to shoot at, even more so than the protectorate, even the one person you can talk to and work with from the cult, your possible companion Aza, is down to kill every other cultist in sight because they were ordered to kill her. 

So there are 3 "real" factions, one is Sub Rosa, which like I mentioned earlier really leaves me wanting for more, and not in a good "That was great! I can't wait for the next game!" kind of way, in the "That's it? Seriously?" way. Sub Rosa is so thread-bare in content. Firstly I'd like to mention the best Sub Rosa quest, you meet a Sub Rosa agent who's undercover as a member of the Order in Laplace's Garden where you find out The Order is secretly drugging the garden residents, the agent just wants a sample of the drug, so you're tasked to infiltrate the water treatment plant to steal a sample for Sub Rosa, this one is exactly what a Sub Rosa mission should be, no notes. Aside from that quest, the first Sub Rosa quest is the next closest you'll get to actually doing any stealing or smuggling, but the stealing is already done, your job is just to gain access to a computer terminal to delete the stolen goods from the inventory records. The only other memorable/notable Sub Rosa quest is the quest to get get Tristan an audience with the Sub Rosa information broker, which leads to the chance to recruit Tristan as a companion. The job you're given to get Tristan his audience is to investigate a refugee camp who used to communicate regularly with the broker, but have suddenly gone quiet, you show up to the camp, turns out they were all brutally killed so it's an investigation into that, not really the kind of smuggler-y work you'd expect to do for Sub Rosa, but still a good quest. Overall there's only enough to give you a taste of the faction.

Maybe Sub Rosa will have more presence in one of the expansions, like how Sublight Salvage had a presence on Gorgon in the first game's expansion Peril on Gorgon, and if you finished their quest line in the main game and became a Sublight Vice President your status could be useful on Gorgon. On the flip-side, given there isn't really such a quest line and status to achieve with Sub Rosa in this sequel's base game, and there are so few factions this time around, maybe it would be best if each expansion added and focused on another faction.

Now to discuss the Order.
They're fine.
Matriarch's Mercy has some good quests.
I liked that upon arriving on Dorado where you're first properly introduced to the Order, you first see an unprovoked explosive attack on Auntie's Choice and when you reach your Order contact in Matriarch's Mercy you're told that they didn't launch that attack and they ask you to investigate who launched the attack and deliver a message to Auntie's Choice that the Order didn't launch the attack, that's a solid quest line.

I also like that the Order isn't just portrayed as the perfect obvious choice faction, while you may believe they have the strongest morals, it's made clear that they are, pretty ironically, organizationally challenged, you can see this in the poor state of Matriarch's Mercy, the fact that they lost control of their archive supercomputer, and the fact that one rogue person was able to launch one of their explosives in an unprovoked attack on Auntie's Choice and nearly successfully frame another person. So you have to decide (well unless you succeed in peace talks, then you don't have to decide) do you side with the Order, hoping they do the right thing with the power and information to stop the rifts, and hoping they don't fall to a competing faction like the Protectorate or Auntie's Choice, or do you side with Auntie's Choice, who will undoubtedly use the power and information to commercialize the control of rifts for profit which is risky, but they have the strong leadership, power, and influence to safeguard the knowledge from getting into the wrong(er) hands.

Which brings me to the last faction to discuss, Auntie's Choice.
One one hand it's a disappointment that compared to the first game where you had Monarch Stellar, The Board, Spacer's Choice, and Auntie Cleo's, (plus Rizzo's existed but they weren't a full faction) you now just have one capitalist group as THE company, on the other hand they have some of the best quests, including Milverstreet's quest to convince the people of Westport to defect to Fairfield, Inez's companion quests to hunt huge beasts to get parts for an Auntie's Choice doctor familiar with grafts to redo Inez's graft, and to investigate the deaths of enhanced soldiers, including a friend of Inez, at the incompetent hands of another Auntie's Choice doctor at a medical research lab in a frozen cave on Cloister. There's an under the table quest given to you by a few middle managers on the ACS Undisputed Claim looking to gather dirt on other employees to blackmail their way to a higher position. There's also the missions given in the head office to earn an audience with Auntie, one you need more information on mental refreshment so you infiltrate the Protectorate's mental refreshment facility by submitting yourself to mental refreshment, such a great mission, another you have to help negotiate to end a strike at an Auntie's Choice factory.

Also, I picked the dumb trait and loved the dumb dialog in Arbitrary Measures, and the dumb way to try and help the wounded guy in the Fairfield clinic. I won't spoil. You NEED to try the dumb trait for yourself, I insist, it had me laughing out loud for real multiple times.

Overall, I liked the game and I can recommend you give it a chance.

Why, I don't recommend it for two reasons mostly: the pricetag is a bit too high (especially since it's mostly pocketed by Microsoft now) and the game is quite broken in its current state.
Myriads of issues that scream "lack of time" plague the software, which is odd since much time Obsidian supposedly had with this one. To pick an example among many: even if running as administrator, I have to constantly reconfigure the game settings (graphics & key mappings) everytime I reopen it. Or the simple yet eloquent fact that the default key mapping is QWERTY only, which is something so rare nowadays among middle range games that I was genuinely baffled to witness it here.

To talk about the game itself, if you somehow are brave enough to endure this overpriced faulted version: it's quite good. The introductoin was pretty meh in my book but the first planet is well designed. The focus on reactivity is greatly appreciated this time around. Though, one major mishap still troubles my mind after roughly ten hours of playtime: they did the same mistake as in TOW1 yet again = you can't side with the antagonistic faction (The Protectorate). Actually, I don't know if you can, but what I know is they don't have any sort of reputation mechanic like other factions. This is even odder when you realise, quickly, that you can actually work for them, and make their life easier on the starting planet (Lost Paradise).

Overall, this game needs fixing, and it should take a couple of months of work considering the sheer amounts of issues they're facing right now. And 70 dollars is way way way overpriced. Microsoft are insane for doing this and putting such a needless pressure on the devs who, ostensibly, are not that comfortable with a game of that scope.

I'll change to recommend once both these aspects are addressed because, truth be told, I'm having fun.

EDIT +20hrs : I've read here and there that this is an improvement over the first game and now that I'm close to finishing it, I can affirm this is mostly false. The game is very much similar to the first opus. The change to skills was welcome but the game design paradigm didn't really move a iota, so don't expect much reactivity to your build beyond the way you fire your weapons and gadgets.
I've also read here and there that this is "finally new vegas in space" and, again, this is (very) false. This game was actually more reminiscent of Mass Effect 2 than New Vegas. What I mean by that is that most areas are basically levels set for a specific mission, and once said mission is done they barely exist in the world. Hubs are extremely static and NPC interactions are barebone. More often than not, you'll find yourself simply unable to talk to NPCs due to the status of an active quest that simply disables any dialog trees on their end, leaving a generic response similar to those of generic npcs.

Come to think of it, this game doesn't have dialogues. It has NPC monologues that you (or your companions) can interject with inane comments or pointless skill checked lines. Now that I played the game more, I'm starting to believe that the first Outer Worlds, somehow, was more of an RPG than this is. I expected nothing and I'm still immensely disappointed. Odd feeling...

I said I would recommend it once they fix it but frankly the flaws are so fundamentally rooted in the very game design that nothing can make this experience less boring than it is. It's very very similar to Outer Worlds 1 but with less choices somehow. Also the photography was better in the previous game,which at least had the merit of being eye candy from beginning to end.

So, whilst I was initially disappointed in this game, and posted a review not recommending the game, I have subsequently put another 40 hours into it, and completed it, and have changed my opinion to a tentative positive recommendation.  The game isn't everything it could and should have been.  The graphics look dated, the armour sets are almost universally ugly, the lack of any traversal gadgets (jet packs, grapples, scuba etc) apart from a double jump make the verticality of many zones a pain, and force boring fast travel.  The story is ok, it's nothing spectacular.  The voice acting is ok. Combat is fun.  The skill system whilst initially appearing wide and diverse and full of opportunities, just isn't.  You hamstring yourself if you try to do more than 3 things well (I strongly recommend speech, followed by hack, and then probably the combat skill of your choice - there were several speech 20 checks towards the end of the game that you'd miss if you multiskilled).  The level cap is probably 5 levels too low, I hit 30 with several companion quests still to do, and hadn't even ventured into the archive yet, never mind the two small zones following that chunk of content - so the final quests started to feel like a slog with no xp reward and no further progression.  7/10.  Could have been much better, but worth a playthrough.

Fun SF RPG shooter.

The setting is light-hearted (similar to Fallout), with broad satire of both capitalism and totalitarianism. The gameplay is very much like their previous release (the much more serious-minded fantasy, "Avowed") - it's not a massive area free-roam, it's set in decent-sized but limited play areas. After that, usual stuff: kill things, collect money and ingredients, craft stuff, have some companions, etc. Pick which factions to support, be a hero / antihero / scumbag, etc.

It all works well. It's not a AAA game so don't expect that sort of scope and polish, but it's entertaining enough. Little is exceptional, but everything is done at least okay, and most of it is good: the RPG, the shooting, stealth, etc. Plot, characterisations and scripting is comfortably superior to many of its peers. Performance is fine for my mid-range rig, although the graphics are a little fuzzy (TOW2 is not the only recent game that seems to deal with running on modest hardware this way).

So, all in all, I'm happy with it.

Update upon further condiseration:
The restrictive “Only 3 skills” skill system that has no respecs kills all replayability factor for me. In TOW1 you could pick what you wanted to do (peaceful run, stealth run, murder run, etc) and use whatever skills you wanted, if your build wasn’t working, respec, and keep the fun rolling.

TOW2, you really better like your beginning skills, or it’s a new run. Companions also don’t boost stats, so you can’t round out your weaknesses with that either. The devs mistakes here seem to be assuming skills drove replayability, rather than the narrative/playstyle choices. 
This only further adds to the point of “Why did I pay nearly $80 for a game that doesn’t give me the freedom to play how I want when that’s exactly what the first one did?”

if there's one word I can use to describe this 2025 release, it's dated. It's neither offensively bad, nor is it innovative. The game design is pretty much what anyone should expect from an Obsidian rpg, if it came out 15 years ago. This sequel is much like the first: it's Fallout in space, again. 
Pros: performance is quite solid. I didn't experience any stutter on the first planet, and the framerate has been steady. Writing seems positive so far, but much of the side content is very lighthearted and jokey. Still too early to see where the main plot will go, but hopefully there's more serious payoff.
Cons: this is subjective, but pretty much everything else is less than satisfactory for me. Everything about this sequel is too safe. The combat and loot system is the same as the first game. Enemies are still bullet sponges. The art design is too 60s 70s scifi inspired for my liking. The music is catchy, but still the same 60s Fallout esque music. There's no real space scifi features like piloting ships or driving vehicles. You are expected to sprint across some large and empty landscapes. Leveling up consists of investing skill point and perks, but they give you so little points. I ended up modding to give myself more skill points. NPCs are forgettable. Companions equally so. Depending on your build, options like stealth, diplomacy, hacking, lock,picking are basically locked away from you. A Bethesda, KCD, or BRP leveling system would solve all of this, but that's apparently beyond their scope. No respec and no transmog. Crafting and modding don't offer much benefits. 
All of this might sound negative, but I still think the game isn't bad. It's just DATED. And because of that assessment, I think anyone interested should only consider buying this on a sale, maybe even a deep discount. The same can be said of OW1 and Avowed. The last Obsidian game that really intrigued me was Pentinent, so if you want a recent title to buy, I can recommend that one.

“Order is peace. Obedience is comfort.”
From the seat of the power consoles inside the vaults of the Protectorate you awaken, blinking the stasis out of your eyes—and immediately this game declares: yes, we will make you feel the weight of consequence, and we’ll do it with a grin. The Outer Worlds 2 leaps from its predecessor like a skip‑drive gone hyper‑boosted: new colony, deeper systems, sharper satire. The guns feel heavier and snappier, the movement lives and breathes: you climb, you scramble, you slide into combat as though the very physics of Arcadia want to show off. 
And the Protectorate voice doesn’t hide its satisfaction: “Your mission: bring order. Your reward: clarity.” The world bends to your decisions. Faction radio broadcasts morph, propaganda posters flip, a town you saved cheers your name, then whispers it in fear when you stray. 
The writing? Razor‑sharp. The tone? Fully committed. The immersive scope? Vast. If you love the idea of being the enforcer of order in a universe teetering on collapse, this game delivers.
Score: 9/10

“Freedom. Fulfillment. A future where your only real choice is which Auntie’s product you’ll need next.”
Then you swivel the camera and you’re suddenly in the neon‑washed boardrooms of Auntie’s Choice—a ruthless mega corporation with a smile and a fist. The game lets you embrace that consumer‑frenzy identity: you’re slick, you’re ambitious, you’re selling futures. The hooks are witty, the marketing drones are actual weapons, and you’ll hear catchy jingles about “Crabwich™” between gunfights and literal skip‑rifts. 
Here, the satire sparkles: you’re not just fighting; you’re brand‑managing the war. A mission might have you infiltrate a rival faction’s facility and pitch their tech to your board. The world responds to your profits and your collateral. The humor is sharp, the execution flawless.
Score: 10/10 — if you crave high‑octane capitalism with a side of existential crisis, this is your cheat code.

“We worship patterns. Solve the Universal Equation. The future is math.”
And finally, you slip the lab coat on for the Order of the Ascendant. In this mode the game reveals its cerebral spine: quests full of ritual, revelations about skip‑rifts as equations, and the hush of the laboratory corridors. The mechanics echo: your skills evolve based on how you behave, traits drip from your choices, and each companion’s ideology matters. 
The ambient tone is cooler, the stakes deeper—this isn’t just gun‑and‑glory, it’s calculation and consequence. If you enjoy games where your brain works as hard as your trigger finger, this segment rewards richly.
Score: 9/10

Final Verdict:
The Outer Worlds 2 does what so many sequels promise but rarely deliver: it expands the universe and deepens the mechanics, it keeps the heart of the satire and injects new weight. Whether you pump rounds in the Protectorate’s war zone, broker deals for Auntie’s Choice in its marketing carnivals, or crunch algorithms for the Order of the Ascendant in midnight labs—you’ll be fully immersed.
From the dynamic faction war that reshapes towns and radio waves around you , to the upgraded combat and movement that make every encounter satisfying , this game stands as a triumph.
It is, in short: exemplary.
Overall Score: 9.5/10 — Go play it. Choose your badge. Decide what you believe the future should be. And remember: they’re all watching.

Pay no mind to the negative reviews, New Vegas was an amazing game, but we need to stop comparing to it. I am a huge Bethesda game enjoyer myself who loved Fallout 3, New Vegas, Fallout 4, Fallout 76, Starfield, Oblivion Remastered and Skyrim. Though this is just a Bethsda-like series (made with the help of some Obsidan Devs who have worked on Fallouts) I wanted to give it another chance after never finishing Outer Worlds 1 due to a mission bug. 

I will start by simply saying, this game instantly gave me nostalgia to simpler gaming times. I don't know why, but my first 10 hours have felt strangely reminiscent to my very first Fallout 4 playthrough (the game that first got me into Bethesda/Bethesda-like games). The deep dialogue, pressing conflicts, moral dilemmas, interesting sidequests, quirky characters, skill checks, and old timey radio roped me in for the long haul. The gunplay is smooth with brain itching sounds, the gadgets and companions have some really cool abilities, the flaw system is one of the coolest systems I've ever experienced and the map is packed full of activities. I am thoroughly enjoying my time in Arcadia on Paradise Island (first planet) so far as has a very similar vibe to Planet Akila/Akila City in Starfield. 

These games are not meant to be played by speed running through main quests and power leveling, it is a journey that is meant to be fully explored at a slower pace. Enjoy your time, look around, collect Pitchball Cards, solve puzzles, hunt achievements, experiment with builds, and IMMERSE yourself. Too many people today attempt to play rpgs like this as a run and gun loot goblin. Stop and actually ask yourself, would this decision fit MY characters morals? Play into your story whether it be as a level headed hero, blood thirsty corporate ladder psycho or something beautifully in between. 

Most importantly, don't forget to try Purpleberry Crunch (now with 10% more flavor.. or crunch?)

The new and shiny nature of the game has worn off, now to give myself a moment to rant about this game's positives and abysmal negatives.

I'm gonna keep it short and sweet.

Positives: space western aesthetics, armors and outfits are full of style and have all the bells and whistles of looking good. So far the in-game atmosphere is pretty much Outer Worlds style hyper corporations run amok with no governmental oversight on how they're boot-heeling everyone's neck. Going full Silverhand never has been more justified.

Negatives: After reaching the Vox-station, which is a pivotal point for the first big chapter of the game, I feel consistently underpowered. Dead in 2-3 shots despite wearing any combination of armor and mods to try and beef myself. Literally having to cheese-fight by abusing the AI with cover tactics or just getting lucky with enemies. Enemies at this point are EXTREMELY bullet-sponge absorbing so many shots that comparing myself to them is like being the NPC while fighting an endless barrage of player-characters.

More negatives: if you don't do a meta-build, don't play on anything higher than Normal. You get no rewards for higher difficulty, enemies just deal double damage and get crazy more health on top of being all the more spongey to soak up those bullets.

Even more negatives: Companions are there to be conversation pieces, they're like the mall-ninja katana you bought when you were a teenager and felt so cool and edgy. Then you actually swing the katana at something stiffer than a 2-liter water bottle and the blade breaks into pieces. Companions are exactly that, pretty cool to look at and horrible in a fight.

Get used to back-pedaling a lot and discovering new levels of frustration as every lock, skill check or dialogue option is shuttered behind having just one more level in a skill. Don't take flaws without deeply consulting at least three different guides on flaws and/or communing with the Oracle of Delphi. Some flaws seem like direct boons or will make the game interesting, only to severely kneecap you to the point of making the game unbearable.

tl;dr -- wait for a sale, the game is all flash and style with little substance to make me feel heroic. 5/10

wake the ♥♥♥♥ up, samurai, this isn't the company that made New Vegas, it's a corporate skin-walker using the hard work of developers who were fired or retired to pretend at being amazing. I regret paying full price for the game.

                                                         ---Edit Below---

Okay! So quick addition as I have indeed played this game again, and again, and some more! Would I recommend this game at full price? No. Get it on sale. Is it a good game? Kinda? You'll wanna Silverhand run this game most likely, eventually, as I did. There's some neat stuff about your companions, the writing for them isn't terrible and even kinda made  me feel great about getting to know the NPCs in my party.

As for the gameplay, the game begins to shine after the first chapter, guns start having impact and fights after you pump 10 points into Guns and 10 points into Speech start slapping enemies pretty well. My build was 10 Guns, 10 Speech, 10 Medicine for a while, then I realized you will only get 60 skill points throughout the entire game - that is 2 points per level. Do NOT fall into the trap of taking the Jack of All Trades flaw as it'll mean you literally cannot get any skill above 7 or 8 throughout your playthrough and there are some meaningful conversations where you need 17+ Speech, Leadership, or a 7+ in two different skills, or a combination of the Brilliant trait while having 7+ Science to talk someone down.

The game really does kinda hit you hard if you don't hard-lock focus into two or three skills for an entire playthrough, and while that could be awesome for someone who really, REALLY wants to run through this game multiple times, I just don't see the replay value here. A lotta decisions come to the same conclusion, there's at best maybe six or seven world-affection decisions.

If this game had New Game+ and lemme replay it with my gear and levels, but allowed me to totally re-build at the first mission, it'd be golden and would have that replay factor to just blast through and see what choices actually matter when trying vastly different builds.

In the end, this game is NOT made by the same staff that made New Vegas, it's the Ship of Theseus, it's a skinwalker company that wants to pretend it's the same squad that made the epic games it once did and dare to claim that through advertising. It's like saying that CD Projekt Red that made "The Witcher 1, 2 and 3" is the EXACT SAME as the ones who made Cyberpunk 2077. Any bit of knowing is that "crunch" in the gaming industry burned out a lot of great talent and they shift out of companies that try to squeeze their talents too hard and too often.

That said, this game would be fine for 40 or 50 with the Deluxe Edition being a 70 USD bolt-on. Instead they went for "pay us more as we wear the dead skin of former, better games". I'll be doing a Silverhand playthrough of this game and will edit this review after such. And by Silverhand I mean "total corpo death". It'll be on-sight for anyone working for or being a complete soulless shill.

Until next time. Still rating this game as a "No". I'm petty for my buyer's remorse.

I had a head-cold when I bought on release at full price. Since that's only reason I can think of for buying [i]this[/i] game at [i]this[/i] price.

Since I have played Outer Worlds 1, the two unexpected flaws in Outer Worlds 2 are: 1) It played as a shooter, not a RPG; and 2) Side quests did not adequately contribute to the world building.

The crystalisation of both of these unexpected flaws for me was the Act I boss - about 15 hours in if you've done all side content - which clearly wasn't designed as a survivable encounter if you had sunk your skill tree in other non-Shooter traits for broader game enjoyment. The devs even decided you can't revive companions (to role-play winnable boss-battles that way), since revive cancels on chip damage and the boss has a constant AoE weapon. Once I realised I'd have to re-play the last ~15 hours to re-spec as a suitable beefcake, I then realised I couldn't [i]even remember[/i] any rewarding side quest events (maybe the rush to save the rebel captives?) in Act I.

So no. The problems for me weren't the usual launch-day blues - but structural game design flaws that'll either require re-playing from scratch or using WeMod; but even then, memory modding can't fix unmemorable or badly paced narrative content.

The best Obsidian RPG since the original Pillars of Eternity. A lot of lessons learned and applied from Avowed and Outer Worlds 1. Writing is good, companions are compelling, and with the focus moved away from just greedy corporations to a more diverse set of factions the world feels more alive, yet still vibrant with its original retro-future satire.

Mechanically shooting feels good, so does melee and stealth in general. Between the traits, skills, perks, and flaws the game really does feel like a proper RPG. The only minor thing I can say is that flaws being a hidden mechanic you have to discover will make you unable to figure out "builds" until second or third playthrough where you know what's available to you. That said even with a mostly linear story there is enough choices to be made that a second or third playthrough is still rewarding.

OW2 is an improvement on its prequel, OW1.  It improves in most aspects of the first game such as; expanding on each of the open world zones by making them bigger, a wider selection of guns along with unique weapons with unique designs, better weapon modification system, also a wider array of outfits to wear.

The story is decent, a lot better than Avowed, that's for sure.  There are some things in the story I would personally change, like having one of the very important characters you meet in the first half of the game become a companion, but here is to hoping they will make them be an actual companion or ally in the DLC should the player choose to let them go.  Other than that, story is enjoyable enough.  Graphically, OW2 is pretty similar to OW1.

Lastly, I do hope Obsidian will give modding support to OW2 (mod support means giving official modding tools to help create mods, like how Bethesda has the Creation Kit for Skyrim and Fallout 4 or GECK for Fallout 3 and New Vegas.) and if they don't, I do hope they will if they ever make OW3.

My only other minor complaint is the lack of respawning enemies in the open world like in the previous game.  After awhile the bigger and improved open world maps in OW2 feels very barren and empty, so with regards to the open world being bigger but having no respawning enemies, is kind of like one step forward, two steps back.

Other than that OW2 is a great game if you enjoyed OW1.  If you didn't like OW1 then maybe give this one a skip.

After roughly 20 hours, I feel confident I can give a decent review.


In short, 
The gunplay is massively improved over the first game, at 20 hours in I'm still excited to get new uniques, because they actually *feel* unique. Stacking elemental damage, and explosives feels rewarding.

That being said.

The combat is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ attrocious, enemies will shoot you without animation, bullets feel like they appear out of thin air.  I played on Very Hard, so I'm fine with the accuracy, what I can't stomach is the animation cancelling, I can't tell who the threat is, shotgun shells will go off without a sound, and sometimes enemies will still fire their guns while staggered.

But my biggest complaint, and from a Obsidian game, this is a damning one, is that there is no ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ agency on the players part. 

Not a single time did I feel like I was allowed to play a unique character, either i blindly agree to what a character asks of me, snarkily agree, or leave the conversation with the expectation I'll come back to agree. Oh and sometimes for no reason I'll have a dialogue option to let them know im about to attack them. For some reason. 


The companions suck. Like I had blinders on, so I didnt see how much the commmunity hated them, and came to hate them all on my own. I never felt like anyone was actually *there* with me. Super intense story-heavy events would occur, and my companions would just flouride-stare at me, with no implication that they understood the weight of what was occuring. they suck in combat too, they do ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ nothing and spend the majority of the fight downed and screaming at me to revive them. which I would, if they were at all useful. they do very little damage, i cant shange their armor or weapons, instead their progress is locked behind the 'upgrades' panel, 8 different upgrades, thats all you get. 

The tooltip says 'significantly increased damage' V.A.L. went from 9 damage to 11.

We're fighting enemies with 2k health.

Rant over, I just wanted more from an Obsidian title.

I....  just don't see why people think this is "better" than the first one, which is all I'm going to compare it to.   The first game had polish and love put into it. OW2...   it's a copium trip to keep you going and "give it a chance" in the beginning.  But really, it disappoints right off the bat with dullness.   Can't customize your companions, like changing their armor or weapons, and man do they suck at fighting even if you're using them right.   But forget all that, the biggest drag is the story which started with intrigue but continues into nonsensical arguments between extremes of laissez-faire capitalism, authoritarian socialism, and theocracy based on science..  yeah try and swallow that one.   I mean did the writers fail history of political philosophy and go on a bender??    Anyway, I put 24+ hours in, and had some ups and downs, but after you defeat the first antagonist you get introduced to other places you can go  and they're not open worlds, just boxed in clutter with tasks to numb your mind.   It's work, and I hate when I feel like I'm doing work when I play video games.    Outer Worlds 1 was flawed but had love and charm that made you adore it for what it was.   This game is a bloody slog.   Just being honest.

I have very mixed feelings about this game. On the pro side, there is a lot of fun to be had. The game has a great sense of humor, the settings and level designs are very well constructed and fun to roam around in. The quests are often (not always) fun to carry out. Thus, this negative review is not exactly meant to say you shouldn't play it, but if you are like me, you will find it frequently intensely frustrating. 

The problem is the skill system. Many important options and opportunities both in sandbox exploration and in quest completion rely on what skills you chose and your levels in those skills. And your ability to level those skills is very limited. The game pressures you to focus on a small set of skills because you simply won't have enough skill points to choose more widely. So far so good, except that the game constantly points out to you what you CANNOT do because a certain skill is not high enough. For example, in crucial dialogues with NPC, dialogues that determine important outcomes in the game, you are shown at the top of the dialogue what you CAN do, and you are shown at the bottom of the dialogue what you would have been able to do if you had chosen your skills differently. I find this intensely frustrating. This also happens just in roaming around, where (for example) you encounter a machine that you would be able to fix if only you had raised your engineering skill. But, again, the limited skill points available means that if you raise your chosen skills enough to make them effective, you will constantly be failing skill checks in those areas that you did not raise. After a bit, it feels like the game is mocking me. 

Many aspects of this game remind me of Fallout 3 and 4. In those games you also encountered frequent skill checks. But in those games, if a skill was too low there was often a work-around, usually in the form of a drug or aid that would temporarily raise a skill. No such luck in this game. You will never get an extra skill point and never be able to alter or respec those skills. This wouldn't be so bad if the game didn't constantly inform you of what you were missing and could do absolutely nothing about having missed it. I suspect that the devs are trying to give us incentive for multiple play-throughs. This just doesn't work for me. Especially when the game assigns moral and ethical outcomes to your choices. It frustrates me no end that I cannot bring about the moral or ethical outcome I would choose because, for example, my hacking skill is too low. But the reason my hacking skill is too low is because, if I raise it, then some other skill will be to low.
consumerism consumerism consumerism

The writers went out of their way to either invent stupid words for no reason, or to use the most ridiculous versions of words no one's ever heard of, all to make UNINTELLIGENT npcs sound "smart". There's no reason for this, either in game out out of game. It's needless and juvenile.

The game hard-locks you behind skills to such an extent that if you don't *happen* to have the exact right skills, your main story is forcibly altered. Want to save the people you've aligned with? Better have that one skill high enough to matter. Oh, and you don't have the option of grinding out levels after you learn how much of that skill you need. 

Companion AI is the most atrocious garbage I've ever seen. The game would LITERALLY be easier to simply never, ever have them with you. They spend half of their time running face first into enemies and dying instantly, and the other half is spent ping-pong-ing themselves back and forth in front of you while you're firing so you can't see what you're even shooting at. Unlike the first game, you have absolutely no control over their combat tactics, range, anything at all. You cannot dictate anything they do in any way.

Speaking of companions, they're yes-men. Every one of them. They have the emotional and intellectual depth of a can of tuna. You can never change their minds about anything, they will always preach about their faction, they will never use common sense in any conversation, and speaking to them is an absolute waste of time. Developers these days have completely lost sight of how to write compelling character stories, so they default to this narrow-minded, self-indulgent dribble that no one in their right mind would give a damn about. "Oh, they threw me aside like trash and left me for dead while refusing to allow me to save my own life! But, don't you dare say anything bad about them! I love them!" Pathetic.

And finally, your own dialogue options are a joke most of the time. Your skill checked words are stupid half-jokes that aren't even half funny, have no bearing on anything in any way, and god forbid you do something because you literally have NO choice but to do it! Your companions think it's your fault, and due to your pre-scripted responses, SO DO YOU. Killed a town because you lacked the skills necessary to save them from death caused by NPC badguy #32? Tough! Your fault, and your responses suggest that your own character is taking the heat. No means of defending yourself, no means of explaining; you just take it on the chin like a little %&#%&. 

If this is what passes for a 70 dollar game, the RPG genre is dead. ME Andromeda was bad enough. DA Veilguard was profoundly worse. And now this? No thank you. I'll play the game on mute and just kill every NPC I see, because that provides more entertainment than their so-called "story".

The Good:

This is the game Starfield wishes it was. Absolutely stunning graphics, incredibly well done game play, lore, gun play, amount of unique guns and animations. Exploration is just "chef's kiss". I always find myself wanting to push further, to look just around the corner and to never let a nook be un-searched. 

The Okay:
* Role play while good does seem a bit shallow and more of just a skill check that locks you out of stuff in an effort to get you to play again.

The bad: 
* If I have to tolerate a loading screen even on top tier hardware I shouldn't have to also watch my scene be built in front of me in the first couple of seconds after the loading screen. 
* Performance is pretty good for a UE5 title but of course could be better.
* Hardware ray tracing is broken somehow. I'm not sure how but every time I turn it on I can't utilize 100% of my GPU even using a 9800X3D or a RTX5080. Hopefully they fix this.
* Software ray tracing also leaves strange, noisy areas in the shadows from time to time. Worse with hardware ray tracing.

Summary
If you liked the first one you will love this one. Overall I feel it is a great game that stands out in a year of "meh" RPGs. UE5 strikes again (a little bit) but thankfully they did spend a considerable amount of time optimizing and it shows. Hopefully they get the hardware ray tracing figured out sometime soon. Definitely gets a "buy at full price" from me.

P.S. That full price is the current MSRP of $69.. $79 on the other hand get's a "HELL NO" for me for any game and I sure as heck wouldn't encouraging the behavior of buying early access.

gameplay spoilers 
[spoiler] 
Gunplay: Id say alot more refined, weapons although sometimes cartoonish, have good sounds, crisp enough reloads, and with the array of gadgets and perks you can really become unstopable depending on your build, like the greater variation of calibers, and that the wonder weapons sometimes had their own fuel source, my only real gripe is I feel they had so many different options for weapon mods, but made like 2-3 for specific weapons, I wish there would be some more customization with them, but I understand they choose to have more models and types of weapons, lots of animation 
Consumables: honestly this is my only major issue with the game, consumables are simplfied to two types fractional health restoration (out of combat) and combat health stims that function as fractions of uses, this is the only major downgrade from the original game, Personally I would have enjoyed consumables: being a little more dynamic but its mostly alright, minor nitpick but i also dislike that spectrum vodka is only one colour now, wheres my spectrum!
RPG Aspects They kinda dumbed down some mechanics, instead of the Six attribute stats theyres special tags, that act i suppose as strengths, they do sometimes come up both in the gameplay and Dialogue options, with the option of taking a deficit tag, for an addional positive trait, backgrounds were great, Many options for who you end up selecting and can show up in mission Dialogues, so pretty good, Your choices do matter enough for me to feel like I exist in Arcadia, they changed skill points, but not really, its still technically the same as the outer worlds one, as the max is 20, and you get about 2 each level, its just done differently, kinda like going up from multiples of 5, regardless, its the only change that felt, needless? it really doesnt make much a difference, what is different, is there is less skills, never a fan of that, but I understand, Perks got an overhaul and largely where improved in my opinion, Im not a fan of how the game balances damage, based on skill and perks, while some perks that give sneak attack bonuses are cool, the guns skill literally scales damage, I didnt realize how easy the games combat gets if you rush guns. still perks where cool, and some provided addional dialogue options.
companions: Honestly, They did a good job with pretty much all of them, I enjoyed doing they're quests which had multiple missions and plotpoints, doing them helps later with the main story, Tristan, is quite possibly my favourite outer world campanion, All the VA's nailed it, they're abilities are better than in the first game, especially with how they rebalanced health. and other mechanics 
Story Length is significantly improved, id say the base game is like twice as long as the original, Im very happy as that was my main concern from this game before release. 
MUSIC: they made their own corpo music thats just the best thing, I really appricate the small things devs to make the world feel real, like the 8 different vodka consumables of TOW1
