[b] [/b]
[list]
	[*]Starts off as an average game but still manages to gas itself out by the final act. 
	[*]It's better than the first game in every way but still remains consistently AVERAGE much like the first. 
	[*]Gunplay is way more satisfying and the effects look great but there are hardly any enemies to shoot! and they don't respawn!
	[*]Don't expect a sci-fi sandbox RPG. The Outer Worlds 2 is a semi-open world action adventure visual novel with familiar RPG mechanics sprinkled in. 
	[*]Ironically, the most important decisions you will make in the entirely of your 50 hour playthrough is in character creation. There is NO RESPEC. Compared to other modern RPGs that allow you to tweak your character as you play. What that means is the developers want you to ,[i] god forbid,[/i] actually [b]ROLEPLAY[/b] as your character. 
	[*]The flaws system and my previous bulletpoint is where you will find the bulk of your fun. Flaws are funny and dynamic, truly unique mechanic that will add good fun chaos to your game. 
	[*]This game is not bug-free. I have had 2-3 quests break on me. Incorrect quest markers and dialogue bugging out until I restart the game etc. 
	[*]Final act really starts to gas out. The game will have you teleporting from planet to planet, loading screen to loading screen, in order to talk to a few people or shoot a couple of soldiers and get a small pat on the back and do it all over again. True corporate experience. 
[/list]


Reason why Outer Worlds 2 feels like a flaccid experience is because it's highs are not high enough. There is a looting game, but no loot is gonna change the way you look at the game. There is a shooting game, but there is not much shooting to be had. Then there is the roleplaying game, but the only story stat that really matters is Speech. 

The Outer Worlds 2 takes tried and true RPG mechanics and presents them competently. That's it, no more no less. To the games credit, Obsidian wants you to play by their strict ruleset and the game is better for it. Forcing players to accept their decisions and weaknesses is essential to the game's framework and encourages thinking outside the box.

It feels like Obsidian has learned it's lesson from Outer Worlds 1, this game is a great FPS RPG that feels like it has choices that matter.

Companions are interesting and their questlines remind me of NV. Locations are more interesting than the first game, and many objectives can be accomplished by utilizing tons of different skills. In fact, many parts of quests were blocked off for my character simply because I did not have the necessary skills to pass certain checks. 

Factions feel important, your choices dictate whether or not the main story will follow the fate of the universe you want. For example, I chose Auntie's Choice and by the ending slides I was left wondering if I had made the right choice.

Seriously, do yourself a favor and play this one. It's good. REALLY good.

Let me preface this by saying that I'd give the game 3/5 stars if there was a star-based system on Steam.
I paid for the early access version (AKA Premium Edition), and was mostly... underwhelmed. The gear is only good for the first few levels when you get it (after the intro/tutorial).
I left it as vanilla until I was about 85% of the way through, and even then I tried to keep it as close to vanilla as I could. I just grabbed a few things to help with stuttering.
I accidentally maxed all my skills at one point, and found out which perk-point adding mod works best for my playstyle.

The good:
-The story is interesting... Provided you make it past the first planet AFTER the tutorial/intro (same as the first game, the first planet is a dud). The themes feel much darker than the first game, which I like a lot. 

-The companions can be enjoyable at times and it's interesting to see them interact with NPCs.

-SHIP PETS!!! More than just a canid that came with a DLC, you can have ship pets. However, you can only have one free at a time. I wish it was 2-3.

-Some of the weapons are fun to use... I found one and kept it for almost the entire playthrough.


On the fence (stuff I kinda like but kinda don't):
-No weight limit. You've become Link, carrying a giant metal mace in your tights' pocket. While the weight limit drove me mad at times in the first game, it was a TINY amount of realism in a not-realistic game. As long as I could fast travel while overloaded with stuff, I was fine.

-Platforming. Yep, you read that right. There's platforming in this game. It killed me more times than anything else did. See lethal fall distance below.


The Bad:
-The stuttering... Good Law, the stuttering... You can tell, they left the Engine defaults as they were.

-Generating shaders. Every. Single. STARTUP. There's a cache for a reason, use it. I'm not changing my computer's graphics card every night, ffs.

-Not enough skill points. Yes, I accidentally maxed all my skills, but before then it was IMPOSSIBLE to do anything unless you choose 3 skills to focus on. It's ridiculous. Now that I know the max, 10 points per level is too much, but why not 5 per level? Here's the current breakdown if anyone wants to know: [spoiler]12 skills total. 2 skill points per level. Max skill level 20. Max character level 30. To max all skills would be 240 points (excluding 'specialties', which would drop the total points needed to 236 or 234), but with 2 points per level and a character max of 30, you only get 60 points to do anything with. If they went with 5 points per level, it'd break down to: 5 x 30 = 150 points, which I find MUCH more reasonable.[/spoiler]
I was more frustrated than anything just trying to play my normal style. I like to be good at lockpicking, hacking, science, medicine, and sneaking... Come to find out the sneaking skill doesn't work right. Hostiles noticed my presence right after I killed one or two people, nothing like the OHKO of nearly an entire group in the first game. Even after the accidental maxing of my skills, the enemy was alerted to my crouched presence after one or two kills. This is with the added bonuses of [spoiler]camouflaging after a kill.[/spoiler]

-Ridiculous skill checks. In the same vein as the above, it feels like early on you get skill checks for over 10 in one skill... 
 
-Flaws don't give you perk points anymore.

-Hardly any companion interactions that I saw. I was 60 hours in before I saw the first on-ship companion conversation, and they didn't talk much when exploring either.

-No ill effects from ANY pharmeceuticals, and that includes BOOZE. HOW?! Is your character a robot? Watch the next DLC be a FNAF crossover...

-Too many hostile creatures. I won't go into specifics to avoid spoiling it, but there's a certain creature I feel shouldn't be as hostile as it is. They should adjust some of them to have a more cautious approach, maybe like "I'm warning you, but not gonna attack you just cause you're in my space."

-The lethal fall distance is INCREDIBLY small. Rather than just tanking your health a lot, you get an insta-death. At first, I thought I could come back with enough trauma kits, but nope... I had like 13 on me by end game and I was still perma-killed from a 'lethal' fall of about 15 feet.

I have enjoyed every moment of playing this game. It has all the silliness of the first game while being a bit more serious at the same time. I completed my first playthrough on very hard, which at the beginning was extremely difficult considering my skill/flaw choices were not great. The gameplay has been upgraded significantly from the first game. The story is pretty interesting but unfortunately i dislike that one too many things are left up in the air, unless they're hidden in one the many codex entries. While the worlds themselves are quite pretty to look at, they are not swarming with enemies and feel a tad bit empty at times. You would think an active warzone would have at least one situation where you accidentally wander into an active battlefield that lets you assist your faction of choosing but maybe that was too complex to develop, I wouldn't know. You are however "rewarded" in a sense by trying to explore every nook and cranny, there will be the obvious loot but sometimes information about events in the world that you can then use as a talking point at crucial moments, don't forget to look vertically, might find hidden passages above or important notes under furniture. The companions are not necessarily useless, but they do almost require you to do their personal quest to unlock gear upgrades. Otherwise they kind just get in the way of your shots, but once you get their first of two upgrades its a pretty big difference. First upgrade always ups their gear and lets you choose and switch between one of two elemental attack/effect and a choice of element resistance, while the second upgrade adds more of a unique ability/skill upgrade. Their personal quests are usually more dialog than gameplay but are very well written and engaging .The UI is simplified and not overly cluttered and gives you all the information needed, although enemy FOV cones on the map are only unlocked via a skill and there seems to be a bug when you or your own FOV cone wont appear properly on your map. The radio was a nice addition and great to listen to, those damn jingles are in my head as im trying to fall asleep. I haven't had a game that had me thinking of it randomly in my day to the extent like this since cyberpunk/helldivers. After completing my first run I was only disappointed in my own choices as stated before, but I was quite happy to give it another go with better choices. My only real advice i can give if you wanna play this game is, IGNORE THE "EASILY DISTRACTED" FLAW ON FIRST PLAYTHROUGH and maxed out speech is needed for the "good" ending .

I wanted to love this game so much, considering I loved everything about the first one - enough to buy it and fully play through it in multiple platforms. The problem is, this did the sequel thing where rather than being like the first game, they decided to overhaul EVERY. SINGLE. THING.

Not improvements, just full blown overhaul. Every mechanic from the first game works differently in this one, and some have been more drastically altered than others. A few to the detriment of the experience for players who actually want to be able to explore their options during a playthrough.

I figured I might be in for a rocky game when I got completely locked out of even seeing what the quest option was from one character because I'd already spoken to a different one they didn't like. It felt very Edgewater Power Regulator at first, until I realized the second character was going to completely block me off from even having a dialog tree with them because I'd already said "eh, yeah, that doesn't sound bad" to the first one. But sure. Fine. We want choices to matter. Then I hit the quest step where I needed to get a specific person to go somewhere - oh... I didn't invest in speech. Sucks to be me I guess? Let me try the other peaceful way into this location - ... So I have the quest item for this and they STILL won't let me in... coool....

Then. I get slammed with a trolly problem I had no hand in putting together, and since I apparently didn't make the right choice during character creation or choose to put my extremely limited skill points in one specific thing, I don't get the "everyone is alive and happy" options. I either have to leave it alone and let people die, or actively be the one to choose to kill different people. And lose a companion I just spent an hour or two running the quest for, after she yells at me for decisions I literally had no choice about making.

I like the concept of "oh, your decisions have consequences", but at some point you're punishing me for how you wrote your own story. And this game feels more and more like I'm being punished for not playing it "correctly" at every turn. I genuinely don't even know if I want to proceed now that I've cleared the starting zone, because this isn't fun. I expected a game like what the first one was, and instead I keep getting kicked in the face and told I'm doing it wrong for trying to actually explore and do things.

I haven't been this let down by a game in a long time.

I’ve got just over six hours into The Outer Worlds 2, and overall, I’ve enjoyed the story, combat, and atmosphere so far. It feels like a worthy follow-up in tone and style. Sure, there are a few bugs here and there, but that’s pretty common with new releases, so I didn’t mind at first.

What made me give it a thumbs down is the save system. I put in hours leveling up my character, exploring, and completing missions, only to come back and find my latest save showing me as level 1. I always manually save before quitting and double-check that I’m signed in. I use multiple systems (desktop, laptop, and Steam Deck), so I thought maybe it was a sync issue between Microsoft and Steam. After checking all three, they had synced to the lowest version of my save file, wiping out all my progress—no autosaves, no recent manual saves, nothing.

It happened once before and I brushed it off as my mistake, but now it’s clear the issue runs deeper. Losing that much progress makes it hard to want to start again. Right now, this is a “play it in one sitting or lose it” kind of game, which is not how a major release should function.

Fun game, but until the save and sync system gets fixed, I can’t recommend it.

The storyline is a snoozefest. I couldn't care less about the 'factions' and their stupid stupidness. 

The combat is ok, mostly, except you never get any really cool weapons. There's loot everywhere, but it's all the same stuff. I never got a single weapon that made me happy or excited, even off the "boss" battles. 

The "puzzles" aren't really puzzles, they are just "search until you find the keycard or other thing you need." 

You get blocked constantly because you don't have a particular stat high enough. 

The whole thing is just infuriating.

Strangely, despite a billion gajillion dollars from Microsoft, this doesn't really solve the core problems with TOW1. The writing is pretty good, the art direction is great, and the ideas with some of the RPG mechanics are fine. 

But this is just a shockingly shallow AAA RPG for 2025. The shooting feels like an ARPG from 2015 (i.e., it feels like Fallout 4). There's not much to do besides sprint from place to place, talk to an NPC, shoot some enemies, then sprint somewhere else. None of the systems have that much depth to them, besides flaws. Crafting is really lame and weak, there's no personalization on your base, it's hard to feel like you're playing a defined character with relationships in the world vs. a generic PC. Et cetera. 

The choice and consequence is really good. The writing is quite a bit better and more nuanced than the first game. But it's just not a very fun game. It's just really surprising how they made a game which feels like a bad Fallout 4 clone with this much hype and funding.

[h1] The Outer Worlds 2 is a very "safe" sequal [/h1]

Was waiting for this game since the anouncement trailer and had release date marked on my calendar. Liked first game for it world, characters and its humor. Sure, it had it's flawes, but it was good for a AA game. Some parts of me were hoping, that with Microsoft's help + more budget and time, they would make sequal much bigger and better then predecessor. 
And then some info started to slip, that Obsidian were releasing two game in 2025 and some of my hopes got humbled, especially after reviews for Avowed dropped. Don't get me wrong : this game is much-much better then Avowed.

[b]Game[/b] got bigger, numbers won't lie : I cleared first one (without DLC + most of side content) in like 23 hours. This one took me ~56, it's like double the time.
[b]Shooting[/b] got better, in terms of "feels", animations and weapon variaty. 
Feels like more [b]agency[/b] for player, more places for stat checks.

Now for parts, that I wish could be improved based on experience from first game:
- [b]companions[/b], are, again, weak and, I would dare to say, toothless. They do no damage, die from 1-2 hits, cooldowns are abysmal. Somehow, non of them truly stick out, as, for example, Parvati did in OW1. All of them feel "safe" and most of their stories feels rushed and very predictable.
Some "problems" with companions can be fixed with mods (which I honestly recommend), just to see alive when you fight and have some impact on battles. Maybe my problems can be solved with "leadership" build, but we have problem with that and that's my next point.
- [b]skill points[/b]. I didn't like new skill tree system at all. As for me, you don't get enought points. Yes, you can get flaw to gain +1 point every level, but it has "flaws". You start to feel a little powerful once you close to level cap (which is 30 without DLCs), but some bad choices in stats distribution could result in you NOT getting that powerspike you were hoping too. Aaaand you cannot respec them - understandable and not at the same time. Idk why there is no system in game, that could give you extra skill points for completing some quests or finding, for example, books that can give you +1 to some stats. You have collectible cards with minor boosts like +1 critical chance or +5 max HP, but why nothing to boost stats...
- [b]enemy HP scaling[/b] is not great - especially at Eden they feel too spongy, but that problem get's solved when you slowly level up and reach cap, get some weapons and have good "gun" build, but it could all be better at the beginng, if we had more points and it all comes back together to my previous point.
- [b]enemies don't respawn[/b] - maybe I get it, why it was made like that, but I still I feel conficted about it : you spend more development time to make "shooting" feel better and then there is not enought things to "shoot". When I was clearing first two main planets I didn't feel dreaded when walking same roads would of "bodies", because if was still than honeymoon period. But for last ones, man,I felt it really heavy, especially when walking roads and seeing same bodies of local fauna that you "hunted" like 5-6 hours ago. Some enemy partols would be a decent solution and we don't have even that.
-[b]performance[/b] is a tricky question : had stable 100-110+ FPS on 1440p and 7900xt (XeSS on UltraQuality Plus and High setting), exept for some dips to 60-70 FPS in specific 2-3 areas on Dorado and sometimes after fast travel. For my whole playthought I had 2-3 crashes : one was causes by outdated companion mod and two were accompanied by amd driver crash (game or drivers problem, idk, didn't bother to delve into that question). At the end of the day, it is a UE5, sadly. One part that stood out the most for me, is that horrible shadow shimmering - never so that one in other titles on UE5.

While playing The Outer Worlds 2, I always had thoughts about "what this game could be, if Obsidian got ALL its development time and resources on it, without need to share it for two games". At the end we have this "safe" sequel : with parts that worked and made first game good - just got better here; and small or non-existant innovation/work on poorly designed systems, that predecessor had.
Don't get me wrong, I had [b]good time[/b] in this game, even with that cons section being larger and more specific, then pros. After all that, I'am gonna wait for all DLCs to drop (and reviews for them) and if they are any good, then will do a new fresh playthought. Maybe they can fix some problems that me and other players have with this title, but my expectations will be mild.

I very badly want to rate this game highly, but I can't. Frequent fatal error on load bugs, broken quests, out of order dialogue triggers and non-functioning interactables. 

This game isn't done yet and it shows. I can say with confidence it will get better, all Obsidian games do, but undeniably frustrating to have paid full price.

This game isn't "bad" but it isn't good either.  Right now I'd give it a thumbs down because it isn't worth the retail.  When it drops down sub $30 it might be worth it for some people.

- Choices don't matter.  Don't get me wrong there are a lot of choices in this game and your skills appear in dialog options a lot, but most of the non-skill options seem like they were added just as filler and often the skill options don't actually do anything even with regard to the conversation you're currently in.   There is literally one important skill at the end of the game but I'm not going to make a SPEECH about it.

- It has a lot of Bioshock and Fallout dna in terms of the way things work.  Since I came from both of those games there was some enjoyment just playing so that's why I won't go as far as to say this is bad, but it's just a downgrade from those games.  

- There's so much generic crap in the game that might as well be called Generic Food, Generic Heal Charges, Generic Ammo

- The story wasn't engaging and the dialog was tedious and for the most part felt like filler.  I didn't get emotionally connected to anyone or any thing in this game.

- I don't really see a lot of replayability.  I thought about doing a second game to try to get the good ending which requires one particular maxxed skill.  Without that regardless of any choices you make you get the exact same ending (unless you could failing as another ending) with a little bit of 'what are this characters doing now' which does rely on your choices but really didn't affect the game at all.

Is the shooting better? sure. Are there a few RPG skill checks throughout? kinda. Do these skill checks matter? no, besides speech maybe. The story is disjointed and completely lack luster. Would refund if I could. Gunna go re-play New Vegas now...

Disclaimer:  I wrote this while I was drunk and feeling especially introspective

I can't really say the game is bad, just that I'm very disappointed in the direction Obsidian has been trending lately. I think that a lot of the more superficial elements of this game are better than the first, the presentation for example in nearly every case is better, whether it's the graphics, or the animations, or the models. Aside from this however, this game really doesn't tickle my brain in the way that the first one did. The plot for example, went from "oh no! rich elites are trying to sacrifice the lives of the lower class to avoid an economic/ecological disaster of their own making!" in the first game, to "oh no! a magical space anomaly is going to destroy the whole solar system, and ruin the entirety of our galaxy-wide civilization!" Practically every other plot element has been similarly watered down, in order to give us an experience I would think is much more comparable to Borderlands 2. A lot of the themes that were present in the first, that carry over to this one only feel like they exist within this game because they already exist within this setting, kind of similar to Fallout 4 in that way. I'm not calling this game stupid, because I think it still requires more thought than most mainstream games on the market today, but it still seems to be regressing in that regard. 

In the early to mid twentieth century, European, and North/South American artists explored the concepts of surrealist, dadaist, and impressionist art, but the dominant authoritarian regimes of the time scorned these styles, stating that art should be purely realistic, and depict men and women working for the benefit of their country. The Soviet Union subsidized the arts exclusively when artists depicted them in a positive light, and Hitler called these unique movements "degenerate art." So, what does this say about our modern culture, where games like Vampire the masquerade Bloodlines, Outer Worlds, Fallout, et cetera, are having the complex social issues explored within erased in later installments, and otherwise require no critical thinking skills? Since when did RPGs become "go here, and just shoot everybody, then pick up the item for a prize?" 

While I did like a lot of things about this game, and I think it is technically better than the first in many ways, I did not think it was nearly as interesting, and as such, I did not enjoy playing it. I think if you didn't spend 95% of the time shooting people over and over, I would have a very high opinion on this game. I actually got to a point in the story where these two characters are debating at what point, if ever, armed conflict is justified, and whether you can account for the consequences of inaction or appeasement, even if it is in favor of avoiding violence (Neville Camberlain comes to mind) so I do think that there are some pretty interesting topics explored from time to time. With this in mind, I still feel like this takes a backseat to the (honestly kinda mid) gameplay, as it still took me more than twelve hours of shooting random guys to get to this point, and the philosophical subjects are kind of just brought up out of the blue, rather than being woven into the story. I feel like my opinion of this game keeps flip-flopping, because the presentation is amazing, and vary rarely, you get some extremely interesting dialogue, but the vast majority of this game for me was spent on mind numbing combat and loot gobbling.

The last thing I'd like to say, is that if you enjoy this game, and if you think it's great, that's totally valid. I think there is a lot to like about this game, but based on the metrics I tend to use to critique games, I don't think very highly of OW2.

Obsidian's done it again! If vanilla ice cream could represent a video game, it is undoubtedly The Outer Worlds series. This is my vote for the most alright and the safest video game of the year. 

Settings: I really liked the corporate frontier vibes of the first game with everything branded in cursives like it's a bottle of horse spit from 1892, and it genuinely made picking up things fun. The inventory magic of the first game is now funneled down into a constant stream of unremarkable consumables that you hit one button to consume to recover HP, the rest you sell in a copy-pasted vending machine that sell the exact same thing as the one next to it, or you breakdown into raw components to make another 3000 bullets. You will constantly find these copy-pasted elements in the game, sprinkled in a few named placeholder NPCs that has exactly one dialogue of background. Placeholder, ... That might as well perfectly describe the general vibe of this game. You can feel that things are there just to check off a list and it kills immersion very quickly once you no longer cares about the factions, the characters or the story beats. That being said there is like one really cool map with a system of trenches but the illusion quickly collapses once you realize the pieces don't really fit the whole and all the NPCs and all the environmental storytellings are just part of a video game checklist of "List of Things that could be cool to put in a video game battle" (also dead bodies don't always make good environmental storytelling especially if that's all you're doing.)

Factions: Straight up pulled from ChatGPT on how to make the most one dimensional factions, each with their own cliche. I get that it's probably supposed to be satire but if you're going to use your genuinely interesting villains to try to carry your 1D factions, things will stop making sense halfway through the story. Still it has some strong initial world building but things get rushed toward mid game onward. 

Companions: I think the devs highlight that this is a strong point of the game but honestly I could shoot everyone of them in the head at the end of their arcs and nothing would matter. I think they tried to emulate Mass Effect but it fell flat toward the end. And companion quests literally takes you on a wild goose chases from one planet to another, occasionally right in the middle of the same quest, and quite frankly it's really annoying. And most of them are also useless in combat. Overall bland, unoriginal, sometimes annoying and completely forgettable companions.

Gameplay: You can make certain choices within the story and side quests based on your skills and perks, but these side quest choices hardly matter because they usually lead to the same outcome, offscreen consequences or NPCs that you will never hear from again. For big story beats you can count on one hand choices that do make a difference in your playthrough. 

Combat: This game suffered the same thing that the first game suffered, which is at one point the combat falls flat. At first I had fun exploring different weapons and were excited to find and use some unique weapons. But once you put points toward your chosen skills and perks, the game basically tells you "Just stick to these same weapons and steamroll everything" and that's exactly what happens. You can argue "Then just use the weak guns if it's unique and interesting" but then what's the point of the game being so stingy with skill and perk points if it didn't want people to experiment? And the enemy AIs (and the precious little variety of them) are dumb as brick and they exist only to stand still and be shot at. 

Music: I ADORE Auntie's Choice Radio. Some of the most corporate brainrot but ironically 🔥 🔥 jingles you ever hear. Literally this is the only 10/10 part about this game.

In conclusion: overall it's a decent one-weekend Xbox game, but there's no value beyond that unless you're gunning for achievements. It's a sterile, risk-free and checklisty experience that I already forgot about, which is a shame because the worldbuilding has potential, but I think like every game that Xbox puts its ♥♥♥♥♥♥ hands on, this is a dead end series.

I struggle, again and again, with The Outer Worlds 2. 

It's a game I dearly want to like, even love, and there are aspects of it I find worthy of praise. Momentary glimpses of good writing in twists and barbs that aren't immediately blunted by being overwrought. It is absolutely better than the first Outer Worlds, and yet, in the question of whether it's worth your time and dollar, I again find myself giving a rather pointed no.

The modern Obsidian is too obsessed with being funny. It's been felt in nearly all their games of recent years; sometimes it hits, but most of the time, it absolutely doesn't. The original Fallout games absolutely had black humor, as did New Vegas, but these were lightening touches on decidedly macabre worlds; the purpose of humor was to prevent you from taking everything so seriously *all* the time, not to rob the setting of gravitas, or its characters of the compliment of sentience. In The Outer Worlds 2, just like its predecessor, the only description I can muster for the setting and most of its cast is 'ridiculous.' There is no logical belief I can conjure for how these organizations work in the short term, let alone the long term.

It is popular, now more than ever, to critique capitalism and fascism, but Outer Worlds 2 welds its writing so deeply into satire that little point can be made aside from the obvious. Capitalists are obsessed with advancement. Fascists are deaf to logic and reason that goes against the state. Why? How do these systems interact? What appeal do they have that draws people in? What about them gives rise to conviction that they're the right way to live life?

People might think it strange that I'm beggaring sociopolitical discourse from a video game, but Obsidian has done it before, and better than pretty much anyone else, in New Vegas. People have argued for years about the pros and cons for each major faction in that game, because the setting grounds and establishes each overarching belief as reasonable. The Outer Worlds 2 would turn 99% of Caesar's Legion hostile, and make the NCR completely, irrationally irreverent to its citizenry, while Mr. House rambles in scientific sermons in a tower that's painted like the Sistine Chapel. 

Aside from the writing, the world wandering falls eerily, confusingly flat for me. Once more an aspect that was done so much better in New Vegas; there was next to nothing I stumbled on in my time with this game, I just wandered between block to block on the map. Enemies don't wander or interact with each other. They are preset in scripted encounters that only serve to make the world feel more artificial. This was the same design direction that guided Avowed, and it's one of the pieces that I feel most severely harms the moment to moment experience.

It's not my belief that The Outer Worlds 2 is awful, but in many of the things I want from a RPG, it comes out to below average. There are parts of roleplaying, and a fun enough shooting sandbox, but without writing that grips me, it all feels meaningless. I would rather go play a better RPG, or a more dedicated shooter. Not everyone will feel this way, and I respect that, but with the resources Obsidian has, it's a wonder to me that they've left all their ambition behind in the comfort of producing consistent mediocre content. It feels completely alien to the studio I once cherished, and it is with this release that I must at last acknowledge that the change seems irreversible. Worse, in my mind, they seem convinced that this direction is for the better.

Perhaps I'm simply too old.

Consequences and decisions matter, but the game is not an open world sandbox, it's a gated-hub system, which is less appealing to many RPG fans, but fine for the most part.

Worst thing about the game is that it is optimized for a 50-inch screen and a console controller and a user who expects to finish this on his gamepass before it expires.  So yeah, not gonna win any best-of-year awards (I hope).

it seems developers continue to remove more and more features from their games with the excuse of "Modern Limitations" as if somehow we are losing technology to time like some long lost civilization. For example the walk button, Fallout 3, Fallout New Vegas, the elder scrolls series. yet somehow that function no longer exists, and many, many others from gore, to body customization, hit stop when enemies block melee attacks, limbs and limb damage, drugs and drug addiction, faction joining, even status effects are gone. Why are we going backwards? This game feels like New Vegas in space if it had a PG-13 rating back in the 2010's. The writing is deadpool or borderlands 3+ style irony, cringe, and throwing in as many curse words to make it feel more "mature" honestly it feels like it was made by some brain rotted middle schoolers. Your character dialogue choices are probably the worst I've ever seen in a game ever. I think the best description of this games theme I heard was "How bad can we make this game look?" which honestly isn't really a great selling point. Your character will probably look stupid, say stupid things, and interact with stupid people who also say stupid things for no reason. The actual gameplay such as combat, or traversal is fine, melee still feels terrible compared to older games (such as new vegas) and I know we should stop comparing but that is an obsidian game and when you play outer worlds 2 it will play exactly like new vegas, from player movements, animations, even the way they hold the guns, but that's about it for comparisons. They seem to really be trying to ride their New Vegas mechanics after a lot of backlash to Avowed and Outer worlds 1's gameplay, but that goes against the theme of far future scifi, using ball and cap guns, old single action revolvers and repeaters just goes against it's own theme.

For $70? Brother you're better off getting a nexus premium subscription for like $7 bucks and downloading fat mod packs for their older games like new vegas, a game with infinitely more game mechanics, things to do, player agency and freedom. Lastly the devs have been quite rude to their own player base, and the gaming community as a whole. Mocking criticisms and people pointing out their game mechanic dementia it's shameful to see. 

TLDR: A good game for mature 15-18 year olds looking for something funny, without gore, drugs, or anything remotely edgy or controversial. A very safe game, but not a very good one. I cannot recommend this game, I don't think updates can fix it, but if you are truly interested or cursed with the obsidian stockholm syndrome like me, I'd wait until it's on sale, a fat sale cuz this game should NOT be anywhere near $70 US, probably a $40-$50 range would have been more appropriate and could've got them more sales.

Better than the first one but still a bit slapdash if I'm honest. Certain skills feel required if you want the full experience, while others feel like a joke. Certain character traits feel more impactful than others both in function and in dialogue. The writing is amusing and occasionally clever in a moment to moment sense, but the overall story lacks a strong theme which makes it hard to truly care about what's going on beyond the Saturday Morning Cartoon basics of defeat bad guys and be cool, which is a problem when the game wants you to spend a lot of time coming to understand and care about the various factions involved in the story, and the people that make them up.

I debated recommending this at all, and for a long time it was a definite no from me, but in the end having seen it all I can't say that. It's a decent game and it does do a lot of things right as far as exploration and discovery are concerned, I just wish that Obsidian would try to actually say something with their games. 

Also, play the game with Speech. Without it there were too many quests that felt railroaded to a specific conclusion, which was so bad it made me restart the game after forty hours. Optionally, hacking, too, though to a lesser extent. All skills are viable as far as the actual act of combat and exploration are concerned, but you do too much talking in this game for dialogue to feel empty without Speech. If I can still win a firefight without points in guns or explosives or whatever, I should still be able to convince people without speech. It should just be harder. 

I realize all of this sounds pretty negative for a positive review, but I still think it's worth playing, especially if you aren't jaded by years of playing this genre of game like me lol. If nothing else, Tristan is a great guy to have around. :)

Just wrapped it up. This is far and a way a better game than the first. In some ways it reminded me of octopath 2, in that, like octopath, the first game gameplay was fine but the characters were boring as well. Despite what some people are saying about the companions in this game, I didn't find them one note at all. They were very engaging, interesting characters. If interacted with and challenged in the right ways, they have very satisfying character arcs. They are far an away a highlight of the game to me, my only gripe being that they needed some tied skills so they should do things for me other than combat stuff. They show that a little via the field tinkering and Tristan's observations, but they needed more than that. That is, I think my only main complaint.

I am slightly annoyed by the limitations of the leveling and skill system, but i think that is largely an issue of insufficient tutorials of it, to indicate upfront what the skill max is. Had I know that it was 20 and not 10, I'd have speced a bit differently.

Everything else felt great. Combat felt very fun, lots of really cool unique item mechanics. Every companions had cool niches for combat so you could get interesting combinations of things. Dialogue was great, as was the responsiveness of quests and characters to my actions. There were a couple places I was not given a choice in things that I wish I had and felt I should, but those were few and far between. Most quests were enjoyable, though I skipped many collection related ones. my companions responding to things, and then the person or people I am talking to responding to them in kind was really well done.

I guess my only other issue is that the best looking armor is, of course, the worst at actual protection cause its all light armor. I think that's an issue of gameplay style, I am not good at rapid mobile shooting, so I had to do medium and heavy the whole game, which, while having cool looks, were not as cool as much of the light armor.

Not as much humour baked in as the original, but a good story with solid, enjoyable mechanics. Appreciate there being multiple paths to achieve your goals and perceptible consequences to your actions.

As of writing this I have made it to the second planet, and have played enough to gather four of the companions. I'm not sure I have any interest in continuing.

The writing is no better than the first. None of the NPCs that aren't directly related to the main plot (and a bunch that are) really have anything to say. So most of your dialog with them is the same forced jokes and quips as the first. The writing is so tongue-in-cheek that it's causing pressure wounds.

The combat is a little better than the first, but enemies are more bullet spongey. So the slightly better combat encounters always overstays its welcome.

Companions are made of wet tissue and do little to no damage when it comes to combat, and really only exist as totems with combat abilities. Combine that with the fact that they no longer provide skill boosts, companions feel utterly useless.

But probably the worst aspect of the game is how it feels like it puts more effort into telling the player what they CAN'T do, than what they can do. Every quest usually provides the player with one or two options to complete it that depend on which skills they are putting points into, but then also makes sure to show the five options that you are locked out of due to failed skill checks. I'm not saying the player should be allowed to do absolutely everything. Picking a role is kinda the point of an RPG, but the game shouldn't have to constantly remind the player of the roles they didn't pick. Especially if they're not going to include an option for players to respec their characters. It feels like Obsidian is afraid that players won't want to replay this game unless it is thrown in their face that they are missing out on different routes they could go with different builds. I'm already tired of the FOMO in my multiplayer games, I don't need it in my single player games too.

Both Outer Worlds 1 and 2 are competent RPGs, and even good ones in spots. They just aren't that great, and The Outer Worlds 2 is certainly not worth $70.

TL;DR: You know you're in a bad place when your story is making me compare it unfavourably to the latest Borderlands game.

Obsidan Entertainment have been dining out on the goodwill of having made Fallout New Vegas for 15 years now, and I was already beginning to question whether they still deserve it. This game made me wonder whether FNV was, in fact, a fluke. My opinion of the game is this: it would actually have been better if it were worse. This game is shockingly mid in just about all respects other than the visuals.

The Outer Worlds 2 does not solve any of the problems of the first game - cramped worldspaces, lacklustre characters, stiff gunplay - and adds a whole new and incredibly frustrating one, which is "Hey, Did You Know You Can't Do This?" You get two skill points per level. Just two. You start to encounter skill challenges very early on that require 4 to 8 points invested in a skill, with no warning that you'll need it that high until you see it. The game is really eager to tell you about things you can't do because you don't have enough points in a certain skill. This was fine in New Vegas, where it only occurred occasionally. Now, every single conversation seems to want to tell you "damn, if only you had 8 points in Medicine! Shucks."

One of these checks is required to avoid committing genocide at the end of the first planet.

The companions are similarly lacklustre, ranging from the Dude Who Bases His Entire Personality Off Of One Dialogue Choice, I Can't Believe It's Not Ashley Williams From Mass Effect, Obligatory "Funny" Robot, Borderlands Psycho But With Less Personality, The One I Haven't Met Yet, and a guy who I could only describe as "Wait, This Dude Is Just The Gestapo."

No, sorry, I have to elaborate on that one a little more: his recruitment quest is him rudely demanding you help him investigate a mass murder at a refugee camp, after which he determines that the problem wasn't that a bunch of innocent people were killed, it's that they weren't killed according to proper judicial process. This man then joins your party.

The story can't commit to the anticorporate, anticapitalist theme of the first game, which was by far its strongest feature. Like Disco Elysium's evergreen critique of centrists, it's trying desperately to dilute its own message, to escape the responsibiliity of having to stand for something. This game stands for nothing, believes in nothing, but most unforgivably, it isn't even bad in entertaining ways. It's the very definition of "mid." I'm going to finish it, just to see where its story thinks it's going, and then I imagine I will uninstall it and rarely think about it again.
The combat is fun and varied, and the dialogue tree is great. There is a real sense that you influence the world around you.

This game is very underrated. Fantastic RPG with an interesting story. 

It is not really an open world game, nor will you be able to maximize all your skills a-la Skyrim each playthrough. However, limiting player to just a few skills creates a solid role-playing base, on which the flavour dialogue builds throughout the entire duration of game's story.

Cool weapons, interesting mechanics and unique dialogue options kept this game interesting for me for multiple playthroughs.

As someone who enjoyed the first outer worlds but never felt completely engrossed, the outer worlds 2 has gotten the job done and completely sucked me in. I love the characters, the worlds, the skill/perk/flaw system, etc. the gameplay is smooth, the quests are engaging, the consequences feel real. 10/10, feels like new vegas in space
Fun continuation of the first game
100% fun experience.
I love Obsidian RPGs. the cheesiness, tongue in cheek references to consumerisms, and the story telling are all top notch.
Everything good about the first one feels fine-tuned, and everything that needed some work got it.  Loving this one, just like I loved the first one.

Alright, so disclaimer, I'm a decent way into the game, haven't beaten it or anything.

But man, if i had to describe this game in one word, its just, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ ENJOYABLE. It's so much fun. The first one was great, and this one just straight up improves in every single direction. There's so many skill checks and dialogue checks that make my choices feel super relevant, ALL of the characters ive met have been a hoot to jaw with, its traded a bit of the overt goofyness from the first (which wasnt a bad thing!) for more black humor, which works really well with the more, you know, system swallowing threat.

There's so many little animations for every single thing and action as well, which helps with the immersion a lot. The movement is also crisp as hell, and the addition of third person as an option is neato. Movement gets even better with the [spoiler] double jump. [/spoiler] 

By and far the best part (so far) is the combat/gunplay. My biggest gripe is basically that i want MORE enemies in each combat, which should tell you all you need to know. Gun variety is better, each gun feels good to shoot, hit effects and damage are satisfying, the sounds are great.

If you like action packed shooter RPGs (and DEFINITELY the RPG part), i wholeheartedly recommend this game. I think the only people i wouldnt recommend this to are if you dont like a game to have a lot of jokes, cuz this game is pretty humor filled.
Good game
Took the first game and improved every aspect. If you enjoyed the first, you'll love the second.

Premium price for indie quality.

- Hardware shadows are broken (not denoised), which prevents using hardware ray-tracing as a whole.
- HDR causes awful artifacts when using DLSS frame generation, you have to choose between HDR and FG, they don't play well together.
- Game saves only when it's in a good mood. Save / autosave works, then it stops suddenly in the middle of your session and you have to restart the game after losing some progress.
- There are some HUD-related bugs and bad design decisions.
- There are perks-related bugs.
- Your companions are cringefest.
- Protagonist animations in 3rd person look from 2010.
- Your footsteps are noisier than your guns.
- And I can continue but this should be enough to get the idea ...

Not everything is bad of course, I still have some fun with the game but it absolutely doesn't deserve the asking price.  Buy only on sale, at least 3-4 months from now when they fix some of the bugs.

Still yet to finish the game, but have had a lot of fun so figure I can recommend it.

I enjoyed the first game but it lacked depth, and many of the story tangents I'd love to learn more about left much to be desired.

Outer Worlds 2, at least to the 50% mark has improved on all aspects of this. But, if you want new vegas, this ain't it. There is a certain vibe/feeling that the creation engine provides bc of how you interact, and I don't think that can be easily replicated outside of it.

That being said if you more so want just better writing, interesting factions - then this will provide that.

Honestly I think Obsidian should have aimed for a more immersive sim style gameplay loop - there is so much in the game that I think could be improved to allow immi sim gameplay, and I feel it'd flesh well with the story and gameplay that already exists.

Last note. You cannot max all skills. You cannot be perfect. I tried multiple times not realising this harder limit of skills etc, and it has caused multiple restarts - if I didn't enjoy the game enough this would have been very annoying (tho this is more user error)

Game turned into... Borderlands? The same rocky biomas, sparse buildings in a map/planet. It's been a weird experience so far. 
Companion lore doesn't quite fit in the idea of the game lore. E.g.: you can enter a Protectorate building and shoot all soldiers with Tristan in the party. Huh...
Plus the bizarre work with the scopes. It is way better to use weapons without attaching any scope, otherwise you won't have a clue of where you're shooting at.
Good point: it's well optimized. I'm running it beautifully on an i7 11th gen RTX 3060 laptop.
Worth it on a 60% sale, unfortunately.

The story is not really interesting. The companions are kinda of lame and bland. The 1st Outer Worlds left you wondering what happen to Earth, that is not answered, this just has a whole different story, not going to say what it is. There are numerous bugs in different quests that makes you load from an earlier point to get the quest to work or not work either way to point you just skip the side quest. The graphics are on the same level as the 1st game, and the price is kinda of high; I did about 80% of the before I got to the point of just wanting to finish, so if I did everything it might have taken me 40 hrs total, so game is over priced. I'd either play on gamepass or wait till it goes on sale.

I thoroughly enjoyed this game. The RPG angle really worked well, as well as the important dialogue choices.  Could've used a few more boss fights. It ran beautifully on my rig, especially after the quick patches/updates that Obsidian released. I never played the first game and wasn't sure what to expect, so was pleasantly surprised. Wish they would've developed companion relationships a bit more than they did. Good weapon selection.
Excellent game. It improves on much from the first one. The writing, game play, and combat are much more enjoyable.

A mixed bag but an overall positive experience especially if you can get it on deep sale. This game was developed at the same time as avowed and wow is it ever obvious. From the writing quality, world design, visuals, systems, ect, it all feels very familiar and that is not always a good thing.

The writing is what I’ve come to expect from modern Obsidian games outside of pillars and a project like pentiment, it tends to be fairly simple, a bit bland, nice branching, but ultimately leading to very similar outcomes. It’s not the most offensive thing I’ve ever heard/read but is pretty forgettable and milquetoast. I don’t really knock the game for the plot because an original plot is damn near impossible but the way they tell the stories is underwhelming. Factions and characters feel very surface level with their existence still feeling like a single issue billboard instead of something you want to delve deeper into. Companions are just not interesting, from visual design to personal quests it’s all just painfully OK. Games don’t always have to be some work of literary brilliance but they should make you feel the desire to learn more about the world/characters and here (and avowed somewhat) that is never the case. Avowed for me was bumped up because it is in the Pillars universe, which I love, but here you only have OW1 (that had many of the same issues).

Combat is fairly fluid and feels nice to shoot most weapons. Enemies are still dumb as rocks and lack variety. I played on the hardest difficulty and enemies still mindlessly sat in the open most of the time with the real difficulty being in their insane DMG and aimbot. It never got tedious because you are pretty OP by the end of the game oneshotting most enemies. There is a wide range of weapons but you probably won’t bother using most of them because they overlap quite a bit with very few ammo types.

Exploration feels rewarding but by the end of the game you have so much extra crap you stop bothering being thorough. Aside from crafting ammo sometimes with the occasional mod, I never felt like most of the crafting materials were useful. Again, this was on the hardest difficulty. There are obscured locations, hidden encounters, and hard to see items scattered about but start to become less interesting as the game goes on. The tossball cards were easily the hardest to spot despite being the most numerous of the collectables. Each has the same 4 or so ways to enter/move around them but all lead to the same areas and outcomes. It’s obviously designed to allow whatever build the player has to easily find their way through encounters but ends up making your specialization less rewarding. Avowed was designed the same way with more hidden paths but less “gadgets”.

Performance was weird. It doesn’t run as well as you think it should for how it looks and the static nature of the world. It is heavily CPU limited at times even with a 7800x3d and 5080 at 4k. FPS on maxed settings was really bad (barely above 60) but you can get huge gains for virtually no visual loss by dropping shadows and GI to high. The game looks decent but should definitely be running better than this. Also I would recommend getting the console enabler mod to remove lens flare because it is blindingly obnoxious. Did have a few quest breaking bugs depending on the choices I made and some bugged achievements so be aware of that.

Overall it’s a decent, ok, alright, whatever word you want to use game. Again, similar to avowed I’d say its around a 7-7.5/10 but nothing I gotta go tell someone about. I think OW1 came at a better time when people were frustrated with other similar games but the landscape has changed a lot since then and this second entry doesn’t do enough to bring the franchise forward. Hopefully, like the 1st game, the DLCs are a further step up.
As someone who enjoyed the first game, this one just took the good and made it a much better experience.

A 7/10 is very fair rating for this game. While it was enjoyable, it has some noticeable issues that drag it down. If you've played an "Obsidian RPG" before and liked it, you will probably like this, as I feel like that is exactly what this ended up being, for better and worse. 

Good: 

- Story much improved from the first game, better paced and more present throughout the main quest
- The overbearing satire and humor is toned down a bit . It is still very much there, but it feels more natural and fits into the story better. 
- Every skill, trait, and background feels useful here and it felt like there was a point where everything had a moment to shine. Your background actually comes up outside of the tutorial and your traits matter. 
- Weapon variety and combat options felt varied. Having the right build obviously helps, but various ranged, melee, and stealth options all felt viable. 
- The game does not overstay its welcome, and doesn't pad out the story for the sake of run time. 

Bad:

- This game does not run well. It seems "fine" once everything is loaded in, but I still encountered crashes, bugs, and other technical issues ( the most common offender being a noticeable stutter or lag when coming out of load screens, slightly annoying when running around, very annoying when it truncates the beginning of cut scenes or dialog). 

- Character development is extremely restrictive. You get a VERY limited amount of skill points to invest and ONE very early opportunity to respec. You will max 1, maybe 2, skills if you focus on them only, spread your points out and you will not meet checks late game, there are several instances with checks requiring max (20) in a skill.  Companions cannot help with skills checks, and there are no temporary boosts. Some will say this is good, its "an RPG making decision matter". I counter that most rpgs in the last few years have figured out how to avoid this issue,, and still have "choices that matter". Even other Obsidian games have not had this issue so its weird to see it here. 

- The factions feel ultimately pointless. Only 2 get real development. A third may as well not exist. The last feels like a wasted opportunity, you barely interact with them in a meaningful way, or with anyone of importance, every plot development seems to happen off-screen to be told or read about later, and the main use seems to only be "over-the-top evil space bad guys in armor" that are between you and the objective. From a story perspective, its hard to care about what happens to people living in/under a faction that barely exists outside of faceless enemies and one-off nobodies. 

- Its hard to describe this one, but a lot of this game feel very...dated. In a sense of, I've seen this before. There's been lots of rpgs recently that have reinvigorated the genre and done cool things with old systems, this just feels like, the same old thing Obsidian has made before, That isn't bad in and of itself, but I really would have liked to see this game take more risks and try new things, rather than play it safe. 

Overall, if you like Obsidian rpgs and don't mind some technical weirdness, go ahead and try it. If you like rpgs but are a bit unsure or bounced off other Obsidian titles, maybe wait until its cheaper. If you already don't like the genre, there probably isn't anything here that will change your mind.

I'm a huge Obsidian fan and completed Outerworlds 1. 

While I recommend this game, I also feel a bit personally torn on the overall experience. 
It's a really good game, but something isn't quite "hooking me" in like the first one did. Albeit i'd be finished the first one twice over in the amount of time i've put into this one. So that may seem rather contradictory. 
But this one is setup to be a much longer experience. 

I think the way skills are setup it causes a lot of Fomo and choice regret. So i ended up restarting 3 times before I settled on my final build. But now reaching the final planets the game is kinda wearing on me rather than calling me back for more. The first 2 planets have a great game play loop and scratch the dopamine rush of leveling and making that perfect build. 

But by the final planets you sorta just feel like you're going through the motions without much to strive for other than the credits screen. I feel if there was a higher level cap and larger final planets, the game would be near perfect. Hopefully the future DLC resolves this feeling. 

Overall it's really fun though!

It does seem like an ok (but nothing more) sequel with my limited time of playing it. However i very much regret buying it at the asking price of $110 AUD. Should have waited until it becomes more reasonable. Until this changes I can’t recommend it.
A more expanded version of the first game, would reccomend getting on sale, 70 seems like too much for this.

Darn save bug was a bummer, but loved the storylines/questing, good ole OT combat and social system. It was an interesting end, but well inside the OT brand, hehe. Great game, was waiting TOO long for it to come out!
w game, but maybe not a $70 game
fun guns better than first game

I thoroughly enjoyed the first one. Anyone who compares these games to Fallout New Vegas compares solely on gameplay feel. Same old slide around gun in your screen shoot em up with a go here go there and loot. Animations are early 2000s and have not been improved beyond a fancy blur when the gun moves fast (woah cool, flashy). The dialogue seems like some over the top soap opera written by someone who's never talked to real people before.

All that aside, I really wanted to like this game, but I've played only a little bit, and can't find myself strapping in for the long one. Definitely not worth the price at all whatsoever and if anyone buys this game full priced, I'll just say, you probably have better things to spend it on.

Note: I did get this game as a gift from my best friend that I've known since I met his brother at the bus stop in 5th grade because of Pokemon cards LOL 
In fact! He even bought my steam copy of TOW1, even though I had already played it on GamePass back when it first came out. That's a game I'd recommend as well. Has it's flaws, but it's a really fun game to play through (I've played that game through no less than 10 times).

Ok, on to the review. I personally love the setting and dystopian sci-fi extremes with the political and social ideologies in this franchise. While some find this aspect lacking, I personally love the picture that is painted through the conversations, terminals, notepads, and audio-logs. Even the settings you find yourself in tells a story of the struggles that are occurring both in the plot and the Arcadian Colony. While the Halcyon Colony was great in TOW1, I did enjoy the Arcadian Colony more. The various Hubs all having their own distinct biome was refreshing. On top of that, there was more to do and explore.

I highly recommend treating each area like you would a questing zone in an MMO or something. Just blasting through the plot can leave you wanting. While I don't hate the plot, it is arguably weaker than the side stories, quests, and gear/loot that you get off the beaten path. The only reason it lacks compared to the side quests, is that the side quests are REALLY GOOD!  

I'm on my second play-through doing a completely different build. First character was a Professor who was Brilliant and Innovative but Sickly (you can either have 1 positive trait, or two positive traits. Though, if you pick 2 positive traits you HAVE to pick a negative trait as well to balance it out) Science, Hacking, and Speech were his focused skills (lots of options to choose from: backgrounds, traits, and skills that can also be used in the environment, and as dialogue options). Right now I'm playing as an Ex-Convict who is Nimble and Lucky, but Dumb. His focused skills are Sneak and Lock-picking. 

The levels/zones/buildings even cater to having side entrances, and crawl spaces if you want to play stealthy, and not just charge into fights. Stealth combat and the AI's reaction to stealth in TOW1 wasn't very good, this game greatly improved on it. 

If all of these features sound interesting to you, then I highly recommend checking it out. I'm not gonna say its worth $70 since I got it given to me for free LOL but If it's $40 or less, and all of the above sounds dope for you, BUY IT!!! 

If you don't prefer games that focus on dialogue, reading, variety in choices in dialogue, role-playing as a character, then the Outer Worlds may not be for you. The combat is fun, but it is less Borderlands, and more FPS RPG in space. 

Also, IMO, The Outer Worlds Games are not "Boo, Capitalism Bad!" it's more "Corporatism can be awful when its unregulated by any over-seeing body of government, dictatorships can seem peaceful until you see how restricting having all of your freedoms taken from you can be, and math is cool but please govern your people and stop spending all day doing math (yes, that last one is actually a freaking faction LOL The Order's funny.).

Anyways, play it, don't play IDC.

I'm just glad I did.

art style and graphics are really good but basically everything else sucks

boring party members. Only got the first 3 but miss after miss after miss. Kind of tired of obsidian's obsession with factions and faction based party members. I know they say they dont like including romance but tbh it'd help bring life to these boring people. This is coming from a pillars fan.

This game gets a recommend from me but only just. I really enjoyed the first and I quite enjoyed this one too. This is the first game but more of the same so if you liked it then you are on the right track.

Obsidian continues to produce great dialogue and an interesting world to explore dense with things to find and people to interact with. They like to explore extreme ideas with the factions and it's often an interesting framework to explore ideals. The story mission zones are designed well and were great fun.

What I will say is this game goes on too long for me. I'm a lawnmower type player so too many big zones and I burn out which this game has more of compared to the first. Similarly, without spoilers, a huge plot point and villain is solved about 2/3 of the way through with no real human drive from that point which removes a lot of desire to push on and drive to the story. Most quests stop feeling like the impact as well due to the size of the game because you realise it can't have an impact (though some small decisions have a very disproportionate impact on your ending credits info dump).

The companions are fine. There are some I enjoyed getting to know and others which were there. Obviously everyone has their own flavour of companion but I think Obsidian's lack of desire to right romance plots is causing some harm. Romance is often a real skip in the barrier to personal feelings. People when they start to want to pursue something romantically have to let down barriers and let people see more of them. This can be achieve in the friend quests and I'd say 2 of them achieve this more (depending on how you handle them) but most don't and overall it's harder to achieve in the friend space compared to the romantic space. So overall the companions were there rather than an addition to me. Also another thing, there was no real narrative justification of why most of your team just sits on your ship. I know we don't expect everyone to come with us but COME ON! It's a narrative cop out for us to just know we can only take 2 others for mechanical reasons, you have to tell us why they can't be actively helping us save the world rather than sitting making dinner on the ship.

Combat is fun but ruined by the shield gadget you get early on as you functionally become invincible with it and enough energy. I took guns as a skill and needless to say I became a functional one person army. Some skills are more useful than others and I'd have liked to be able to fill the gaps on some skills with companions. For example, Science isn't that useful overall while Hacking, Medicine, and Lockpicking are. Occasionally a companion might help with a certain skill check but it's rare. I'd love it if we could use half of their expected skill, eg if under the hood Inez had a 10 for a Medical check then we can pass a 5 check with her. It doesn't take away from that the hardest skill checks need to be done by the player but does mean that a number of quests / outcomes you can't solve for unless you have that skill become a lot more doable.

TLDR; I think this is a flawed bit of fun. Don't expect to finish it as I think you could get bored before the end but I do recommend it as a good bit of fun.

People comparing it to new vegas are crazy. Its a good game, but if I could id refund it and buy it for $40 max.

The second game I paid full price for in 2025, Silksong being the other.

Fantastic game, I hope it does well financially because this is the closest we've gotten to New Vegas 2 and it is a lot of fun.
If you liked the first game, you will love this one.  Game play and flow is much better than the first one.  Really enjoying it. I hope there is DLC in the works for this one like the first one had.

What I liked:
- The music was good, not great, just good. 
- The guns are amazing, way better than OW1 and much more variety. 
- Companions are significantly better and have much more depth compared to OW1 companions. 
- Good length (even though it said I played 62 hours, I had to restart because I did not enjoy my initial build. Campaign is really like 50 hours._ 
- The tone of the story and game in general. I really appreciate lowering the comedy and taking the story a bit more seriously.

What I dislike:
- The missions that requires going between planets. DO LONG DISTANCE CALLS NOT EXIST?
- Not being able to Fast Travel from within my ship when I land on a planet. Just let me teleport from my ship, since I already explored most of the map. 
- Avowed (Obsidian's previous game) had transmog features, why not here? Some of the armor was a bit excessive for me, and I wanted something a bit more mellow (Protectorate sniper armor, looks awesome). 
- No respec option. Make it an option in the settings.
- Spoilers [spoiler] The final boss, the Consul, did not have a presence at all. I was not interested in them at all. The game made no real effort from the beginning to care about them. They should have a bigger presence in ACT 1 and 2. It felt like his faction should have been a part of the game. [/spoiler]

What could be improved:
- The radio could have been better. Why is it in the journal tab? I wish the radio stuff have their own "tab," or something. 
- The major npcs. They weren't bad. I just don't care about them. The only interesting NPCs, to me, were Bishop Ruth and de Vries. Everyone else were very forgettable. 
- They should have added the Protectorate faction as a faction we can interact with. There are a lot Protectorate NPCs that felt like they should have a bigger role. 
- Give us more Skill points, like 3 or 4 per level. 

Overall Thoughts
It's a good game, not a great one. I will probably replay the Outer Worlds again just to try style. I have a feeling after the 2nd play, I will not return to the Outer Worlds 2 again unless there's DLC. The story feels lacking. There's a lack of conviction on the player character. They keep trying to both sides things which frustrates me when it is obvious that the corporate faction is just going to squeeze everything they can out of the Arcadia System. I got nothing else to say, buy it if it interests you. Buy it on sale if you are unsure.

I absolutely loved The Outer Worlds 2. From the moment the opening narration kicked in, I knew I was in for something special again, and it delivered on every front. The writing is sharp, self-aware, and consistently funny without ever losing the emotional core of the story. The characters feel genuinely memorable, with companions that are not just sidekicks but full personalities with their own beliefs, quirks, and arcs.

The role-playing options are fantastic. I felt like I could shape my version of the protagonist in a meaningful way, not just in dialogue choices but in how quests unfolded and how factions reacted. The game encourages creative playstyles, whether you want to talk your way out of everything, hack every system in sight, or just bash problems over the head with something large and questionably legal.

Exploration is also a huge step up. The worlds feel bigger and more alive, with side quests that actually matter and feel handcrafted, not just filler. Performance and visuals were smooth in my experience, and the art direction nails that retro-corporate sci-fi vibe that made the first game so distinct.

If you enjoyed the original, this is absolutely worth your time. And if you never played the first one? This is still one of the most charming, clever, and genuinely enjoyable RPGs available today.

10/10—Would tell a megacorporation to shove it again.
the story is nice but the gameplay is such a chore

I barely managed to finish this game — it’s actually worse than OW1. The RPG systems are downgraded, the loot is downgraded, and the story is just as boring as in OW1.

It’s fun for the first 5–6 hours, but then it becomes a slog — uninteresting characters, tons of unnecessary dialogue that adds nothing.

There are some QoL improvements copied from Avowed, which is nice, but there isn’t much else good to say about this game.

There are tons of choices and plenty of skills to pick from, but none of them really matter. Most “choices” just come down to whether you go through a door or a vent depending on a skill check. And when there are choices with real consequences (like someone dying), you don’t care, because most of the characters are so forgettable that it’s impossible to feel invested.

Man, first Avowed, and now this — Obsidian has really hit rock bottom.

While I did enjoy the game for the most part it has some very serious flaws. 
First, there are some game breaking bugs both visually and story related that will lock you out of progressing or even corrupting your save files. For a finished game it feels more like an Early Access product especially with how some of the terrain is just cookie cutter with floating rocks and trees. Secondly, the actual content of what you can enjoy playing is fairly short and feels lackluster after a point. Third, some of the game mechanics, perks, and skills feel almost irrelevant. There were so far a few between moments to use some of them it just never made sense to ever spec into. Finally you do get to a point where you can finish leveling up with about half of the game left, leaving a feeling of frustration of what points or perks you could have used that xp for. I truly regret paying 70$ for this game and wish I had just waited till a sale for at least half off.

 Now for what I did enjoy. 
The companions are a definite upgrade especially with the voice lines, companion quests short as they are, and the lack of a forced view of a companion ability made there presence more enjoyable than the first game. I'll keep it short and sweet, this is a 8.5/10 game. It scratches the New Vegas itch and blows Starfield out of this universe (pun intended). This game has lots of build diversity, and a ton of entertaining quests. If you were looking for fallout in space this worth picking up. 

I do have a couple complaints which are as follows.

Companions - They will grow on you a bit if you do their questlines but upon first meeting them I was only interested in digging into one of their backgrounds, the rest fell into the "meh" category.

Level Cap - I think the level cap should be 35 and not 30. Since there is no respec (which I don't mind) I think the player should have some "throw away" levels to get a grip on the character they want to play as outside the prologue. The first area has a ton of quests with very low skill checks and you can fall into a trap spreading your points out too much too early (well I did anyway).

Prologue - The prologue does it's job and teaches you the basics but it didn't lock me into the game in the sense of "I can't wait to play more", but luckily the game grew on me quickly in the first area.

It's a shame Xbox/Microsoft ruined a lot of the hype for this game with the price increase (then decrease) and poor pre-launch marketing. If it got the same pre-launch backing as Starfield I think this game would be selling like hot cakes. No it is not perfect but it checks enough boxes that leaves me wanting more. I hope more people will try this game because I think it is being slept on by most.

If you are still iffy about picking this up then wait for a sale and grab it, I think you will be surprised how enjoyable this game is. Also for reference I think Starfield is a 6/10 game.

TLDR: might be a better purchase at a discount. 

I like the new guns and all the flavorful animations that come with them. Except melee weapons, because I feel like not a lot effort was given to them. 

The world space is larger and thorough exploration rewards you, which I like. 

The companions are kinda meh. Perhaps they're a notch more memorable than the original, but none them really impressed me. 

The main story itself was fine, but one thing I was mixed on was the dialogue. The dialogue is basically 75% fluff and 25% content. Not to mention that there are also terminal entries that also feel like they just too long. I've basically resorted to just opening a message and closing it immediately, hoping that it would come up at a later time. All of it just became one big slog later on. 

I think what I hated the most about the game was its over indulgence of gatekeeping content through skills. If you're the type to spec into a combat heavy character, be prepared to miss 35% of content through dialogue and locked doors. I think all types of playthroughs should be able to experience all content provided. If they have a high skill, then it should be easier for them to get it. If you don't well you have to jump through a few more hoops to get it.  

It really soured my experience when the game dangles a satisfying outcome at me only to be met by a skill requirement too far away from my own playstyle. Which is a strange choice since the game LETS you achieve these types of outcomes without a skill check so long as you were thorough in your exploration. It's inconsistency, for me, is the game's worst quality. 

There are also some minor bugs. No game breaking ones thankfully; with the worst glitch I encountered forcing me to restart the game. I encountered multiple bugs involving audio.

Overall, I did enjoy my time with the game, mostly. I think the original is better by a margin.
