[b]TL;DR:[/b] If you're looking for something like The Outer Worlds 1 or a Fallout-style RPG, this isn't it. The game feels more like a light action shooter with empty environments and a story that never truly involves the player. Most of the time, you feel like a bystander.

[b]Writing: 3/10[/b]  
Characters feel flat and lifeless. The game gives the illusion of choice, but your decisions rarely matter. The narrative feels like disconnected mini-scenarios glued together without clear logic. The lore team clearly had ideas, but the execution is weak.

[b]Gameplay: 4/10[/b]  
Mostly basic fetch quests with objective markers guiding you step-by-step. Very little player agency or discovery.

[b]Graphics: 5/10[/b]  
The environments feel dated and empty. This would have looked good about ten years ago. However, character animations are well done.

[b]Combat: 1/10[/b]  
The real-time gunplay tries to feel like an action game, but there's no tactical pause, no weight to the weapons, and the balance is all over the place. Some enemies die in one shot, others randomly turn into bullet sponges. Sometimes enemies can nearly one-shot you for no clear reason while others nearby barely scratch you. The gunplay lacks impact and feedback. In the end, it's both a weak RPG shooter and a weak action shooter.

Honestly, I was expecting way more, but, sadly, Obsidian these days just keeps disappointing.
The first game already had some core problems that made it dull as hell: bland world, forgettable NPCs, weak dialogue, and mediocre gameplay.
And guess what - all that crap’s still here. They only fixed a few minor things, but the real issues? Still untouched.
A massive upgrade. A richer world, fun shooting, a deep perk and flaw system, and far more interesting characters. I really recommend this, even if you didn't quite get on with the first game.

The Outer Worlds 1 was a no less than perfect game for my tastes from the style, to the story telling, and detail put into every little creep and crevis.

The Outer Worlds 2 is no different, each NPC has such a unique story and personality its hard not to love each of them, especially the Companions with their amazing quests that build their character incredibly, with the added addition of your dialouge choices being able to shape their beliefs and personality far greater than what was possible in the first game.

The simplification of the Skills system being balanced out by the massively increased arsenal of weapons to use, such as the Big Bang and The Oracle, and the greatly expanded perk system with over 90 perks to choose from manages to allow you to play exactly how you want to play. Be it a stealthy rouge focused on lockpick, hack, and sneak skills for maximum sneak attack damage; or a raging Psychopath who rips the hearts out of their enemies to increase maximum health; there truly is an infinite amount of possible builds you can run, and each perk can also unlock unique dialoge options too, and not to mention the huge increase of flaws that grant you boosts and debuffs depending on how you play, aswell as even Backgrounds and Traits, theres just so much to build your character with its hard to remember even.

The 60s Sci-fi Aesthetic is also still present in this game, if not made even better than it was in the first game with the new weapons and such, and the landscapes of each planet never fail to dissapoint, even on the lowest settings where they look absolutely amazing.

But I might've lied, there is several problems with the game that can slightly tamper with your experience. Because the game runs on Unreal Engine 5, it has bad optimisation for older hardware, although this is no fault to Obsidian, and besides its not like "Anything less than a 3070 will cause this game to not run" bad, I ran the game on a 2060 Super Mobile and got a stable 25-30 FPS, which felt quite alright all things considered. The game also has minor bugs here and there, visual or otherwise, aswell as some major, such as the saving glitch that causes you to be locked out of saving your game by any means, including autosave. However the game is new, so these will likely be fully patched given enough time.

Overall, amazing game, good job Obsidian you done it again and I cannot wait for the Outer Worlds 3!

While I can understand some of the complaints folks have towards this game, many of the negative reviews are exaggerating. This game is an overall improvement on the previous Outer Worlds game; it's obvious that the developers listened to the complaints folks had about the previous game and improved upon them.

Here are the things I love about this game and see as an objective improvement over the last previous: 

• No more attributes; characters focus on skills and perks. The way the game has shifted from focusing on attributes to skills and perks is amazing; I no longer feel like I have to min/max attributes in order to have a competent build. As a bonus, there are a lot more perks than the last game.

• Utility characters are more viable. There are perks and mechanics that allow you to be functional in combat without having to focus on a combat skill. Yes, combat skills are more effective, but they're no longer absolutely necessary.

• Mobility feels fluid. Movement and scaling in the last game were clunky and frankly frustrating at times. Now with the parkour mechanics, sliding, climbing, double jumping, leaping, etc. It just feels better in every way. 

• Third-person view. I don't really need to go that much into this; it's really as it states. There's a third-person view, and it works well; it doesn't feel like it was just crammed in.

• Gunplay feels better. This ties in with the mobility praise, but the fact we can leap and slide into cover and scale onto higher ground makes combat feel more 3-dimensional and less restrictive overall.

• You can now automatically consume enough food from your inventory to heal you fully out of combat. I didn't even bother with food in the last game because it was just so tedious and felt ineffective.

• It actually feels like an RPG. I left the best for last. Skill points are not handed out like candy, so you have to pick and choose wisely. You won't be able to explore every avenue and style in a single playthrough; you just can't. You're going to miss stuff and miss choice options; there are certain paths you won't be able to take, and that's okay! You roleplay a character, pick your skills, pick your path, and then do something different for the next playthrough.

There's more that I love about this game, but I don't want to spend too much time typing. I would highly recommend this game to anyone.

Blimey, The Outer Worlds is an absolute visual treat from start to finish. The art style is a wonderfully quirky blend of vibrant colours and a sort of retro-futuristic charm that I simply adore. The level design is equally impressive, creating these intricate, hand-crafted locations that are a joy to simply wander through. And can we talk about the radio stations? I've had the three stations on a near-constant loop, as they provide a perfectly curated soundtrack for my planetary misadventures. Admittedly, the gameplay does feel rather familiar if you've spent time with New Vegas. It's hardly revolutionary, but it's executed with such polish that it remains thoroughly enjoyable. The combat is pleasantly predictable, and the systems are straightforward to grasp. In many ways, its comforting familiarity is its greatest strength, allowing you to simply soak in that magnificent world. All in all, it’s a cracking good romp through a beautifully bizarre universe.
Also, me, the achiever, unlocked several of the rarer achievements simply by using a bit of common sense. So, if you find yourself with a less-than-ideal ending, you've no one to blame but yourself. The game truly rewards you for paying attention to the dialogue and acting appropriately within each situation. Go, buy it, I know it is rather expensive. But, if you don't — no AAA games for you in 2026, I do promise!

A very okay role playing game with satisfying enough gunplay.
Most of the character/skill check events are flavor, with proper meaningful choices being less common, but still impactful.
While most areas you'll visit don't stand out too much visually, there are a few areas that really show off the world design team's ability for scale and awe. 

I don't find the setting compelling. None of the areas grabbed by interest, none of the factions were interesting to explore and learn more about, and I feel like that's mainly due to how unserious so much of the setting is. They dialed back the humor from the first game, but I still so very rarely felt like the game took itself seriously. In those few moments when the story, characters, and atmosphere were serious, the game was extremely engaging. However, that's dragged down by how over-the-top evil and 'dystopian' the factions are presented.

In terms of aesthetics, the color palettes of the outfits and worlds all felt just unnatural. Why is the grass purple? Because alien planet. Why are military uniforms so brightly colored and look like they're made of glass? Because advertisements! Why do their helmets look like snail heads or big germs? Because science! I just couldn't buy that anything in the world was made by real people for real reasons; everything felt like it was designed, not engineered. 

There are several times where a character is meant to do some kind of action (loot something, enter things onto a terminal, fix something, etc) and the screen just fades to black instead of showing what that character does. It's 2025, this is a $70 AAA game, why the hell can we not have properly rendered out cutscenes? 

While I got good frames throughout the game, I had to very regularly adjust settings to keep my game from crashing. I could play at native resolution 100+ fps no issue for some areas, and others I'd crash if I didn't limit my FPS and use upscaling. 

Overall, can't recommend

My verdict: 8.5/10. Wotjek is a big, beautiful mantisaur. It's definitely worth a look, especially when it goes on sale. I enjoyed the story. Nothing too groundbreaking in terms of depth, but fun light satire. I laughed out loud many times. Though I wouldn't necessarily describe the overall tone as a humor focused game.

The difficulty on normal is not hard at all, except maybe in the beginning when you have no skills or good weapons. Eventually certain weapons like "Last Whisper" make the endgame completely trivial, at least on normal.

I like the Arc Deco / Belle Epoque inspired art design overall - 9/10. The sound design is pretty good but not outstanding. 8/10. The music is certainly interesting; it's not going to be everyone's cup of tea, but it's very well composed, performed and recorded. 9/10.

I really liked the material designs - just the way things like cloth and metals and certain other things looked. And many of the animations, like when you interact with various devices, is very satisfying and often looks really cool. As well as decent quality character animations. I honestly like the clever way they did the main menu.

The RPG elements overall are pretty light but enjoyable. It's not a super sweaty RPG, nor is it meant to be. It's for light FPS RPG enjoyers. In general, decide on just a few skills and advance only those. Don't distribute them around. But ultimately you can finish the game pretty much no matter how badly you f'up your build.

Performance at 4K Ultra hasn't been an issue for me, but I have an RTX 5080, and I don't mind (and in fact prefer) DLSS framegen (and RT off), so performance is very smooth.

I was a big fan of the first Outer Worlds. While this game wasn't everything I hoped for in a sequel, it's still really good. It keeps and expands on that thing the first game did where every skill gives you unique opportunities and the game seems to remember and react to each little thing you do. It's one of the best parts of the game and I'm glad to see them keep it alive and even get a little meta with it at times. On top of that, the combat feels great and there's a lot to do.

As far as negatives go, I prefer the skill system of the original game a lot. Skills went from 0 to 100 and you'd put several points in each level. Additionally, skills were grouped so that they would all level as a unit until you reached a certain point, then they would level individually. It felt really cool how all of my gun skills could increase until I hit a point where I really needed to specialize into a certain kind of gun. Now there's a smaller selection of skills that go from 0 to 20 and you get two points per level. I'll admit I can see certain advantages to this, but overall I find the system less satisfying. I would also say that for a sequel and a $70 game, I was expecting to see a greater increase in scope. The Outer Worlds 1 felt like a smaller proof of concept built so that the sequel could be a massive game. I'm still early on but based on my experience so far and what I've heard elsewhere, it doesn't seem that this game is too much bigger. It's not the end of the world and small games are still cool, I had just hoped to see Obsidian outdo their own work on Fallout New Vegas with this one.

I will say the game is decent and a small step up from it's predecessor; it's worth playing but not at full price.   I'm far too lazy to write up a lengthy review, but I agree with most of the criticism that has been levied at the game and I will list a few of my biggest grievances:

- Buggy, particularly quests getting bugged.  It's in need of patching
- Loot is mostly pointless.  I only crafted ammo (rarely) when I got low but always had the money to buy it if I wanted.  Most of the stuff I picked up never got sold or used because I was never in need.
- Mods are not very good.  I had the same mods on my weapons/armor for most of the game.  Felt no need to modify weapons or armor.
- Enemy and companion AI are bad.  Companions have no sense of self-preservation and enemy AI don't care if you drop someone right next to them as long as it was a silent stealth kill.
- Game doesn't take any risks and "lock" you out of anything based on your skills.  You can still pretty much do everything regardless of build and I'm left with no desire to replay it.
- Story is meh, I never really felt invested in the world or characters.
- Humor is stale.  The game seems to recycle the same cliche jokes over and over in different verbiage.
- WHY the ♥♥♥♥ are dead monsters still making noises? You can kill an entire nest of them and as you run past their corpses they are still making noises.... why?

Anyway, ymmv, but the game needs some work and again I can't recommend anyone paying full price for this game.

Note: I played through the entire game doing all of the quests available on Hard difficulty.

[b]Forward[/b]: I finished the story and did most of the side missions in 25.7 hours. The game lands around a solid B+, with great production value and some notable technical quirks. I won't be 100% the game.

[b]Graphics: 90[/b]
Visually sharp and vibrant, with excellent environmental design and character models. However, DLSS issues caused flickering and occasional blur, which detracted from the overall polish.

[b]Control: 80[/b]
Even after adjustments, the controls felt slightly floaty and unresponsive at times. Gunplay and movement are serviceable but lack the tightness expected from a modern RPG-shooter hybrid.

[b]Audio Quality: 93[/b]
Excellent work on the soundtrack and ambient design. Weapon sounds, dialogue, and environmental cues all contribute to a strong sense of immersion.

[b]Stability: 85[/b]
Mostly stable with a few minor frame drops in larger cities and during particle-heavy firefights.

[b]Loading Times: 70 (SSD)[/b]
Load screens were far too frequent for a 2025 release, even when running on SSD hardware. The experience is smooth once loaded, but the interruptions pile up.

[b]Level Design: 92[/b]
Fluid and intuitive. The game does an excellent job guiding the player without handholding, and exploration feels natural across planets and facilities.

[b]AI Competence: 70[/b]
Enemy AI is muted and predictable. Companion AI fares better, but combat encounters often lack tension due to weak opponent behavior.

[b]Writing + Replayability: 88[/b]
The humor and tone fit Obsidian’s style, but the lack of romance options and limited narrative payoff leave the story feeling just short of great. Still, multiple builds and choices encourage replay.

[b]User Interface: 90[/b]
Clean, easy to navigate, and visually consistent. Menus are efficient, and quest tracking is clear and customizable.

[b]Game Balance: 78[/b]
Enemy damage spikes feel uneven, particularly early on. Difficulty options help, but I dropped down to Story Mode for smoother pacing.

[b]Grade: 84.0[/b]

Loved this game. I was a big fan of Fallout NV and Outer Worlds 1 so it's not a big surprise that I would enjoy the sequel. It's been quite some time since I've played any game like Skyrim, Fallout 3,4 Starfield, etc. so this game scratched this particular itch and did it well. 

I spent over 80 hours playing on hard difficulty and I'm thinking about going back into another play through after I take a little break to play some other games on my back log. 

Breaking my experience down:

Controls and feel: The movement feels smooth, and each gun type feels nice to shoot. I didn't get a chance to play with melee weapons, so I won't comment on them. There is an added double jump you can get as an early quest that makes traversing a ton of fun (personally speaking)

Bugs: Biggest bugs I experienced was the save button being grayed out randomly. I say randomly only because I didn't attempt to look for a pattern. I simply saved frequently enough that it didn't end up being a big issue. I ended up loading old data 4 times which set me back maybe 20 minutes total. Out of 82 hours it wasn't a big problem for me personally, though as a warning, buyer should be aware of that issue. 

Main Quest: [spoiler] The first half of the game is a man hunt to find a mysterious character who has wronged you, the second half is saving the world from the bad guys. Nothing out of the ordinary, but the stakes ramp up effectively and I thought that the it was adequate enough to keep my interest.[/spoiler] 

Factions: Besides companions, I live for the faction quests in these types of games. In Outer worlds 2 you effectively have 2 factions to support or oppose. Antie's Choice and The Order of Asendence. The both have their own wacky vernacular and fantastic clothing. There isn't a lot of depth (personally) regarding their political/ religious philosophies, but they both have enough interesting characters that kept me interested in their fate up through the end game. Don't expect some deep discourse on political economy and morality and you'll be fine. I personally focused on the characters you interact with in each faction and their individual stories and had a good time. 

Companions: Loved them all, except your robot companion. The Robot just didn't have much dialog or personality to be of any interest to me personally. Each companion gets 3 missions to delve into their character development that you will shape with them. The choice in every instance is boiled down to pacifism (healing) or violence. While that may seem limited, I never really got tired of convince my companions to improve their mental health and embrace a less violent life. Maybe next play through I'll go for a more violent jerk angle.

I do wish that they each had one or two more quests. While I enjoyed all of their quests, I did think some of their personal growth came very quickly with only a few missions to change them. In particular I'm thinking about Aza, as their change in personality is quite drastic and with only 3 missions to see that change, it felt a bit too quick. I also would love to see a lot more dialog between characters both out on the field and in the ship. Aza seemed to have the most dialog among all of the characters I played with Val having the least. 

Skills: Unlike other games of this kind, you will only get to maximize a total of 3 skills. That being said, I didn't know that to start with and picked 5 that I invested in. Speech, observation, lock picking, Medicine & Science. The only skill I had at 20 was speech and that's only because I wanted to make sure I could always rely on that skill check when it came up in the end game. I still had high enough skills in observation and lock picking to get everywhere I wanted and enough skill in science and medicine to pass quite a lot of skill checks. You simply can't do everything on a single play through. 

Speech Opinion: One thing that I would personally like to see games like this adopt in the future is an elimination of the speech skill. This is entirely based on my personal play style, but since the speech skill is so often a pre-requisite for peaceful resolutions I always max out speech in damn near every game that has it as an option. This is entirely selfish on my part I know, but it always feels like I'm always limiting my options in skill selection when I see the speech skill. Besides that, I personally think it would be interesting to design the peace full resolution more around alternate skills or secrets found.

Speech cont: Something I love about OW2 is that many of the dialog options in the game revolve around secrets you find in terminals or side quests. So much of my fun in these games comes from exploration and finding hidden dialog or secrets about the world and characters. Tying that experience directly with peace full resolutions in dialog felt fantastic, hope they continue that in future games. 

Overall: I love OW2 and highly recommend it to anyone that has a love of Fallout NV and all of the other games like it. My deepest criticism would simply be that I dearly wish for more communication between the companions.

The Outer Worlds was an inspired, but quite flawed experience when it released in 2019. It suffered from some technical issues, issues in the pacing of it's main story, and mediocre gunplay. The Outer Worlds 2 is an all around improvement over the first installment, while also still being a good, but flawed experience. 

The game is a true sequel in it's game design, retaining all the core elements of the first, while making many minor changes to improve the experience. At the same time, the story is almost entirely removed from the first game, being set in a different part of the galaxy with only passing references to the first game. This makes it quite easy to pick up even if you haven't played the first one. 

Maybe the biggest improvement over the original is the improved story, both in writing of it's characters and plot, as well as the general pacing. The writing is not as good as classics such as Kotor 2, but it is quite good. The game is, however, also quite a bit longer and thus I would recommend anyone on the fence to still pick up the cheaper and shorter original first, if you haven't played it. A standout in this game, and a marked improvement over the original, are the companions. They are complex characters with longer and more in-depth stories than the first games' companions. Niles and Inez in particular really stood out to me, and were probably my most used companions because I just liked spending time with them in the world. 

A really cool element of this game are flaws. At times, the game will offer you a choice to pick up a character flaw, that will come with some bonus at a sometimes considerable drawback. The game also warns to not pick up certain flaws on a first playthrough, so if you skip those there is something to look forward to in future attempts. In general, replayability seems high, between different characters builds via talents, flaws and choice of weapons, as well as the various ways in which quests can be resolved and factions to ally yourself with. In this way, the game continues the legacy of the original and really all obisdian rpgs. 

The game maintains the weaknesses of the first game, especially in the rather mediocre combat and balancing. On normal difficulty, I reached max level 10 hours before finishing the game, and I didn't even do all the quests. There are a lot of cool weapons, but hit feedback and general encounter difficulty make many fights quite easy and boring. I'd recommend playing on hard or very hard for anyone whose decent at first-person shooters. I can't say much about melee combat, since I played only using ranged weapons.

All in all, the game is an easy recommendation for anyone who loved the first one, while I'd give a cautious recommendation to anyone who generally likes Obsidian's rpgs or rpgs more generally. It's not perfect by any means, but I did have a lot of fun and would be interested in a third game a few years down the line.

Very satisfying game for those into a funny Bethesda-like RPG that has a generous dosing of humor and levity. The humor/surreal aspects kept the exploration (Auntie's Choice ad drones), conversations, and log reading interesting.

I pity those that don't experience the radio, especially the Order channel. Some really catchy songs on it, like Pi/Prove It/Irationality - good stuff that dig into the mathy nerdiness of Order.

Gameplay wise, the combat/stealth was fairly solid. Took a while to get used to it but I used a combo of stealth and direct attacks throughout the game (played on Normal) to good effect. Felt like the game could accomodate either playstyle fairly well, but does require some leveling to get a competent Commander.

Environments and planets were plentiful and interesting to explore. I liked the diffs between Order/Protectorate/Auntie's Choice environments, felt very fresh exploring each and every one of them.

Just like in OW1 the story felt too convoluted and unfocused. Either I'm getting filtered or I'm just not interested enough but I couldn't keep track of all the characters and their motivations and back stories and kept getting confused on who I was siding with vs. not (spent way too long thinking Protectorate was going to be an actual choice vs. enemies forever).

The lack of polish was fairly detrimental to the experience. I spent most of the game with a Flaw that steals items indiscriminately,  but a bug made it so that even if I moused over an item from a distance it would ping and notify that a theft occurred - even if it didn't _actually_ register as a theft. The lockbox opening experience is boring as hell too, and switching gadgets didn't feel intuitive. And I can't believe in TYOOL2025 mouse smoothing needs a .ini fix.

And the experience curve is totally busted and made the last quarter of the game much less rewarding - I got to max level way before the end of the game and basically was left with an FPS game rather than an RPG that allowed me continual progression. Quite a bore other than some interesting story bits. Difficulty curve is busted too, highest diff is just very unfriendly to low level stealth characters because the damage doesn't scale well and sneak attacks are gated behind a minimal dmg threshold (who does this?!).

3.75/5 - Overall an enjoyable RPG, but marred by lack of polish and an endgame hurt by the lack of a reasonable XP curve that provides continual progression.

I'm enjoying The Outer Worlds 2, but I can't recommend it while many perks are bugged, the leveling system punishes you for not picking the correct skills at the start of the game, and there's no respec option. Also, this game is not worth $70. I bought it with money in my Steam Wallet from selling items, but if I had actually paid $70 for The Outer Worlds 2, I would be pissed. This is a $40 game at best.

I had extremely high hopes for this game given how much I loved the first game. It still has the same feel and it still reminds me of an outer space Fallout game. I do very much recommend that you watch the Outer Worlds Secret Level episode to understand a bit more of a few characters. It's not game changing, but you'll appreciate moments more when you get to it.

Combat: If I recall, you could give your companions weapons and armor from your inventory to use. It's a lot more simplified here where now you can't, but upgrades to either are dependent on your advancement on their story quests.

A lot of perks mention weak points, but it's not explicitly shown what a weak spot even IS. I can assume it's a head, but why not just say head? It feels like there should have been a better way to display where one was. I personally always just aimed at the head. Some weapons are a lot more satisfying than others (Big Bang).

The option to swap gadgets whenever and use them was nice, but mostly I just used shields. Time slowdown seems nice until you're filled with bullets.

Environment: I don't remember fall damage hurting this much in the first game, but I do kind of like the limitations on how far a person can really fall before it becomes a huge problem.

Worlds still look pretty cool.

Skills: This is my biggest gripe. It feels like the game is telling you that you can only be truly good at 3 things. Or in my case, only truly good at hacking and not good enough for anything else that feels like it really matters. I personally had 11 in engineering/lockpicking with 20 in hacking and 15 in speech. imo a big mistake. I knew like with all games that have a speech option that it was going to be basically the most important skill. I was horrifically correct.

It doesn't feel like there are enough skill points to go around. There's a flaw to increase the amount, but you guarantee you never get a 20 in a skill with it.

Story: I VERY much like the variance of what you can do. However, I combed what felt like everything in the game, but kept seeing dialogue options say that I was missing the required information. Like where?!

Some companions gave a really nice story to go through. Not all of them though. Val I feel is kiiind of the stand-in combination between the cleaning mech companion and the ship computer in the first game. Yet feels lesser than both.

3 of the companion quests felt kind of ad lib. There's a certain "twist" at the end of each of them that feels a little overdone.

What I really don't understand is that each faction has their own rep bar. I've done every quest I could find in each hub and never did anything that net me a loss in reputation. Yet somehow I couldn't even get respected with one of the factions. I didn't read every perk, so maybe there was one there meant for rep gain.

Conclusion: I still really liked this game and I can't wait for the DLC. Once they finish those I'm basically sold on a third game if they ever make it.

This game is pretty fun, there are some interesting and amusing characters, the satire is on the nose (more like a punch in the nose) and a bit hit or miss. Overall the RPG element of the game is quite good, although the narrative is not as deep and branching as I'd like it to be, it's still engaging. It's a definite improvement on the 1st game in regards to weapons and combat, the gunplay is servicable and sometimes even good, once various unique weapons and skills get unlocked. Overall I'd recommend this game but it has some major issues that are severely undermining my enjoyment of it.

1. The performance is laughable, it's downright garbage. The game struggles to pull above 20-30 fps in most places, that is at 1440p with a top end GPU. You can get it to stay above 60 by reducing the AA and shadows settings to low, but dear lord, the game looks like absolute ♥♥♥♥ when AA and shadows are at anything below very high. It ends up looking grainy as hell and for some reason there's absurd artefacting when those settings get dropped. I have a freesync monitor and use frame gen which makes the game playable but it feels like ♥♥♥♥ due to the low base frame rate.

2. The enemy AI is complete amateur hour, the melee enemies just zerg rush you, and they have a super annoying unavoidable lock animation that'll slide them towards you when they want to swing, they also get a strange speed boost during that animation, it looks like ♥♥♥♥ and feels like ♥♥♥♥ to play against. The ranged enemies stand still and fire, occasionally when the AI realizes it can take cover it does so in places where you can just keep shooting them because half their body is sticking out. Then there's the grenades,  some enemies will stand out of your los and chuck endless grenades at you with pinpoint precision. You have to find them and shoot them, while they keep standing still chucking grenades. The AI is awful and ruins the combat experience severely, enemies tend to get stuck on each other too. 

3. The companion AI is equally ♥♥♥♥, with your companions standing still out in the open and shooting until they get knocked down, now you have to run out into the open to revive them, I just ignore them at this point, they self res when you're out of combat. Companions will also stand still and get knocked by grenades, making no attempt to avoid the explosion.

4. I had to disable mouse acceleration via a config file, the game felt like ass to aim, eventually I found through a reddit post that there's mouse acceleration that can only be disabled by manually adding a config file to the save directory.

5. Weapons are useless garbage unless you heavily invest in the guns, or explosives, or other weapon damage related skill. The scaling is awful without investing perk points.

Having loved Outer Worlds: Spacer's Choice Edition -- I decided to pay for early access for this sequel -- and I've never purchased a Steam game at full price before (except for No Man's Sky).  I just love the storytelling and the way it unfolds -- mixing a warped sense of humor into a series of beautifully rendered settings.  The weapons and mods are really neat-o -- and I love mowing down the baby raptidons the best.  I've also been getting my stealth skills up so I can do silent take-downs of Spacer's guards with my crazy-sharp sentry's blade.  Man, does their blood squirt and gush so perfectly silent as I caress them to the floor to go through their pockets.  I'm taking my time with this one, reading all the messages from the various terminals -- and burning pass-keys onto my card when I can.  In other games, I'll go "yeah, yeah, yeah" and click to skip all the blah blah blah.  But I'm so hooked on the story in Outer Worlds 2, I take my time.  Centuries from now, the Outer Worlds series will be taught to 5th graders in the same way Huckleberry Finn is today -- a coarse, thematic, genius and, at-times, appropriate-adjacent saga of liberating underserved communities from their fascist, brand-foisting overlords.

At least Obsidian can put a game out without waiting 10 years..

Do I think this was a good game? Yes. Was it worth the $70 price tag? Hell no.

I'll start with the positives. I found the story to be well crafted and really enjoyed playing through all the missions, exploring what my companions had to offer, and immersing myself into the world. Is it a bit silly in how villainous it was? Yeahhh, but it was done in a tasteful manner.

This game looks really good. Look back at the first Outer Worlds and then look at this one; it's impossible to deny the facelift this series underwent. While I did enjoy the stylized appearance of the first game, I think the more realistic graphics really emphasize how amazing the settings you visit truly are. 

This game is a miracle in the current AAA gaming-sphere because of how polished it is. I only ever encountered one bug, and it was a companion having a dialog bubble over their head when they didn't actually have new dialog to explore. That's incredible compared to some games coming out nowadays that feel like being able to properly run is entirely optional. It runs incredibly smoothly without any major issues.

Finally, I can't wait for when the DLC's drop for this game simply because the first game really set my expectations high. If the DLC's come anywhere close to the fun and intrigue that Peril on Gorgon and Murder on Eridanos provides, then I'll be very happy.

Now let's get to the negatives. Respectfully, I need Obsidian to stop telling us they made Fallout: New Vegas because no matter what they put out, it'll always be disappointing compared to the prestige F:NV has elevated the company to. People hold that game to such a high regard that nothing will ever reach that level of love despite the hype of their name being on a project. It's not that I expected this game to be solid gold in every regard, but there were occasional hiccups that left me wanting more.

The game felt like it moved pretty quick at times, at its detriment. It felt like I was 3/4th's of the way through the game in the blink of an eye. I think it has something to do with the amount of location you can explore compared to the last game (OW1 had 10, OW2 has 6). You don't feel like you're progressing as much when you're stuck between 2-3 planets versus ending up on 6 different locations (ships and planets) in the same amount of time. I don't even mind if the planets are smaller, I just feel that variety makes you feel like you're making more progress.

In my opinion, this game is not worth the $70 price. I know that the industry is moving towards raising game prices, but I genuinely think this game is only worth $50 to $60 max. It really speaks to how much gaming has changed economically in the seven years from the first game to this one, and it's a trend that's really frustrating.

This is a game I would highly recommend getting on sale. Even if it drops by a few bucks, its better than paying that inflated price tag for a game that (in my experience) takes about 30 hours of game time to beat.

The companion system was very underwhelming and does not synergize with other aspects of the game.  You cannot equip them with gear you find, and are instead restricted to specific upgrades that are locked behind companion quests.  What is worse there are only two sets of upgrades per character.  So imagine playing a RPG where you only twice upgrade the armor and weapons of your team members over the course of the game?  And if you do not feel like doing their quest, well tough they will never be able to perform at their full potential.

Companions will also not help you out with skill challenges you face even if logically it is something they should be very good at.  If you have a level 20 engineer companion in your party and come across a door that needs 3 Engineering to open, you cannot command them to open the door for you.  Considering how many things in this game are behind skill checks and the shear variety of skills available means that you will be missing out on far too much content for any given play through.  The game will become a near constant stream of things you cannot do, while your companions stand around useless.

And if you are thinking of making a non combat character focused on as many skills as possible to avoid this fate well... there are forced boss fights in this game that make characters with no combat skills miserable.

This game can be described as aggressively mediocre. If you were expecting a worthy successor to Fallout New Vegas, this isn't it.
- Tonally, the game can't decide if it wants to be some Futurama-esque sci-fi comedy or a gritty commentary on consumerism and totalitarianism. The two themes constantly clash and not in a good way.
- Most characters are bland and unmemorable.
- Environments are uninspired generic alien worlds with little life to them, and I never really got a sense that there was more happening outside of the little bubble we get to play in. It feels like being in a theme park, you just go from location to location completing the relevant quest. You spend quite a lot of time running between these points with not much in between.
- The skill system is extremely restrictive. If you invest in the "roleplay" skills like speech, hacking, mechanics etc you damage will suffer greatly, and you will spend ages slogging through the bullet-sponge enemies. On the other hand if you invest in combat skills you won't be able to participate in any of the roleplay skill checks. Only 3-5 skills can reliably be invested in.
- The game does little to support a pacifist play-through. Even you do decide to limit the amount of killing you do, you get punished for it since XP is awarded through kills, and the game does nothing to award you for not killing.
- Enemy variety is poor, you have a couple alien creatures per world and humans, and even the creatures are mostly reskins with some melee ones and others that spit stuff at you. The game also likes to throw hoards of the creatures at you. There are also robots but they don't come up all that much.
- Companions suck. Unless you invest in the leadership skill they are entirely useless. There is very little customisation (you can't even change the way the look besides having their helmet on or off) and 
- Performance and optimisation was very poor. I have a 4070ti and even low-medium graphics settings it could barely manage of 40 fps with quite a bit of stuttering. The are some unreal engine patches that can alleviate this, but even then the performance is still sub-optimal.
- The game is overpriced for what you get.

Maybe in a couple years when there is a game of the year edition and it's on sale it might be worth picking up but until then it's a hard pass.

I wanted to love this game. OW1 is one of my favorite games, and the cliff hanger at the end about losing contact with earth made me eager for a sequel. And the sequel starts promising, youre an earth directorate agent. Youre on an important mission, and then...youre just caught in a war between corporatism and fascism. It is constantly bleak, and there are huge tragic events that can only be stopped with a specific singular skill check, and theres no alternative. 
The lack of background music in favor of the radio system is a shame. I already have to listen to ads and political puff in the real world, let me enjoy atmospheric audio tailored to the environments. Even if the radio still existed and just muted ambient noise that would be fine. 
The world was just, bleak. Everything was bleak. And the followers? Such a let down. They suffer from an overarching problem, a lack of dialogue lines. They dont have much to say, ever. And hearing the same npc lines EVERY TIME you walk to matriarch mercy just felt like getting yanked out of immersion by the scruff, id rather they say nothing.
And guess what, theres a sort of implication about what mightve happened to earth. But even that isnt a definite answer on the matter. Its still left open ended.
I put nearly 60 hours in, I gave it my all. But listening to audio of someone getting lobotomized, and just feeling like thats part for the course on the tone, yikes.
I had to go replay OW1 because I was genuinely feeling a bit hollow after doing ALL that, just to have the result be, well the universe isnt going to implode anymore, the rest of the issues are still around, but hey, who cares, that wasnt our job!
The parkour aspect was cool, but a lot of the first person animations (i.e. centrifuge and auto docs) look and sound like theyre trying to be a bioshock ripoff
It was also a huge let down to not have the armor customizing from OW1, being able to use a helmet or armor to boost a stat to help with some of those skill checks really wouldve been helpful since the game doesnt really give you enough spare skill points to have a diverse skill set.
And a nitpick, the armor/outfit designs suffer from a severe lack of editing/refining. They've just got so much crap stuck on to them at every joint, plate, edge etc etc etc its a mess.
I WANTED to love this, I was prepared to adore it. And i just felt sad and deflated, kinda wish I could get a refund, at least for the dlcs since I dont plan to play the game again.

im conflicted on this game, it has good elements:
- the gunplay is fun
- the enemy AI is reasonably competent
- the game has good replay value when it comes to the main quest and it allows you to tackle the same quests with many different builds (science, stealth, violence, speech, etc)
- it has a unique scifi steampunk aesthetic that i personally like
- the game doesn't come with 20 years of existing lore you need to know to understand what's going on
- the inventory is unrestricted so you're never to a point where you have to sacrifice items you're carrying, you can loot everything you find
- the consumables and crafting are reasonably simple, there is no "information overload" where you need lots of knowledge on the game's meta to make use of the stuff you find

but also lots of bad things:
- an €80 (€100 for the collectors edition or whatever they call it) pricetag for a single player game is very steep, if i didn't like the first game i wouldn't have bought it at full price.
- too many strong independent quirky girlboss characters
- much of the dialogues and storylines feel like they were written by terminally online redditors, at times it comes off as a caricature of itself for how bad it is
- you can't side with the protectorate, unlike TOW1 where you could side with the "evil" faction almost from the start
- the melee companions are a liability most of the time, they rush into combat and get downed, so you're always at a disadvantage, if it wasnt to complete their quests i wouldnt bother having them in my party
- 95% of what's in the game serves the purpose of advancing the main campaign and nothing else, it feels lifeless in a way because the NPCs don't just exist and live their lives in the game, almost everything is going to funnel you back to the main quest, there are no locations or towns where people go about their lives normally or you get to do strange sidequests that are completely unrelated to the main campaign
- the level cap and skill points awarded are kind of low and force you into specialized builds because you won't have enough to distribute around and play different styles in a single playthrough

all in all im still going to give it a positive vote since i had fun playing it but there's room for improvement.

EDIT: in hindsight im changing my review to negative, because some aspects are a clear step back from the original, i hope they get more ambitious in the future and deliver a better game.

Beat the game and have had time to ruminate on it. I think in general, there's a lot of things it does better than the first. 

Firstly, the combat is much better. Where in the first game combat was simply serviceable, in this game it is actually fun and has a wide amount of ways to play. There's also much more maneuverability and verticality to the maps, which I appreciated. In addition, there's a lot of hidden weapons and collectibles which I liked. 

The companions were well done for the most part, though I do wish that more of the dialogue bits we learn from them would come as they leveled up, something akin to SWTOR, so that you can't exhaust everything to learn about them in one conversation, but they were all interesting and surprisingly complex, even Aza who I figured from the beginning would be predictable but ending up having the most interesting background. In fact, the character I figured would be most interesting (Marisol) ended up being my least favorite character and had my least favorite companion quest.

The story was fairly good, though I will admit I preferred the side content and characters over the main story. The voice acting in this game is killer and has one of the most stacked voice casts I've seen in gaming. I liked most of the locations, my favorite being Cloister and Golden Ridge. I wasn't too huge on Praetor, though there were bits I liked, such as the Protectorate base and the mental refreshment place.

The skills, flaws, and perks system was very improved from the first and I think it forces the player to actually commit to a playstyle, and the flaws are no longer garbage. They actually give both debuffs and incentives not just perk points. Also the perks & backgrounds now can unlock dialogue options and the skills affect interactions like opening doors and disabling automechs or noticing things in your surroundings. In the first game it was just hack and lockpick that were interactable with the world so it incentivized speccing into those.

All in all, I really liked the game, it was a solid 8 out of 10, definitely room for improvement, but it's already a big improvement over the first game (which I loved). Give it a try. If you're unsure, just get it on game pass. I was all in since 2021 lol, so I was always going to play it, but if you like RPGs, you won't regret copping this game.

I'm nearly 60 hours into the game and don't feel anywhere close to the end. I hope I'm not because the story so far has really not quite gotten interesting enough.

I'm hooked enough to keep playing, I want to see the game through, but I don't know if I'd say I'm having fun playing. I'm mostly driven forward by curiosity. It's easier to cheese my way through combat than to actually fight. The fighting doesn't really feel all that satisfying. The exploration would feel pretty okay if it weren't for the amount of random things that just instantly kill you. It feels like this game wasn't very well play-tested because there's a lot that just doesn't feel good in the game.

Touching knee-deep water, falling from a bit of a distance, touching anything that glows, everything seems to one-shot you which kind of discourages exploration. The floaty jumping has on a few too many occasions sent me careening into insta-death. I've taken to saving constantly just in case. I am more likely to believe that I will simply get stuck somewhere or die than think it's worth exploring, and I'm the kind of person who likes to explore.

The stealth seems meh so far, there's weapons I don't understand why anyone would use, and some that I can use to basically just clear anything no questions asked.

The dialogue isn't bad most of the time, but I'm not getting a lot of soul. Dialogue options that were intelligent were one of my favorite parts of Outer Worlds 1, and so far it's been kind of lackluster, relying a lot on fantasy tech instead of making engineering and hacking dialogue sound real. The characters are a little bland, and don't have as much to say as I would hope. The Vicar and Parvati had some very real character in 1.

I get the sense that they spent too much time making the game big and not enough time making it good. I'd rather have a game where I was about done now than one where I'm spending so much time meandering where most of what I'm discovering is just loot or some half-baked side-plot.

The characters are zany and too unrealistic for my taste, the main plot isn't being very generous. Talking people down honestly seems way too easy, it doesn't make sense for the dialogue options to have worked. Like talking down one of the bosses basically boils down to saying "I read your emails and I know you don't really wanna do the things you're doing" and he's just like "you're right, I guess I'll let them hang me. Here's my gun and any loot you'd have lost from not killing me."

I feel like so much of this game could have been really cool if they just spent more time polishing and less time making the game vast.

That being said, I think it's kind of cool the way your background, and skills have a lot of rewarding elements. No matter what you're building towards you probably won't feel too left out. Once again, 1 did it better in some ways because there were 3 or 4 ways to solve everything, but in 2 it's more like 1 or 2.

I don't regret buying it myself, but I don't think I can recommend the game at full price.

With Fallout, Mass Effect and Deus Ex dead, there's not much competition for The Outer Worlds 2 if you're on the market for a sci-fi -themed action RPG. Luckily, it's not half bad!

All the essentials of the genre are competently executed. Both melee and ranged attacks feel satisfactory, and combat can usually be completely avoided by stealth or by passing speech checks. This allows for multiple character builds and various equally viable playstyles.

In addition to the usual skills, perks and attributes, The Outer Worlds 2 introduces flaws, which are trade-off perks triggered by the player. For example, if you sneak/crouch often, you unlock Bad Knees, which gives you +50% crouch speed but makes you emit sounds that nearby enemies will try to investigate. It's an interesting concept for a mechanic, but lacking in execution. It would be preferrable if the flaws either clearly emphasized a playstyle or forced the player to vary thing up to avoid them. Now they are kinda minor buffs and debuffs, and since you can always opt out, the flaws won't necessarily have any impact on the game. Hopefully, Obsidian doesn't have a patent on the flaw system, so other developers can steal the idea and employ it more creatively.

Audiovisually, everything is in place. The 50's pulp scifi aesthetic is very much Fallout in space. The caricatured factions of capitalist Auntie's Choice, evil orwellian empire of The Protectorate, and the technopriests of The Order are well written and function logically. The faction quests are on par with the main story, which is passable.

There are some minor nitpicks, the usual DEI garbage being one of them. Not a single one of your companions is a straight white male, and you'll struggle to find any of them portrayed in a positive light. Oh well, I guess that's why it's a dystopia? Omissions aside, DEI or other agendas aren't constantly rubbed in the player's face, so it's tolerable and doesn't ruin the game.

Another complaint is the inventory system. When shopping, you cannot see both your and the vendor's inventory at the same time. For example, if I think I need more lockpicks, I need to first check my inventory and then go back to the vendor and check if they have it. There's real estate on the screen, why not make both visible simultaneously? Also, the inventory system can be sorted (in a limited fashion), but not filtered, making management occasionally tedious. 

I ran into some audio glitches here and there, but otherwise my experience was relatively bug free. Obsidian is not Bethesda, but it's still very impressive to have such a clean code at launch in a relatively large game.

In summary, The Outer Worlds 2 is a fun, pulpy sci-fi action RPG that is competently put together. Fans of the genre will undoubtedly enjoy a playthrough or two. Which is good, since there's really no competition to mention, unless you're willing to play Fallout NV or Mass Effect 2 for the thousandth time.

I've played through the game twice now, first on Normal and again on Very Hard. I think the best aspect of The Outer Worlds 2 would have to be the character builds that are possible thanks to the various Background, Traits, Skills, Perks and Flaws you can choose. They make for some fun and varied gameplay, not just in combat but how you interact with the world around you, be it skill or dialogue checks. You can dumb your way through security checkpoints. 

Pick two positive traits and pick Dumb as your negative trait, bonus points if you pick Roustabout as your characters Background, you will never lack for funny dialogue options. You might think it's bad to lock yourself out of five skills by choosing Dumb, but it really isn't. Dumb helps focus your build and gives you funny, powerful dialogue options.

There isn't an option for a purely non-violent playthrough but high Speech, and exploring to unlock additional evidence, allows you to talk your way out of pretty much every boss encounter. If you want you can play as a total villain as well. You can choose Abrasive as a negative trait, this never allows you to go above neutral standing with every faction and there's a perk that increases the damage you can deal based on how negative your standing with them is.

The other major improvement in The Outer Worlds 2 is the unique, named gear. You can find them on vendors, seriously talk to every vendor, earn them as quest rewards, find them via exploration in the world, find encrypted lockboxes that require special keys and being opened on your ship. Any armor set or weapon you find can be the pillar of a build. At level 2, in the first town on the first planet you can buy a pistol from a vendor and use that as part of a build to carry you through an entire playthrough on Very Hard. If you take the time to explore and do every side quest it feels like you constantly find interesting gear for various builds.

There's a level cap of 30, you can specialize in 2 to 3 skills and get 4-6 skill points at the start of the game. You get 60 skill points without using the Easily Distracted Flaw, and more if you do depending upon how early you unlock the flaw. This upsets some people. You can't make a character that passes every skill and dialogue check. You don't need to level every skill up to 20, the cap, skills provide raw stats and act as a gateway to Perks. Having the right Perks and Flaws are as important if not moreso. And your gear matters as well. I beat the game on Very Hard using a light armor set, the Patient Step, with a damage reduction value of 3 but it allowed me to stay in permanent Tactical Time Dilation by late-game. It's like that episode of Futurama where Fry drinks so much coffee time simply ceases. Only instead of helping your friends and saving them from disaster, you're zipping around, dodging projectiles, blasting everything in the face with a pistol and ending fights before they even start. Or almost getting one-shot. 

This is probably one of the better implementations of Unreal Engine 5 that I have played but it's not entirely without issue and the performance will depend on your set-up. There is no shader compilation stutter in-game because there's shader compilation on start-up, how long that takes is dependent on your cpu. The initial boot is a lengthy compile, but every boot up after was the shader compile was short. There was the occasional bit of world partition/traversal stutter and slow-down in two to three settlements in the game. Matriarch's Mercy on Dorado has crazy slow down at night due to the raytracing/global illumination. Overall though, the zones are larger and there's fewer loading screens.

I still have one more ending, more achievements and a villainous playthrough to complete. And I am looking forward to whatever DLC they put out, the first Outer Worlds had some fun post-release content.

The combat is "OK", in that it's serviceable enough to carry me through an interesting story or world. Unfortunately, the Outer Worlds 2 offers neither.

Story-wise, there's no narrative hook that keeps you invested in resolving the conflict (mostly). The 'big bad' is a rift in space-time that will eat the solar system the player is trapped in, which would work well, if it weren't for the fact the rift is specified to only be dangerous in a literal decade. I understand they wanted to avoid the common disconnect of the hero going off to do side quests while a dragon is eating reality, but this solution reduces the stakes of the main plot enough to the point to kill motivation.

The uninteresting hook isn't in of itself a death knell, Obsidian's own Fallout: New Vegas is proof of that. 'Chasing a guy who stole your FedEx package' isn't exactly super motivating. The difference in New Vegas is that it doesn't rely on you caring about the plot in order to progress it; You are naturally drawn along the plot's path as you meet likable characters and engage in interesting dialogue/sub plots. The characters in Outer Worlds 2, unfortunately, are insufferable. No one talks like actual human beings, instead belching out quips, insults, and exposition. NPCs feel more like caricatures designed to fill certain archetypes rather than people.

World-wise: I hate it. This is subjective, obviously, but I *really* despise this weird sci-fi/steampunk/western/whateverthehell is going on. I genuinely can't even tell what's meant to 'high tech' and what's meant to be jury rigged. Outfits look atrocious, guns look and feel like toys, and the world design is just ugly.

The game is clearly attempting to satirize capitalism, but it utterly fails at any form of nuance. Corps are cartoonishly evil, with no redeeming factors. It doesn't help that the society depicted doesn't even make sense, with (awfully designed) ads being plastered all over corp owned settlements despite the fact it makes no sense to advertise in a town where the only products are theirs. It's equivalent to the government advertising the low low prices of your driver's license registration fee: Absolutely pointless.

In summary, the story isn't interesting, and I hate the game's world alongside its inhabitants. Combined with lukewarm game play, I just can't recommend it. Unless you're a Redditor, in which case you'd probably love it.

The first game was ok at best. This one is much larger, clearly has more put into it and more resources behind it, but I think I actually enjoyed playing the first game more than this one. 
     
The loot is mundane enough that you don't really care much what you're picking up after a little while. Armor doesn't really look cool enough to care, and doesn't protect you enough to worry about it much. The best gun I've seen is one that you can find on your ship immediately after finishing the tutorial mission. It's fine, I guess, but there's nothing inspiring there.    
     
The skill system is pants. Two skill points per level to build a character on is just dumb, frankly, when there's four skills that exist mostly just to open up doors and loot boxes. You pretty much need lockpick, you'll always feel like you should have Speech, and you need a weapon skill. That doesn't leave a lot of room for experimentation, especially when all the experimentation does is let you interact with things that the game is constantly telling you that you didn't spec right to open. It's a terrible system that shoves your character creation choices in your face as a negative rather than a positive right from the start of the game, and there isn't anything positive I can say about it.     
    
The backgrounds aren't much better. You can choose criminal, other kind of criminal, Lawgiver, another kind of criminal, or moron. The game seems to want you to play as a moron, because Roustabout with the Dumb trait is about the only way to make the dialog interesting. I don't want to play as a moron, though, and the backgrounds never really feel like they fit the story of who you're supposed to be. If I were incompetent, I wouldn't be doing this stuff. If I were a criminal, I wouldn't be going along with the quest I'm on. It's a simple thing, almost a throwaway choice, but it's another example that makes it hard to get invested in what's going on.     
     
I wanted to like this game. This is the kind of game I love. I've spent more hours than I should have trying to get into it, but I just don't care enough to finish it. Inane dialog, a world that isn't interesting, the first companion being the one companion that I like (same as the first game), gameplay that's fine for what it is but isn't something where you're booting this up just to experience it, and an awful skill system that's detrimental to the rest of the experience as a whole since it's constantly rubbing your face in the choices you either didn't or couldn't make. The game is bland and kind of soulless, but the worst part about it is that the core systems it's designed around make it impossible to enjoy. Even with a mod to give yourself a bunch of skill points every level so you can ignore the absurd limitations of the skill point system, it's still just not an interesting enough game to care. It's not that the game is terrible, it's just boring... which is sort of worse, really. 5/10.

Finished my first play-through, unsure if I'll do another at least for now.

It was a solid 7/10 I think (And not Randy Pitchford's idea of one), the story humoured me enough and kept me intrigued enough to keep battling forwards against the RPG systems holding it back, honestly this would have been much better if it leant more into the 'immersive-sim' category instead of trying to be another Fallout : New Vegas style RPG, but I digress.

The first two planets had a lot of interesting stuff to do but when you unlock the final two those worlds feel very under utilised, with a lot more potential for interesting writing and stories but they remain static. It tends to be how things seem to go in modern RPG's strong first act's then the areas later on suffer, I do however appreciate that you actually have to think about your skill-point usage and creating a master of none is not very viable, that also however meant there were a lot of 0's in my skill distribution.

Now a bit further into the RPG-side of things, regarding inventory and equipment, none of it was exceptional and anything with a decent design outwards had to be reshuffled for just small percentage buffs and barely any sets of outfits worked together visually, infact honestly I saw no need to really care about my equipment or the modding there-of, I just equipped a weapon that hit harder and diversified the ammo types. There's no weight limit, so you end up carrying a piece of everything around, or you can scrap it to use in the rather bare-bones crafting system, but really that'll be just used for you to make ammo.

Combat, was fine, can't say it challenged me too much in the later half of the game, the first half the enemies felt a bit spongey but not for long, I never really touched the TTD to slow down time, popped my shield, watched my companions flail uselessly as I tried to shoot the enemy, in the end it was just shoot enemy until dead, hit weakspot if you want it to go a little faster, or sneak for the extra damage.

Story, probably the strongest aspect, the two main factions are interesting enough and the antagonist faction is a cartoonishly evil enemy, which isn't fully bad since the game is rather satirical, none of the factions are interesting enough aesthetically or morally for me to ever truly remember them though, but for the main character and his companions it gets the job done, that said the companion interactions were very very very plain and honestly didn't feel strongly about any of them, except maybe Niles, the others are very archetypical set of companions (Psychopath, Means justify ends, Zealot, Soldier who is loyal to someone who doesn't care) you've met them all before.

Performance, this is a bit tricky, I recently acquired a new PC but I did try a bit of this on the old one, in the end I could do a solid 60fps but it honestly isn't pretty enough maxed out to warrant such a drain on resources, especially if you add in how static the NPC's and world is, it's not a problem to be that but surely that means a lot of processing power is free'd up, the only time my FPS dipped was when I had other programs running.

In conclusion : It's an okay RPG, and I'm fine with that, not every game has to be innovative or world-shattering, it killed 24 hours in totality for me and that I can live with (Disclaimer: It was a birthday gift)

This is exactly the kind of game I enjoy. Anyone who loves making multiple characters that actually feel different will love this one. On my first playthrough, there was a huge amount of content I simply couldn't access because of the skills I had chosen, and that's exciting! The skills that I did choose all had legendary weapons and armor associated with them, as well as a massive number of skill checks both in the worlds and in dialogue. 

The companions are great, just don't expect Baldur's Gate level writing here and you'll enjoy it.

The world and factions are incredible. There is an entire radio station for each with custom songs, all of which give you deep insight into who all of these people are and what they value. Not only do we get a post-capitalist faction like the first game, we also get a police-state and a group of scientists who worship determinism like a god. The writing here is great and expressed their differences and surprising number of similarities (the greatest being that they all thrive on controlling and manipulating their people, just in different ways).

I'm not a player of shooters, but to me the shooting felt fun and full of energy. You have lots of tools to use on combat, including companions abilities and science gadgets.

The main quest is simple, but in a game that provides you with so many options on how to tackle problems, it needs to be. When I say simple, I mean simple objectives. The number of paths I each of the main quest objectives is staggering, and often require different types of characters or different choices to be made.

Performance is where the game failed the most for me, but with performance mode upscaling I can get it to an acceptable level for me. Keep in mind I'm not the kind of person used to playing over 60 fps so your mileage may vary. If you've played other Unreal Engine games you may have a better idea how this will affect you and your system.

Overall a wonderful game that is the perfect size for me, and one that I'm already planning future characters for!

You yourself don't have any meaningful skills and it makes no sense that Niles has a taunt skill when he immediately explodes once somebody looks at him. Considering this is a significant portion of the game, it makes it difficult for me to recommend unless somebody just uses mods or whatever to mitigate the poor balance happening here. I'll probably try and do the same myself so I can actually enjoy playing.
So my thoughts boil down to it's worth playing & it's not a $70 game. Too short to be $70. Grab it on sale & you're golden.

I'm a little bothered that I got duped again by Obsidian. I gave up halfway through Avowed because it was so "bleh". Characters all felt like an extension of a single person, and were all too emotionally intelligent to be believable. Gameplay loop was boring. Performance was not great. Conversations were boring and drawn out way too long.

I played The Outer Worlds 1 and really enjoyed it. I decided to hold off on The Outer Worlds 2 after I was so disappointed by Avowed. Reading review after review of "I didn't like Avowed but this is good", I decided that it might be worthwhile to buy it and give it a play.

[h1]TLDR[/h1]

Not to bury the lede: the game's world is unimaginative, boring, and nearly every character/faction is a simplistic caricature of the underlying philosophy of each group. Almost every aspect of this game is one-dimensional.

[h1]Longer Explanation[/h1]

[h2]Graphics/Visuals[/h2]

Maybe it's just me but the whole world feels "plasticy" and too big for you? Like the world was scaled for your character's head level, but they put the camera on the player's chest? I feel like my character has been shrunk down and the scale feels "off". The plasticy feeling is the sense that the world is supposed to be real but everything feels... fake? It's hard to describe, and maybe it was the feeling they were going for, but it makes me feel like I'm not actually in the world that I'm playing in. The best I describe it as is the world doesn't feel like it has "weight" to it.

Last thing on the graphics: what in the ADD-hell is going on with the color saturation, especially with the green colors? It was nearly this same way with Avowed where everything was so over-saturated, it made the world feel like you were playing through a Instagram filter or something. I spent a significant amount of time trying to fix the colors, but ended up giving up because it always threw off the rest of the colors/shadows in the game.

[h2]Gameplay[/h2]

I started the game on hard but found it a bit too easy so I decided [spoiler] to take the "Flawed" flaw (without knowing what kind of flaws I could pick up) as I made my way through.[/spoiler] Even with [spoiler]all of the flaws I have,[/spoiler] the game is laughably easy to play. I [spoiler] steal everything because I'm a kleptomanic, so I'm flush with money [/spoiler]. I [spoiler] zoom around in crouch mode because of "bad knees", and I move so fast there's hardly ever a reason to not stay crouched everywhere I go [/spoiler]. I [spoiler] have the bad leadership flaw, but it feels like it does almost nothing because my companions are already nearly useless in a fight [/spoiler]. The hardest part about all of this is the way I've allocated my points the game is super easy until I get to a boss fight, and then it requires me to cheese the boss fights because the boss fights have not been tuned for different play styles.

The gadgets abilities are relatively bland, enemy AI just bunches into groups and I just grenade and shoot them using a single pistol that for some reason gives me significantly more damage than any other weapon I have in the game (even though I have a decent amount of points in "Guns"). I've tried multiple other weapons and even heavily invested in perks that increase rifle damage, but this one pistol with the one mod on it that I got in the first area just melts enemies still. It feels like the game actively discourages me from using different weapons other than that one green pistol.

Ultimately this game feels like only a small step above what Starfield did (which is not great). I read an article lately that some head of Obsidian said they want to have "Obsidian" games (essentially, their style is unique enough it becomes an adjective to describe the game style). I'm confused by this, because playing The Outer Worlds 2 still feels like I'm being transported back to games made 10+ years ago, like their own Fallout: New Vegas. If one of the studio heads really wants a "Obsidian games", they should probably start with making their games feel unique instead of copy/pasting the nearly same formula they've been using for decades, which is also just a copy of what Bethesda did with Fallout 3/Oblivion.

[h2]World Building[/h2]

I think this is one of worst parts of the game. Apparently only the people in your crew have any sense of depth to them (and it's still poor at that), but everyone else is just an overinflated version of [insert some kind of guiding theory/philosophy here]. There are no "people" in this game, just random bits and pieces that bring together into a caricature-whole of a idea. I get that [i]some[/i] characters would be hardcore into certain philosophies, etc., but making nearly everyone so one note feels extremely lazy. It makes it hard for me to care about any of the characters/companions/factions or their motivations.

The biggest problem with this is your own character's and companion's moral stances on anything seem vapid and only lasting as long as the conversation is going on. Additionally, you have to wade through a significant amount of dialogue just to get to the "they will remember that" behaviors, and often they are bluntly obvious. Many of the conversations feel about 25-50% longer than they should/need to be.

An example of bad world-building: [spoiler] You go to a farm where Auntie's Choice is knowingly doing experiments on the people assigned to the farm. They all go insane and start killing each other. You can choose to assist the scientist, but I never had any option to sabotage the scientist. Either way, at the end, I helped him create the antidote but was still not ok with what he had done so I killed him. Apparently my companion that I just fixed her arm after she realized Auntie's Choice had abandoned her was upset that I was killing other Auntie's Choice people. At best that companion should of applauded what I just did and at worst she may have not liked that I did that, but would have probably been otherwise unbothered that I just killed a sociopath scientist experimenting on people for profit. Instead it was like "Auntie's Choice killing is bad for me because I'm aligned with that faction, even though I am not anymore" [/spoiler] I think this was the point I couldn't ignore the lack of good world building any longer and it completely broke the immersion for me.

[h2]Conclusion[/h2]

In the end I'll say this: what made me fall in love with the original Fallout and Fallout 2 games, and the subsequent series was because often the main story was almost not important. It was all those little side stories that you would run into, and the craziness that would ensue depending on your actions that really made the games fun. Additionally, during a time when games were doing a lot of "good choices" and "evil choices", Fallout opted for the "cause/effect" choices where the choices you had to make were often complicated and each choice brought with it consequences realized and unrealized. Often all the choices on the table were equal parts good and bad, but in different ways depending on your choice. It made it feel less like there was "right" and "wrong" choices, but just "choices" in general that would shape the game.

I don't get that feeling with both Avowed and The Outer Worlds 2. As much conversation and "choices" you can make in the game, it feels very much like you pick a style and it's in your best interest to stick to that style throughout the game, and that often you're given many "false choices" because it's quite obvious which choice is going to give the best outcome. Even when you make choices in the game it feels often that most of the conversation is filler and very little of what you do is making a meaningful impact in the game world. This is fine if you have some fun and deep gameplay to back that up with, but that also feels simplistic and unimagined, leaving the experience as a whole feeling empty.

Outer Worlds 2 isn't a bad game, but it's grossly overpriced at $70, and has a couple major flaws.  The first is the often-criticized skills points system.  The primary issue is that skills checks are all pass/fail, and passing them requires eventually maxing the stats, in a game where you only get enough points to maximize three.  So if you have 15 points and lockpicking and encounter a chest which requires 16, you're SOL and your point investment isn't doing anything other than the minor combat bonus lockpicking provides (all stats provide some sort of combat advantage).  This is already unfun on its own, forcing players to either gimp their combat potential or forgo stats which facilitate exploration, provide exclusive narrative content, or offer alternative options for resolving quests.  What makes it really intolerable though is that players can't respec past the prologue, which is well before they'll have the opportunity to realize how badly spreading stats around will screw their gameplay experience, AND the game tries the trick the player with a flaw which offers 50% more skillpoints at the cost of guaranteeing they'll never be able to come close to maximizing any stats.  There's mods to fix this, but I'm not going to take those into account when reviewing the base game.  

The other problem is that the game just becomes boring after a while.  If you invest in combat stats, then enemies die almost instantly to the late game weapons, even on the highest difficulty.  If you don't, then every fight is a slog and you're going to spend a lot of your time crawling through air ducts.  Most of the NPCs and factions aren't particularly sympathetic, and after a while I just stopped caring about what I was doing and why I was doing it. 

Wait for it to drop to $40 or less.
