I read that they simplified some systems in the second game and didn't think much of it. My mistake. Any amount of companion customization is gone. You cannot select their personal equipment. They get a 2x8 perk tree but that's it.

The ability to concoct your own medicinal inhaler is gone. There are now inhaler-specific items found in game. Gone are the days of shoving fruit loops and space meth in my asthma inhaler. Any drugs you find in world cannot be picked up and saved. Instead they are used to instantly refill a portion of your energy or inhaler meter. All that food you pick up? Either sell it or wait till you're out of inhaler charges and throat auntie cleo's patented grape flavored 10 pound cheese wheels to get 6% of your health back. 

Your perk points add towards specific damage types (gun,melee,sneak attack) or conversation based ability checks. Engineering no longer allows progressively more advanced crafting options like upgrading your armor and weapons. In the unlikely situation you don't have points in hack, lock-pick, engineering or explosives, then every locked door in the game will stay that way. The amount of game that is locked behind specific ability point levels is astounding, especially when there is no way to temporarily boost stats you're not specialized in. 

The mathematic space mormons are boring, the imperialist intergalactic corporation might be neat if the companion they gave you didn't suck, and this new religious space police guy seems ok i guess. He's got a hammer.

TLDR; companions got a hard nerf and mostly aren't compelling, no more constructive drug abuse, no more upgrading weapons/armor, inhaler nerf, consumables are generally obsolete.

I can't define exactly why I dislike this on the surface "better" game so much vs. the first.  I have a strong feeling it lies with the underlying traits/perks/skills system which seems to encourage multiple playthroughs of the same exact story/content to experience slightly different mechanics.  As with Outer Worlds 1 the story itself is far too weak to ever think about wanting to replay it and the branching quest lines just seem to be a "shrug pick whichever do-badder you want to support".  It's a satire of a universe so why would anyone take any choices in the story seriously?  If the designers wanted to encourage specialization vs. open ended play they should have just used a traditional class/role (Medic,  Soldier, Infiltrator, etc...) based system and hand out progression where you really feel like your character is growing/developing their specialty talents.  The current system just feels like even if I'm nonstop pumping 2 specialized skills the game never rewards me for my specialization nor reacts in any way to the type of character I'm playing.  Gun them down, pickpocket them, hack their turrets, smooth talk them... nothing really changes your always headed to the same bland destination.

Too many hours past the refund period so I'll just put it back in the cloud and wait a year or so to see if some mechanics overhaul patches and the inevitable DLC improves things enough to make it worth the investment in time to play.

A disclaimer to start: I have never played Fallout: New Vegas. So I did not and do not care a single bit about Obsidian's pedigree in relation to that series. I did, however, complete the first Outer Worlds (w/ all DLC) and the base game for Avowed. So I can speak to what the dev is currently putting out, and how this second Outer Worlds game compares to the first.

We good? Good. Onto the spoiler free, useful stuff!

[h2] -- The Good -- [/h2]
I just finished my play through. I did what side-quests I came across, and didn't go out of my way to 100% the game. If you played the first game, you know that Outer Worlds (OW) has a very particular sense of humor. It's still here, but it has been refined in a way I found more enjoyable. The setting, atmosphere, and story paint a much more consistent picture of Arcadia, and the well-written NPC dialogue doesn't have to be as wacky and quirky to paint the picture instead. Note the use of the word "as". It is still very humorous, but it flows a lot better than in the first game. 

The writing: On the whole is extremely good. And I was worried it wouldn't be, going into it. Lots of different options for dialogue, and the choices felt meaningful. I maxed out the speech skill, so I got to enjoy all of the ways to verbally tie people in knots. And the choices tangled together in strange, fun ways that I couldn't have anticipated. The world felt very reactive to me. And unlike recent games I have played, I am actually wanting to play this game again in a few months or a year. I want to go a different build, and will probably go for an anti-corporate run!

Combat: Fighting is very fun, flowing well and punishing me for not adapting to the situations more often than not! I jumped between a couple of different load outs in the first half of the game, and enjoyed all of them. Running and gunning with light weapons felt incredible. Pulling out a hard hitting machine gun made me feel like a slow moving terminator. And the triple barrel shot gun makes you feel like a god early on. All in all, amazingly fun and I was always wanting to get into the next fight.

Performance: I had to upgrade my rig recently (thanks Microsoft, have a middle finger salute), so I am running an extremely high end, all AMD set-up. Besides a few very minor graphics bugs, such as grass floating off of the ground in a single, small area, I encountered basically no bugs. NPC path finding bugged once, but reloading a save from 30 seconds earlier fixed it. 

[h2]-- The Mid -- [/h2]
Factions: They matter, but also don't. Certain narrative paths will be opened or closed based on how much you are helping or murdering a given group. It's really not hard to get them to like you, though. It didn't really bother me, but I was also trying to go for a "friendship and flowers" first run. It mattered for maybe four main story beat, and never again.

Companions: I actually quite enjoyed all of the human companions. The robot one is blander, but that felt right. All of their stories were intriguing, and I was surprised that several of them were companions! I did grow to care about each of them differently, and wanted to try and get the best out come for all of them. They were reactive, had funny additions to situations, and I loaded a save more than once to make sure a specific one was present for an event that felt important to them. However, something was missing. Maybe it was how their stories were spread out to favor the back half of the game? But regardless, each companion's arc felt like it ended with a wet blanket of "I think I have resolved my issues and have changed. Thank you, player! You made all the difference!." It didn't ruin the experience, but I feel like the writers were so close and just dropped it at the end.

Flaws / Perks: Putting these together, because I was thoroughly whelmed by them in the first game. Flaws actually are worth considering in OW2. A boon for a bane. I think it takes a dozen hours of playing before you'll understand if a flaw is fun for your build, or ruinous to it. Great for extra play-throughs! The perks are a lot more fun, but also weirdly vague as to how they might work in the world. And there are no re-specs! The perks feel like a guide to potential builds, and they are quite versatile in what they cover.

[h2]-- The Poor -- [/h2]
Environments: On the whole, they are functionally fine and pretty to look at. Even gorgeous at times! The art style does feel very Avowed. Fantastical and very colorful, especially with compared to the human-made parts. It's a style, I feel neutral about that. The map DESIGN, however feels off. Maybe it's how all of the points of interest are spaced out, maybe its how I felt a compulsion to open the map every few minutes to make sure I was filling it in? Maybe that speaks to a lack of flow and memorable landmarks? But at the end of my time with OW2, I feel like I rarely engaged with the world. 

With this, I also have issues with the quest density in some later areas. I swear, I can smell the cut content. Massive stations, city ships, and structures filled with people... and maybe three to five quests. Combing the areas twice seemed to lead me to believe I didn't miss anything. OW1's later planets felt rushed and small, OW2's later planet's felt like they have been notably "Swiss Cheesed" and felt hollower as a consequence. 

The Ending I Got (Spoilers ahead): [spoiler] I made an alliance between Auntie's Choice and the Order. However just before the end, I did a side mission and got some info about Auntie, then during the last scene was able to convenience Auntie to sacrifice herself instead of myself, or one of my crew. Sounds cool right? Nope... THIS ENDING ONLY MAKES SENSE / HAS IMPACT IF YOU HAVE SEEN THE OUTER WORLDS EPISODE OF SECRET LEVEL ON AMAZON. This floored me! I just thought meeting Amos was just an easter egg, but having included and then using it as the sole reason to convenience Auntie to kill herself for others felt so hollow and wrong. She hadn't been personally characterized to us, the player, in any meaningful way that wasn't via propaganda or from her employees. [/spoiler]

[h2]-- Conclusion -- [/h2]
I very much enjoyed my time with this game, and felt drawn to come back to it each evening. (Unlike the first game, where I kept setting it down at Monarch). The combat, story, and writing were the highlights for me, while most maps felt a bit empty and unmemorable. The companions were very close to being great, but the companion arcs all fell a bit flat at the end in a manner that left me a bit whelmed. 

The BIG question in 2025: is the $70 price tag something you feel is fair for this type of experience? For me, yes it was. I feel like my money was well spent, even if I think $60 is what games should cost in general. (I'm an old man with white hair, sue me!) If some of these points I brought up are bigger issues for you, then wait until a sale. $40-$55 is probably the price point for you.

The Outer Worlds was a clever and genuinely funny satire of the dark side of capitalism on the backdrop of a campy old space serial; it had its flaws for sure, but I still found it a compelling journey and an interesting character study.  By contrast, the sequel is just constant grating Used Car Salesman dialog trying way too hard to outdo what came before.  Brandon Adler and Leonard Boyarsky's idea of "improvement" is to just make every character their personal sock puppets, constantly belting out "Look how smart we are!  Pay attention to our writing style and rapier wit!" while forgetting they have nothing to say that hasn't already been said better in the previous game. The gameplay has at least improved a little, adding in Fallout style perks upon level-up and encouraging more focused builds, though it's often frustrating to try and complete quests when you need a specific skill at a particular level and simply don't have any way around it, other than to quit out and go do more filler quests until you do.  "Flaws", an interesting if undercooked concept in the first game, now seem actively engineered to screw you over by turning you into an ineffective master-of-none character; none that I encountered ever seemed to offer a benefit worth the drawback.  Gunplay is just Borderlands Lite with its moddable weapons and overly spongey enemies that lack any real variety, and your comrades simply don't have much of anything in the way of customization; well, they do have upgrades you can find and perks to assign every five levels, but they have almost no tangible impact on making the combat feel any more deep or rewarding.  Outer Worlds 2 is your typical self-proclaimed intellectual with no real personality and nary a novel idea in its head, but that doesn't stop it from running its mouth and laughing at its own stupid jokes for a solid sixty hours; which is about forty more than I could bear.

T:DR:  I want to send a PSA out to this game's devs as well as the people that made Horizon Zero Dawn and The Last Jedi.

**** FRUMPY MIDDLE AGED UNATTRACTIVE MENOPAUSAL WOMEN DO NOT MAKE COMPELLING ACTION HEROES ****


Real Review:


Guys, this game is totally Fallout New Vegas in space!!  It really is.  well sorta.

Why is it FO:NV in space? 

  Well its a hard RPG of course!!!   Did you guys wait 20+ years for Vampire Bloodlines 2 to be released only to find out they removed all the RPG mechanics you loved from the first one?  Well guess what?  This game has everything you have been waiting for. The RPG gameplay is in here and its great!  Its got the action RPG vibes down perfectly.  It even feels a lot like Knights of the Old Republic in some ways.  All your skills show up constantly.  Even the perks and background traits appear often in dialog (something even FO:NV didnt really have with maybe a few tiny exceptions), making your character feel very unique.  Every skill point spent is immediately reflected in the gameplay.  You can only max 2-3 skills in a single playthrough and since there are 12 skills, the replay possibilities are fantastic.  Roll a different character and you have a totally different experience.  Its wonderful.

Why is it not like FO:NV?

2 reasons, characters and atmosphere/setting.

The characters are BORING!  I'm playing it right now as we speak and I can only tell you the name of like 2 of my companions.  Imagine the most boringest character from Mass Effect.  Jacob maybe?  Well they have a Jacob clone in this game and hes way worse than Jacob.  All the characters are forgetable, not just party members but bad guys as well. 

I want to send a PSA out to this game's devs as well as the people that made Horizon Zero Dawn and The Last Jedi.

**** FRUMPY MIDDLE AGED UNATTRACTIVE MENOPAUSAL WOMEN DO NOT MAKE COMPELLING ACTION HEROES ****

No one wants to play as these characters, no one wants to fight these characters, no one wants to save the world with these characters taking a leading role.  Do you guys find it compelling to track down the top secret interplanetary space assassin and finally take their mask off only to reveal an old woman that looks like your mom's friend from Jazzercize class?  NO ONE LIKES THIS!  Mildred the retired librarian is not playing this game, identifying with the assassin because she looks like one of her peers from bridge class. 

  The setting is hyper space capitalism and at first you might be nervous like this is going to be some lame Rich White Suburban Communist Teenager (RWSCT?) woke capitalism critique by a kid with a Che Tshirt moderating R/Socialism but thankfully its not actually like that (I mean maybe thats what they were going for but it doesnt come across that way).  The space capitalism in the game is so over the top and insane that it really doesnt resemble anything on earth.  It comes across more as a cartoon, which is totally fine.  No one wants to be preached to.  The space capitalism actually sounds like it could be fun, just like how Fallout has a crazy over the top 1950's atmosphere that really isnt anywhere like the real 1950s.  Its wacky and zany and FUN!  It works in Fallout just fine, but in this game it's really not that compelling.  Its not awful, its fine.  Its just not amazing.

 There are tons of games out there with AMAZING atmospheres.  Fallout, Bioshock, Dishonored, KOTOR, Thief the Dark Project, Mass Effect.  The setting and atmosphere in these games is so amazing you just love being in the world.  You listen to every conversation.  You read (actually read) every book.  You sit back and soak it all in because the world awesome.  In this game you just kinda meh and move on to get to the RPG stuff (which again is great).  

The gameplay is fine.  It plays kinda like Borderlands with Mass Effect style companions (as in they help out a bit, but you yourself do 90% of the killing).  

If you liked the first one, this is much better, but if you were hoping for a true Fallout New Vegas in space you might be a bit disappointed.

First thing I want to get off my chest - I think most bad reviews of the game (and the first) are merely a difference of taste rather than an evaluation of overall game qualities.

Pros: 
- If you played the first game, this is definitely an improvement. Lots of quality of life upgrades and refinement in crafting/looting/skilltrees

- The overall dialogue, world art and music design felt more distinct and purposeful. More beautiful than ever, really. Extremely inspired, unique, and immersive. I felt this game really captured the essence of each faction more clearly than I felt in the first game and they were really heavy with the details of each groups motivations. 

- This felt seamlessly like the first game in that it was not so much an upgrade that it felt entirely new, so there wasnt a culture shock or anything that stood out as offputting for being different. This could be seen as a con if people were really wanting a graphic improvement or significant upgrade in design quality. But I liked that it felt like a DLC - picking up right where we left off

- The goals of the character feel a lot more clear in this game plot-wise. I understand distinctly who each faction is, what they stand for, who I am fighting for, and what my missions are. I dont know if it is because I already understand how the world of this game works at this point, but the first game did not feel this clear to me.

- The companions were a lot more unique and interesting as far as their quest lines and dialogue goes. I also thought their abilities and having more control over their upgrades was extremely fun and led to a lot of dynamic combat style. I felt I could actually use each character and had trouble choosing, whereas the first game I only ever used two of the party members.

- real improvement to the diverse wildlife. I loved the new monsters even though theyre harder, but it felt nice to have a change of pace. The ship pets are a very fun addition but I wish there was more I could do with them or have more than one available at all times, like a mini ship zoo!

- end screen telling us how the choices of all of out actions played out was awesome actually! This made it feel like our choices did matter a lot more here or that we can have a moment of reflection to consider how it all played out in the end. Probably one of my favorite additions

Cons:
- I don't feel as attached to the companions, or many of the characters at all, really. I do feel more attached to the companions in this one I think than the first game, but in general I dont think character relateability, Companion or NPC, left me with much emotion. 

- Sometimes dialogue options, especially for game-changing choices, fell flat or felt silly. The way we convince people - I just wish some of the dialogue was written more realistically. Sometimes it felt they surrendered just because I had the skill check, but not because of the words themselves, which often did break immersion.

- A huge complaint in the first game that surprisingly they did not resolve with this game is the low level cap. There is so much of the world still left to explore. I am 20 achievements short that many are combat related and may require me to complete more quests, fight creatures, etc and thus would want to gain experience from this. The refined skilltree actually matters a lot more in this game, but because of the low level cap, it makes it extremely difficult to get a lot of necessary skills. It feels as though 3/4 of the skills I did not even touch because I would not have enough points to use them on quest-imperitive skills. The main 4 being engineering, speech, hacking, and lockpicking - you can basically forget about the rest. Although not really - they are all fun and important in their own unique way, but to be able to build more diversity is almost impossible with the low level cap. Be very careful with your choices in this - The skill tree does matter more especially with the level cap.

- the medical inhaler/toxicity/primer mechanic was even more confusing. I also went the whole game with only 1 primer slot and 2 weapon slots because I could not figure out what gives you all the slots besides 2 perks and a weapon mod. This was not clearly defined and I felt I did not utilize the primers effectively. Also, plasma actually feels like a total downgrade in this game but triple the price

- Still buggy, there is a bug at the end quest where the quest marker falls on people that are not there. So unless you know the workaround - youre basically locked out of completing a quest because of it.

- Achievements could be more unique and fun, like "collect all ship pets" or tailored to skills to make them feel more meaningful, like "beat x boss with only dialogue options" or "unlock all loot boxes by lockpicking"

- because the game felt so similar to the first in a lot of ways, I do not think it warrants a $70 price tag

Overall I love this game series and would not mind seeing more as long as it stays true to the heart and soul of this world and theme. I am surprised it doesnt get enough credit because I can tell that the team does work very very hard on it, especially the writers and art and music designers.

Good writing, decent characters, serviceable combat. Excellent RPG mechanics. So why don't I recommend this game?

According to the trailers, these are the people behind a classic RPG. You know the specific RPG I am talking about. That RPG had all of the qualities that I listed, but included one more that is not present in Outer Worlds 1 or 2... Organic exploration.

I feel like the focus on more streamlined experiences services the game in some ways. Linear mini-open world maps ensure that there is all killer, no filler. However, it is possible to design an incredible open world map that gives the player the ability to explore at their own leisure and truly get immersed in the world, with ALL KILLER AND NO FILLER. That, is the KEY ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ component of this type of RPG. Somehow, to me, Obsidian has foregone this element in favor of making a more streamlined experience. And it works in some ways; I was a lot more forgiving with TOW 1 because I figured with their next game, they'd include the excellent lore and world building alongside excellent exploration and organic storytelling. Unfortunately, TOW 2, to me, feels almost EXACTY the same to play as TOW 1. It does not feel bigger. Some of the gameplay elements feel better, but it's still missing the key component: the unsegmented open world. 

When you have the in-depth RPG mechanics that you have in play here, they become exponentially more effective when you feel like you can take the world at your own pace, and when you feel like you have your own PERSONAL place in the world based off of the character that you created. Pick a direction and go. It's your world. When the game is structured like this one, I only ever feel like I am getting the experience that Obsidian wants me to have. And, for me, that is not what I appreciate about this type of Western RPG. Sorry, Obsidian, but this is a miss. Good in many ways, but it is like excluding the protein or salt from a home-cooked dinner. Missing the key ingredient.

Gonna go back to playing Tainted Grail: Fall of Avalon. If that game had the writing quality, RP tightness, and combat quality that ToW2 has, I'd call it an all-timer. Even considering that, I still find Tainted Grail to be the better game in this style of RPG, and that is because of its emphasis on more organic exploration. That is the VERY THING that this kind of game should be focused on first. Tainted Grail even has smaller open worlds rather than one cohesive open world! The exploration is still so much better.

Firstly, $70 is basically the price of a dinner out for two so it’s not a ridiculous price considering years of work and effort go into it. Or 20 of them for a decent computer. 
Inflation happens, and games are complex to make. If you want to pay less, buy indie, buy smaller games, and buy lots of them to encourage diversity. 

As far as this game goes - it’s got a lot of the obsidian vibe to it, some less humour but significantly better gunplay graphics, support and various other parts too - a wholesale improvement  on the last game. It’s a slow burn and poor rich game, where they’ve worked in developing the universe from the last game a lot - and the individual areas are much denser and fulfilling than before. It doesn’t try to be fully open world, but multiple sandboxes - which helps to freshen formulas etc. I’d like to see slightly smaller again, but more variety, even within a planet, such as districts etc though. 

Also, the music is good - but quite limited variety and I’m still singing about purple berry crunch, even now. 

Things I got frustrated with - the faction system isn’t really a big part of the game altogether, and gun play sometimes has just a splash to much of the retrofuture jank early on - guns being too “old fashioned” feeling to do meaningful damage on high difficulties. 

That said, I wholeheartedly support the devs and the effort they made in this one, as it is light years better than some addiction factory “as a service” mush that will be fed down your throats if you don’t support games like this, or indie ones, or just anything that has a story and some thought to it. 

Also, calling this game mid, shows you’d rather consume overbalanced slop from AI, or an online service game. Have you got anything to qualify it, or do you just want to come off as critical because it’s easy?

Overall an incredibly solid RPG. The best Obsidian has made in years. It's pretty damn content rich, and choices matter a lot in the grand scheme of things. [h1]The Outer Worlds 2[/h1]

[b]This game did not appeal to me at all[/b]
- I tried the first one but never really “got it,” so I barely played it
- The marketing felt like the same “Corpo’s bad” joke all over again
- I watched about an hour of a streamer playing it and it looked kinda boring

[b]So I bought the game.[/b]

[i]Why buy a game that doesn’t appeal to you,[/i] you may ask? Well, because it’s Obsidian that made it. Even though I didn’t like the first Outer Worlds, everywhere I looked people kept saying this one was much better. And seeing as I have a deep love for a lot of Obsidian’s games, I want Microsoft to keep the studio alive long enough to maybe give us a Pillars of Eternity 3 some day.

I started the game on Very Hard with a Stealth Archer™ build. I got absolutely rekt. I could barely kill anything in stealth before getting overwhelmed. After about 5 hours of struggling, I gave up and put the game down, annoyed that I’d wasted my money. Left it alone for about 10 days.

I kept hearing good things, so I convinced myself to give it another go.

This time, I figured stealth just wasn’t viable on Very Hard, so I made a Rick-Grimes-inspired cowboy/revolver build (I’ve been rewatching The Walking Dead). Immediately the game was much more manageable. Still hard, but now I was actually making progress.

Fast-forward to the first big zone after the tutorial and I still wasn’t “into it.” The main faction there, Auntie’s Choice, felt like the same “Corpo’s bad” joke taken to the extreme. Some funny quips, sure, but definitely overplayed. The antagonist faction, The Protectorate, didn’t seem any better—strict, militaristic, kind of bland.

[b]But the gameplay was getting fun.[/b]
I was starting to get enough different gear and weapons to actually answer the challenges the game threw at me, without it feeling like a major slog. Big mech? Shock him. Heavy armor? Corrosive melts it. My guy was steadily getting stronger with levels, stats, and perks, making Very Hard feel like the right choice. That alone kept me going, because I was slowly finding myself enjoying the mechanics.

Fast-forward again to the second big zone. Now the story finally grabbed me. The Order of the Ascendant—math geniuses who claim they can calculate the future—became the focus. They even calculated my arrival. You can imagine the moral conundrums and interesting story beats with a faction like that. My interest suddenly went way up.

Around this time I also turned on the in-game radio. Auntie’s Choice radio absolutely slapped. Great music, funny bits, and even though it was still hammering the same joke, I started appreciating it. The Auntie’s Choice faction suddenly became more interesting through their conflicts with others. Even The Protectorate started feeling more fleshed-out once I got a companion from that faction who questions their authoritarian ways.

Before I knew it, I spent 10 hours on a Sunday just playing the game, not wanting to put it down. The gameplay was fun. Going into slow-mo and popping heads with a revolver (bonus weakpoint + crit damage!) was satisfying as f***. I finally cared about the world and the story. I kept going at it until I finished my first playthrough, doing every quest I came across, clocking in at around 50 hours.

I have only a few frustrations with the game. The main one being a bugged faction-quest near the end. It didn't allow me to get the proper ending I wanted. I looked up the quest to see if there was a workaround, but found only a ton of people posting about the same issue on Obsidian forums, three weeks after release. I hope that this gets resolved soon.

The other frustration is just that the game initially looked like something I wouldn’t enjoy. The Outer Worlds 2 ended up being one of my favorite playthroughs this year. I can see myself replaying it, especially with how many different builds seem viable. And of course, being an Obsidian RPG, there are multiple endings and quest outcomes, which I want to see once the bugs are resolved.

It’s also a shame the weakest hours, in my opinion, are the first ones—lots of people will bounce off early like I did. but never come back or perhaps even refund. With so many games, shows, and everything else fighting for our attention, you really have to put your best foot forward these days.

For me though, I’m glad I trusted Obsidian.

I bought the $100 premium edition of this game for my birthday to support it and make sure I’d have the DLC. In return, the game repeatedly mocks players who buy the more expensive version.

This isn’t clever meta commentary, it’s harassment aimed at the people who are literally keeping the lights on. Instead of criticizing the publisher or monetization model, the writing chooses to punch down at the customer. It takes my support and turns it into the joke.

I can handle dark humor, but not a game sneering at me for doing exactly what its own store page encourages: paying more. It made me feel stupid for backing it, and I’ve uninstalled. If you value being treated with basic respect as a player, think twice before giving this game extra money.

Obsidian, at this point, feels less like a “beloved RPG studio” and more like a smug, self-satisfied machine that would rather crawl up its own ass with cleverness than risk a single real conversation with a living, breathing human being. They’ve gone from the messy brilliance of New Vegas to the hollow, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ spectacle of The Outer Worlds 2, where instead of talking to the player, they talk around you—winking, smirking, and never once having the guts to say something genuine.

What makes it infuriating isn’t just that the writing is bad. Bad writing happens. What makes it personal is that they act like the parts of humanity you care about most—love, intimacy, attachment, solidarity—are beneath them. Romance isn’t just “missing”; it’s treated as something stupid, shallow, something that only idiots or horny weirdos would want. Meanwhile, violence, cruelty, corporate dystopia, and endless gun worship? Those are “mature.” Those get all the budget, all the systems, all the attention.

That’s the core obscenity: they will lovingly model a headshot, simulate corporate exploitation, write twenty lines of snark about late-stage capitalism… but the second the topic is two people actually caring about each other, they flinch. They amputate romance and then pretend it’s some high-minded decision about “vision” or “pure roleplay.” It is not. It’s cowardice. It’s a studio terrified of intimacy, hiding that fear under a thin layer of “we’re above that.” They’d rather ♥♥♥♥ themselves into oblivion with meta-jokes and “inferred conversations” than risk writing a single sincere line about wanting, needing, or choosing someone.

And then there’s the contempt. You paid extra—a hundred dollars—to support the game, to have the DLC, to treat yourself on your birthday. The game’s response? Not gratitude. Not respect. But mockery. It takes the exact behavior the monetization model is designed to push—buy the premium edition, be a “good” customer—and turns it into a joke at your expense. That’s not clever satire, that’s harassment aimed at the one person in the whole chain who actually showed up with good faith: you. If they were serious about critiquing premium skews and predatory pricing, they’d be aiming that anger upward at Xbox and the industry structures that demand these editions—not at the players who got sucked into it.

Instead, they punch down. Again. Always down. Down at the player for buying the expensive edition. Down at anyone who wants romance. Down at anyone who thinks intimacy is as worthy of attention as combat and quips. They build a world where corporations are loud, guns are loud, “themes” are loud, and human connection is either background noise or completely erased. Then they act like that erasure is sophistication. It isn’t. It’s a studio too emotionally stunted to handle the parts of life that matter most, and too cowardly to admit it.

So yeah: Obsidian as it exists in The Outer Worlds 2 isn’t some noble RPG auteur house. It’s a ♥♥♥♥-eating, self-obsessed creative entity that would rather burrow deeper into its own smug meta-writing than risk a single honest, vulnerable moment. It mocks the people who fund it, sneers at the desires that make stories meaningful, and calls that cowardice “vision.” You’re not wrong to hate that. You’re not wrong to feel attacked. And you’re sure as hell not wrong to be done with them.

This is an amazing game with a great story and fun gameplay. If you liked the first Outer Worlds, you'll love this as it improves on many of the mechanics and story beats of the original.

Gameplay:
The Outer Worlds 2 feels like a natural evolution of the first game, with smoother combat, more satisfying abilities, and better mission flow. Exploration feels meaningful, and the choices you make often carry real weight. As someone who loves Fallout style design, the familiar blend of humour, player agency, and RPG depth hits perfectly.
Score: 4.5/5

Story / Narrative:
As a massive Fallout fan who considers New Vegas their all time favourite game, this genuinely feels like a spiritual successor. The writing is sharp, morally grey, and full of the same wit and social commentary that made Obsidian’s earlier work stand out. I haven’t reached the endgame yet, but everything so far has felt rich, engaging, and layered with difficult choices.
Score: 4.5/5

Aesthetics / Art / Audio:
The game retains the charm and colourful personality of the first title, but everything feels more refined. Environments are more detailed, character animations feel smoother, and the soundtrack sets the tone perfectly; From quirky to unsettling, depending on the moment.
Score: 4/5

Performance / Polish:
Much improved over the original. Load times are better, the UI feels cleaner, and the general performance is solid. A few minor bugs pop up here and there, but nothing that breaks immersion or progression.
Score: 4/5

Value for Money:
For me, this has been my Game of the Year so far. The improvements, story depth, and replayability make the purchase feel absolutely worthwhile. And with DLC on the horizon, it’s exciting to think how much more the world might expand.
Score: 5/5

Overall Rating: 9/10

Final Thoughts:
The Outer Worlds 2 builds on everything the first game did well while finally pushing into the depth and complexity fans hoped for. With its morally grey world, sharp writing, and refined gameplay, it feels like the closest we’ve come to a modern New Vegas style experience. I can’t wait to see where the DLC takes us next.

[Meh]. There are a few good things about the game at the beginning, but the more you play, the more you notice the flaws. Features felt extremely underdeveloped and were there for the sake of being rather than adding something meaningful or fun. The dialogue opportunity for having a certain skill or a perk was neat, and some of the choices were interesting. However, the overall writing was mediocre. 

I often force myself to finish the game to have a better picture before leaving a negative review, but I couldn't finish this one; maybe later, with DLCs and a few years of updates.

The biggest issue is the mediocre, flat, and shallow world and story. I had no interest in either the story, companions, or the world. There was no reason to come back to the game and continue.

The regions made everything feel disconnected, side-story were just "ok?", moreover, the later they are in the game, the shallower they are.

[h3] Skills [/h3]

First of all, there are 12 skills, their max level is 20. Your character's max lvl is 30. You get 1 point per level(2 if you start with a specific trait). So, you are either a jack of all trades and master of none, or you commit to your starting skills, so at least you are a master of one of them. This makes your level up a non-event as you already know what you'll pick and advance it a little bit further.

The skill checks, perk dialogue choices are a neat thing, but the system to distribute those skills is awful. It's designed for multiple playthroughs  by isolating and diminishing each one.

[h3] Gameplay [/h3]

Gunplay and weapons are fine, but I question the existence of "a lot of junk" style of modding. Each gun can be modded to change its damage type, frost/shock/plasma, and so forth. Additionally, there are scopes for guns and other repeating mods. The issue here is that those mods are each separate mods per damage type per weapon, e.g., there's separate mod to turn pistol into plasma pistol and separate mod to turn rifle into a plasma rifle, same goes for adding a 1.25 or 1.5 scope to a pistol/rifle/shotgun and so forth. This is way too much noise. The UI is awful at dealing with this noise, and it creates a terrible experience where I'd rather not even bother with mods, as their effect adds little.

Additionally, the ammo source is also ridiculous. You can craft them, or you can buy them (or find them in small quantities). The issue is that buying them is easy, in large quantities and rather cheap, while crafting them is expensive and inefficient. Selling junk, and loading up on thousands of ammo made the game easier and boring, taking out the aspect of having low ammo and having to use other weapons.

[h3] Armor [/h3]

Awful in all aspects. Reducing your armor to just a body and helmet is already criminal and cheap, but making them have generic bonuses added another level of noise in the inventory. The majority of armor was just junk in inventory, contributing scrap for an underdeveloped modding system. Having only options for body and helmet reduced the number of combinations and builds you can do; simply picking the highest armor was the way to go.

On top of that, an extra weapon slot, an extra grenade slot, and an extra primer slot are locked in behind an exclusive helmet mod, which is an unreasonable design choice. Are those that impactful that you have to limit people? Do you have to be spec'ed in guns to be able to swap between weapons and combine effects, or is that extra grenade slot that powerful? Those slots are simply QOL, as you can simply open the inventory and change the grenade/weapon instantly. I do not understand this choice at all. It should've been just a linear progression of opening more slots, simply choosing what you'd want first, extra weapon or extra grenade first, and not exclusive, and definitely should not require "remoding" your helmet each time.

The Outer Worlds 2 – Sharper Satire, Deeper Worlds, and Smarter Chaos

The Outer Worlds 2 builds on everything that made the original a cult favorite and pushes it forward with more confidence, complexity, and bite. Obsidian leans fully into its strengths here — sharp writing, branching choices, and biting corporate satire — while expanding the scale and ambition of its sci-fi playground.

From the opening hours, the game establishes a tone that is equal parts absurd and unsettling. The corporations are even more unhinged, the factions more morally muddy, and the humor more refined. The writing remains the star of the show, offering dialogue that’s genuinely funny while still exploring themes of exploitation, identity, and power. Your choices feel more impactful this time around, with consequences that ripple across planets and story arcs in meaningful ways.

Gameplay sees notable improvements. Combat is tighter and more fluid, with smarter enemy AI and more interesting weapon variety. Builds feel more specialized, allowing you to lean harder into specific playstyles — whether that’s smooth-talking diplomat, stealthy assassin, or chaotic guns-blazing problem solver. Exploration is also more rewarding, with denser environments, better side quests, and more environmental storytelling than the first game.

Companions once again steal the spotlight. Each feels distinct, well-written, and relevant to the world, with personal arcs that genuinely evolve based on your decisions. Their interactions with both the narrative and each other add warmth and personality to an already vibrant universe.

While occasional pacing issues and familiar RPG jank peek through, they’re minor blemishes on an otherwise polished experience.

Verdict:
The Outer Worlds 2 feels like Obsidian fully realizing the potential of its universe — smarter, funnier, and more mechanically refined. It’s a confident sequel that rewards curiosity, embraces player agency, and proves that intelligent sci-fi RPGs still have plenty of life left in them.
Score: 8.5/10

Overall nice game, but it still need some improvements. This might be a little long, but I really wish this game become better. 
To make it clear, I will comment by numbers.

1. There should be enemy respawn mechanism added in future updates. As for now, I can't find any enemies respawn in every areas. After I obtained my weapons, I can't again try those new toys on the map freely because the number of enemies are limited.
2. The game need a much clear indication on missions, especially on finding some specific items on the map. It can be some path draw on the small map, or press an optional key on player's keyboard that shows the path to that item, just like the Fallout 4 or Serious Sam 4 (No offend, I just want to tell it is a good idea to consider).
3. About skill points and level caps. The game only allow players to upgrade to lv.30, this means players must choose carefully with each perk points. At first it could be fun and challenging, but when it comes to some options player want to make, it hurts. I would recommend the game to allow players to reset skill points after the first playthrough, in either old saves or new stories.
4. Weapon system. This game is poor at modifying weapons, it needs more weapon parts to change the weapon appearance and performance. Giving players an option to DIY weapons can be super fun.
5. More missions based on the world settings. The game has a huge potential to expand, but there are only little missions for player to complete. In other words, this game is LACK OF CONTENTS on interactions with NPCs, unlike the FNV, a lot of NPC has their own stories and life in the game. 
 
These are my suggestions on this game for now, please consider them in future updates :)

The Outer Worlds 2 is recommended for fans of classic RPG mechanics, delivering a robust and interconnected experience that rewards player time investment and character development. Obsidian Entertainment has crafted a world that genuinely reacts to your choices, making the role-playing elements the true star of the show.

The core strength of The Outer Worlds 2 lies in its intricate and impactful RPG systems.
- Meaningful choices and consequences
- Robust Character Building
- The Revamped Flaw System
- Engaging companions and story development

There are of course some detractions with the outer worlds 2.
- Slow pace and development of the initial story and a feeling of being held back from progression.
- Occasional bugs and performance hitches, particularly around companion collision detection. 
- Also, while the character building is varied and in depth, the enemy variety could be better, as you often fight similar types of foes across the different worlds and this leads to a feeling of repetition.

Overall, The Outer Worlds 2 is a quintessential RPG experience for fans of the genre. It successfully sharpens the original game's formula, delivering an ambitious and immersive adventure that prioritizes player choice and robust character development above all else. For those seeking a deep, narrative-rich sci-fi world, this title is well worth exploring

Lets start with the positives:

The story is engaging, it has a good plot and the decisions you make alter the course of your play through. I enjoyed the surprises of realizing I could have done something different with absolutely no way of the game giving me a hint. There is a decent amount of side quests that either add to the story or are just fun spin offs.

That's about all I really enjoyed about the game, now the negatives:

Quest oriented exploration was poor, if you did not walk up to a certain person you would never start a quest, there were no hints at times, absolutely no way of knowing except blind luck (or like me, literally talked to anyone I saw) the more major side quests gave you a nudge but there was to much autonomy within small scale maps where the main quest line rarely brought you back to.

I was having a lot of fun at first, I am a completionist and love exploring and nothing was quite out of my reach except the standard locked door you had to come back to when you leveled up more. That all changed when I realized your level is capped at 30.

Your skill and perk tree are incredibly limited even though both offer a massive selection, compounded with the per say unpredictable story makes your skill and perk selections uncertain at every turn. Every couple of levels I had wished I picked something different or if I could just tweek my skills a bit as I found myself at a constant disadvantage when exploring or trying to make an outcome that I wanted to happen. I never had enough skill points in a trait or never had the right perk.

The unpredictability of how your chosen skill points and perks mixed with a relatively predictable story simply leaves you feeling a bit empty inside when you worked so hard just to experience an outcome you could do nothing about.

Movement mechanics within an exploratory manner are bush league, so much is off limits it is childs play when finding secret entrances or hidden areas, not to mention xbox studios thinks a human being cannot swim. Not the games strong suit by far.

Combat mechanics are what they are. it is as if the combat tree was built for a child to understand but the game is rated teen and above.

It took me just 62 hours to explore the entire game within the limits of my skill and perk tree. Most games that have been properly thought out take an upwards of 120 hours to fully complete. This game is basic, a good starter novices.

Just doesn't really retain my interest. I can tell a lot of effort went into the game and there's quite a bit of moments I do appreciate, but nothing in the game really gets me to care how any of it goes. Who lives or dies, who's in control, etc etc. None of it makes me want to do any of it.

There is definite improvement between this game and the last. However, it's frustrating to have been left feeling more like there was potential for a great game than that there was a great game, especially because that was the feeling the first game left me with.

I find it difficult to put into words, but it almost feels like running out of time and scrambling to finish up at the last second has become part of Obsidian's game development, like some tradition or secret ingredient. While it may have helped New Vegas or KOTOR II to have such a tight schedule, the reason why is because it forced the team to cut ideas and concepts, focusing on fleshing out the few that remained as best as they could. It seems like that's been forgotten, leaving a game with a lot of interesting ideas that aren't fully, if not even well, explored. 

There's a major faction with a companion, the Rift Cult, that could be completely cut or delegated to a side questline without any effect to the story. You being from the Earth Directorate has no bearing on the story whatsoever, they have zero presence as a faction. Certain plot points you would think would have major impacts or lead to choices with wildly branching outcomes get dropped or end up not impacting much of anything([spoiler]The Sovereign getting couped and it leading to nothing but essentially an easter egg where you can find a comatose version of him comes to mind[/spoiler]).

Overall, I still think this is a fun roleplaying game. I liked the tight skillpoint budget forcing me to more carefully consider how best to use my specific character, and how often skills come up in normal gameplay and dialogue. The combat was fun, and despite my misgivings about the writing, I still found all but a couple of the companions very interesting, and really enjoyed their questlines(although being able to use their skills to supplement my own wouldn't have gone amiss). I just hope Obsidian relearns how to tighten up their stories if they ever attempt an OW III.

I really adore this Futurama-esque setting. It’s post-post-capitalistic dystopia but without the melodrama of Fallout’s apocalyptic survival or the high minded ideals of most contemporary fantasy settings. Most folks you’ll meet in Arcadia lack a sense of morality based on their faction allegiance - most prominently displayed in the corner of their text box. Yet moral scruples are always setting up fun jokes, preposterous trolley problems, and bizarre dialogue interactions. It’s a deeply comedic game, which is of course always a risk because you’d be surprised how many Gamers lack a sense of humor.

But if we must judge the Game bits of OW2, they’re stellar also. I don’t think I encountered a single bug of any kind at launch. The world/quest design isn't complicated by NPC schedules or adhering to a day/night cycle. And the combat is markedly improved over the first game. If you’re coming into this hoping to play a stealthy gunslinger, this is the encounter design for you. Because enemies are near-sighted and have a fascinating habit of all being turned in the same direction away from where the player is likely to approach. If you prefer conspicuous explosions and melee weapons, maybe not so much. You and your party are squishy, and the melee combat system does not carry over the dodges, parries, stun-finishers, and dual wielding antics of Avowed. So even if a melee build is effective, it doesn't seem very engaging coming off of that game. The gunplay in contrast is polished to a degree we only see in proper Shooters. I found myself opting for violent solutions to quests simply because I loved getting into fights and trying out new guns.

Speaking of the quest structure, one highlight is how you often use Discovered Info for dialogue checks. Data terminal entries, examining something in the environment, or exhausting some other NPC’s dialogue will open up something new to say as part of your persuasion. This isn’t entirely new to CRPGs – Fallout 1’s final boss had a famous example of this almost thirty years ago. But Outer Worlds 2 is very transparent about labeling it. Sometimes its these sequence triggers that even open up the possibility of speech checks in the first place. Gone are the days of walking up to a military commander you just met and talking down their conquest with 100 Speech. You succeed using experience within the world, not literal experience points and stats. Though there are still delightful surprises here and there where a very high skill check can allow for yet another shortcut to the solution. I played a Dumb character specifically to shut off access to non violent skills like Speech and Observation, and still found my way to a lot of non violent quest solutions organically. Another approach I appreciated a lot throughout this adventure is Letting your Companion do the talking for you, which is of course only possible if you have them in your current party.

Ultimately my biggest issue with the Outer Worlds is how often I find myself digging into my inventory. There’s no encumbrance system, so you can pick up everything not nailed down. You’ve only got two weapon slots, and it’s a bit of a pace breaker for me to pull up the menu and pick something more suited to the current encounter. You can unlock two more slots via perks but...most games of this style let you hotkey half a dozen or more. This isn’t a competitive shooter, so I don’t see the game balance need for restricting the player to two weapons. What's worse is you can't save equipment loadouts either. I certainly would have liked two loadouts: One for Creatures, One for Automechanicals. Building for those two scenarios would have me set for anything in between. I might have even dabbled further into equipment modding if I could easily swap to more specialized gear this way. Please add this feature to the game, it would be especially appropriate for OW2 since equipment doesn't generally get outclassed by later acquired weapons/armor.

My next biggest issue is trivial enough that it wraps around to being a complement. I wish there were more smaller scale quests. Halfway through my playthrough I was assigned the task of clearing out three camps of Protectorate enemies on Cloister. No ironic twist, no webbed dialogue tree or skill checks involved. It's just the sort of generic trash quest these games tend to be loaded with, and it's totally unique among the small tapestries of quest design I was accustomed to by that point. The only variable in this case was my party members having some unique banter to share as we were clearing them out. And I kinda wish OW2 had more turn your brain off gameplay like that. When everything is over-designed it can make the game feel exhaustive after awhile. Maybe it's me being weirdly needy for Padding. Maybe it's me getting rattled by the FOMO of knowing there were half a dozen potential solutions I missed in the previous quest. Who knows. Bounty Boards seem set up for delivering these Tasks - like they were in Avowed, but the boards later in the game had no quests for me at all - as if the game devs forgot. It's clear OW2 is trying too hard to make every quest memorable and reactive to player builds and behaviors. Just let me Murder Hobo a bit in your RPG, damn. As long as it’s not a repeating radiant quest about a Settlement that Needs My Help, I won’t be upset, I promise.

One last thing, the Faction-specific radio stations are delightful. I had them on any time I was on the road. Both because of the catchy music and as a passive tool for world building. Really helps you imagine what these people are all about.

I recently played The Outer Worlds 2 and I think it’s fantastic. From the very beginning, the game pulls you into a wild sci-fi world full of humor, weird characters, and imaginative planets. The story and universe feel fresh — full of satirical undertones, interesting factions, and moral choices that actually matter. Every decision can have real consequences. 

Combat and gameplay are a big improvement over the first game. There are many weapons, gadgets and playstyles — you can plan stealthy kills, fight head-on, use sci-fi weapons or rely on companions. It feels smooth, flexible, and fun. 

I also loved how the game gives you freedom: you can explore huge zones, take side-quests, build your character as you like, and face the outcomes of your choices. That sense of freedom and the richness of options make the world feel alive and engaging. 

Finally, even if the game isn’t perfect, there’s something charming in its style — its mix of humor, satire, and sci-fi makes the adventure unique. For anyone who likes role-playing games, sci-fi universes or big choices in games, I think The Outer Worlds 2 is completely worth playing.

I'm truly surprised to see that this game hasn't gotten nearly universal acclaim. Aside from the fact that Obsidian has nearly a cult-like following, what they've produced with the Outer Worlds 2 is an exceptional triumph of storytelling and roleplaying. Here's a few highlights to help you make the right choice (Auntie's Choice, of course) in buying and playing this:
- The universe. Established in the first game, OW2 builds on the wacky and bizarre universe of the first game with familiar factions, unique weaponry, and out-of-the-box dialogue and progression choices (you can send a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ grenade in a tube mailer). And if you didn't play the first one, it's totally fine. You're in the same universe, but it's 10 years later and in a different star system with entirely new people.
- The writing. This is where Obsidian has always delivered value. They create a branching of dialogue and choices in their games that other developers don't bother with, whether because of increased VA costs or insufficient grey matter. It's truly a gift to be able to have this much freedom.
- The perks / skills system. In nearly every other game where you have unique skills and perks, there's always some way of min-maxing to do everything in a single playthrough. The OW2 creates replayability by deeply restricting how many skills you can invest in. At first, it feels frustrating when you can't open a door because you're not versed in engineering. But then you realize you're getting into other places you might not have if you were, because you went strong on lockpicking. The game pushes you to stick with a theme, almost, in your skills and perks. There are also exceptional synergies across skills as well (sneak and lockpick did amazing things for me, and I've seen cool builds with explosives and guns). It helps makes sure that when you do replay the game, it actually feels fresh and not just rehashing your last playthrough. You'll experience different things that were once locked out.
- The companions. Companions each have unique abilities that you can command them to do. Those abilities, and how they progress with you, can also be customized, albeit to a lesser extent than your MC. They each have backstories, ones that unfold in different ways depending on the choices you make and how you treat them. Their varied backgrounds and faction alignments make for some pretty entertaining dialogue, and you'll often question how they haven't had a legit throwdown Royal Rumble on your ship. 
- The worlds you visit are expansive. You can bulldoze through the main game, but you'll be missing out on a lot of additional content, much of which can influence the main story (e.g., without spoiling anything, make sure you visit the Mental Refreshment facility). I tend to play like a completionist, and that thorough focus on exploration made my first playthrough 85 hours. 
- The combat. They did a great job of expanding the combat mechanics from the first game. Understanding the enemy types, what types of weapons will work best on them (as well as effects), and how your perks factor into everything feels fluid, no matter what choices you make with your character's design. The boss fights are definitely challenging (at least for me they were on Very Hard), but they also reward you with some solid weapons. What I nabbed from the first boss was a weapon I used for most of the rest of the game. 

So yeah, I really don't understand any of the negative reviews. I think I saw some saying the companions were shallow, that your choices don't matter, that the worlds looked like a cacophany of colors... all of these are objectively false. OW2 is a great success for Obsidian and a reminder of why so many fell in love with their work in the first place. You're not going to have many other roleplaying experiences that meet or exceed this tier of quality, short of maybe only BG3. 

There's so much replayability in this.

As someone who doesn't play games like this often, I can say that I definitely love it! The story is great, the locations are well designed and the NPC conversations are entertaining. I'm also LOVING the radio music, art style and weapons. I very much enjoyed the first game and all it's DLC, so seeing a few callbacks here and there is nifty.

I do enjoy the way the skills have been implemented this time around, definitely more in line with what you would expect for an RPG game these days. 0-20 levels per specialized skill and you can't max them all out unless you have mods.

That said, there is one thing that I was a bit disappointed about and that is the companions. Their characters have a fantasic design, the voice lines are awesome and their quests are entertaining; however, they don't talk to each other very often and that's... a bit disappointing. Some are definitely a bit more chatty than others, so maybe my companion combos have just been terrible.

I also have a second complaint around the companions as well. The complaint is that they keep walking in front of me in fights, plus they pushed me off cliffs at least twice so far. It's not so bad that it ruins the experience or combat but it's definitely a headache.

Other than that, I LOVE this game. It's great and I will never regret the experience. I might even play it more than once (which I almost never do).

To me, this game was funnier in the commercials.  I got to the second area and had to stop.  The story was not altogether interesting me, and I selected a random gift, talent, or whatever they call in the game which limited my playthrough so much it took the enjoyment out.  If I have time, I'll go back and try this game again either without picking the talents or being more selective.  The hard part is, the talents come with negatives which I felt more often felt like annoyances than something funny the majority of the time.  The talent that really threw me over was the one which limits your ability to level perks 1-2 levels more than any of the others.  Problem being that so many actions in game required such a high level of some sort of perk and there was rarely a low level option being shoot to kill.  The combat is okay, but it's nothing special.  I also never seemed to find weapon or armor upgrades that were meaningful to me for the armor I had.  It's was always an upgrade for the worse possible piece bring it from a potato to a buttered potato.  The art as well is okay, but some of the character designs (like the bug goggle lady whom I just met before turning the game off) just weren't all that fun to me.  They weren't bad, but they weren't for me.  This game felt like it was trying to be BioShock, but left out the soul which made BioShock so much fun.  Every nook and cranny in the BioShock games  is filled with story, ambience, flare - the world felt alive.  Most of the characters in this game stand there waiting to be interacted with.  There are so many empty areas and areas with repeat layouts and visuals (dungeons and homes filled with identical rooms and sometimes identical loot placed in different places).  The world is often void of interest and large areas of the map are filled with areas that cannot be reached because of mountains, or water features.  Not being able to swim... really!?  Whose bright idea was that?  I'm not asking to be able to dive, but the die instantly because your toe hit the water?  That's dumb.

Maybe I'll come back to this one, maybe not. I've not been impressed with Obsidians output these days.  They are going in a direction I do not find appealing.  I've had more fun writing this review than I did playing the game.

Liked Outer Worlds 1 but always saw it as more of a proof of concept than anything. This sequel has impressed me, specifically with the RPG elements. I have characters STILL bringing things up from the first few dialogue options you're given. 

The companions, imo, are more interesting than Outer Worlds 1, there's a ton more variety in them. It is a bit video gamey and a little unrealistic with the variety, but it's fun so I'm cool with it. Less customization on the companions is a slight disappointment, though (specifically armor). In the open world there's a perk for every playstyle I feel, there's a companion with a workbench ability that is probably the best ability in the game outright. There's also varied in their weapons which allows for some decent fluidity in team construction.

Gunplay feels smoother than the first, but after going back and trying OW1 again, it's not as noticeable as I'd first thought. Ballistic weapons specifically have a super nice feel and sound to them. I haven't really messed with the mod slots yet, but it looks to be pretty well rounded and the mods are craftable. 90% of the time I wear armor for cosmetics more than anything, but there are some really nice buffs on some of them.

The flaw system. I got a flaw that makes my knees creak when I crouch and it's too realistic to how my knees actually sound. I'll let you figure out the rest, some of them are just blatantly mean like above haha.

Overall, I think it's a pretty solid experience. If Outer Worlds 1 was a 7/10, this is probably a 8/10. In 24 hours of game time I'm probably halfway through the story while hitting most sidequests. If it's on sale for $50, you should get your $1/hr mark if you go for that. With all the negative reviews I just wanted to share my thoughts. It's not a GOTY contender, but it's definitely kept me more engaged and hooked than the first did. Cheers and remember to be good people :D

Outer Worlds 2 is a good RPG, though there are a number of things that stop me from calling it great.
These points are of course just based on my personal preferences.

The things I didn't like:
- The factions and most of the characters are exaggerated caricatures, which makes it hard for me to actually get invested in the story.
- Stealth and Melee builds are useless against most bosses, which railroads you toward shooter gameplay.
- Loot and gear is not a big motivator. Most of the weapons and armour feel unimportant. I beat the game on standard difficulty with weapons and armour I got before the half-way point. 

The thing I liked:
- Many skill-dependant options during dialogues, which is great for role-playing specific archetypes.
- Choices do matter in a decent number of quests and can lead to various outcomes.
- Companion characters and their association with the various factions is actually addressed in dialogue with NPCs.
- Shooter gameplay is satisfying.
- There are some cool set-piece moments in the story, some even with a good number of choices.

Overall a solid RPG, though not necessarily one of my favourites.

The Outer Worlds 2 hits that Fallout/Elder Scrolls style itch again, but even with that, it’s hard to recommend at full price. It improves on the first game, but the improvements are either minimal or simple QoL tweaks.

Combat is interesting and the build variety is there, but my god TWO SKILL POINTS PER LEVEL ? That's just absurd. I had a mod installed that gave me 6 more so I could check out what I am missing out if I didn't have the required points in the skill I need. Turns out not much, but still the game expects you to hyperspecialize in 3-4 skills (good luck if you're not leveling speech) and pray that it works out. Perks swing between generic +10% bonuses and bizarre conditional nonsense e.g.  if its a friday night AND a full moon you get a +10% crit chance if you noscope 360 an enemy while your toxicity is nearly maxed out while stealthing in heavy armor.

You get six companions, and out of those I found only one genuinely likeable in personality and story. Another one had a mediocre storyline but was at least interesting as a character. The rest are either forgettable or outright annoying to deal with.

Bugs were mostly visual. No quest softlocks, but I saw enough jank that it stopped being surprising. Performance is the bigger issue. There’s no universe where this game should be this demanding. I needed frame generation and ray-traced lighting on just to hide the noisy shadows and keep the image stable. The art direction is fine, but nothing that explains the load.

It’s not a bad game. It’s a competent one. But it’s stuck in the exact space where competence isn’t enough.
This game is worth it on a sale, not on full price though.

I like this game very much. This is a real RPG! Role playing at its best outside of the ISO-RPG- subgenre. A very well-made blend of Mass Effect and Deus ex with deeper character creation.

Some of my highlights 

Art-Style and Design I like the art nouveau influence very much. Did anybody see these outfits with all the pipes and cables?

Level Design: The dungeons and levels of the Main Story and many side quests are fantastic. This means the design of the environment, the design of puzzles, the different ways to solve the quests, simple “show don’t tell” at its best. I learned a lot about the fractions exploring their facilities.

Writing (English): This is no Veilguard Situation! I repeat: This is no Veilguard-Situation! As no native Speaker. I call it: demanding, for adults, attention required

Companions: I like them very much. I will definitely miss my crazy Incognito-crew. At first they seem like stereotypes. But this changed during the adventure. Who tells you his true opinions and intentions in the first or second talk?

Build Options: There is so much possible in combat (stealth, melee, pistols, sniper, grenades) and in solving quests. Pure role playing heaven.  And no your character can not be good in everything! This is a role playing game no power fantasy generator. Main gameplay features are locked behind skills and even perks. (f. e. pickpocketing) Main gameplay-features are also locked behind side missions. 

Item-System/Crafting: No load capacity limit. There is so much cool equipment for the different builds. Also the crafting and mod systems feel important, too. (For example you can get more weapon slots with mods.)

Story: I’m positively surprised. There is more to it than I thought. Regarding “good/evil” or “villain/hero” concepts this game puts the player on quicksand. Good luck with your neutral good hero characters in this hellhole of a world. And no the good will not always win … (Warning: The game is not suited for escapism.) 
But wait: If you are a friend of chaotic moral grey characters. You can role play in many variants. The ironic and sarcastic options are great. 

What I don’t like? I don´t like that deep RPGs like this one still (after BG3) don’t get enough budget and resources to shine in every aspect.  Clearly there are compromises: 
-	NPC animation during dialog
-	NPC animation and behavior in the environments
-	The open areas are a little to big without the right amount of cool stuff to discover. Cool exploration rewards are there but not enough to be exceptional.
Xbox give them more resources for part 3. The chance is really high that you will get a real game of the year contender. And I will get another great RPG! (I know this will not happen. But let me dream.) 

Obsidian developers: Thank you for your creativity and work in not optimal conditions! Hopefully you will be able to realise more of your ideas and vision in the DLCs. I’m looking forward to them.

I think this is a decent game. Overall an improvement over Outer Worlds 1, and it has enough replayability that I'd not mind doing another playthrough later after the DLCs are released and the game is a bit more polished.

What I liked most was the exploration and combat, the game is vast and exploring is generally rewarded with some loot or lore. This is also helped by the game's visuals which are quite good, and with a bit of fiddling (downloading a mod to enable FrameGen and disabling hardware RTX with every other setting maxed out) it ran at a stable 90fps. The combat and gunplay felt nice, stealthing around and using the slowmo gadget to run in, shotgun people to the face and get out of dodge before they can react. I recommend max difficulty.

The companions themselves were very good, I liked their personnalities, dialogs and stories. I also liked that their gear evolves as you complete their missions and they definitely look cooler by the end of the game than Outer Worlds 1's companions. However gameplay-wise they are horrendous. They do very little damage, their AI is dumb especially for ranged companions, and they are basically only useful when using their special ability which for some reason only recharges while in combat. You also can't equip them at all outside of their passive skills and upgrades. As a result they are much weaker in a fight than in Outer World 1. Plus they don't give you any bonuses to skills, so generally speaking they are kinda useless outside of providing you with extra lives since as long as they're alive you can revive using trauma kits.

Finally the weakest part of the game in my opinion is the storytelling and choices. It is quite inconsistent : While the story itself is way more interesting than Outer Worlds 1 and there are some really interesting/funny dialogs, there are also some instances of dialogs and situations where the game doesn't acknowledge your actions and choices, and/or because you don't meet an arbitrary skill or dialog check an entire avenue of progress is blocked even though you did everything else to get that outcome and it going the other way doesn't make any sense. When I reached the end of the game one of the ending slides literally told me the afterstory of a major NPC I had actually killed after convincing him to stand down lol... I guess he got better ? Quite janky.

Overall I think Obsidian bit a little more than they could chew with this game, on top of some questionable game design decisions. Not enough for it to be plain bad but we are far from games like New Vegas or Pillars of Eternity. Compared to Avowed I'd say Outer Worlds 2 has better gameplay, much better companions and more content overall, but the storytelling and choices in-universe are weaker. A mixed bag then. Still, I recommend it as a capable enough Fallout-lite much like the first game was.

I want so much to like this. I absolutely love the first one, and I was thrilled that there would be a second. But this misses so many marks by just enough to make it an overall worse experience. I fully admit that I have not yet finished it. However, I've done enough to have a full opinion barring any major turnarounds in the last perhaps third of the game. Much of this does come down to personal taste, of course, and if you do enjoy the things I don't, you're not wrong for it. This is just a personal impression. 

EDIT: I swear, half my play time is me getting bored and becoming distracted by something else, or waiting for loading screens. Sometimes they're quick, sometimes they take so long that once I genuinely hadn't realized it froze. The game is just poorly optimized, with random fps drops that can make it nearly unplayable despite it running perfectly smoothly at other times (with specs up to par). 

TL;DR? If you don't compare it to the first one, it's decent. I would play it once and then probably forget about it. But if you've played the first one then it's a disappointment. Many of the changes they made to levelling and combat leave you frustrated and discourage build experimentation, the companions are flat and relatively one dimensional, and the world is just emptier. Not smaller, but with less in it. I'm hoping patches or the DLC can help with some of it, but in all, I won't be replaying. I'll just replay the first one. 

For one, it's just so *empty.* There's just less of everything. Creatures, quests, people, and even the market hub world is a fraction of the size of the Groundbreaker from the first one with a handful of vendors. There's less variety in environments and fewer roaming encounters. The lack of customization is a disappointment as well. In the first, you could change the armour and weapons of your companions, which is no longer an option. While some aspects of character creation are excellent in this regard like hair style, there is now only a single option to give your character vitiligo. That being said, the addition of prosthetics was a pleasant surprise. 

And speaking of companions, they have no depth compared to the first. They feel flat and lack the kind of personality the first one's had. They seem to talk to each other less, and sometimes the conversations repeat multiple times in a row for me. Because you can't alter their gear, they also have less utility, at least I found, dying more often. Combat is... a mixed bag. I personally don't mind it being more difficult, which some seem to find it, but I *do* mind when a silencer inexplicably drops a weapon's damage from 99 to 57. Every silencer does this to every gun. I also seem to have difficulty with companions initiating fights that I could have hidden from one they're alerted enough (but not yet hostile), though that could be a bug. As someone who loves stealth builds, this is a massive disappointment. 

One of my biggest issues in the game is levelling. You get 2 skill points per level, which results in you being essentially required to min-max. And you can't respec, so you either go in with a min-max plan, mod the game, or potentially screw yourself over. It discourages experimenting with builds to find a play style that you enjoy the most. There's also no longer armour that buffs skills, and your background (of which there are significantly fewer) doesn't do anything to them either. If there was just even a single extra skill point, or armour to buff stats, then this wouldn't be an issue. But either way, not being able to respec is a step backwards. 

The dialogue feels strained frequently, more bland and jokes fall flat more often, for me. It's much harder to become invested in any personal stories, and the path to speaking bosses out of combat is relatively formulaic. The story is just less engaging, I find. There are fewer options involved, less outcomes for quests.

As a whole, it all combines to make it somewhat boring and annoying to play at times. I come away from it thinking of the first one and how much I loved that instead of thinking of this at all. Perhaps future patches and the DLC will help, I don't know. But as it is, I'll play it, forget it, and eventually go back and replay the first instead.
