This game lacks The Sauce.

When I heard that Obsidian had two games coming out in 2025, I felt a bit worried that they might not be able to make two games in one year with the complexity, depth, and Sauce that I'm used to. I'm afraid that (for what I want out of a game at least) I was right. I never really felt hooked in by the plot or the world building. Actually, this was the rare game where I had trouble figuring out what was going in the story between the prologue exploding, the chase for Augustine DeVries, the rifts, whatever was going on in the war between The Protectorate and everybody else, and the apparent mega-merger of steampunk megacorps IN SPACE, I had no idea what was going on for the first half of my time with this game. 
Actually, in that way it kind of reminded me of my first impressions of Fallout: New Vegas from back in the day but unlike that title, I didn't find catharsis here. The whole never really came together for me so I never had much personal buy-in for the big picture. With that lack of personal investment progressing the main story felt more like checking boxes than losing myself in a compelling narrative. For my two bits worth, there were parts that I really enjoyed, some of the companions were excellent and I especially liked Marisol's story arc. I found the general design of the Auntie's Choice spaceship HQ compelling and enjoyed being in that virtual space. I also really enjoyed getting to know the heads (heh) of Auntie's Choice and The Order, especially the reveal that the Presiding Bishop was a nepo hire. Lastly, the radio stations were excellent. I've since listened to some of the Math Church radio's music in real life and as a general rule I don't usually listen to game soundtracks.

And here's where I admit something must be wrong with me. See, the thing is I can't really recommend this game. But the thing also is, I was so excited for this title after playing Avowed and replaying the original Outer Worlds this year that I purchased the  Premium Edition and I do plan on getting back to the game when the story expansions drop. I don't particularly regret doing this, but I just couldn't tell anyone they need to buy and play this game either. I plan on sticking around and seeing if I find that things get better. We shall see, and I'd like to be pleasantly surprised. 

So...

This sequel is legitimately better than the first game, but (imo) it's still not good. First, I'll say two nice things about it:

-The game does an excellent job of letting any kind of character (with any kind of build/skillset) progress through the story, with different experiences. E.g. If you spec into lockpicking, or hacking, or engineering, then you'll be able to access content that other players will miss. But every player will ultimately be able to finish each mission in different ways. It's quite impressive that the developers have designed a game where so much content is locked behind specific skill trees in a way that always seems fair. Will every player miss at least a little bit of content on every playthrough? Yes, but the playthroughs are still smooth, and the missed content just makes each skill seem like it really matters. If you spec into speech/persuasion instead of lockpicking, then it makes sense that you can talk your way out of situations that other players would need to fight through, with the tradeoff that you can't open doors that other players could unlock.

-The plot and tone are much better than in the first game. The first game was so bleak and dark and morbidly satirical that it felt gross to me. "Haha, the world sucks, everyone is a vapid murderer, and we're gonna joke about it!" This game's plot is much more nuanced, and you can choose whether you want a gory playthrough or a more "PG-13" one.

Onto the negatives:

-Stealth was awful in the last game, and I can confirm that (despite a lot of changes) it's still awful in THIS game. The core problem is that, ultimately, all levels have a fairly linear progression. So if you want a stealthy character, then you need to find the exact ventilation shafts or tunnels that the developers hid randomly around each level. This is not practical to do, unless you're psychic in real life (or you're on your 3rd playthrough and you already know where everything is). Moreover, the really important battles (like boss fights) can't feasibly use stealth anyway, so you're wasting skill points if you dump too many into that area.

-There are other skills that also let you avoid combat, like by using hacking or engineering to flood a level with poisonous gas or deactivate mechanical enemies. However, just like with stealth, there is usually not a clear way to accomplish these goals. I would often clear out a building full of enemies, and THEN find a random hidden computer panel that would have let me flood the room with gas if I had known in advance to do that. Moreover, these alternative combat methods are locked behind very specific skill-checks. (E.g. "You need to be at least level 8 in hacking in order to make this particular computer do what you want.") You also only get 2 skill points to spend per level-up, so that odds are very low that you'll have the exact skill proficiencies that you happen to need at any point in time. Maybe stealth/hacking/engineering would be good for like a 3rd playthrough, when you already know where everything is and what exact skill levels you need at which points in the story. But for normal people, you almost never have the skills that you need at the times that you need them, and you can't figure out where to go in order to use those skills effectively. By 8 hours into the game, I had just given up on all fancy tricks and resorted to spraying all enemies with gunfire.

-Literally no combat skill is worthwhile except for guns. Are there good melee weapons? Sure, but your enemies will often have guns, and so they'll be shooting at you from 30ft away while you try to run over at them. Are there cool explosives? Sure, but each type of explosive has a different effect that's only useful in specific situations. So 99% of the time, you should just stroll into every level with a few guns and a ton of ammo and raze your enemies that way. Most of the mods and upgrades are also pointless, because literally no problem in the entire game is more complicated than pulling out a gun and shooting a lot. (Plus you'll keep finding newer/better gear all the time, so it's not worth modding any gear because you'll just find better stuff in 10min anyway. ALSO, many of the mods do things that are objectively useless. "You gain an extra weapons slot." You can literally always pause the game to change your weapons in the inventory menu, so that mod does nothing except save you 3 seconds at best.)

-All of the game's factions are unsympathetic. This game falls into the far too common trap of saying that "moral complexity" just means that "everyone is an equally incompetent jerk."

-In this game and the first game too, there is an oddly specific problem where the first-person character view is too short/low to the ground. Maybe both games were designed by someone who's like 5'1"? I was sort of going mad, constantly thinking that I'd accidentally hit the "Crouch"/stealth button, only to realize that my character's default standing perspective is like 20% lower than average.

-The quest journal is not well-organized. Visually, it's not clear what's going on. Sometimes the bullet points are even in reverse-chronological order. I mean... why????

-One of your shipmates is an insane serial killer, and (unlike Jack from Mass Effect 2) she doesn't have a great redemption arc. (At least, not as far as I played.) So if you want a "good" playthrough, you're out of luck, because you're just stuck with a murderous psychopath. For that matter, ALL of your shipmates are so morally grey that none of them are particularly interesting or likable. To again compare this game to the Mass Effect series, the Mass Effect shipmates were also morally diverse, but they all ultimately agreed to fight for something other than themselves. They had universal redeeming qualities like selflessness and respect for their teammates. When a game turns "moral complexity" into just a spectrum of brooding depravity, it's not fun. I want likable characters, not just a tasting menu that asks me "Which flavor of dull sociopath would you like to see next?"

-But the last straw for me was that I just didn't care about any of the fictional world. When the factions aren't sympathetic, and the levels are linear and repetitive, and the shipmates are morally fetid, what exactly am I supposed to like? Every time that I unlocked a new area, and the open world got bigger, I never felt excited. I felt more like I was doing a chore: "Ugh, there's MORE that I need to do now??" That's not the reaction that players should have.

Maybe there will be a 3rd game that will improve upon the last one again, and I'll finally like that one. Fingers crossed!

Well, that's it. Almost a full 50 hours to finish the base game in hard mode. I reastarted once after having made some really REALLY poor choices with my build, but it was already good fun to replay the 30 hours I had played up until that point. 
This game feels really complete. As of writing this, there are still some bugs to fix and such, but since I have not experienced them as game breaking in any way I do not mind them for this review.

Let's compare this game to The outer Worlds 1 and Fallout: New Vegas, starting with New Vegas.
New Vegas is close to all out hearts and for many of us the reason we are here, either playing this game or considering to. New Vegas had a lot of lore between it's base game and DLC:s. I feel like there is a possibility for this to happen for The Outer Worlds 2 as well, once the DLC:s are released. But for now, most things in this literal universe is so new that it is hard to see what is just everyday lore of it and what could be specific stories told between large places.

The gameplay in the New Vegas is rather forgiving. You survive a lot of hits and usually have little issues with weapons, ammo and armor. Dialogue is mostly based on the speech skill, but other skills can be used some times as well.
A lot of this carries over to TOW 2. Ammo is rarely scarce, but damage from enemies seem a lot less forgiving in TOW 2 than New Vegas. I did my first playthrough in hard mode, thinking that my experience with NV and TOW 1 would carry me, and it largely did. But I found it a lot more interesting to have to duck for cover, choose when to peek out to attack, make the sprint when I notice the enemy reloads and much more. I found the combat in TOW 2 much more likable, although it is possible to just ruch everything like NV but at the expens of healing items.

In NV, the choices lead to rather predictable outcomes. The outcomes are a lot less predictable in TOW 2. To me, this is a good thing. I really felt like my choices, even small ones, mattered. But since I have only played through the game once so far, I can not say for sure if the actually mattered xD

The Outer Worlds 1 was closer to New Vegas that to TOW 2. It was rather forgiving, even at higher difficulties and choices was also rather rather straight forward. I see now that there is little to add to the discussion that has not been mentioned when discussing NV adn TOW 2, so that feels a bit silly now. But I do feel like there has been a large step away from what TOW 1 set up in terms of gameplay and experience of the game. And for the better as well. 

I really REALLY look forward to what DLC:s will bring to this game. The DLC:s for TOW was really good, one with an excellent story and one with an actually good murder mystery. So I will now have to force myself to set my expectations to low-medium, so that I may be as pleasantly surprised as I was with playing this base game. Thank you Obsidian, you are truly breathing life into this "genre" again.
I really feel like this is where the "meh" button would come in handy.

Once again, [strike]Microsoft Gaming[/strike] Obsidian succeeds in making a thoroughly mediocre RPG but with the price tag and hardware requirements of a AAAA game. The gunplay is significantly improved over the predecessor and I'm so happy they scrapped the ridiculous levelled gear system, which frankly belongs in the past.

However, what the game gained in improved gameplay it lost in writing and world building, which feels like someone just made a checklist of all the things Obsidian games are known for and passionlessly went  down the list. The first game's humour was its main selling point, but the charm is lacking here. The companions are forgettable, the jokes are flat and the story is cartoonishly bland. It's like New Vegas but with a script from Doctor Who.

Content is not that much more than the first game, maybe a bit more open world, but it doesn't add much other than rinse/repeat generic encounters. It took me virtually exactly 50 hours to 100% the game, though granted I usually finish games fairly fast even when I'm doing everything I can find (and using a walkthrough if I can't find it.)

Overall it's [i]only[/i] a decent game, but personally I don't feel that it's an improvement over the first game, and certainly does not justify its outlandish hardware demand.

Outer Worlds was an average game. Combat was passing at best, the dialogue was often stilted and the writing was overall pretty forgettable. But when you finished it, you were left with a sense that there was something special here. It was unique - plenty of Sci Fi stories out there, but none had the feel of Outer Worlds. Fallout would be the closest, but this was different - this wasn't a story about humanity after the apocalypse, it was the story of humanity on the precipice of it. Where the best and worst aspects of humanity were at war over whether we would tip over the edge, or step back from it. That is where Outer Worlds shone: it had truly superb themes, and despite the overall averageness of the production, it explored them in a deep and meaningful way.

Outer Worlds 2 continues that tradition, and does away with the averageness altogether. Combat feels better (enemies are smarter, dodging and using tactics, more varied ways in which you can build your character etc.), the dialogue is broadly excellent, and the writing is far more mature and developed. Outer Worlds had great atmosphere, Outer Worlds 2 has transcendent atmosphere. The factions are more varied and interesting, your choices feel like they have greater impact, the worlds are considerably bigger and there is just generally far more to do. Even something as inconsequential as a small cave will often have incredible visual storytelling - the world feels lived in, and you are constantly learning about the struggles of people just trying to survive, or the hubris of the greedy that led to their downfall.

I would recommend people pick up Outer Worlds if it was on sale. I would recommend people to get Outer Worlds 2 right now, because it is one of the best games I've played in a long time and it is worth the asking price.

I don't write reviews often but felt with the mixed reception I'd throw my hat into the ring. 

The story is fun and interesting, the locations are beautiful, the combat feels great, and the companions are decent in a fight. Not all of the companions are interesting or useful, but there are definitely some who are solid in combat. The cult companion is really funny to listen to as well, which is great because some of the others are bland. Character skills and perks are varied and all seem useful, replayability there is high and so are the impactful decisions you can make. The game has a lot of options on how to traverse missions depending on your skills and perks, including conversations which is nice. Overall its a pretty polished game, I have encountered zero bugs, and smooth gameplay (open world areas can lag sometimes). There is a lot of item customization options as well, so you can have a really varied playthrough in any one build. 

Some flaws would be slower performance in big open world areas. The corporate humour can be a bit thick at times, but is entertaining enough in moments to be overlooked with some hilariously absurd world building moments. The visual look of the game univerise is probably not going to be for everyone. The guns look stupid not very cool design, but make up for it by being satisfying to use. Character faces are pretty bland and dated despite the rest of the game being easy on the eyes. They definitely missed some opportunties as well with their companion deisgn, very reserved and zero romance options, not that you really would want to even romance these ones. I just don't care at all about these companions. When you look at games like cyberpunk for example or BG3, if you make characters appealing and open romance options, people get more invested. A big miss in my opinion. My whole team could be replaced with NPC bland soldiers and aside from the random dialogue comment, I wouldn't notice. 

Overall people will find an RPG with good mechanics, combat, fun story and interesting places to explore. Don't expect it to be a serious or heavy RPG. I can sum the game up with just one word "FUN". It's a lighthearted adventure that actually has consequences for decisions and character build impacts real world interactions. I will say $90.00 before tax seems steep for any game, but I think if it's on sale for like $70 it's worth it then, but not full price. No game is worth $90 in my opinion. I defintitely recommend this game overall.

Outer Worlds 1, with better ceilings!
1. The first game was great, but for one issue: all the buildings and the rooms had really bad ceilings. Then the good folks at Obsidian practiced making better ceilings in the DLC's and finally used this knowledge to make some truly spectacular ceilings in this here Part II.
2. If you value player agency, this is the game for you. If you hate freedom and everything doing-whatever-you wanna-do-all-the-time stands for, you should play literally almost any other game.
3. There are no kids in this game despite a pretty large world. This is because if there were any, they'd have to make 'em killable, which you only get away with in an indie. So, Obsidian pre-killed all the kids for your playing pleasure.
4. There's like 40-something original songs on 3 distinct in-game radio stations. They're all really good. Some are even 10x as good as your favorite band.
5. Can't join the "evil" faction. The game is vast in variability, with sextillions upon sextillions upon sextillions of permutations, so I understand, but... woulda been cool...
6. You can play really fast if you want which I always appreciate in an RPG.
7. If you expect "story" like "The WItcher 3" or "Mass Effect" had story, you might end up disappointed.
8. If you like excellent ceilings, you'll absolutely love this diamond of a game!

Ok, so this sequel offered what a sequel usually should: the same, but bigger and better. It also fixed the worst problems with part 1 (which I did also enjoy, esp. the DLC):

- more engaging combat and enemies (but not as good as, say, Starfield or even Fallout 4)
- all companions were more interesting this time around, albeit also maybe a bit more traditional 
- less zany and cartoonish approach to satire, although still more so than Fallout
- interesting perks and skills that provide different approaches to playing the game

So if you liked the first game and are eagerly awaiting anything in the mould of Fallout, Elder Scrolls etc. this is a good fix. But there are some problems that might still put you off:

- the fresh initial start promises a lot but then throws you into an environment that feels too much like the beginning of the first game, giving it a recycled feel
- the hunting aspect and Inez esp. feel like they're lifted from the first game - and that part of the first game was not interesting IMO.
- the factions and the main story don't quite make sense all the time, the satire is luckily toned down from the first game but it still borders on the annoying.... the main story just feels like it's being made up as you go along, which is also a distraction. 
- endgame doesn't offer enough satisfying combat just when you get your hands on all the cool weapons

Other than that, it's a fine looking space adventure and quite bug free too for such a large game. Here's hoping for more.

Just completed the game on Very Hard. Here are some thoughts.

I can see why this game gets hate. But I can also see how folks just want to hate for the simple sake of hating. Is it woke? No. There are some "woke-ish" things present that are present in most games. But this isn't Dragon Age: Veilguard level of cringe that's for sure. It has the whole "body type" thing. It says the word "fascist" a couple of times. Both "body types" are similar in build outside of male and female facial features. Did it ruin my experience? Nope. I loved the story. And will love continuing playing this game. 

I would recommend this game to those who liked/loved the first game. Everything outside of how companions are used is better. The companion storylines are all awesome. And if you like playing the "bad guy" this game actually can compliment you with more than 1 companion who can meld with your roleplay.

This is my advice to anyone wanting to play this game:

If you're skeptical: Wait for a sale and give it a shot.
If you liked/loved the first game: Buy it.
If you are absolutely against "woke garbage": Avoid it I suppose. I would say this game is far from woke. But like I said... beauty is in the eye of which you hold her.

I'll be continuing my other playthrough as a Brilliant, Lucky, Ex-Convict Guns, Sneak, and Observant fellow in search of bits and banter.

Thank you if you read this and I hope you have a wonderful day/evening/afternoon.

This is one of the games of 2025, if Steam allows a "meh" review I would give it. The story, gameplay, and characters are all passable, and to be fair, overall the game did have some fun moments that make you want to boot up the game again. 

However, the design of the game as a whole lacks immersion, and does not feel organic, like a actual world. The game design philosophy feels like it came from 15 years ago.

The world building just feel flat. 
For example, each faction in the game have a simple and single faceted "deal." There is a science faction, their whole deal is science and maths every thing. There is an authoritarian faction, their deal is to 1984 the ♥♥♥♥ out of everyone. So on and so forth. 
Every NPCs sticks to their factions style hardcore, like they belong in a hivemind or something. Once you speak to one NPC it feels like you've spoken to them all. It feels like caricatures rather than real factions with real people in it.  

The world is also empty and bland. 
Quest objectives are very far apart, and there is nothing interesting in between. Sometimes you walk for minutes without seeing one point of interest. When you do see a POI, it belongs to one of the single-faceted factions, with similarly dressed NPCs and samey looking buildings, so it ends up being boring anyway. 

For a game that places heavy emphasis on choices, the morality system also falls flat. Kill is bad, not kill is good. 
In a cutthroat and unforgiving world in this game, surely there are some people that have done some horrible things and are better off dead. But no, every encounter makes you feel like you have done the right thing by sparing that horrible person. It feels very whitewashed and immature. 

Also, quest design makes you feel like you're ticking off items on a list rather than interacting with real characters and helping with their problems within that world. 
For example, if you didn't collect a pre-requisite before starting dialogues, you get this indicator telling you one of the dialogue options is not available because you didn't have an item or collect a datapad. How does this in any way reflect an organic conversation, if you know what knowledge you are missing? 
The mindset of "you know what you're missing" makes the players do stuff for the fear of missing out, rather than to explore because it's fun or the world is cool. Again the design feels antiquated and does not feel organic at all. 

Also there are weird bugs that reloading don't resolve, and requires searching online for very specific sets of actions to bypass. And there are constant crashes for some reason.

The Outer Worlds 2 is a near-perfect RPG because it nails what matters most: your choices actually matter. The faction system is legitimately complex—you're constantly weighing whether to side with the corporations or the rebels, and the game doesn't tell you who's right. The companions are great too—they're not just damage dealers, they have their own stories that tie into the main plot in meaningful ways. But what really makes this shine is the character progression system. The interplay between traits, skills, perks, and flaws creates insane build diversity. You're not locked into being "the stealth guy" or "the combat guy"—you can mix speech skills with sniper abilities, or combine science with melee combat in ways that actually work. The flaw system is genius: accepting permanent debuffs (like reduced accuracy in the dark after getting wrecked by nocturnal enemies) creates interesting trade-offs that either complement your build or force you to adapt your playstyle around your character's weaknesses.

What pushes this to a 9/10 is that Obsidian absolutely crushed the writing and gameplay balance. The dark humor and corporate satire are hilarious without being preachy, and you'll genuinely care about the characters. Combat is way better than the first game—guns feel punchy, the slow-mo mechanic adds strategy without making things too easy, and fights stay interesting for the entire 40+ hour playthrough. Skills actually open up new dialogue options and quest solutions throughout the game, so investing in lockpicking or intimidation fundamentally changes how you experience missions. The only reason it's not a perfect 10 is some annoying bugs and occasional quest glitches that can block progress temporarily. But honestly, those are minor complaints for what's easily one of the best RPGs in years.

The “New Vegas spirit” reborn in space
The Outer Worlds 2 is Obsidian’s latest RPG. the sequel to their satirical sci-fi adventure. While the first game leaned heavily into humor and parody, the sequel takes a more serious tone, closer to Fallout: New Vegas. The result? A richer narrative, deeper systems, and a game that feels like Obsidian finally embracing their RPG legacy in full.
🗺️ World & Structure – ★★★★☆
The game isn’t a seamless open world, but its zones are larger, more detailed, and packed with branching paths. Exploration feels rewarding, with multiple routes and immersive-sim style design that adapts to your playstyle. It’s structured, yes, but the freedom of choice makes every area feel alive and reactive.
📖 Story & Characters – ★★★★★
The narrative is a huge step up from the first game. The more serious tone fits perfectly, echoing the weight and maturity of New Vegas. Dialogues are sharp, voice acting is excellent, and the companion quests are well-written, adding emotional depth and variety. The story feels cohesive and compelling, keeping you invested throughout.
⚔️ Gameplay & Systems – ★★★★★
This is where Outer Worlds 2 shines. Companions are not only memorable but mechanically impactful, with buffs and combat support that matter. The upgrade system for weapons and companions is deep and satisfying. Crafting is meaningful, side quests are varied, and the branching mission design encourages experimentation. Whether you prefer stealth, brute force, or clever dialogue, the game rewards your choices with multiple outcomes.
⚖️ Difficulty & Progression – ★★★★☆
Progression feels balanced, with enough challenge to keep combat engaging without being punishing. The companion buffs and upgrade systems help smooth difficulty spikes. While not brutally hard, the game still demands strategic thinking, especially in tougher missions. It strikes a good middle ground between accessibility and depth.
🎨 Graphics & Sound – ★★★★★
Visually, the game is stunning. Environments are richly detailed, with lighting and art direction that make every planet feel unique. Animations and effects are polished, and the overall presentation is top-tier. Sound design complements the visuals, with strong voice acting and atmospheric audio that enhances immersion.
✅ Final Verdict – ★★★★★
The Outer Worlds 2 surpasses its predecessor in almost every way. With a stronger narrative, excellent companions, deep systems, and gorgeous presentation, it feels like Obsidian channeling the spirit of New Vegas into a sci-fi setting. After 25 hours, it’s already one of the most enjoyable RPG experiences in years.
Highly recommended — this is Obsidian at their best.

The leveling system is kind of a downside of the game. You’re not informed about the level cap of 30, and there’s no respec option. Since I was doing everything in the game, I hit the level cap at around 70% of the playthrough and my interest in the RPG side started to fade because you can’t adjust your build anymore. Thankfully, I focused on only a few specializations, so I didn’t have the same issues as many players who went for a jack-of-all-trades build and ended up unable to pass skill checks in the later locations
Overall, it’s a solid game, and with future updates and DLCs, I’m pretty sure they’ll fix most of the issues people have, especially since they’re relatively easy to address

Outer Worlds 2 is quite a fun game, filled with interesting locals, unique characters, and a good story. However, despite the fun gameplay and great graphics, I can't help but get hung up on a few small issues.

The skill system, while interesting, does prevent a skill monkey way of playing. I went through the game unable to pickpocket or hack because I worried of not having enough speech points. Effectively, you're randomly guessing at what skills you want, and then hoping that your build sees you through. It would've have been as bad if your cohorts were more useful, but most of the time they're only good as social flavoring and as combat companions. 

There are a few side-encounters that can only be completed with the right skills...which you may not have. It sucks not being able to recruit a robot or save a civilian because you don't have enough in lockpicking or hacking.

I do miss the variety of Corporations that the first game had. Now it's just Auntie Cleo, who's great mind you. But I miss the variety. 

The final level has quite a few bugs, and I had to look up a guide to find my way through the final ship.


Still, despite the gripes, I enjoyed the game as much as I did the first one, and would recommend.

For an RPG enthusiast this game is good, its almost great but there are some bugs and flaws that made this game just "good".

For instance, if you are a completionist and a bit of OCD there is 1 particular quest that has a bug in which, if you progress through the main storyline far enough and do not know that this quest exist. You could be locked out of the quest or not get the reward from the quest altogether. Weirdly enough, you can get the said reward instantly if you talk straight to the quest giver instead, but if you talk to other npc about this quest. It will stay in the "Mentioned" section and can never be completed for the rest of the playthrough and you are locked out of getting the reward. If the said reward is just some weapon or loot, i might be able to overlook it. The main problem is, you need the reward to progress on some of the quest (although you may not need it tbf), but it's as if you have an itch you can never scratch, just bugging you or distracting you for the whole playthrough is just annoying. But nothing is perfect, and this game certainly is.

Another bit of a let down is in the story. Not the lore of the story but the progression of the story. Personally, the story is a tad bit short, i thought i would go through a lot more quest and listening to some more lore and stories, sadly after around 24+ hours of gameplay, i was a bit disappointed with how short the story is.

Also enemies do not respawn, i think Obsidian tried to make it like an actual RPG experience where you kill a bunch of enemies in one area and then you just progress on through, just like in Baldurs Gate 3. So unlike its predecessor, there's no more grinding.

TL;DR the game is great for an RPG enjoyer, role-playing is good, combat is great (a bit hard but still great). Story is also cool, though lacking in moral dilemmas like Fallout: New Vegas but still got some good moral choices to it. Overall i'd recommend this game, just give it a try!

This game is weird to judge, in many ways it's considerably better than the first, but in others it's definitely worse IMO. I miss gear giving you bonuses and having condition, the character creation system in general has been hyper streamlined, yet this game has at least 100 available perks, when even with DLCs the first had 42 or thereabout. The characters and stories are much better though. I may miss gear condition and the skill system, but there's so much more to this one, more to explore, more to learn, more to see. Gear mods are also surprisingly less expansive in this, or at least they "feel" that way.

I recommend it more for players who HAVEN'T played the first even more than I do for those who have. I think if you played the first, this is going to be a bit of a mechanical system shock. Mechanically it's not the same game at all, there's good weapons, dialogue is fantastic, etc, but building your character and leveling up and such is entirely different, along with the previously mentioned gear related stuff. I'm personally a fan of the original mechanically, but this one for locations, story, and characters. If you played, and like, Avowed, but prefer a more sci-fi direction, this is absolutely a game for you. If you played Avowed and gave up because of mechanics, and prefer older Fallout to newer Fallout, you may be disappointed here.

Overall, I do recommend playing this, with or without a grain of salt, depending on if you did or did not play the original TOW respectively. Personally, I'm very likely to play it once or twice more, and leave it be after that. It's damn good, but deviates too far from systems I greatly prefer to newer versions of those same systems.

Pros 
[list]
[*] Lively world full of colorful characters and locations.
[*] Welcome addition of third person perspective option. It is sad that such a thing is no longer the norm in an RPG with character creation. 
[*] Same goes for a normal save load system. 
[*] Brilliant RP and progression that reacts the player's behavior. I especially love the dynamic flaws system, and even more the fact that they are optional.
[*] Some people think the satirical story is too on the nose. Those people clearly did not play the first game.
[*] The gunplay is crisp in a primitive sort of way. There is a great variety of weaponry.
[/list]


Cons / suggestions
[list]
[*] The protagonist feels like an empty vessel, They have no voice, No inner monologues, You can't see their face / expressions during conversations. They do not feel like a real character. I understand some players prefer it this way so they can "inhabit" the character, but I find playing as a mindless mute deeply unimmersive. How about you people make the protagonist voiced and mocapped (Fallout 4 style), but give players the option to disable them if they prefer?
[*] Options to morph the lower half of the face (mouth size, position, chin etc) are missing.
[*] Skill checks for lockpicking or spotting mines etc should not just be applied to the main character but also to companions present.
[*] While I appreciate the addition of third person, combat is still restricted to first person conventions - i.e. cover fire, blind fire, advanced melee attacks etc are not possible.Hands perpetually stuck to the front.
[*] In a game full of good looking people, why are companions such eyesores? 
[*] I accept that whether to add romance or not is the writers' choice. But personally, I feel more invested in a stories with a bit of romance.
[*] The world could be a bit more packed. Too much walking around empty locations, at least in the first planet.
[*] Some enemies feel like bullet sponges.
[*] Weapon mods have debuffs so large that they feel like zero-sum additions, rather than improvements. So in most cases they feel pointless.
[/list]

Finished this up last night and really enjoyed my time with it. It's definitely worth a single play through. The writing was improved upon from the first one and nowhere near as annoying as Avowed. The quirky nature of the dialogue actually grew on me and fits this universe infinitely better than in a fantasy setting. The companions definitely made the game for me. The growth allowed through each of their progressions was a highlight, less two of them left lots to be desired. Aza, Marisol, and Inez were strong points - Tristan felt lackluster, and Niles was okay. The gunplay is great, the flaw system is dramatically improved upon and feels like it actually is impactful to different builds, and the unique weapons and armor felt worthwhile without being game breaking. Don't think I'll be revisiting to be a full completionist but being able to hit most of the achievements in a single run was available as an option, the 11 I missed were mostly due to this being a "good guy" run. I enjoyed my time but it definitely missed the mark as something from veterans in the industry, especially at the price point.

The Outer Worlds 2 improves in every way on the first game, though it does tone down the anti-capitalist rhetoric somewhat, unfortunately.  

The combat has been improved, though not enough for any combat purists out there, and the RPG elements are interesting and engaging.  I'm still not a great fan of flaw systems, but it is not too egregiously implemented here, of course you can just ignore the mechanic entirely if you wish.

The humour is enjoyable overall and the artistic direction of the game is quite good and it is full of Obsidian's excellent storytelling.  The controls are easy to work with, with them not making so many concessions for controller use that they have hobbled mouse and keyboard, which tends to be a major problem with cross platform games lately, with devs programming for the lowest common denominator.

I have yet to have anything to do with any of the companions, other than the two you start with, so I can't really comment on them as of now.

I enjoyed it..... I've completed 1 run through of the game and put a good 80 hours into finding as much content in the game as I can. I didn't feel as engaged as the first game though, even felt a little more railroaded regarding character stats and story plot choices etc. It's very easy to get locked out of entire chunks of game just by the choices you make on a playthrough. This starts very early on in the game and at a point where you don't really know who to side with or why one faction is better/worse/more agreeable than another, I don't know how many hours of replays I really want to put in so I can experience everything the game has to offer.... random exploration didn't really pay off for me so probably best to talk to lots of NPC's and run around on their behalf. 

I loved one of the companions, I wish it could have opened up more backstory to her life and it's a shame her character glitched half way through my game (not in a bad or incompletable way but noticeable). Also the gadgets are a cool idea. Too many item collection side missions for my liking though. Small spoiler... It has 2 'point of no returns', so at the first one I was getting worried that it was a much shorter game than it is and put off going forward, only to discover after that I was only about half way through, so felt a little silly for that one. 

It is not a $70 game. There's nothing wrong with it, glitches will be patched and refinements will be made, a lot of the negativity will melt away. I will be getting the expansions at some point but I feel that they should have been included in the base price of the game so I will be waiting on some kind of deal. Mostly because the main game just feels like its lacking a little. It's like there's nothing wrong with the game but it could have been bigger, better and more (it still really feels like the first game was bigger, better and more unfortunately).

I think the 1st game felt more complete and the DLC was a really nice extra later on. I have a bad feeling that the rest of the game is going to be sold to players in the coming months in pieces (these story expansions mentioned in the premium edition), and yes, you'll get to pay extra! [h1]10/10 – A worthy sequel[/h1]

[h3]Raphael Sbarge alone earns this game a 10/10[/h3]
First things first: What a casting choice! [b]Raphael Sbarge[/b] (the voice behind Mass Effect’s Kaidan Alenko and Knights of the Old Republic’s Carth Onasi) is back, and the moment I heard his voice, it felt like reuniting with old friends. It’s maybe a big deal for me, but the little things that make these games feel !!! :D 

[h3]Graphics and gameplay: A huge step forward	[/h3]
The game is a clear upgrade from the first. The visuals are stunning and sometimes overwhelmingly so, with breathtaking vistas and immersive atmospheres. I did run into a few graphical glitches and one quest that refused to progress, which was mildly infuriating. But in the grand scheme, these are minor hiccups.

[h3]The story hooks you: and won’t let go[/h3]
The plot is really good. I found myself repeatedly falling into the "just one more turn/quest/storyline" trap, a feeling only the best RPGs can evoke. The leveling system is slow and deliberate, encouraging multiple playthroughs to unlock everything. I love that!

[h3]Could use a bit more lore: but that’s okay[/h3]
I wouldn’t have minded a little more depth in some of the backstories about how the world (not just Arcadia, but maybe building upon the whole galaxy) became what it is. But hey, that’s perfect DLC material! And it gives me something to look forward to.

[h3]Final verdict: A must-play for RPG fans[/h3]
Now I’m taking a break. But only to recharge for my next playthrough. The Outer Worlds 2 is a gem that understands what makes an RPG great: choices, characters, and a world that feels alive. I can’t wait to dive back in!

The Good: It is the same sense of humor that you fell in love with in the first time around.

The Bad: Some of the RPG elements have been "simplified" in disappointing ways (e.g. clothes do not have skill modifiers, you cannot reset skills)

The Ugly: Not well optimized. The game is pretty but is it that much prettier than the first one? I have a high end system and it runs hotter than the first.

As a massive fan of Fallout as well as Obsidian's prior work, this game lived up to my expectations. After beating the game, I can confidently say that The Outer Worlds 2 is an excellent RPG, which is unfortunately marred by online discourse. The game features a near-constant satirical tone, which may become annoying for some. Luckily, I enjoyed the humor, and I found that it strengthened the extremely fun and well-designed First-Person RPG gameplay loop with genuinely funny comedy. That being said, the tone of the writing seems to be a point of contention for some online, not the plot itself. 

When designing the gameplay, it feels like Obsidian studied what gameplay systems worked well in Fallout New Vegas, then brought those systems back with much-needed modernizations. This game forces you to commit to a build from the start of the game, which allows the game designers to make your chosen playstyle feel more meaningful than many other RPGs. Skill and perk checks are frequent throughout the game, and each skill has purposes in and outside of combat. Each skill point and perk investment feels meaningful, as the game would constantly provide feedback and options based on my stat distribution. So many alternate paths opened up based on my build that I feel like I barely scratched the surface of what this game has to offer. Also, the character flaws system adds a nice touch of personalization to this game. Flaws tend to reward you for doing an action repeatedly, but debuff you in some other way. Almost all of the flaws go above and beyond flat stat buffs and increases, instead altering core gameplay systems to reward the players' bad behaviour.

The only detractor from the experience was the game's poor optimization. Unfortunately, The Outer Worlds 2’s gorgeous art direction is muddied by its overreliance on DLSS to maintain a shaky framerate. To be fair, I played this on a mobile RTX 3060. Despite poor performance, I encountered no noticeable bugs.

First game I've ever thought was worthy of a $70 price tag. If Fallout New Vegas was provided to you for free with your autism diagnosis, this game will scratch that same itch again. 

side note: the playable tag for Steam Deck is "correct", however it runs beautifully at medium 60fps if you use lossless scaling (I played half the game on the deck that way) and it is a very enjoyable experience if that fits your use case.

A lot of people are comparing it to Fallout, which makes sense. However in reality, the Outer Worlds series is much more of a spiritual successor to Vampire the Masquerade: Bloodlines. Think about it:

1. Open Zone worlds where environments are paced via moderately linear begins that then open up when quests branch out.
2. Ever aspect of things is cranked up to 11. Everything and everyone is a caricature, not an in-depth critique of the thing that is being satirized. 
3. Something from Fallout - Retrofuturism. I genuinely love Outer Worlds' aesthetic. It's one of a kind and probably my favorite part. The scope of the 1890s Retrofuturism in detail and in scale is genuinely amazing.

Anyway, go in expecting VtM:B, not Fallout and certainly not FNV.

TL;DR: Rating: 5/10 ~price & depth of the game its rough around edges, No actual choices.
Here is my opinion (Technical detailed):

It’s a decent game overall, The game engine is amazing it looks amazing the feel of the game is very good.
It’s fun, replayable, and you’ll still see different outcomes and content on repeat runs. The main issue is that the game feels like it wants to be a choice-driven RPG, but several core systems don’t fully hold up.

The biggest disappointment for me is faction roleplay. You’re placed in the middle of a conflict involving the Protectorate, but the game doesn’t really let you commit to siding with them in a meaningful way. That’s odd when the story frames the situation like you should be able to pick a clear stance. Whether you like the Protectorate or not, players should be able to support them properly if the narrative sets it up.

Another big problem is state tracking and reactivity. I’ve had multiple moments where NPC dialogue didn’t match what actually happened in my playthrough. For example, companions aren’t in my party anymore, yet I still get lectures from Marisol about Inez even after I helped Inez get her graft and we parted ways. The strangest part is that no one else was in my party when i met Inez, so those conversations feel disconnected from reality. This kind of thing breaks immersion fast.

I like the stealth gameplay, but disguises don’t work. If disguises are meant to be a tool for infiltration, they need this in this game very badly, with security check points or those restricted area's. (when you’re “safe,” when you’re compromised, and why).

There are some good “play your way” moments; e.g. hidden paths and alternate routes (like getting across the bridge without starting a fight, or crossing illegally). That’s the part I wish they leaned into more, because it shows the game can support alternative solutions when it chooses to. (this gives the feeling of freedom of choice)

Faction choices and making enemies can be fun, and going full psychopath/serial killer is an option (I genuinely enjoyed that (the perks)), but it also seems to remove huge chunks of options without offering enough alternate content. A strong “evil” path should replace content with different content not just reduce the game.

One more issue they really should address: the Auntie Choice commercial audio. You can’t properly turn it off unless you disable “Effects,” but that also removes important gameplay sounds like shooting and footsteps. This needs a separate audio option.

Overall: I have fun, but it feels like a great game held back by unfinished reactivity and half-implemented systems.

Score: 5/10 considering the price. If the price were 50% lower, this would be a 6~7/10 for me.

The Outer Worlds 2 is a fantastic RPG if you're looking for something similar to the 3D Fallout games -- particularly New Vegas, as it's developed by Obsidian and has a lot of their usual motifs. It's a great improvement on the first entry in terms of gameplay, graphics, and character progression. There is a lot of build variety, whether you prefer to play with a focus on guns-blazing combat, stealth, or seeking out peaceful resolutions (with the Speech skill). There are also other supplemental skills to help navigate areas by opening locked routes, or to pass skill checks for different dialogue options or outcomes to quests. The skills feel a lot more refined compared to the first game, and they are nicely integrated in a way that lets you roleplay your character however you want.

One of my favorite things about the first game was the comedic worldbuilding, and the sequel continues to go all-out in the writing department. There are so many subtle jokes sprinkled into every NPC interaction that are just funny in clever ways. As much as I can't stand Auntie's Choice, that's kind of the point! The corporate satire is spot on with exaggerated hyper-capitalism and cutthroat bureaucrats. 

One last thing I want to touch on is the companions. I've read other reviews where some people felt they weren't very memorable, but I actually enjoyed getting to know them. They are characters with deeply rooted beliefs pertaining to the faction they belong to, but their questlines see them confronting the flaws in their ideologies and challenge their values. They also butt heads with each other through passive background dialogue, and I think those interactions did a great job at showing the nuance of their differences. No faction is ever portrayed as solely good or evil, and that allows for individuals to rethink their priorities and morals.

Anyway, I highly recommend this game for a fun retro-futuristic space adventure. $70 is a pretty steep price, but I got about 70 hours out of my first playthrough and thoroughly enjoyed every minute. DEFINITELY worth it if you get it on sale!

I really enjoyed this game - played through the first game before playing this to remind me self of the story and what it was like. This is better in almost every way.

The start of the game is more interesting and the level design. The worlds are now much more open with lots of verticality and much less areas outside the full bounds of the world you can't explore. They obviously took a lot of good design choices from Avowed - taking the movement parkour-esque system definitely made the game feel so much better.

The factions and companios are so much better. To be honest in the 1st game most of the companions, characters and factions are just blended together. In this, each faction is quite distinct visuals and motives. The companions are also almost all interesting, a stark contrast to the 1sts companions which I barely remember. They also have much more involvement with the game world as well - designs you make will affect how they feel about you and what they do. I didn't see much conflict between companions though, which is weird because some of them are from completely opposing factions.

The main story is also more interesting and the pacing is much better, I feel like it's split between two major parts and has a much better pacing and natural feel to the end. Although, at least for me, There wasn't much build up to the actual villain of the game. You do have a clear villain for half of the game, though.

Character building was still a bit simplistic but much better. having different combinations of stats unlocked different interesting traits to select from that actually felt useful. Inventory weight was also taken away and companion inventory was simplified to choosing a few different traits that unlocked based on their own stories.

Combat also felt much better - although I used mostly ranged weapons so was unsure about melee. However, as for itemization I did find weapons I liked early and felt like I really never had to switch away from them. I liked that they added more special abilities where as in the 1st you were restricted to only using time dilation.

Honestly - I'm not sure there is so much hate going around for this game. It's way better than the 1st, it's better than Avowed in a lot of ways (the game feels way less static.) Obsidian seems to have inhereted the old Bethesda mantra of "a mile wide but only an inch deep". There are multiple worlds to visit, many large areas to explore, countless characters to interact with, lots of weapons and armour... but much of it feels generic, repetitive, and forgettable.

It doesn't help that most of the NPC dialogue is, depending on faction, either: 1) stilted and wooden (the Protectorate) 2) Pedantic and technical (the Order) or 3) full of buzzwords and coorperate jargon (Aunty's Choice). It's midly entertaining the first few times, but after several hours it becomes tedious and makes everyone come across as more than a little thick-headed.

The gunplay, like everything else, is serviceable but unremarkable.

Though it all feels reasonably well polished, it also seems to lack any real ambition - or even a particularly engaging vision. I don't regret buying it, nor do I miss the hours I spent playing it. I had fun... but I doubt I'll ever think about it again after posting this review.

This game is a solid successor to the Outer Worlds 1, having improved on many facets of the original.

While you don't have to have played the original, it helps to set the tone and in-game universe.

The RPG & fighting mechanics are interesting, at least for the first 20-30 hours of play.  Like other similar games from Obsidian, this game offers a number of fun ways to solve issues, ranging from stealth to offensive approaches.

I found that the game became repetitive & stale somewhere around the ~50 hr mark.  It was around that point that my character became so powerful, and money became so plentiful, that fight sequences were largely trivialized (even at harder difficulties), making "crafty" resolution methods -- like stealth, speech, and so on -- less necessary compared to just storming the gates.  Puzzles become trivialized when you can simply kill everything on screen and loot their corpses for the solution.

There was a major bug that required me to restore (to a save game from a few hours prior), to get the ending that I wanted.  There was a different bug (when entering a side-quest zone) that caused my AMD graphics drivers to crash.  But, overall, the gameplay was pretty solid.

Obsidian is a company that has been struggling to exist for, arguably decades, and largely consists of very talented people known for some of the better gameplay on the market.
However, you wouldn't know it by playing this.

My biggest critique is that lessons from their game Fallout New Vegas still go ignored to this day, and it is absolutely baffling as to why.

There is skill disconnect between player build and level environment, a classic problem we saw in the newer Deus Ex games where stealth or nerd builds cannot compete with mandatory bosses, either due to level design not accomodating it, or just a sheer lack of brute force power because it doesn't match a player's play style.

Some dungeons are also like this, which is particularly noticable if you play on the hardest difficulty, suggesting it wasn't play tested.

Certain routes, be it hacking, lockpicking, engineering, also fail to overlap and compensate for each other, you will need all three, plus the observation skill, if you want to guarantee your way into every nook and cranny.
There are alternatives that try to compensate for some of this, but they are not fool proof, such as certain background skills, or objective items that can be found in lieu of skill, such as the Brawny or Brilliant or Innovative traits.
Companion characters don't even supplement your own skills by allowing you to utilize theirs in a pinch.

Gods forbid you try to build an infiltrator, because you are left unable to kill much of anything that is even low level.
Weapons could have compensated for this by not requiring skill points and simply being the deadly tools real weapons are, but unfortunately these fell victim to the RPG skill investment requirement.
Giving a player more than two skill points per level also could have helped compensate for this.. 

Something not necesary but something I am still noting is a lack of NV style stat-counter environment perks ("kill x amount with x thing"), and no ability to masquerade as a particular faction at a distance for subterfuge, they're little things, but I miss them when they're gone.

I'm not sure if I should comment on the reductio ad absurdum that is factions and how they converse, after all it's sort of the point and the style of humour, it wasn't meant to be near as serious as New Vegas, and that to me is alright, it still has some biting, on point commentary.

I'm saying yes, I do recommend, but it's nowhere near as strong of a recommendation as the first game. Don't get me wrong: The Outer Worlds 2 is still a really, really fun FPS RPG, just like the first. My problems stem from the revamped RPG mechanics. Let me explain.
In the first game, leveling gave you 10 points to dump into stats. All stats fell into categories that, until level 50, would be levelled in groups. So your ranged skills, such as small arms, long arms, specials, would be leveled all together until level 50. Then you'd level independantly. Outer Worlds 2 only gives you 2 skill points, and you must put them in fewer skills that reach a max of 20. Now, it's not a bad system, but the problem with it is that this game NEEDS YOU to specialize to get things done. In the first game, you had several options to deal with, for example, a room with traps. Wanted to disarm the security? Use that hacking skill you put a few points in. Not a hacker? Go sneaky sneaky in this closed vent that needs Lockpick. Rather fight? Then your melee skills will let you close the gap, or your ranged weapons will take care of it. You had options, is what I'm saying. In the same scenario in outer worlds 2, you will find a control box that asks "Hey did you dump all your skill points into hack? No? Too bad, but at least you picked this random trait at the start of the game right? No? That's a shame." Then you're FORCED to go the dangerous route throughout the security. If you're like me, and play a more charismatic "Max speech to win" type character, you will SUFFER. There's so many checks and observations you won't be able to do because you were expected to dump your stats in Explosives or Science or Observation or Hack. Which, by the way, if you're going to play this game, SPEECH IS ONLY USED AT MAX FOR 1 CHECK. It is hilarious how bad maxxing speech is if you don't want the cooperation ending. Do Hack, Science and Observation, they are BY FAR the most frequent things you will need. Lockpicking was just a little convenient, opening a few ways here and there but hack opens WAAYYY more stuff. Science is used all the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ time, for so many checks that would make conversations easier. Observation is needed to get better rewards and results from things, as well as lots of loot. The skills just feel generally bad to use, it's like they do nothing. In the first game they were unique, spread equally, and you would actually get lots of bonuses and different unlocked paths depending what you maxxed, including weapon skills. Can't science your way through a convo? Your gunslinging skills let you dish out a little moxy. Don't have the brute force to intimidate someone? Use your leadership to appeal to their ego. It was all really, really well done and this game falls flat there. 
The guns however are a marked improvement. There's more of them, and more ammo types, and combat feels really good. You take a bit too much damage imo, and armor just does nothing from what I feel. Like I had the best heavy armor in the game and I took just as much damage as in light armor. Weapons are a bit the same. I had my main weapons on hand at all times: A shock weapon for bots, the trench shotgun for general combat (holy BALLS that thing is good), and a sniper rifle to start combat with sneak attacks and strike from a distance. The shotguns definitely feel better than the first game. Melee is a joke, there's no real reason to use it unless you're building pure melee damage, but if you're doing that you're sacrificing buffs and perks for melee damage which gimps you for everything else in the game.
Perks. They are weird. They unlock as you level skills, but it really feels like they're not balanced. Leadership has TWO PERKS, and they buff companions a bit but that's it. Ranged weapons have like TEN, and they all just make you shoot better. The perks are really really weird and kind of unbalanced, both in quantity and usefulness. Like the Space Ranger perk. If you do like me and max Speech, congrats, that's a 50% bonus to all your damage, because obviously I can talk really well so my bullets will fly faster or something. 
The companions are fine. They are nowhere NEAR the charm of the first game's companions. You have robot that is deadpan, teammate with trauma, weird bio-mutant soldier that isn't as interesting as she sounds, hyper zealot with a big hammer (he was my favourite, himbo Tristan ftw), murder cultist, murder cia agent. Their quests aren't as fun or as detailed as the ones in the first game. Let's take my favourites from both games: Parvati in the first and Tristan in the second. Tristan is trying to solve a murder. You help him a bit, fight some bad guys, if you're me talk to people to keep them alive, find the murderer, he feels a bit sad about it and then nothing else. Parvati is recruited, gets a crush, and asks you to help her prepare a first date with all these cute little scenes of her getting ready and panicking about looking nice enough. Tristan feels like an NPC quest. Parvati feels like a person asking a friend for help. The vibes are just not the same.
The endings are not great either. I tried my best to get the BEST ending, supposedly, which is the cooperation one. In the end, both factions I worked hard to get to work together hate eachother and ruin everything for the colony despite my best efforts. Companions don't get very happy endings, and if they do it's very milquetoast like "Oh I'm less murdery and have friends" like cool I guess. 

All in all while I still recommend picking up The Outer Worlds 2 for it's gorgeous levels, the fun designs and fun combat, don't go in expecting the true quality that was in the first game.

7.5/10, the Starfield we shoulda had and just a good RPG in general. Fallout New Vegas in space. 

*edit* : after reading other reviews i need to mention i had no problems with save errors or crashes. maybe 2 in 92 hours but i DID have to play around with the settings to stabilize the frame rate, which is weird cuz i have a 5080 and a AMD 9950. if you have issues try running the game in fullscreen with an unlimited frame rate. 

8 years ago this woulda been an 8 or 9/10. a massive improvement over The Outer Worlds and everything that Starfield should have been. this game also left me with something that both of those games didn't: a desire to replay the game again. 

is it worth $70? debatable. it was worth it to me but i can afford it. its definitely worth your time tho. 

Outer Worlds 2 is a great RPG in that most builds are viable and your skill points matter in the way you interact with the story. wanna be a charismatic nerd? an idiot murder hobo? ninja? a lil bit of everything? this game will reward you no matter what you wanna do. its lighter on the comedy than OW1 tho. there is still plenty of comedy and satire but its a much more serious game than OW1, more in line with Bioshock in the mood of the game. instead of waiting to see what new zany critic of capitalism and human greed they had in store next i was actually concerned with how my decision to help the Corporatists would effect Arcadia in the end. 

this game made me actually care about the consequences of my actions. i sympathized with the socialist faction and even the corporate faction to some degrees. behind the corporatists were low level people just trying to live a better life. behind the socialists were trying to elevate their society to be something better. i felt bad when i gave an advantage to one side or the other. 

the actual gameplay is adequate for 2025 but just so. the combat mechanics are fine and the platforming is adequate. this game is very good at giving you multiple ways to get through a level. do you wanna sneak all the way through? or murder hobo? maybe even find some credentials that allow you to get through unscathed? 

the companions and characters, usually Obsidians strong point, are pretty weak in this game. i expected a specific party member to revolt when i had to constantly wipe out his people. instead, they gave a half baked explanation as to why he excused it. they also had pretty cookie cutter archetypes for the other companions. 

the story was much more mature and serious than OW1. i went in expecting zany antics of the first game but i was actually drawn into a serious battle for control, and the continued existence, of a solar system. it still had plenty of sillygoofy but it was well balanced with a serious mandate to save Arcadia. 

overall this is one of my favorite games since Fallout New Vegas and one of the best game i've bought since Mass Effect 3. it may not be worth $70 to most people but it is certainly worth your time.

Outer Worlds 2 impresses with its rich character creator, excellent missions, and fantastic art. It leaves something to be desired with its relatively simple gameplay, and is emotionally less ambitious than competitor titles, but I really liked it.

–

The game really is all about the choices you make in the creator. It defines your experience, and who you are is reflected throughout the game.

You choose a background, two of several game-defining “traits”, such as “brawny” or “brilliant,” starting skills, and appearance. Appearance options are fantastic, though sadly in dialog you never see your character: a strange choice, and mistake, I think, on Obsidian’s part.

I loved playing around with the character creator and deliberated at some length on what sort of character I wanted to play. Perhaps an abrasive half-robot brawny cop? Maybe a dumb but inspiring space Frodo? I ultimately settled on a sickly professor who makes up for his lack of martial prowess with knowledge of science, medicine and engineering.

–

The best of Outer Worlds 2 are its missions. These are relatively self-contained, story-heavy, linear-ish levels that proceed through several rooms in an interesting indoor space. There are perhaps 20 of these. 

You can choose all sorts of approaches to completing these missions, including stealth, hacking, running and gunning, and occasionally talking your way out of combat.

One of my favorites was a level set in an old Protectorate museum, complete with dioramas and artifact-filled secret back rooms. Each mission has an interesting story, NPCs to talk to, usually lots of interesting stuff to look at, and you’re offered opportunities to express your character by deciding someone’s fate. I liked these a lot.

–

The combat in Outer Worlds 2 has been improved over Outer Worlds 1. There are now four uses of energy, up from one. There are now grenades. Stealth takedowns have been added. Loot seems to be a bit better. There are more, and more interesting, perks and the “flaws” system has been expanded and improved.

I liked the “Very Hard” difficulty and recommend it.

I played the game as a run-and-gun heavy-science build, playing ranged for the first half of the game, swapping to melee for the last half once I stumbled upon a build that made me nearly invincible. 

I liked combat. It’s straightforward, FPS-style, and I’d describe it as “not strategic, but fun.” I enjoyed struggling in the beginning, then transitioning into something like an unstoppable Space Marine towards the end.

–

Outer Worlds 2 expands on the lore and setting of the first game. I liked the new factions, and liked how they weren’t just flavors of more corporations. 

The companions are where Outer Worlds 2 improves significantly over the first game. Every faction is represented by a different companion in this one:

* From the Earth Directorate, you have Miles, your fellow agent, and VAL, your flying robot. VAL is funny (her banter is the best of all companions), and Miles is a good hang.
* Tristan, from the Protectorate, was a standout. He’s an Arbiter, a kind of Judge Dredd, and you can encourage him to embrace learning facts before executing people. I liked his naive but sweet persona. 
* Marisol from the Order is another standout. Her voice acting is just fantastic. She’s a nice old grandmother-type who is also something of a deadly agent. She never left my party, once found. The game improved dramatically once I discovered her.
* Inez from Auntie’s Choice is the weakest companion, I think. I didn’t like her quests, and she’s a bit hard to look at with animal parts grafted to her.

(There’s a final companion from the evil cultists who I did not recruit.)

Tristan, Marisol and Miles made me care about the world, and I think Obsidian did a good job with them, certainly better than Outer Worlds 1 and a little better than Avowed, even, in my book. Though, I do think they fall short of non-Obsidian competitors.

–

The game looks and sounds great. Indoor scenes are well-lit and full of interesting stuff. The different factions all feel quite different: Auntie’s Choice with its seedy Times Square feel, the Order with its golden cathedrals, the Protectorate with its art deco and early 20th century propaganda posters.

Characters look fantastic, as do their costumes. With just a couple exceptions, it’s a beautiful game. I also absolutely love the 2D art in the game, shown on posters and paintings in-world, and on loading screens and certain other contexts. There’s this space/art nouveau style that I’ve never seen before and is just fantastic. The animations are wonderful as well: I never got tired of seeing my character mix some chemicals or use engineering to rejigger a contraption.

The game is also very solidly made. I encountered no crashes. I had one quest get stuck in the journal, but could complete it. Otherwise, I found the game to be solid and well made.

–

And now, for some gripes.

While most of the levels are good, the open world areas aren’t as good. Exploration isn’t really rewarded as in better open world games (including Obsidian’s own Avowed). The first open world island, Paradise Island, is especially bad, and you’re forced to stay there for several hours before the game gives you the next big mission, and the game opens up and markedly improves. Paradise Island also has one of the worst quests in the game, having to do with two towns of competitor factions – you’re forced to choose one faction and literally cannot help the other.

Combat, while fun, is not deep, and I like deep, crunchy systems. In this game, combat is fast, combat pause is tied to a resource that you want to spend on other things, companions can’t be given orders, there are very few abilities, and there are relatively few perks compared to other RPGs.

I have to complain about emails. I read emails in real life, for my job, and the last thing I want to do is play a game where I have to read email. In Outer Worlds 2, the world is littered with terminals, and the game relays much of its story on emails within those terminals. I want the story, but I don’t want to have to interpret an email thread to get it.

Skill checks in dialog aren’t as interesting as Obsidian thinks they are. There’s not much gameplay in having a conversation with someone and picking the option that I decided 30 hours ago my character would be good at. Compared to combat or exploration (both of which are rich in gameplay), picking a dialog option is sort of lame. I wish there was something more to conversations; maybe a mini-game, maybe even a dice roll. Something more.

While I like Outer Worlds 2, I also found myself sometimes falling asleep playing it, especially on that terrible starting island. When I think about why, I find that, in comparison to other RPGs I’ve played this year, OW2 frontloads all its most interesting gameplay choices early (how you build your character), while you merely sit back and see how those choices pan out over the course of the game, rather than engage you with interesting systems *throughout* the game.

There need to be more systems, and the systems we have need to be more interesting.

I also wish the designers would swing a little harder for emotional payoffs. I want a game to try and get me to cry, or be angry, or amorous even. I want it to make me feel things. I want heroic sacrifices, and dramatic transformations, and sex – or at least the option to have it. Outer Worlds 2 is a little restrained in terms of emotions. The strongest feelings it elicits are “huh,” and “neat,” and the occasional chortle. I do appreciate its humor, though.

–

I enjoyed the game. It’s well-made, and looks and sounds great. While I’d have liked deeper systems and more engaging session-to-session gameplay, I still found myself wanting to return to it for several nights in a row, once I graduated beyond the terrible Paradise Island.

The Outer Worlds 2 is an amazing game. For starters, both Outer Worlds are fantastic, but the gunplay was  incredibly fluent and fun in this game. Overall, I thought the story was interesting and darker compared to the first game, I enjoyed it a lot. I honestly liked most of the crew except for a select few. There were some really good companion quests that were some of my favorite quests in the game. The upgrades and passive buffs each companion gets over time is also super cool and unique.

Max level should be increased to level 40 as you can get to level 30 fairly quick if you enjoy completing every side quest/ companion quests as you progress.

The re-playability in this game is fantastic. You can make some extremely fun builds with the skill points, weapons, armor and abilities at your disposal. If you want to try something different, you are always able to do so.

Adding an option to re-spec your characters skills and perks should be added for players that don't want to create new save files, but I personally believe that is the best part of this game. Starting a new game after completion and trying something different can give a whole new experience and perspective on this game. From a melee slasher, to a gunslinger, sniper or science weapon enthusiast; each game can give its own play-style that makes the experience fresh and fun. The world design, story and overall craft of the game is excellent.

The game is fun and very well crafted but I can not recommend a game with a glaring save issue that has been present since the first game (The Outer Worlds). For those not in the know, there is a common bug where saving your progress will sometimes just, stop working. There is a fix for this by either creating a directory for a new save path because the installer just misses it, or by logging into you Xbox account via the game load in. This can not be done while in-game nor can it get your saves back while you are playing.

Regardless for the fact there is a fix I truly can not recommend a game that just stops allowing you to save until you notice it and subsequently makes you lose 5-7 hours of gameplay.

One caveat: the game still has a really terrible bug that causes saves to sometimes randomly stop working. It's not an issue if you pay attention to the save confirmation popup when you quick save. But if you are unfortunate enough to not notice right away, you risk losing significant playtime (the first time I experienced this I lost 2 hours of progress, but once I realized this could happen, I just made sure I checked that my quicksave confirmations were popping up). I can't believe the game has been out for over 2 months and this still hasn't been resolved!

Otherwise, I'm giving this a "recommended" because I feel like the game's rating is a bit skewed.

It's a really fun experience, and scratches an itch. For folks chasing that New Vegas high, this is the closest I've come in some time. I know some people complain about the way character specs work but I think that's a far more enjoyable challenge than just making all combat souls-like in games, which seems to be the favored trend these days. I really hope we get more titles in this series. This was an improvement on the first OW, and I hope they continue improving and listening to player feedback going forward.

Anyway, the game isn't perfect, but it's a hell of a lot of fun.

I have finished my first playthrough and am ready to offer a review. It took me roughly 44 hours to complete. While I did not 100 percent the game, I am confident I experienced the vast majority of its content.

I played through the first Outer Worlds multiple times and, while I would not call it a bad game, it always felt a little too shallow and underdeveloped for me to fully enjoy. The Outer Worlds 2, by contrast, represents a clear improvement across almost every area. It builds on the strengths of the original while addressing many of its weaknesses.

Exploration is more engaging, the gunplay feels more satisfying, and companions now have distinct personalities alongside meaningful side quests that encourage genuine investment in their development. I also appreciated that most quests can be approached in multiple viable ways. Quest design shows far more variety than in the first game, where objectives often felt repetitive and increasingly uninteresting by the endgame. In contrast, I enjoyed my entire playthrough of TOW2 from start to finish.

The factions and their associated lore are more compelling, and the in-game radio stations add a great deal of atmosphere while exploring the environments. I also found the main story engaging and felt consistently motivated to keep playing to see how events unfolded. It had a few interesting revelations and plot twists that did well to keep me intrigued.

Overall, this sequel is a substantial improvement over the original. I hope its success leads to a third entry in the series. I would strongly encourage anyone reading this to pick it up and give it a play.
