However, they added some great stuff that sorts some of the annoyances from the first (such as not needing to micro manage the companions loadout) as well as some new mechanics (being able to stealth kill will always get a thumbs up from me). No game is perfect and we can not expect it to be, it's fun and the negatives are so tiny they don't really matter. Just let us save Trooper Ashby!

I really wanted to like this. Outer Worlds 2 is a game pretending to be a role-playing game. It’s packaged beautifully, everything looks good, everything functions, the presentation is slick, but beneath that polish there’s nothing resembling real choice. There’s no meaningful role-play here. You can’t be truly cruel or morally complex, you can’t meaningfully go off-script, and far too often you simply aren’t allowed to complete quests unless you invested in the “correct” skill ahead of time. Didn’t build a hacker? Too bad. This quest is now locked behind a number, and the game can’t be bothered to offer alternatives: no passwords to find, no side entrances, no creative solutions. I played without quest markers or a mini map, so believe me, I looked. There’s nothing there.

The writing is “fine,” I guess. The story occasionally hints at becoming something interesting, but instead of exploring depth or flexibility, the game just buries you under hours of dialogue that mostly exists to compensate for how shallow everything else is. And when most quests boil down to “walk to place, press a button, walk back,” the slog sets in fast.

The characters feel bland, contrived, and assembled to fill obvious demographic or tonal quotas rather than to be genuinely compelling. The quests are short, shallow, and unimaginative, padded out with endless conversation instead of substance. In pretty much every meaningful way, the first game was better. This one feels hollow, an RPG in presentation only, never in spirit.

early review so things may change, but i'd give this a tepid recommend depending on how desperate you are for This Sort of Game. it's much better than the original in terms of world design - the weird silent emptiness of every single area in OW1 has been at least partly filled by loot, lore, and FNV style radio - but it definitely lacks something. there's funny moments, but fewer than the first game, and instead of replacing the jokes with lore or character work or, y'know, any of the stuff Obsidian used to be known for, it's mostly just filled with the sort of vapid nothingness Bethesda churns out. none of the characters are particularly interesting and none of their views or beliefs come off as genuine. everyone's just a stock character - their dialog is better written than Bethesda could ever manage, but i don't care about them any more than i would a Skyrim character.

the game side of things is genuinely pretty good, but i miss the days when Obsidian would go out of their way to one-up and roast their predecessors' (BioWare, Bethesda) bad habits and sloppy world building. here, and in Avowed, the only bad habits are their own. now that all they have to react to is their own corporate takeover there's just no chance of genuine satire shining through - Old Obsidian would have reveled in the chance to mock their new overlords, but new Obsidian either sold that freedom away or isn't inclined to bother. doesn't help that the first game had a lot more bite, at least so far in my playthrough. even the inexplicably straightlaced opening is the sort of tired cliche Old Obsidian would go out of their way to lampshade in games like KotOR 2 and FNV. maybe it's for the best that Microsoft doesn't go ahead with New Vegas 2. i'm not sure the Obsidian of today is up for filling its own shoes like that.

Honestly, I'm a big fan of The Outer Worlds 1 and I was very excited for the second game... that said, I like this game a lot, but I don't love it like I did the first one.

In the first game, I could accept some uninteresting side locations, low monster variety, overall easy gameplay and subpar combat... in fact I expected it since it was a more experimental game with lots of uncertainty.

However, the second game having most of these same issues is a bit of a let down I'm not going to lie. 

- Monster variety is still very low, you have all these planets and potential alien lives yet not many interesting creatures living there.
- Many side locations are very uninteresting, and the interesting ones are small or unchallenging and mostly lore stuff.
- Game is once again not challenging overall, enemy AI is stupid as hell and easy to exploit.

That said, this is NOT a negative review; the game is still Outer Worlds at its essence and like I previously said, I loved Outer Worlds; but make no mistake, if you are looking for more Outer Worlds, BUY THIS RIGHT NOW, but if you are looking for a significant improvement like I was; you will probably be let down.

If I had to give it a score, I would give it a 6/10; not a bad game but far from perfect and has not only many areas for improvements but also a lot of untackled potential.

EDIT: 
Read this if you're having constant crashes, and you've tried everything on the forums with no luck. It worked for me, maybe it will work for you.

I was getting a random UE crash error every 20 minutes or so, on average. I searched for answers, and it seems to be a common complaint. I tried all the things people suggested; lower settings, turn off frame-gen, limit FPS, monitor temps, update drivers, etc. None of that worked. But then while looking at temps in HWinfo I noticed that my CPU's built-in iGPU was active. I'm running an RTX5070, and I assumed Windows would just default to that for games, but it seems like the game, Windows, or both were trying to use it for overlays or something, which I assume was causing resource conflicts. Idk, but finally, I disabled the iGPU in BIOS, and voila! No more crashes! I was even able to crank up my settings back to high. Obsidian should really add this to their troubleshooting page. Hope this helps someone else.

I know this is a small thing, but you can *tell* where they spent time and where they didn't. All over the place, even with graphics maxed out, you can **SEE** where the high-poly models are and where the lower-poly models are - and they'll both be in the world, and they'll both be NEXT to each other. And on all the different terrain models, everywhere, you can see huge, 4-pixel-wide textures of "grass" spread across several textures. And the "high v low" poly differences feel completely random like, it'll be a 2 rocks in the distance that you can walk to but it will ALSO be just like, literally the road I'm walking on, that I'm supposed to walk on. Then the buildings will be high res. Combined, this gives the entire game a very rushed feel, and it pulls me out of the game each time I see it.

As I typed this review, the "Generating Shaders" view (which happened both times I've started the game so far) crashed - but the crash window is hidden behind the game, so like ... I couldn't tell it crashed. That's just disappointing.

To be clear, I loved the first one; but the executives really didn't seem to love the sequel. The corners cut really disappoint me.

The Outer Worlds 2 - Overall IMHO score: 6/10 - a solid "meh"

The Outer Worlds 2 has it's charms and has it's flaws, like any modern game. The unique setting is much the same as the first game in the series and very little has changed, "more of the same" is the best way to describe this one. So if you enjoyed the first one, you'll like this one.

SCORES:
Sound: 5/10 - nothing special
Voice Acting: 7/10 - occasional humor makes for a good time and possibly a LOL moment or two.
Story: 4/10 - fractured and disjointed, very hard to follow past a very broad sense.
Theme: 6/10 - Feels about right. Still fun and entertaining enough.
Combat: 5/10 - Still a solid, "Meh" nothing special here.
Graphics: 6/10 - A slight bump for the unique art style of this series but nothing special here.

PROS
- occasional humor in dialogue makes for a few chuckles now and then and reminds me of days gone by, when it was ok to have a joke or two in a game. Before everyone's butts got all puckered up and super sensitive. 
- Same gameplay as the first game which is both a pro and a con as on one hand it's familiar and safe but on the other hand not enough has changed to make it feel fresh or new.

CONS
- I absolutely hate the quest tracker in this game. The way they have the missions laid out often left me feeling like I had no idea what to do next. This caused the story to feel like a disjointed distraction, a MAJOR disappointment for me. The markers often left me feeling lost when in a large compound and trying to find my way to the marker. Not fun, IMHO.
- I could be missing something but most every weapon feels like a pea-shooter. Sniper shot to the head on a standard enemy and it doesn't kill them??? mmmk.
- Enemies AI is quite is a bit short on the "I" portion of that equation, lacking intelligence. Example; you agro an enemy, they run to cover and immediately leave the cover to run around for a moment before returning to the exact same cover they left. DERP!
- I never came across a moment where I felt the need to stop and "smell the roses" meaning, I never had a moment where I had to stop and appreciate the artwork in a scene. While it's not needed it is nice to have in a game like this.

OVERALL
It's a game worth getting if you find it on sale and need to scratch that itch. It has a place in anyone's library but it's not a GOTY type game by any stretch of the imagination.

TL;DR. I have a few hundred hours in KOTORs/MEs and about 3,000 on FO/ES games- and I do recommend this game, absolutely. But anyone who's played the aforementioned knows all these games have their issues, mine with this one are as follows (scatterbrained, just bulletin notes):

The character creator SUCKS. We can't even edit the chin, cheekbones or even mouth for the face. And the face is ALL we can do. I'd like to see those options; PLUS at least some small body modification. Even if that modification is as simple as the fat/skinny/muscle triangle from FO4 with the addition of a height slider. BUT the addition of prosthetics was a 10/10 amazing idea, keep that please.

The movement is a bit fast and loose. I like the speed you get, and the climbing. But accidentally flying over a railing 50m into the ocean from a standing start made me salty. Oh and as a side note, PLEASE let us swim next game. I hate dying to water in 2025.

The combat seemed stiff at first, but after playing it and getting used to it, it got a lot better. And now honestly it's probably my favorite combat out of these games. With that TTD it really feels like a fight scene from Kingsmen or something with some bad*** slo-mo shots.

The story is good, nothing crazy but kept my interest. Personally, I think it's as good as any other story from this genre of game.

The companions aren't sycophants, they will leave your ***! THANK YOU. My only request is to see deeper relationships/romance. I know Obsidian never really did that, KOTOR2 closest we got (I think) and that's still not really a romance. But I'd love to see that next game. The banter between them was really nice, would love to see more of that.

The radio was a really cool idea, but I think it definitely falls short for a long-term source of entertainment. The music is well produced and sounds like the genre intended, but after awhile the quirky lyrics get a bit egregious. I'd like to keep 1 or 2 stations that way maybe, but have a station with some classic oldies would be nice. Or just don't have EVERY song be so on-the-nose. For example, CP2077 did a great job doing this with Run The Jewelz & D.O.H's songs.

My first completed run, I did a melee stealth build. Which is always my favorite to do in these games (assuming it's possible and plausible). I really enjoyed the stealth and melee way more in this one than the first one. The progression was SUPER satisfying overall. Started rough and slow, but mid levels and you start really terrorizing the shadows. And by late/end game, you just become an outright killing machine (stealth or not), which I don't think I've ever seen a game do for stealth. It was super fun. 
Killing an enemy hideout, then spending 30 minutes picking up random objects I'm never gonna use, THAT'S the true ScrollOut experience. And I love it still.

Overall, I really enjoy this game.

Honestly, I was expecting more from this sequel, but it feels like they just recycled the first game with very few improvements. The maps are still small and feel kinda limited. There aren’t many new guns to play with, and it honestly just feels like the same game with a price tag that went up. Disappointing for a sequel — not worth the hype.

The first game felt like a great mod for FNV, but the sequel has a fully realized universe and RPG system with original ideas. It's also a really fun shooter with amazing reload animations and unique loot that scales with your character. Every skill provides some sort of passive combat buff and so everything feels viable. If you have half-decent aim, then the "Observation" stat admittedly makes the game too easy cuz you can farm ridiculous damage from headshots. Stack weak spot damage, sneak damage and crit damage for not a lot of investment.

So many other fantastic things about this game. Level design is great, movement feels smooth, the graphics are excellent and well-optimized. The writing is funny and also genuinely emotional at times. There are 3 radio channels with ALL ORIGINAL SONGS; an astounding amount of work went into writing these and they're all satirical masterpieces. Some of my favorites are "The Uncomplicated Loaner" and "Fatal Attraction."

I can tell this is so much better than the first because I have been starting new playthroughs constantly. I played the first game once and never felt interested in touching it again. The world and NPCs feel extremely natural and reactive even compared to other ImmSims of this style, and the static enemy levelling is fair since they show an icon next to enemies that are way stronger than you. This lets you know to ♥♥♥♥ off until you level up and then come back. Somehow they manage to make conversations NOT feel incredibly awkward. Even when involving more than one NPC! I think it helps that the camera actually breathes and moves during the scenes.

Downsides? The wireless does not persist into loading screens which must be an engine restriction because there's no way it's their choice. It breaks the momentum and immersion instantly. Also some of the achievements are bugged and that can be incredibly frustrating when you've earned them fairly. 

Some of the mods are completely useless: extra weapon/grenade holsters are a joke because you can switch your loadout anytime by just pressing tab, and applying a scope to a sniper rifle is actively nerfing yourself. 

It also feels like such a shame that we get pages of awesome loot and can't equip our companions with anything. I imagine they wanted to streamline companion customizing for the idiot action-only gamers, but really they missed an opportunity to let us go nuts with the itemization. If we could fully modify the armor, weapons and skills of companions it would open up much more opportunities for buildcrafting.

On the topic of buildcrafting, the final and major flaw I have is the lack of any real stats overview. Why can't I view all of my character's stats in a ROLE-PLAYING GAME ffs. This game is weird AF like it's amazing but also a bit sloppy in places.

Update: initially I complained about no walk button for PC players, but they added it in version 1.1. Nice!

I truly do not understand the weird phenomena of hating everything Obsidian does post New Vegas.

Is this a perfect game on par with RPGs like Cyberpunk and Witcher? Absolutely not. Does that mean this game doesn't deserve a playthrough? No. This is a VERY SOLID Space/Sci-Fi RPG. There are many choices for you to make, deep progression and a solid amount of areas to explore. Despite what people like to say about this game, your choices do matter. Your decisions directly open or close dialogue options to key NPCs that can affect how the quest proceeds.

One of my biggest gripes from the first one was that each planet kind of felt like the same environment. This game fixes that and has created some truly beautiful areas that are fun to explore. The story is also a lot better than the first. Each character you can talk to gives more context to what is happening and enriches the story.

The factions are less "blended together" if that makes sense. The first game, all of the factions felt very similar, while in this one, you can definitely tell which NPC is in what faction just by the way they talk. I will admit, they could have gotten a little more creative with the factions, but they still get the job done. There is no "right answer" for picking a faction to follow or get your reputation up for. Each one has flaws and the game is designed to not have a "preferred option". I see people complain that every faction has flaws, and I do not see it as a negative mark on the game. That is good game design, because now you aren't pushed in the direction to follow any certain faction and you are given ultimate freedom to follow who you want.

The new types of guns they added are SO FUN! Doing a Science build and finding unique Plasma and Shock weapons was so gratifying. Hitting enemies with the shock effect and stun locking them was so funny.

Each companion brings a little bit of their faction to you. It's a nice way of bringing representation for each faction into your little squad. Each companion has opinions on your actions and what is going on in the story. I really like Niles. He's your little protege that you can shape to your liking as he looks to you for a lot of guidance. I genuinely felt a connection of brotherhood with this companion and always brought him with me.

The enemies do feel a bit like bullet sponges in some areas, but this is largely due to you not putting points into Guns, Melee, Leadership. You'll need that extra damage later down the line. This brings me to one of my biggest critiques though. Skill point allocations feels very slow. There's too many skills that alter gameplay, yet they only give you 2 points to allocate per level. I think 3 would be perfect here.

Now, for the price point. Definitely way too expensive. There just isn't enough here to justify a $70 price point. I'd say this is worth it for $50 or less, so keep an eye out for a sale.

For my final paragraph, I want to say the complaint of the "anti-capitalism" satire is really goofy lol. This is the narrative and soul of the game. You saw it from the trailer, so why did you expect any different? You made a bad purchase based on your own beliefs, don't take it out on the developer.

No, Obsidian this is not good enough in 2025. You KNOW how to make a good game so what is this?

Graphics: 8/10  
The game is visually quite successful.

Performance: 5/10  
Another unplayable game without DLSS/FSR on an RTX 4090/5090  
and unplayable on lower-end graphics cards without frame gen + DLSS/FSR.

Gameplay: 6/10  
Too many things recycled from the first game: the same weapons, the same items, the same armor, practically the same textures, buildings, etc...  
The combat is still as bad as in the first one: too easy, too messy, not tactical enough, not enough enemies.  
Companions are totally useless both in combat and as friends.  
The spaceship isn't featured enough, we spend so little time inside it that we even forget we have one it just serves as a TP point.

Story/Lore: 6/10  
The main story is decent but nothing extraordinary; the lore is still as wacky as in the first game, Let's hope that if there's a 3, it takes place in a very very distant galaxy or decades later so we get new stuff, extraterrestrials, etc...

Soundtrack and Sound Design: 8/10  
The sound atmosphere and music are successful and fit the game's theme well.

Characters: 6/10  
The characters are generally well-designed; their personalities are a bit too caricatured, but that's the game's universe, so it works.  
On the other hand, the companions are really average—they hardly ever talk, and the friendly/romantic relationships with our companions are still just as non-existent.

Conclusion: Overall, it's not too bad, but compared to the first one, there's been zero evolution, zero risks taken—the same flaws and qualities as the first.  
To the point where it feels more like playing a DLC than a real sequel.  

If we compare it to older games like Mass Effect, for example, we end up thinking that others were already doing better in 2007 in almost every aspect of the game (except graphics, but that's logical).

In the game's current state, it's really impossible to recommend this game to anyone but fans of the original. It's a pretty stiff 5/10, maybe 6/10 if this is your kind of game (the quasi-open world FPSRPG type), but it's ultimately held back by obvious cuts and a rush to the finish line. Light spoilers below.

The first maybe 10-15 hours of the game is fun and fresh, it's a good setting and it makes use of its aesthetic really well. However, once you hit around the 20 hour mark and on it slows way down into an almost slog that gets progressively more sluggish. Around the time you finish Dorado, or maybe the halfway point, you'll start to notice the malaise kick in.

Once you hit Cloister and Praetor, the last two planets, you notice the content cutting. You can feel where they meant for there to be more in the way of major (and maybe minor) factions, more quest-givers and stuff, but the settlements at this point become largely lifeless with maybe one or two people to talk to (as opposed to the first planet which had several, several people to talk to and quests to do, and the second planet which is somewhat where it begins to dwindle).

Cloister is particularly dry. Entire encampments of faction bases with nobody to talk to, no quests, nothing. Targets to level the Rookie's Reward with, I guess, if you ran out early. Praetor is where I felt like they had intended for there to be more interactivity with the villain faction of the game, but that all falls pretty short too. Lots of dead ends.

Just overall, kind of a messy ride. It's no New Vegas though Obsidian continues to try and capture that magic again, to no avail. I think that's fine, but they need to stop pretending it's what it isn't. Also it's *extremely* dark. Like, grimdark almost. I understand wanting to have morally grey factions, but all of these guys suck. They're all some variation of authoritarian regime, you just pick between religious or capitalist, with the third unincluded/cut option being monarchist. But even that could arguably be fine if any of them felt satisfyingly competent, but they don't, they're all losers. You *can* make the two major factions work together, but it's very shallow. Cool for moments, but they're still losers who just happen to be working together to be joint losers.

Also Sub Rosa is a thing for all of half the game? They vanish. They exist in sprinkles. Why the hell do we have a reputation meter for them? Maybe I missed something with them, but it feels like they had the third faction's reputation lying around in the code (for the very obviously cut third option, [spoiler]the Protectorate[/spoiler]) and they just substituted with a third, even less fleshed out smuggler faction because...reasons? I don't know.

Companions for last here: they're fine. Nobody stands out as amazing quite like Parvati did in the original, but nobody's outright obnoxious either IMO. They're here and there on a scale though, and some are certainly better than others in terms of content and characters. They have very little interactivity with quests, though. Sometimes they outright stay silent when you'd otherwise expect them to, you know, speak up in a conversation that seems like it would be important to them. Banter is also basically nonexistent, and the Incognito somehow feels emptier once you finish their quests despite having them all there. Also no Sub Rosa companion, which furthers my theory from above.

Anyway, decent chunk of playtime all that aside (mostly roaming and clearing out enemy spots), granted only worth it when it's on sale. Not worth $60 USD, and barely worth maybe $40 with how incomplete it feels. If you didn't like Outer Worlds 1, definitely stay away from this one.

This is a good follow up to its predecessor. Like the former entry and Avowed, this game is a kind of "wide linear" game. You have a bunch of planets that have smallish hand-designed open-world areas, rather than being a giant open world game. Throughout them are a bunch of side quests and the game is packed with humor.

A number of things have improved though. The combat feels much smoother than the former entry and is simply more fun to play. The companions also feel more fleshed out in this one as is the general role playing options.

Regarding the companions, while they haven't reached the quality levels of something like Mass Effect or BG3, they're still improved. The thing holding them back is their stories still feel a bit too siloed. While there is some ambient banter between companions when you take them out, something is missing that makes it feel too much like you're the only one they're responding to. Maybe we need more interactions between the companions on your ship or something like that.

Regarding the RPG options, there are a lot of different ways to do things and lots of different conversation options for different character specs. In particular, I was a big fan of the flaw system. Flaws are bonuses with tradeoffs that get conditionally offered to you based on how you play the game. That is, you'll be playing the game and if you do enough of something (like steal a lot of items, let your companions be downed enough times, etc.) you'll get a dialog asking if you want to accept a flaw related to that. The flaw will have a positive effect and a negative effect, and they can be quite creative with them. For example the "kleptomania" flaw will let you sell stolen items for more, but your character will automatically occasionally steal items you are looking at, even if it's a very inopportune time! I ended up taking the "Flawed" flaw which required me to take every flaw that was offered to me. And boy did it force me to take some that I otherwise would not have taken! But this added to the experience! My character was not broken despite all the suboptimal flaws and it added flavor and humor to the experience.

If you liked the previous entry, you'll like this. If you didn't like the previous entry but enjoy these kinds of games more generally, there is a decent chance this one will change your mind, but it's not a sure bet. If you don't like these kinds of games, I don't know why you're here to begin with.

The biggest flaw the Outer Worlds will ever have as a game is that it will forever be compared.

The Outer Worlds is a fantastic game. The Roleplaying and RPG elements of the game go beyond and far for a modern triple A game. The character building is tough and fair, it allows you to spec into a character you want to play as, and the perk system allows you to fine tune your progression in a way that effects both inside and outside combat. The background and baseline character trait offer such a unique way of play and background nuance to how you play that it was downright fun. It made the game fun.

The  game itself so much above from the Outer Worlds 1 that it felt new. Every system that they could improve they improved. From the skills and attributes to the flaws system that rewards failure and quirks of being in a video game, the game got better. There were moments in the art direction of the game that had me at a state of wonder. This game is such a straight improvement on the Outer Worlds 1 that it feels like a different genre.

Thats the main issue that this game has, it is new. The time between the first game and this game was a generational leap. But to a lot of people from the outside looking in, this game was never viewed in that context. It and the Outer Worlds are still new to the gaming sphere and just new in general, it will always be judged as thus. The Outer Worlds 2 will always be judged as staying to close to its original, while changing things to much. To give you context of the time between the Outer Worlds 1 and 2, the timeline between those two being conceptualized , built, created, launched then did it a second time was within the window of not only a new Elder Scrolls being released, but a new Fallout is incredible. Before the second came out Bethesda themselves released a Sci-Fi game, failed, and the Outer Worlds 2 still released with more than Starfield ever could.

Thats the main issue with the Outer Worlds as a franchise. It will always be compared to everything else on the market. Obsidian invites the comparison at every moment it can. It was written by the guy who made Fallout, it was developed by the company that made a Fallout, because of that it has roots to the Elder Scrolls style of exploration, combat, quest. Its DNA is Bethesda, it was raised by Fallout, it was made in the modern age, its a product of our time.

But its not. I wrote it that way because thats the story the marketers are trying to instill in your heads. While its true, the Outer Worlds is also its own thing. It can stand on its own two feet and be what it is without the comparison. If you look at the Outer Worlds as it is, what it represents in the modern age and how it does that true game-play its one of a kid. Obsidian really took the time to craft out a really interesting story out of the world that Tim Cain made while taking it in an interesting direction. Its not my personal favourite, and I quite like the tone and feeling of the first game more, its still good. Its Bioshock 1 versus Infinite. Both fantastic game,s Same DNA, same world, just done differently. Its fantastic.

But because it has to also carry itself with the game-play and story directions of 3/NV and Skyrim because the connection is a given, it fails. Because while it takes from those games a lot, it does not take everything and builds itself upon its strengths. While there are places I wish it took more from Fallout/Bethesda, and other moments I wish the later took /much/ more of Obsidian, where it stands, the Outer World 2 is one of the finest First Person RPG's of its time.  Bar none.

I will love this game. It will be a game I always think its great. I just wish the game could get its own recognition without the obvious comparison.

There is no "Choices Matter" in this game. You do not get to make any choices, you only get to allocate skill points, blindly (2 points per level). If you're lucky, you might get to "choose" a dialog option in the future, that was unlocked by one of your skills. This is so absurd, having to "buy your choices" first.
You're not a psychopath because of your behavior and choices, you are a psychopath because you unlocked the perk called "psychopath", mid game, if you like. (the game designer is also the companies book keeper?)

The story is liner, with some "must kill or let live" and companion dialog thrown in. Shortcut here and there, via hacking, but you'll deprive yourself of content... replay value of a few lines of dialog.

All the gear only supports combat. No gear boosts skills, no companions offer skills, it's all you and your 2 points per level blindly allocating toward your "choices"... so you might go with combat, right?

Well, too bad, because this mediocre combat game, at best. All mobs are sponges, and the mods and effects have been "balanced" in a way where they provide no real distinction (add silencer -40% base damage, add plasma -40% base damage).

I think someone engineered the fun out this game, hell bent and offering "replay value" that is ultimately not there, rendering player choices irrelevant in the story and in combat.


So, is the story and setting worth it?
Well, maybe. Depends on how well read you are. There is a lot of "1984" with some "WW1" thrown in.
Some levels have very good design with well hidden alternate path options. Some levels have triple A graphics. But not all levels.

If you're looking at getting this game thinking it'll be like Fallout: New Vegas in space, don't bother. Ever since Obsidian's original writing team left all of their releases have been misses in that regard.

This game opens with you and your team being betrayed, and i spoil that with zero remorse because it's dumped on you with no ceremony whatsoever. I didn't even realize i was being betrayed until the cutscene took over, because there's no foreshadowing or buildup to it whatsoever.

Choices matter, world building is timely at a period where dystopian satire is particularly poignant. I don't think there's much like this out there now -- it's a bigger, better (in some ways), TOW with very loose ties to the original. 

There are some references and points from the first game that you can find here, and the companion stories are much more fleshed out. 

You navigate through morally gray areas with a gun -- all packaged in degrees of psychosis -- and your choice of skills, perks, and companions actually has an impact on gameplay, the world, and the story. That last part warrants multiple playthroughs since your decisions and company have consequences that stretch from early decisions and investments.

Looking forward to the DLC.

I’ve finished this twice and went out of my way for most of the achievements because the gameplay is just so fun. Combat has a ton of angles, and the traits/perks/flaws setup actually changes how you play; my very hard melee-only run feels like a different game to my first character.
*minor spoliers*
Politically though, the Protectorate stuff drives me up the wall. The game gives you plenty of time around them, but it won’t let you properly commit to that route; and the way the story frames it can feel like it’s insisting “the socialist/communist system is inherently worse” even when the alternatives are a corporate empire and a cult. That “you can’t even choose it, therefore it’s doomed/invalid” vibe really undercuts the roleplay.

And then there’s Auntie’s Choice; they invade a peaceful system, light the fuse on a war to shove capitalism in, and the writing barely lets you call it what it is. Even with the anti-choice (yeah, it took me until this review to realise the wordplay) companion in your crew, you can’t properly challenge it or express real disgust at what’s happening; you’re funneled into politely accepting it as background noise. The mechanics kept me playing, but those parts of the story sucks, hard.

Still; better than Starfield.

moment to moment gun play feels good so I was always excited to start a fight. While I appreciate not having to worry about carry weight and being overburdened, the near unlimited resources did make the game's difficulty trivial, so it might be worth playing on a harder difficulty (I usually default to normal for a first playthrough). The skill variety is nice too, I never specced into stealth but using the right companion and a silenced weapon, I could still stealth around and take weaker enemies out silently, but going loud was always fun. Using engineering, hacking etc to find better loot and alternative paths was always rewarding, and having a high speech skill feels good to talk your way out of encounters.

Story wise, its very good with well written characters. As is common in sequels, its not as impactful as the first game, but was still more than good enough for me to want to do optional content, to stay in the game world, and engage with its systems, while wanting to see the companion's stories, all the side quests I could find, and the main story itself.

It did feel a little short for the kind of RPG that it is, but being left wanting more is hardly a bad thing.

Also runs well on Steamdeck, obviously lower framerate and resolution, but still very playable (if you ever played the first game on Nintendo Switch, its pretty much that)

i really, really disliked outer worlds 1 for a few reasons relating to performance, quality, and world design, but after avowed was so fantastic i decided to give this a shot. 

so far i still don't like this game as much as avowed (which feels weird to say considering my general preference for science fiction over fantasy), but it is a noticeable step up over the outer worlds 1, even though it doesn't get over a few of the things that made me quit the first.

1. the visuals and feel of the game are much improved from 1: the first person locomotion feels more solid, the weapon models including reload animations are largely fantastic, often surprisingly so (the energy machine gun reload, holy guacamole). it still has that *weird* color palette from the first game, that bizarre green/orange mix on everything, but it works a bit better here than in the original. 

2. unfortunately, it's unreal engine 5, which often means the game doesn't run anywhere near as well as it seems like it should, and that's certainly the case here. i don't know what quirk of the engine means that developers can't get their UE5 titles running as well as Croteam did with Talos Principle 2, which looks fantastic and yet is much more smooth than any other title i've played on this engine.

3. they have done some work to break away from my biggest mechanical irritation from the original, which was the slavish copy/pasting of bethesda rpg mechanics into their world. i get that outer worlds is meant to be another take on the 3d fallouts that obsidian has IP control over, but the first game even copied technical limitations they didn't need to, including things that are an artifact of Gamebryo being a real old engine. the biggest example to me is that this new game actually has seamless interiors - in the first (and every single bethesda rpg) you'd have to watch a loading screen just to walk into a small house and loot two items. huge improvement, in my opinion

4. though this game has a lot of mechanical improvements over the first, they didn't take some considerate quality-of-life things i really appreciated in Avowed and now very much miss. for example: in avowed, being able to fast travel to your camp and then right back to where you left off was a small change with a big effect. in outer worlds 2 though, after you fast travel to your ship and get your business done, you then have to run all the way to the back of your ship, re-pick your party members, loadscreen out of the ship to the landing pad, fast travel to the closest marker, then walk the rest of the way to where you're going. every time. at least your first human companion lets you do workbench stuff on the go, but that just means that even if i didn't like him (i do), i won't ever want to replace him if i find an otherwise more useful/interesting character because it's a huge quality-of-life convenience.

5. tone: i rolled my eyes a little bit when the moon man guy started his meta monologue when i launched the game, but the cinematic cutscene upon starting a new game was an absolute tonal bullseye and made me feel much better. i usually don't even like or care about cinematic trailers/cutscenes because they feel detached from the actual game world, but in this case it was EXACTLY the kind of satirically-inspired depiction of old 1950s serials that seem like this series' whole point - the first game didn't ever do anything this effective. it was a good start, but after the pretty good tutorial level, once you actually get out and start exploring, the tone suffers because you run into what i think is outer worlds' biggest flaw:

6. world building: i haven't finished the game yet, but so far other than your first human companion, almost nobody feels like a believable person; everything and everyone feels like a series of jokes in the form of RPG quest objectives. even minor characters feel like a suspiciously self-aware archetype, like how GTA's version of "satire" usually involves characters just stating the bad thing they're doing. i get that everybody's supposed to be brainwashed towards either the fascist cult or the capitalist consumer-state, but every single non-directorate character so far feels like they are ONLY that relationship. it feels like how, in Deus Ex: Human Revolution, nobody talks about ANYTHING but robot arms. they may love the robot arms, or hate the robot arms, but it's all anybody expresses anything about. it makes it really hard to care in any way about anybody or the world.

the tutorial/opening sequence gave me hope that the game would break away from this dull depiction. you could feel a real tension between the language used by the elite, higher ups, and the fully brainwashed, and how actual people on the ground floor operate. this happens especially during the conversation with the bridge guard: you've heard the noble prince or whatever looping his "you're just an ant beneath my shoe and that's great!" dialogue over and over, and then you meet this guard who is clearly torn between what he's been taught and what he feels from his lived experience. he recognizes but chafes against the contradiction of turning you in to his superiors to specifically stop you from helping him prevent his imminent lobotomy. he's taught the lobotomy would happen either way, that it's HIS fault, and deep down he knows that's not right, but he's so immersed in this constantly reinforced ideology that he defaults to believing that the way things are is inevitable.

i saw the bridge guard as a great sign, as that's how most people at the bottom of oppressive societies actually operate. they know what they're taught is supposed to be right, but end up having to do a lot of mental work to keep themselves convinced that it's right, even and especially when they FEEL that it's wrong. this results in insecurity and lashing out at anything or anyone even implying that they can think differently. unfortunately, when you get to the first planet, it's back to outer worlds 1, with every auntie's choice character saying "by golly gee, exploitation is the auntie's choice way!" even when that exploitation is actively hurting them. there is no expressed tension between the individualism they were taught by the company, and at the same time being actively exploited for the good of *different* individuals, even when the latter is exactly what the cult, their enemy, is all about.

you can see this working better in fallout new vegas: even when characters are indoctrinated into the NCR, effectively an auntie's choice parallel that is also modeled after 1950's american capitalism, characters at the bottom rung often recognize and acknowledge flaws in the system and why they result in bad things. they then make little concessions against society's rules when possible, based on that recognition, to do something to protect themselves and/or their loved ones, even if they ultimately still believe in said system (see: the farmers near new vegas who aren't getting enough water to meet their crop quota). auntie's choice characters, at least in the first town, don't recognize these flaws, they LOVE them. they say them constantly as if they're good. and not in an insecure way, but in a convenient way that expresses to the *player* how silly this society is, but the characters themselves are just there to spout fake propaganda with fully empty brains. this behavior would make sense for the leaders of the first town, or auntie cleo, or many of the propagandic documents you find laying around, but if the society doesn't actually work, the people at the bottom will FEEL it. 

all this makes the world feel like one big series of jokes that unintentionally prop up the society we're supposed to be opposed to, rather than an actually flawed system that normal people are forced to inhabit. it's not satire when people just say "haha isn't our society so corrupt," you have to show the actual negative effects of that society.

Unless you're hankering to consume the most bland RPG ever made, skip it.  

From a story perspective, it's somehow even more shallow than Outer Worlds 1.  The companions are boring and uninteresting, the factions have no nuance to them (being unlikable cults, corporations, or communist dictatorships), and there's basically zero world building.  It makes rather odd story choices, like having the player start as a legendary government agent when ,you'd think, it would start you out as a rookie who has to work their way up to being legendary.  There's an unnecessary 10 year time jump that adds nothing to the plot and a revenge plot where you try to take vengeance on a character you knew for all of ten minutes.  Just bad.  At least the humor is dialed back and the tone is more serious.  Unlike like OW1 it's (mostly) not forty hours of "Heh, capitalism, amirite?".

The maps are way too large (in comparison to OW1 which were mostly too small) so enjoy hoofing it for five minutes to get anywhere.  The shooting feels better than the first but enemies are bullet sponges, so enjoy wasting tons of ammo.  The perks are an improvement.  Unlike OW1, where perks were mostly generic "Plus 10 percent to walk speed" crap, OW2's perk feel like what perks should be: gamechangers that define your build.

But that brings me to the most frustrating part of the game and the only part that stands out due to how bad it is: the skill system.  I'll be blunt, it sucks.  It's way too limiting (you only get 2 skills per level and, like all bad RPGs, the game has a level cap) and locks you out of most of the game.

If you're dying to bore yourself to tears, this is the game for you.  Otherwise, just replay OW1 (or better yet, stick with Fallout New Vegas).

I am really enjoying this game so far. It has a host of differences from the first one, at least in how it *feels*. The first one felt campier, with the definitely-not-Rick quest giver, and some of the theatrics. This one definitely feels a bit grittier. The hostile merger of the corporate overlords and the lack of decent factions rings so timely. 

This feels different from the first one, but is just as enjoyable to me.

I enjoyed my time with this game. Although not breaking any new ground, the story is solid. The gameplay is fun. The characters are well written and well voiced. The setting is varied and interesting. The levels are well designed.  Upgrades to the player character, companions, and gear seem impactful and are well implemented.

Each of the factions in the game is interesting and has a very unique design choice implemented for them. The totalitarian Protectorate, the Auntie's Choice corporatocracy, and the technocratic Order are all distinct and fun in their own way. At a certain point you head to the flagship of Auntie's Choice and there are near constant advertisements playing that often make it impossible to hear anyone even in dialogue. It's perfect. The protectorate has a 1984-style thought police where "mental refreshment" ensures strict adherence to social and political norms and bureaucratic hierarchy. The Order plays relaxing music that is "scientifically proven" to improve cognitive function. There are some real gems here, even in just the ambience surrounding the factions.

Like I said, the story doesn't really explore new ground. It is entertaining enough to make the number of hours invested into the game seem worth it. I played a speech-focused mostly passive build. I tried to deescalate as many fights as I could and I often attacked from stealth or avoided some enemies entirely. There really are many different ways to solve problems.

The combat is engaging and enjoyable. It is, for the most part, a first person shooter. There are options I didn't mess with that allow you to switch to third person camera. That's nice, but never seemed necessary. Although I was mostly focused on talking my way out of fights, I still had plenty of skill and perk points to invest into combat and stealth skills. The companion upgrades are generally good. Depending on your complement, you could have an entire group of ranged shooters or melee combatants, stealthy guys or healers. The combat, at least on normal difficulty, was straightforward but sometimes overwhelming. Some of the enemies are bullet sponges. I'm sure if I had invested in more combat-specific skills and perks, I probably would have had a much easier time in the fights, but most fights were manageable or had non-combat ways of avoiding or simplifying the attacks. I don't think a passivist run would be possible, I distinctly remember multiple story quests that required "clearing" an area of enemies. There's probably a way to knock enemies out but I never noticed it if there is.

The stealth system is pretty good. I found myself able to sneak around and not get noticed very often. Early in the game you receive a spray bottle gadget that dissolves enemy bodies so they won't be found by other enemies. This is a fun (if disturbing) way of keeping the action moving along without having to fiddle about with hiding bodies in containers or long grass.

In one particular mission I was able to sneak around a building with well over two dozen enemies. One by one I took them out and made the bodies disappear. By the end, no one had spotted me and everyone was dead and gone. I wasn't even that good at stealth, I just had a decent blade and a silenced sniper rifle that ricocheted weak spot hits into another nearby enemies' weak spots.

Now for the bad things: crashes, ambushes, and dialogue trees. I'll tackle these in order.

The game crashed several dozen times. A few times it was while it compiled shaders. I updated my graphic drivers and that mostly helped, but it still happened a few other times. Some certain actions seemed to trigger gameplay crashes but I can't really pinpoint an exact reason. Still other times, the game would just stop responding; this was usually when I tried to move in and out of the menu system too rapidly. Some of the crashes forced me to perform a hard reset. Other times it would pop up an error message. The problem is it would pop up an error message without bringing the error message to the forefront of the running programs. Several times I thought it was just a long loading time when, in fact, an error message was minimized in the background. In no situation did the error message tell me anything specific about the crash. It just said the program stopped working. As a related point, the game never properly closed when I quit to desktop. For whatever reason it continued to stay running in the background until I clicked the stop button in the steam interface. I have no idea what caused this.

I mentioned before that I focused on speech and stealth. In many situations, scripted ambushes take place. Enemies appear (sometimes out of nowhere) and attack. This occurs even when I am in stealth and have otherwise not revealed myself in any way. It wasn't that big of a deal, but it did break immersion. I would have liked to have had a full passivist playthrough available. That being said, for the most part, I was able to approach most situations in multiple different ways.

Finally, dialogue trees. There is a ton of dialogue in this game. It is a RPG game, so that is to be expected. What I didn't expect was the sheer number of skills and abilities which would show up as options in dialogue. That's not a bad thing on it's surface, but I frequently found myself missing out on dialogue because I would click on a skill/perk related dialogue choice and it would prematurely end the conversation or move past the other options in the discussion. Sometimes I would misunderstand what the dialogue check was suggesting (or it would have no context whatsoever) and it would lead to random outcomes. While not really game breaking these often led to frustration and a mild case of FOMO. I still feel I got quite a bit out of the storytelling, so this one isn't as impactful as the others.

In all, this game is great fun and is a well-told bit of sci fi. Some of the quests tackle very dark subject matters. This is especially true of the environmental storytelling. Piecing together what happened at a location can yield some rather grisly insights. Still, the game is worth a playthrough and generally remains lighthearted (at least in tone, if not always in content.) The final two areas of the game (the Archive and the Protectorate flagship) have one of the binary choices you get in lesser RPGs. You can even select dialogue options that completely conflict with how you've played the rest of the game. I haven't gone through and seen if those options vastly change the outcomes in multiple events, but my suspicion is that they do. On a small level your actions have consequences. On a bigger level your choices in the game seem to have no real bearing on the major story beats. The illusion of choice is strong in this game. Some events really do effect the rest of the game. The problem is that most of the choices seem to change nothing overall. It's still worth a playthrough. Maybe even two. If I do return, I plan to make a combat focused character who picks all the options that lead to fights. I have a feeling the game will turn out much differently even if the major story beats are generally the same. We'll see.

Interesting game, pretty similar to Fallout series in some key ways (skill-based dialogue options, perks and skill points), but with some unique twists and a much more important influence of character traits on story line outcomes. 

Some definite weaknesses as well. For instance, although there are several rich and fun maps, they all seem pretty linear, i.e., there is not much to explore that has absolutely nothing to do with the main story. 

The political messaging is over-the-top and fun, but I would have liked to have a third option not bound to the corporatists or religious order. Maybe like a left-wing or revolutionary option, rather than the same old status quo stuff again and again. 

Overall, well balanced game and lots of fun.

Truly a fantastic sequel, worthy of the vast library of amazing games Obsidian has released over the years. I was honestly a bit scared after Avowed, as despite that game not being as bad as many people claim, it was for sure not on the same level as other Obsidian titles. Fortunately, The Outer Worlds 2 kinda blows Avowed out of the water and delivers one of those amazing games Obsidian is known for.

- Overall major improvements over the first game in pretty much every way — more weapons, a longer story, more main factions, radios, and much more freedom of choice.
- A fantastic, funny, light-hearted atmosphere (mostly) that doesn’t take itself seriously and offers a lot of obvious criticism of modern-day society — from authoritarianism to consumerism to over-reliance on science.
- A great cast of followers, each unique and fun to interact with, as well as a strong cast of side characters and villains.
- A massive amount of freedom, especially in dialogue — just a classic RPG experience; say whatever the ♥♥♥♥ you want.
- A MASSIVE amount of weapons and armor, almost all of them great and fun. I had like 50 weapons by the end and didn’t want to sell any of them at all. So fun, so distinct.
- This also made gameplay feel unique — fights were more about testing different weapons you bought or picked up.
- A classic New Vegas–style slideshow ending, which gives tons of feedback after the game so you know what happened to the factions, characters, and locations in Arcadia. I’ll take 20 slideshows over any cutscene ending, ngl.

some negative points:
- By the end of the game, I was kinda jumping from place to place just to finish the final quests, which got a bit annoying — there’s some over-reliance on fast travel at that point.
- Many sections between missions were really empty; traveling from point A to point B would’ve been very boring and slow if it weren’t for sprinting and double jumping (which is fun, ngl, but still — a lot of empty space between major locations).
- The radio songs were really ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ funny for the first ~15 hours, but after that I was singing them in my sleep. More variety would’ve helped, though you can obviously turn the radio off.

TL;DR: Obsidian is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ baby back, and this is where the ♥♥♥♥ is. Massive hype for the next installment in this franchise!

This is a fairly conditional positive review. Outer Worlds 2 improves on the original in almost every way including stability (in my experience), quality of life, and exceptional gunplay, yet still suffers from my main point of contention with the first game which is just an overall shallow narrative. 

If you are expecting NV levels of faction depth and dynamics, you may be disappointed. The narrative choices feel more akin to a 'Choose your own Adventure' book as opposed to a fleshed-out story with branching paths. Dialogue options that matter are very polarizing and while there are some interesting consequences, most feel rather unimportant and boil down to skill checks or fight.

Mechanically, this game feels very polished and promising. Despite some minor crashing, I have run into very few optimization issues. The vast majority of the open world is seamless and what loading screens there are, are sufficiently fast. Despite the negativity, there is so much potential in this franchise, and I hope to see them cut loose narratively and really dive into these awesome factions more and create a longer experience in general. Overall, I would most likely not recommend this game at full price. Buy it in a year or two on a big sale and you will have a positive experience.

NOT FOR $70, ABSOLUTELY THE HELL NOT. 

This game is exactly what you'd expect, it's The Outer Worlds... again! It plays exactly the same as the previous entry. Quests boil down to 'go here, do/kill this and come back for a reward'. There's 6 companions all with their own questlines with two paths you can lead each down. It's still that retro-futuristic Bioshock-like setting but now with a new golden age comics evil fascist faction. The game looks and sounds absolutely gorgeous, Obsidian loves their art department and it shows. The flaws system this game introduces is fun, some are even beneficial to take and they're all optional to take. The reputation system also makes a return though has much less impact in this game as the previous one. 

Where the game starts to drift into unrecommendable, at full price, is in the ladder half of the game. There's this general feeling that there was more planned for this game that they just couldn't get to in time, like the game was rushed or shoved out. Some quests feel like they're meant to lead to more things or bring up topics that are just never readressed. The faction system feels like it was meant to include the Protectorate or at least some more factions than just Auntie's Choice and The Order, maybe even Sub Rosa who is more or less just a background faction much like the Glorious Dawn. The game feels like it should have more, so much more especially for the price but it doesn't. It's The Outer Words again but somehow with less content at a higher price. 

I can't recommend it at full price. $70 is a WILD amount for this game as it is. The Outer Worlds itself is only $30 and it has so much more polish than it's sequel. Hell, you can even get it along with ALL OF IT'S DLC for the SAME PRICE of THIS SINGLE GAME. If there's a sale and you loved the first game, pick this up. It's on par with the first game's DLCs, easily. But don't you DARE pay full price unless you like getting ripped off. Maybe you do. I don't know you. 

Moon Man's banter in the menu screen is cute though. It's like he was programmed to know my every game state. If that's where the extra $40 went I could forgive them for the price.

The good kind of open-world game starts with a densely developed world full of things that might logically exist in that world, with lots of nooks and crannies to explore, and then layers a story and quests on top of that.  The Outer Worlds 2 team took a different approach.  They created some quest locations, added a bunch of empty space in between them, and called it an open-world game.

The story isn't particularly well written.  You can easily be locked out of quest completions for reasons you could never anticipate. And despite having an extensive weapon and armor mod system, exactly zero of those mods have any meaningful effect.

You can tell the devs want you to replay the game to see the outcome of different choices, but the game is barely interesting enough to complete once, certainly not twice.

This game reminds me of a paint-by-numbers painting.  It basically resembles the thing it's trying to be, but it contains no artistry or magic.  You'll forget about it the second you stop looking at it.

This game was plagued by terrible design choices: from a gameplay perspective, from an environmental ambiance perspective, and from a story perspective (I won't spoil anything in this review). I sorta feel bad about posting this review because I'm sure a bunch of people worked hard on this game and felt (mostly?) good about shipping it, but I disliked it so much that I'm going to review it.

tl;dr - 
This game is terrible, but not for a single glaring reason. It's a myriad of poor design choices that come together to make it bad. A story with potential delivered unseriously by flat writing, marred by attrition-style gameplay (bullet-sponge enemies + low-damage weapons). Lackluster companions. Feels like most design decisions in the game were invented by figuring out how to make that system/dialogue/choice more quirky. So it's all quirky. And that's terrible.

Note: the worst aspects of the game show up post-prologue, so by the time I was super frustrated I was outside of my refund window. Instead I rage-uninstalled the game for a day or so before digging through mods to just get through it.

-------------------

---
Gameplay
---

Neutral: 
* If I'd loved this game, I would probably be questioning why no NG+ system (because there is not one). Rather than playing with Flaws in a first-run through the game, the Flaws system should have been rolled into a NG+ mode, to allow for a  lot of replay variety.

Pros:
+ 3rd person perspective. I really enjoyed this actually. I wouldn't have played otherwise. FPS is not for me.
+ Fortunately, the issues I hated about the gameplay were mostly solved via mods, and I was able to get through the game. I definitely will not replay the game though.

Cons:
- 2 you get skill points per level, and there are something like 15-20 skills. So: scarcity of skill points, and you'll have to consider how you invest them as you go. Cool. You also notice as you play that the number of points required to make skill checks increases as you get into more difficult areas. So investing one point in lock picking isn't going to open anything in late game. If this game were remotely replayable, I would've been excited to go through and play NG+ to get more, oh wait there's no NG+. 

- Enemy AI & its ambush nature. I played a lot of this game with the slow-mo gadget turned on and definitely noticed that as soon as enemies spawn they're swinging/shooting. There was this one part where you open a door and whatever was on the other side just blasts you with some attack. Why spawn enemies mid-fight when they already know exactly where I am and they're already swinging? It's not additional-difficulty, it's additional attrition, which gets tiring when it's the default for most encounters. 

- Melee combat without the ability to dodge just means bum-rushing everything and many of the enemies will run away to assail you with various ranged abilities (almost all of which seem to apply a DoT). That's ok where it makes sense, but it seems like it's basically everything: humanoids (of all of the various factons), spiders, ranged robo-drones, other space-bugs. This just makes it really tedious to play.

- Melee combat also sucked because I put my points and perks into that sort of build immediately, while unknowingly I wouldn't find a melee weapon (as loot or to purchase) for several hours into gameplay. I think I finally found something midway through the first zone in Act I. 

- Because I opted to put points into melee and another non-combat skill, I didn't put any points into ranged combat (which boosts ranged damage). Effectively that meant that any ranged combat I engaged in boiled down to attrition. I could either use a bunch of ammo gunning things down, or use healing items during- or post-combat since closing the distance is rarely something you can do without absorbing some hits. 

---
Environment / ambiance:
---

Pros:
+ Overall, I thought the graphics were fine, and I didn't suffer any stuttering or crashes
+ Reasonably detailed environments with multiple ways to interact with stuff and/or solve puzzles

Cons:
- The in-game "ads", be it actual ads from the capitalist faction, or faction-propaganda-ads from the communist faction, it made me crazy immediately. And as the game wears on, sometimes it's ads playing over ads. You can't turn it off in the settings. The settings allow you to turn radio volume to 0, but it's not always playing over the radio. I honestly hated this and rushed through these parts of the game as much as possible. Thankfully, the story not-mattering was a boon for this frustration.

- Weapons-wise, and armor-wise, nothing really vibed with me. There were no cosmetics I found where I was like "oh sweet, this is hands-down super cool"

- The needlessly complicated pseud-anachronistic dialogue. It's not quirky when it's literally the default for everyone. It's super annoying.

---
Story
---

Neutral: 
* Either I missed it, or there are no romancable companions. Each companion seems like they're plenty happy to live their own lives and not too invested in the story. If you don't recruit them, you aren't missing much.

Pros:
+ Plenty of voice acting
+ You can skip the bits of dialogue as you go so you don't have to listen if you don't want to, but if you missed something you have the option to scroll up through the captions and catch whatever you missed
+ The player's choices shape the ending slides in the game. 

Con:
- Lifeless, and not-engaging. Fundamentally, the plot is life-and-death. But you really don't feel it. The stakes are never really elevated to the point where the story takes itself seriously. Everyone speaking in faction-appropriate anachronistic speech patterns is particularly offputting. The default seemed to be a highly-educated old-timey speech pattern (well-represented by George Clooney's character in O Brother Where Art Thou, actually). But then the communist faction has their own anachronism, the math-faction has theirs, the capitalist faction has theirs as well. I was actively annoyed by this, and it pulls the player right out of the story. There are no stakes.

- It's like every character is moulded from a Han Solo archetype, even the player's character. That's a choice I suppose, but not one where the story will be very strong. There's a reason why the Lancer archetype stands out in a story that takes itself seriously versus not. And this game's story isn't a comedy either. It just doesn't come together in a way that makes it fun or engaging.

- Since the game could not take itself seriously, why should I? It's like it actively encourages murder-hobo'ing with it's incredible flippancy.

- The game's ending doesn't redeem everyting or make it all worth it. You get ending slides based on what you did, but it's all basically on-par with the rest of this review.

Abstract:
Following approximately 60 hours of controlled exposure, the game subject under study demonstrates properties consistent with a 2025 Game of the Year, or at minimum the dominant RPG specimen observed within the current release cycle.

Narrative Analysis: 10/10
The narrative exhibits a high concentration of story content, enriched with satire, humour, and repeated plot discontinuities (hereafter referred to as “twists”). Companion entities are both memorable and behaviourally distinct, despite the absence of romantic subroutines. Player character dialogue variability is extensive and produces substantial causal effects on endgame outcomes.

Environmental Observations: 9/10
The subject brings the player to multiple planetary environments, each with a unique emotional wavelength and aesthetic profile. These conditions are consistently reinforced by an auditory stimulus package (soundtrack) of notable retention value.

Character Progress Systems: 9/10
The character build framework conforms to established RPG taxonomies: abilities, perks, and flaws. The latter can be player action-triggered, creating a feedback loop in which certain actions & behaviours, questionable at times, have consequences but also are rewarded.

Equipment Assessment: 10/10
Weaponry and armour are neither inert nor decorative; many specimen introduce distinct mechanical effects, increasing player engagement and occasional delight.

Technical Stability Report: 10/10
Remarkably, no crashes, bugs, or systemic failures were observed across the full duration of the study. 

Conclusion:
No adverse effects were detected. Continued exposure is strongly recommended.

Compared to the first installment, there's much more going on - much larger maps, many more activities, impressive selection of armor, weapons, mods and such. The art is also quite amazing. BUT: it's extremely dull. The side quests are not compelling in the slightest, and even the main quest is simply boring. 
The game borrows a lot of things from other successful titles - in particular, elder scrolls and (of course) fallout. But it doesn't all come together. There's no actual sense of adventure, exploration, discovery. There's no depth to the story either. None of the factions are compelling - they all seem equally worthless, and the game's treatment of them is trite and nihilistic. Even all the weaponry, armor, mods and crafting are uninteresting. You can't tell easily if there's any progression and the strengths & weaknesses are not clear. 
Simply put - despite the effort to make the game very appealing to the eye and adding praised features from other games (crafting, wider item selection, and elements from other successful titles), it's just plain BORING. At this point, I doubt I'll put in the effort to finish the game - it's not fun. It's become a chore.

Honestly, I wish there were more 'play your way' games with good stealth mechanics on the market.  It is my favorite style of game.  This was a good entry to the genre, with plenty of reasons to play through multiple ways.  I had a lot of fun with this game.
While there were some drawbacks, the one that no one seems to make clear is the sheer amount of noise pollution in this game.  Usually, out of respect for the artistry of the work, I do a full playthrough with default-ish settings.  I like to do my subsequent playthroughs with settings that eliminate whatever annoyed me in the first run.  While they give you several volume controls, the only way to stop the loudspeaker loops in settlements is to mute the main game sounds.  How long is it going to take for devs to learn that incessantly repeated lines are an irritant?  At least include an increasing duration of pause between each loop.  I understand that thematically a maniacal corporate organization is going to constantly advertise and that a brainwashing commune is going to drill, but seriously allow players an escape if they so choose.
Other than that, I'd definitely recommend this game to anyone who liked the Fallouts or Dishonoreds.

This is a weird game. While parts of it are enjoyable, I feel like the flaws are too numerous and glaring to recommend. Starting with the positives: the writing and the art direction are great. The story is interesting, the companion characters being shaped by your interactions with them is neat, and the game looks cool. However, the down sides really bog down the whole experience. The leveling doesn't feel rewarding. Unlocking new weapons doesn't feel like an appreciable increase in power. Leveling doesn't give you new or meaningful abilities. The combat feels very underwhelming. It is really annoying to have to manage ammunition. The crafting system is weirdly large but also not rewarding to engage with. The fact that the end of the game is a slide show summing up your choices feels low budget. I can't tell if microsoft gutted the vision for what this game wanted to be but it feels bland and under baked.
Obsidian has an odd track record. The games they make feel like a weird bastard child of Bioware and Bethesda but notably lesser quality than either. 
If you're looking for an RPG, there are better ones out there. If you're looking for Fallout in space, this isn't really that. If you're looking for fun combat, you won't find it here.

The Outer Worlds 2 is an alright space RPG. I think my review of The Outer Worlds was "Fallout: New Vegas in space". The Outer Worlds 2 is "The Outer Worlds but fallier and outier".

The setup for the game is you are trapped in a remote star system that is being torn to pieces by reality-warping rifts, which is the (weak) excuse for why everything, everywhere is in an epic state of disrepair. Just like the Fallout series, there's inexplicable trash and corpses all over the place because in this universe everyone is incompetent, nobody ever cleans, and human life is worth precisely zero.

All of the factions are full of religious nut jobs with a cultish devotion to their respective totalitarian leaders. There is no attempt at meaningful social commentary - each faction simply fills the role of being one another's ultraviolent antagonist. None of the conflict feels believable or authentic, but it's clear from the outset that this is just dumb, schlocky pulp fiction. The game pushes its Jules Verne retrofuturistic aesthetic to the max. The in-universe "technology" is supremely silly and it soon becomes clear we've been hoodwinked into playing a fantasy RPG with only the barest coat of sci-fi paint on it.

And yet it's fun. The story doesn't really matter. The characters are mostly flat. But it's neat to explore the world and fix broken machines and shoot at space bugs and loot all the things. You can pick your favorite playstyle and develop your character and team to suit. There are a bunch of locations to explore and odd little dialog snippets and funny bits of lore to discover. It's such an aggressively stupid world that you could never mistake it for the real world, which makes it a pleasing distraction. I doubt I'll ever replay it, but I enjoyed the 80-odd hours I got, which makes this a solid thumbs up. Fine RPG, 7/10.
