The first entry of [i]The Outer Worlds[/i] franchise was the first game I played and finished on my first gaming PC, and it still holds a special place in my heart for that reason. Over the years, there has been a growing contingent of people who criticised the original for its writing, pacing and quest design. I never felt those issues myself. I thought the writing was comically on point, the pacing criticism overblown, and the quest design offered narratively interesting arcs and consequences. When the sequel was revealed, I was excited to recapture that feeling. When reviews called it everything the first was but better, my hype reached its peak. Now that I’ve finished it, they say to never meet your heroes.

It starts off well. The visuals are breathtaking and the combat refined. Early conversations suggest deeper role-playing mechanics and more meaningful choices. Initially, it feels like the reviews were right: an improved version of the first game that finally had the budget to realise its ambitions. That impression largely holds true throughout. Everything is more fleshed out and higher in production value, yet all that money, time and effort couldn’t buy true engagement. I was playing it, going through the motions, but rarely feeling absorbed. This made me question whether the criticism of the first game was right after all. Perhaps nostalgia played a role, but the core issue here comes down to the narrative.

The Outer Worlds 2 sets you off in a new region of space called Arcadia. Outside of a merged company comprised out of two of the first game's factions, the characters and factions you will meet throughout are newly invented for this story. Newly invented might be a somewhat of a generous term, as the four factions are all varying shades of generic. Auntie’s Choice returns but feels like recycled humour. The Order is a standard scientist society with predictably shady ethics. These are the only factions that meaningfully affect the overarching narrative. The Protectorate serves as the enemy faction and is evil simply for being authoritarian and territorial, without nuance. Sub Rosa exists as a black market network but has no depth and no relevance to the main plot. You could remove it entirely without losing anything.

In a genre driven by narrative, weak factions are a major flaw. There are no twists and there is no insight into the stereotypes they portray. What is there is entirely surface level, with possibly a few ethical or philosophical debates thrown in to momentarily convince you there might be some deeper writing at play here. Calling it elementary-level writing feels harsh, but the feeling persists. The moral choices are largely moral in name only, as there is almost always a clearly optimal outcome if you’re prepared. Any character built well will rarely leave a confrontation surprised. That’s disappointing, especially from a studio with decades of experience.

The hope that the main story or companions might compensate is also unmet. The narrative shifts from a revenge arc into a standard save-the-galaxy plot. I enjoy revenge stories, but the foundation is missing: player investment. The game tells you someone betrayed you, tells you companions want revenge, and tells you how important it is to stop them, but it never earns that emotion. You barely know this character, and that never meaningfully changes. Even after roughly 65 hours on the in-game timer, I couldn’t tell you anything about their backstory or why I should hate them beyond what the game insists I feel. There is nothing personal to react to, only impersonal plot beats.

Later developments are slightly more engaging, as saving the universe is easier to grasp than forced betrayal. The end-of-universe scenario is somewhat interesting, but underdeveloped. It does culminate in an exciting finale shaped by your choices, and the ending slides provide a thoughtful epilogue. While this ends the story on a decent note, it doesn’t elevate the narrative as a whole. The companions are the most fleshed-out characters in the game, though that isn’t saying much. I appreciated that they hold firm beliefs and don’t instantly agree with you, and their development is handled well. Still, none of them are particularly memorable or likely to become beloved icons. Some companion quests are highlights, but they fall short of being characters you deeply root for. This was also an issue in the first game, which similarly lacked standout companions.

It’s unfortunate to be so negative about the writing, especially since the game improves on the first in nearly every other area. That contrast made me question my memory of the original. The writing here isn’t terrible; it’s mostly competent, which is faint praise. It may even be better than many video game narratives, but this genre relies on story. Without a compelling narrative to role-play in, the foundation collapses. Other games can compensate with different strengths, but that doesn’t apply here. Combat is polished and fun, with a wide variety of weapons featuring interesting gimmicks. Stealth attacks are satisfying, even if it took me a while to realise guns worked with them too. Companions in combat felt largely useless, often going down immediately, leaving me to fend for myself without choosing to be a lone ranger. Combat difficulty is well balanced, with no major spikes and no point where I felt overpowered.

Character progression is poorly paced. I reached max level long before the end, a complaint echoed by many players. For dozens of hours, I gained no experience and received rewards I no longer needed, removing incentive when the story already struggled. I did appreciate how strict the skill system was, making character builds feel meaningful and sometimes limiting your options. This is one area I wouldn’t change.

Visually, the game is stunning. The art direction is exceptional, textures are top-notch, and optimisation is excellent. I noticed none of the usual Unreal Engine 5 issues like stuttering. It’s a technically impressive game that prioritises style over substance despite genuine effort on the latter. The only thing I would've asked for graphically is the sorely missing photo mode. The animations are also impressive, particularly in first-person. Movement, interactions and actions like reloading or performing skill checks feel smooth and satisfying. NPC animations are more generic but varied enough not to distract. While reused animations weren’t always top-tier, they didn’t detract from the overall quality.

***

[b][u]Verdict[/u][/b]: All in all, it's hard to call The Outer Worlds 2 an actively bad game. The sum of its parts includes a lot of good on a mechanical and visual level, but it's all dragged down by the mediocre writing of the game's characters, narrative and factions. It's disappointing that after a long wait and a prospective successful franchise, they seem to have shot themselves in the foot with making this sequel the worst of all: largely boring. It's not going to convince anyone who didn't enjoy the first to become a fan. I will assume it will only validate their feelings of the first one. For me, it made me question my views instead of validating anything, which is easily a worse feeling. I think many people who will come into the sequel who enjoyed the first will similarly be disappointed, but I genuinely hope their mileage will vary for their own sake. It's a shame when a game does not live up to your expectations, even if I may have put them unreasonably high after all. I had a hard time deciding whether I should grade this game a five or a six, but ultimately a five felt more appropriate as I consider that to be a truly mediocre game. Sadly, that seems the most appropriate word I can use to describe this one. A mediocre game with certain strengths in all the worst places for its genre. Alas, may we possibly meet again in future under better circumstances.

The Good: A solid RPG experience with fun writing and quests, to me that's almost all that matters in game like this. Character building, perks, the flaw system and equipment meaningfully change gameplay in fun and interesting ways. 

The Bad: The game doesn't have the visual fidelity to justify how poorly it runs. NPC companions are for the most part... uninspired.

A refreshing change from the usual absolute slop we’ve gotten over the last few years.
 The game is nice to look at and well-optimized.
 The story isn’t very original, but it’s still well thought out. And finally: actual choices to make.
 The characters feel alive and are fun to interact with.
 The companions are interesting and endearing.
 The world clearly rewards exploration. It’s not meant to be finished in 25 hours. Get ready to read entries and interact with NPCs a lot if you really want to enjoy this game.
 There are a lot of weapons and mechanics to play with, and I guess removing the upgrade system forces you to try new ones.
 The guns feel good, but you really notice when your weapon has become obsolete.
 The melee weapons didn’t get much love. If you want to use melee, you either spend all your points on it, use it for stealth, or use the slow, overpowered one exclusively. No backup knife.

Of course, you can rush the game and finish it in 20–30 hours if you want. It won’t be a good experience, unfortunately...

This was a very disappointing sequel to a game that, although had flaws and didn't age well in every aspect, was a breathe of fresh air at the time of its' release. Funny enough, this game was a thousand times better than Bethesda's attempt at an RPG Space adventure, which isn't saying much since they relegated most of their 
releases to new Skyrim remasters for every computer in existence. Here are my reasons why I do not recommend:

1) The story in this game is boring and a step down from the first game. The story of this game just feels like a redo of Fallout New Vegas story: someone did wrong by you, now you need to go find them. None of the missions draws you in like the first one with interesting characters or plots that keep you glued to the story and the outcome of your actions. I got bored with the game by the end, because I didn't care about any of the factions you're forced to work with, and the games' story failed to make me care about the outcome at the end. I didn't even beat the game, I uninstalled it after one of the big decisions, because after that I didn't care about what happened because the game didn't do anything to make me care. 

2) This game is horribly optimized and runs like garbage. I have both a steamdeck, and a gaming PC that both have above the reccomended specs for this game. It runs like trash on both, and the framerate, horrid graphics on lower settings, mixed with animation glitches take away from the experience and make me continually change my graphics settings during gameplay to try and get the game to run at a stable frame rate and not  have the environment look like the backdrop in a PS2 game. 

3) The worlds you visit in this game are just as uninteresting as the first game. It is as if they didn't expand on the first games environments, and went back to doing the same thing where they used the basic designs in the Unreal engine to make worlds that are generic and uninteresting. 

Don't buy this game. If it's on sale for any price under $30, buy it then; $70 is way too high for a game that they obviously didn't put time into to create a sequel that builds on top of and improves elements of the first game.

The Outer Worlds 2 continues the series with a solid, if uneven, follow-up. Its comical, non-serious tone paired with decent action makes TOW2 a fun adventure overall, even if it struggles to stand out in some key areas.

Graphics

Visually, The Outer Worlds 2 feels dated. The graphics don’t meet the standard set by games released in the last 24 months. Having recently played titles like Indiana Jones, The Last of Us, and Stellar Blade, this falls noticeably short—and I’m not even convinced it matches the visual quality of the original game.

The technical implementation is also familiar territory for the series: buggy. HDR, in particular, caused issues in low-light areas for me personally. Performance is acceptable overall, and I suspect it will run fine on Steam Deck, but you’ll likely need FSR or a Decky plugin to achieve a consistently decent frame rate.

Story

Fortunately, the story is where the game truly shines. There’s plenty of lore, interesting characters, and multiple story paths that genuinely play out differently across playthroughs. While your character’s dialogue options are somewhat restrained, the universe still feels alive, and the immersion steadily builds as you progress.

Gameplay

Many players have criticised the gameplay in TOW2, but I personally enjoyed it. Gunfights are fun, and the upgrade paths are easy to understand and navigate. My main criticism lies with the inventory system—it’s genuinely awful. Switching weapons often requires opening the menu mid-fight, and you quickly accumulate large amounts of redundant gear.

That said, once I settled on a weapon loadout that matched my traits and perks, combat felt satisfying, and I ended up tearing through anything that got too close.

Music & Sound

The music is solid but understated. Environments and gun sounds are well realised, and the overall score fits the game nicely. It’s not emotionally driven, but that’s intentional—the game isn’t trying to be that kind of experience.

Summary

Overall, I’d give The Outer Worlds 2 a 6.5/10. I didn’t encounter any crashes, and aside from the HDR issues, performance was stable for the most part. The technical implementation is weak, and the story does most of the heavy lifting. If you can pick it up on sale in a few months and play it on Steam Deck, that could be a great way to enjoy the game.

tldr; - If you liked the first one, you will like this one. I still wish steam allowed a neutral rating. This is a solid 3 or 3.5 out of 5.

Overall -  The world design is decent, the mechanics are fun, and the performance acceptable. 

Value - This is a AAA price with AA product, but worth the money if it's on sale. 

Story - The story is ok, nothing you will remember or think about outside the game. It feels like the story was written to justify the aesthetics, so if you like the spacepunk dystopian theme, you will just accept the plot as a device for the theme. 

RPG mechanics - The RPG elements work most of the time. They really leaned into the idea that you need to pick a lane and not be a jack of all skills. That would have been great except you can't really lean into a full roleplaying style because not having some specific skills locks you out of some major decisions seemly randomly. I am fine with this these "blocked paths" if it makes sense. If you are doing a speech / intelligence build I get that the brute force options will be limited. The problem is there are a few key decisions that pivot on skills that you might not associate with the type of build that would make that decision. For example (hypothetical to avoid spoilers), you are doing an evil playthrough and the evil choice is locked because your hacking isn't high enough. Or, you are doing a good guy play through but the choice that a good guy would make is locked behind sneak. Sometimes multiple ways to complete an objective are very clever and built in a way that lets you really roleplay your few chosen skills. But the major choices, usually at the end of a mission, often have skill checks that just make no sense. 

Weapons - This is an improved from the first one some ways. The annoying "glass gun" durability is gone, and ammo is more reasonably obtainable by crafting or purchase. The weapon types are still interesting and satisfying to use. The weapon upgrades are a bit of a let down. Many of the weapon effects detract from the base damage too much to be useful. I found that optics and capacity upgrades were worth it, but  damage type modifiers were just not worth it. 

Optimization - It ran fine in 4k on a 4080 super with hardware ray tracing off. Hardware raytracing still seems broken honestly. The software raytracing is fine. I didn't have any real issues with crashes or stutters.

Companion System - I had to really think about whether it was worth bringing anyone at all more so than who to bring. Sometimes bringing the logical party with you makes a big difference. Specific companions unlock options that make sense in context sometimes. Other times they get in the way and just make the whole mission about aiming around the idiot AI companion trying to attack the main enemy head on. There is a fair amount of jumping up on things and crawling around that the makes the AI just get stuck or teleport up behind you. Overall, the companions are best used when obviously required for the area.

Bugs / Completeness - The game feels finished compared to a lot of other new releases. I had a few softlocks from bugged quests that were usually companion based. Usually a reload fixed it.

Better than the first one in almost every way.


The good:

Interesting and complex writing in terms of both story and world-building.

Humerous and serious at the same time

Most guns are satisfying to use

Unique items are often clever without being overpowered

RPG skills are very well balanced

Runs smooth, looks sleek

All available companions are interesting and fleshed out.

Game feels complete and not rushed.

Tight/responsive controls, interface, and environmental interactions. The game just feels good to play.

Compared to OW1, this has larger areas, more items, more quests, more companions, and a longer story. You get more bang for your buck and it feels like a complete game rather than an experimental discount game (which the first one did a little bit).


The somewhat disappointing:

Sometimes feels like it lacks enough combat opportunities.

Open-world maps are completely unnecessary.

Discourages exploration.

Missing most of that harsh, anti-capitalism vibe from the first game.

Almost everyone outside of the Earth Directorate is brainwashed and stupid.


To elaborate:

Sometimes it felt like there weren't enough enemies to shoot, but this goes along with balancing the other RPG skills so that combat is more optional (though it's not possible to be completely pacifist). In terms of marketing itself as a shooter with lots of guns and armor though, combat can feel a little underutilized.

There are multiple open-world maps, but you're discouraged from exploring them. Every explorable area has a quest associated with it somewhere else. So if you go somewhere too early, it can ruin the experience of some of these quests. You're better off not going anywhere unless the game specifically points you in that direction. It might as well be comprised of isolated interior cells without open maps at all.

Where Obsidian is often compared to Bethesda in terms of the RPG expertise, this world-detail stuff is where they fall behind bigtime. Vastly explorable maps with random details for world building/immersion just isn't their thing.

It's missing some of that hard critique of capitalism that the first game was so unique and respected for. The story features three major factions, and they all have vastly different governing styles. Only one of them invokes that extreme capitalism from the first game, but they're somewhat watered-down by comparison. They're also presented as being one of the better factions to side with, as most of the game will force you against the dictatorship faction (who are clearly the worst). It just wasn't as interesting since video games tend to explore the evils of authoritarianism a lot already. I would have preferred more unorthodox evil like the first game.

My last point involves something that was also present in the first game. Pretty much everyone you meet is brainwashed, and nearly incapable of questioning their faction's status-quo. Everywhere you go, NPC's are repeating mantras from their faction and overselling their beliefs. Even most of your companions are completely warped by whoever they're loyal to when you first meet them. Several of their story arcs involve getting them to critically think enough to actually grow into characters that aren't complete schills. The craziest one I wasn't able to convince, and she died in my ending as a direct result of it. Despite this, I will say there is still a lot of interesting writing surrounding these characters.

Perhaps this is all part of the series' style or humor at this point, or maybe it has something to do with the retro-futuristic early 1900's vibe (did people not really question anything back then or something? I don't know). Maybe they're trying to say that any colony which exists light-years from Earth would have completely indoctrinated populations due to isolation, but would that still be the case no matter how terrible their lives are? Surely they would question things after learning about other factions doing things better/differently? In fact, that is a theme the game explores a little bit, but primarily only with the Protectorate where authoritarian brainwashing actually makes sense. The other two factions don't really explain why anyone would be loyal to them, or at least they both make pretty terrible cases for themselves.

Whatever the reasons are, as a player in this universe, the rest of the population just starts to lack depth because of it. It's hard not to feel like almost everyone else is downright stupid. Even the Order faction, who are so intelligent in math and science that they can predict future events with near certainty, were completely lacking in simple common sense most of the time.

I don't mean this to sound negative. It's more like I see the potential in this series and want it to do even better. I'm overanalyzing the few areas where it fell short for me.

Honestly I thought this was a major improvement over The Outer Worlds in pretty much every way.

The factions were fun and unique feeling.  The combat and movement were improved.  The perks were decent, and flaws add an interesting element.  They added third-person mode for this one.  It's graphically an improvement.

I played for 42 hours and I completed most quests except a few smaller side ones that didn't really interest me.  Plenty of quests (both big and small ones) can have different outcomes, which would justify playing more than once.

If you are going into this somehow expecting Fallout: New Vegas or Pillars of Eternity, don't buy this.  It's not those things, and Obsidian will probably never meet those expectations again. 

But if you are looking for a shorter, fun, casual RPG, or you want an improved The Outer Worlds, then by all means pick this up.  I thoroughly enjoyed it and will likely play it again when the DLCs are released.

I think the bravest things about Outer Worlds 2 is how it basically flips every mechanic from OW1 on it's head. Sometimes, a sequel to a game can feel like more of an extended DLC pack; new map, new characters, new items, but all built around the same basic core. Not here, though; when they built this, they clearly decided there were no sacred cows.

Baseline stats are gone, as are the prime/sub skill groupings; there are just skills now. There are now per-perk requirements to unlock them individually, rather than just having a whole new tier every so many levels. Items (both equippable and consumable) no longer apply skill boosts/debuffs; your skills are your skills, and the only way to improve them is levelling. Weight is no longer a mechanic; carry as much as you like. Weapons no longer degrade. Companions don't use your inventory now, having their own dedicated-per-character equipment (with its own separate system for getting upgrades). Companion perks are reworked, all being character-specific and getting a choice of one-of-two every few levels, and with additional "secret" perks to unlock later. The list goes on.

The result is a game that, while still fitting the basic [i]feel[/i] of an Outer Worlds game, is also sufficiently distinct from the first to avoid just being a retread. Whether you'll feel the changes are positive or not is mostly a matter of taste.

If I had a complaint... and I do... it's that progression feels more confined this time around. With the increased focus on skills, only getting 2 points a level really hurts. Do you want to have a lot of options; hack each terminal, pick each lock, talk anyone round? That's technically possible, you can max three skills out, just as long as you can accept having no points at all, anywhere else; get used to seeing jammed doors that you'll never open, because it requires either a specific trait, or 5 points in a skill that you can't spare.. And remember; perks have skill requirements now. Hyper-focusing on a few skills means harshly limiting perk options. If you have 3 or 4 perks you really want to aim for, it may be literally impossible to get them all even if you dedicate to it among all else.

Some people are going to dig this. They'll talk about how cool it is to have to focus on a build, and you can try something else on another run. I am happy for them, genuinely. For me, it sucks to be almost max level and have many options still completely locked out and never attainable, and I'm probably not going to run this multiple times (at least not any time soon), and so I'm glad that mods exist to tweak this. Half way through I bumped myself to 5 points a level, and that felt better, like I could effectively respond to the things I'm trying to do as they come up. YMMV.

With all that said, this would be a light recommend. But they made one other mechanical change that resulted in something that makes this an easy thumbs up for me; the wireless stations. Like in Fallout, you can now tune into faction-specific radio stations to listen to rather than the general soundtrack. That was pretty nice.

Then I unlocked Grand Plan Wireless.

Reader, you know how sometimes a song is so good it gets stuck in your head for a while afterwards? Grand Plan Wireless is a radio station of nothing but the best kinds of ear-worm. It's a station for, and buy, a bunch of quasi-religious scientists who are obsessed with thinking about thinking. The songs are odes to their favourite numbers, operatic durges about realising your research was on based on a fault premise, a bittersweet ballad about a failed romance between two chemists. And it's some of the best videogame music I've heard in a long time. When I'm not playing the game, I'm listening to the music. When I'm not actually listening to the music, it's still playing in my brain. It is possible that this is not a videogame, but a cognitohazard. If that is the case, I'm afraid I'm here to spread it.

I wholeheartedly disagree with all of the mixed, and negative reviews amongst this page. I played the first Outerworlds within a year of its release, it made me sob, uncontrollably with satisfaction with my first ending. This did not lack in this aspect by any means, in my opinion it amplified on its ability to do such a thing.

My personal view; I was raised in a cult. Westboro Baptist, some may be familiar. The way Tristan Rao is written.. Aza.. I see verbatim lines I said when I struggled with the SAME questions posed to me. These two companions alone, speak to me, they see me. They are me going through my religious trauma, my guilt, my shame. Still having a chance at redemption when they didn't know they needed to find it in the first place. Or thought they already had achieved it. 

I don't understand those saying this game lacked depth, even without my own personal experience surrounding religion, these characters are profound. 

Niles, a picturesque image of loyalty, trust, but conviction nonetheless. Stays true to his morals, honest, hardy. He is resilience in a bottle. He has been through more than most at his young age, and he still has conviction in his beliefs and does not let much outside his control shake him in such belief. He waited, and searched, and he found you. 

Inez is a broken soul, someone who needed a leg up and got a 6-foot-deep contract hole. She deserves to be shown better, and has never been. Despite this, she still believes in the good in everyone. She may have some company biases at first, but she is intelligent, emotionally in-tune. She reminds me of Pavarti. 

I haven't wholly got a feel for Marisol, but she reminds me of my grandmother who saw both wars. Hardened from pain, loss, years of sacrifice to make it through. Some how still retaining a sense of humor, an ability for growth, change in perspective, love. She seems shielded, but kind and soft. The kind of introvert you walk into a room and gravitate towards because the way they speak is captivating. 

And Valerie is just a cute wittle robot!



Additionally, the gameplay mechanics i feel are much more thought-out and executed. I felt much less motion sick, visually lost, and downright ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ confused in Outerworlds 2 than in the first. I didn't even write the first one a review, I dont think. I felt a lot less stressed out, granted on the lowest difficulty, but in the first I still struggled. 

If you WANT a challenging game, turn the difficulty up. Otherwise, don't bother with this game if you dont GAF about story and character growth, you're shallow. I come here to be good and do good that is not possible in real life at times. Have ♥♥♥♥♥♥ fun.

As someone who played through The Outer Worlds 1 three times, The Outer Worlds 2 is a game that, for the most part, understood the flaws of it's predecessor and worked to rectify them. While the game has it's quirky moments, it does know when to actually be serious (unlike the first game which, even when it tried to be serious, zany character designs or bad dialogue proceeded to ruin the moment) The Doomsday Moments do actually feel like Doomsday and not 'It'd be rather unfortunate if this went wrong' and the fate of Halcyon always felt a little too overemphasized compared to the Rifts (The way the narrative framed it, Hacyon was basically the last remnants of Humanity in the galaxy which made ♥♥♥♥-all sense. The Rifts are a danger to the fabric of reality, so them being what they are does make the tension appropriate)

While I do enjoy the new cast more, it feels like they don't interact with each other as much as I'd like, the only exception being in one of the main quests in an underground facility (you probably know the one if you played) but they are far more interesting to play with. For example, we have a Mutant who can get Grafts that affect how her companion power works, and we have two melee companions with very different philosophies. In the Outer Worlds 1, I basically had no reason to bring anyone except Nyoka in my first slot (Assault Cannon user, so naturally highest DPS) and usually brought SAM as my second (he has a Chemthrower, in addition to being very tanky) Outer Worlds 2, on the other hand, gives you two(ish) options for every role in the party and the difficulty can be scaled down enough I don't feel bad if I want to keep my medics on the ship for whatever reason.

I do have a few criticisms though:
1) It's 2026, [b]Area. Loot.[/b]
2) No Transmog System.
3) Flaws are not properly shown past the third. Especially problematic given that one flaw in particular will force you to take ALL of them.
5) Unique items do not scale. That is to say, a helmet with 5% maximum health will always have that 5% even when you find items with much, higher values. This makes sense for normal items, but not Uniques.
6) Augmetics will always show over the clothing, meaning a Parapalegic will never get to see how armor looks below the thigh. This is a shame because I like a lot of these designs, but I chose augmetics early on (I do like replacing limbs in cyberpunk/sci-fi settings) and now I'd have to make a whole new character to fix this.

The sequel that boldly goes where slightly sociopathic humor and explosive weaponry have an even closer relationship
The Outer Worlds 2 is one of those rare sequels that doesn’t just reheat leftovers it chucks them into a geyser of neon lasers, corporate nonsense, and companions who have zero chill. After 40 hours with this game, one thing is clear:
This is capitalism in space, but somehow funnier, louder, and more chaotic.
Combat feels like an interplanetary dance where the rhythm is “please do not die.” Every firefight has momentum  you slow time, line up Precision shots, and watch enemies explode like corporate secrets leaking into the press. The Tactical Time Dilation returns, and it feels even better this time. Turning a firefight into a slo‑mo symphony of headshots and plasma makes every battle feel cinematic and absurd in the best way.
And this sequel doesn’t just reuse gameplay  it expands it. You have more ways to approach every encounter. Sneak, blast, talk them into renouncing their life choices  it’s all valid and gloriously chaotic.
The Outer Worlds 2 has the signature sharp writing fans love  witty, sarcastic, and so relentlessly self aware it could host its own comedy podcast. The worlds are gorgeous, weird, and home to factions so dramatic they make reality TV look subtle.
The story mixes political intrigue, existential dread, and improv comedy. The plot twists happen fast and hard, and just when you think you understand what’s going on, the game goes “Nope! New twist!” with the enthusiasm of a toddler on espresso.
Your companions are back with brand new quirks, conflict, and opinions on everything you do. They will judge your decisions, steal your snacks, and probably insult your wardrobe choices and you will love them for it.
The narrative sometimes jumps between very intense and very absurd, which can feel chaotic if you’re trying to be serious about… anything. Also, the number of choices is so massive that you’ll finish a conversation and immediately wonder if that one offhand answer you gave will come back to haunt you five hours later.
Mostly though? It’s intentional chaos, and it works.

Final Verdict 🚀
The Outer Worlds 2 is a worthy successor that doubles down on everything fans loved sharp writing, meaningful choices, and weapons that feel like performance art. It’s loud, funny, smart, delightfully weird, and more explosive than a corporation caught without insurance.

I picked one early on that gave me 3 perk points per level instead of 2- but the side effect was I could only use the points on the lowest skills. When you're just starting the game- that sounds like a solid trade off. Nope- I slogged through the game with all my stats right around 5-6. When I was playing the first half of the game I couldn't, for the life of me, understand how people hated on this game... Then I got to the second half and everything fell apart. It feels like such a step back from the first game. Which is crazy because the first game was mid at best. I'll just regurgitate my thoughts without much structure below. 
The amount of bugs related to quests is staggering. I was locked out of various quests because I had done something out of order or an NPC just simply wouldn't talk to me. OR! They would talk to me too much. For example, every time I landed on Paradise Island, one of my companions would spawn in and yell at me about dropping a ship on the town. Sometimes I would be given the option to lie with a speech check, but sometimes I wouldn't and I would have to reload a save and pray that RNG would give me the choice when I spoke next. Another example, I stormed this big AI driven station by myself and because I did that, the game bugged out and didn't give me the option to ending the war between two of the major factions. It was incredibly frustrating.
Also, Obsidian's style of abrupt ending with no post-game beyond their splash screen crawl is incredibly dated. If you have a high enough speech skill, you can just end the game without a final fight. Which I understand, is sort of a staple of Obsidian Entertainment games, but it felt very cheaply done in this game. Fallout New Vegas had the benefit of being well written enough that it really FELT like you were persuading the final boss. The writing for the end of the game felt kinda abrupt and rushed. The final boss is just suddenly, "Oh I did a bad. I shall sacrifice myself. Sorry."
Another thing, Companions. They felt so boring. There were so many times that I would be talking to them and challenging their world views, only for the game to say "This will be remembered" and the companions carrying on as if they DIDN'T remember. Not being able to romance companions in an RPG this day and age feels a bit sacrilege. I know Obsidian, or more specifically, Josh Sawyer, doesn't like romance options in their games, but it is SUCH a missed opportunity. It takes an entire element of companions and just throws it away. What I'm left with in the Outer worlds 2 are companions who are forgettable and boring with my influence on them being negligible at best. 
My biggest gripe with this game, beyond what I have listed already, HAS to be the second half falloff. It was like a switch got flipped and I could feel the vibe totally shift. The world-building, narrative pacing and quest's all declined significantly. Each quest felt the exact same, Go here-do this-comeback. The two worlds they give you before the final "world/quest" are two super bland, repetitive worlds. The narrative and its pacing was so strange. The narrative dropped its charm and urgency, replacing it with a weird disjointed plot that felt like it was all over the place. This drop happened in the second-half. Which sucks because the first-half of the game's narrative and climax point felt so well done. Finally getting revenge on the traitor was paced perfectly. I almost wish the game ended there, instead, I was forced to sit through ten hours of the most disjointed, boring story that felt super rushed.

The gameplay is rough at best. The main questline feels fragmentary, bouncing between having to allow one, both, or neither "major factions" involved, so it frequently stumbles when it's been written into a corner that had to flex around that. The side quests are almost universally padding for length in a game that already struggles with having empty worlds you sprint around until you can fast travel from quest marker to quest marker.

They learned nothing from their own release with the Outer Worlds; gunplay is still as spongy as ever, the different planets are different flavors of wide open mostly empty field full of random spawns, and there are entire trees of player development rendered useless. Equipment matters even less. You can no longer modify gear in a meaningful way. Hyperspecialization is not only encouraged, but outright required to not have the game become a painful slog. And if you don't hyperspecialize in a weapon, well. You're going to find out the hard way that the level gating is entirely fixated on "Things get more hit points". Should say, hyperspecialize in guns - haven't tried a melee playthrough, haven't bothered; Explosives are pretty useless in the current patched state, melee requires running down things that don't want to be while being peppered with shots, stealth... exists, and the less said about how useless the status effect boosts are from most "soft" skills, the better.

Some of the writing is well up to snuff for Obsidian. The rest of it, mechanically feels play-balanced solely around bland gunplay with pseudo-MMO half-baked in and parkour because it's what all the cool games do.

TL/DR: The Outer Worlds 2 is a very solid RPG from Obsidian that improves on its predecessor in almost every way. It's not without a few flaws (pun intended) but, imho, does not deserve the vitriol that has been directed against it. No, it's not another Fallout New Vegas or Mass Effect - but it is one of the more original and entertaining action RPGs that I've played in recent years. It's a UE5 game so you'll want to be sure your system can run it. Like many games, TOW2 will be looking a whole lot better a year or two down the track in the form of a discounted GOTY release, patched a little further, and including DLC. 

World-building, level design, aesthetics

Both Outer Worlds games vividly imagine future societies among the stars in thrall to unscrupulous corporations, authoritarian governments, and various existential perils - all in the face of a mysterious silence from planet Earth. This world-building is presented with much charm and tongue-in-cheek humour.

Unlike FONV, the Outer Worlds games are not set in a single, large worldspace but rather in still-expansive exterior play areas, set on various planets and moons, as well as 'dungeon' levels located in building interiors, space stations, etc. TOW2 repeats the design logic of its predecessor, but substantially expands on it. TOW2's main exterior settings are around twice the area of those featured in TOW1. This aligns with the 80hrs it took me to complete TOW2 - compared with ~40hrs for TOW1 (~54 hrs including the two DLC). Let's be clear: TOW2 is a generous game. Its serially-connected, discrete play areas are easy to get lost in whilst also maintaining requisite narrative focus.

To my eyes, the art design of both games is very distinctive and mostly very appealing. TOW2 exploits the UE5 engine to hype the graphical fidelity, lighting, etc. even further over the UE4-driven TOW1. If I had to make comparisons, I would say that the Outer Worlds aesthetic reminds me of the Baroque-Art Deco atmosphere of Bioshock's Rapture or, possibly, the Steampunk style of the Dishonored games. Serpentine curves and gold trim grace objects and architecture in a way that takes the utilitarian to the brink of decadence. I'm also reminded of the bulbous and organic spaceships and devices that appear in the sci-fi illustrations of Jim Burns. It's a refreshingly different look to the NASA-punk design ethos of Starfield or the Star Wars-inspired Mass Effect. By and large, I like it.

Story and NPCs

TOW2 is not the greatest RPG in history. It lacks the labyrinthine twists and turns of FONV - not to mention the wonderful characters and thrilling existential urgency of the Mass Effect games. There's a reason why these titles are the standards against which other games are judged. But TOW2's story and characters are better than many.

In TOW2, you are an agent of the Earth Directorate tasked with hunting down a rebel agent in the star system of Arcadia, whilst negotiating tensions between three main factions - the authoritarian Protectorate, the scholar-mathematicians of The Order of the Ascendant, and the invading megacorp, Auntie's Choice. Complicating matters, the Protectorate's misuse of 'skip drive' technology has produced a potentially catastrophic interdimensional rift. Nothing like the threat of impending cosmic annihilation to add a little spice to the narrative.

TOW2's NPCs are tonally more restrained than the zanier outliers of FONV (especially the Old World Blues DLC, which dials the Sci-Fi lunacy up to 11), but I'm not sure this means that the Outer Worlds characters are necessarily 'mid' or 'bland' as some reviewers claim. NPC dialogue is, in general, very well executed, contains plenty of choices in line with player character skills and attributes, and does not outstay its welcome. It's also often very funny.

Nearly all TOW2's player companions are fun to spend time with, have decent backstories to uncover, and even develop a little in response to the player character's choices as the game progresses. My personal fave was Marisol - a grandmotherly agent/assassin of the Order of the Ascendant. It was worth more than a few chortles to have Marisol's character referring to me as 'dear' - often only seconds after terminating hoards of baddies with maximum prejudice.

RPG builds

Just like a good RPG should, TOW2 presents the player character with a lot of choice in the 'builds' department. The skills and attributes one chooses do seem to have a meaningful impact on the way the game is played, how one accesses certain areas or items, and how one negotiates with NPCs as the game progresses. It's impossible to respec skills or to maximise more than a few key skills, which forces you to engage in strategic thinking and to make some difficult choices. Whether you like to sneak or talk your way around trouble, or just go in blasting, TOW2 puts plenty on the table. It's not hard to envisage tackling another play-through with a different player build - especially once DLC materialise.

PC Performance and Technical Issues as of version 1.06

Whilst I did not encounter game-breaking bugs or insurmountable technical issues, my experience with TOW2 certainly wasn't flawless. On several occasions during my 80hr play-through, the game froze and/or crashed, requiring a re-start. That said, context is important here. I still recall what a crash-monster FONV was back in the day.

As has been well-documented by Digital Foundry and others, the UE5 implementation of TOW2, in conjunction with hardware ray-tracing, was not ideal at release and is still troublesome as of vs 1.06. The way shadows are implemented in TOW2 also is rather buggy, with visually unpleasant shimmering or boiling artifacts evident at times. These issues are exacerbated by the necessity to use some kind of upscaling to attain a decent level of performance - by which I mean comfortably over 60FPS.

My solution, after much mucking around? Ditched hardware ray-tracing altogether as it was causing intolerable shimmer with grass and foliage on Paradise Island. I gained some useful FPS and, frankly, given that TOW2 is well-served in the rasterisation department, I really did not miss it. Turned shadow quality all the way up to very high, which minimised - but did not completely eliminate - shimmering and boiling. Kept Global Illumination to high. Most other settings maxed out. Employed DLSS 4, Quality upscaling, at 1440p resolution, which seemed to work well most of the time. I also used the Optimised Tweaks mod from Nexus Mods. Hard to tell whether this really made a difference for me as I still experienced characteristic UE5 traversal stutter - but some people seem to have benefited from it.

On my system (9800x3D, RTX4070ti, 32GB DDR5 6000MHz RAM, game loaded on a Samsung 970 EVO PLus NVMe SSD), I achieved FPS typically in the mid 70s in the more intensive areas like Fairfield. Elsewhere, FPS could exceed 90, 100, or even 110 FPS. In short, the UE5 stutters aside, perfectly decent performance. However, turning on hardware ray-tracing and switching to DLAA would tank these numbers. 

Verdict: solid thumbs up. Enjoyable game. Minor technical issues. Compelling story and well-written NPCs. Game-play has some depth and player character builds and choices seem to matter. Very distinctive art design. Original take on the space-based RPG genre. Look for it on sale - especially once DLC are released.

The Earth Directorate has detected a flaw in me: Hasty-yet-perseverent! Because, you see, I initially disliked this game, or at least had a [i]very[/i] hard time getting into it, then pushed on and found out it [i]actually[/i] isn't [i]that[/i] bad. In any case, the world that has been created here is absolutely [i]exquisite[/i]: looks great, plays smoothly, and attention to detail-slash-production-value, from random NPC chatter to the dialogue options to the fully-voiced radio stations is just insane, in a good way. [i]However[/i], that rich atmosphere isn't used to tell a compelling story, reducing the whole experience to a mildly-entertaining slog. It has its moments, sure, but after the first third of the game, the story just sort-of goes away, and you're left with grinding extended and recursive vaguely-thematic fetch-quests (some more cleverly/entertainingly dressed up than others, but still all pretty basic) to reach one of a handful of rather predictable and same-ish outcomes.

The overly complicated gameplay mechanics don't help: there are [i]tons[/i] of character and skill options (not to mention a superfluous variety of health recovery items), but getting anything playable requires a laser-like focus on a specific style, which you're then pretty much locked into. Stealhy hacker? Fine, but prepare to spend a [i]lot[/i] of time running away from any enemies and/or wildlife, as they'll be absolutely [i]lethal[/i] for the rest of the game, although "story mode" does alleviate that issue. Go in guns blazing instead? Sure, it will sort-of work, but the poor performance of the weapons and the constant need to obtain more ammo will still make basic survival [i]tedious[/i], so, yeah, "story mode" it is. And sure, you'll level up and you can loot/craft/trade, but the options in that department, while plentiful to the point of confusion like so many things in this game, seem mostly cosmetic, with nothing (other than a few hard-to-find-and-obtain items) making much of an actual difference.

Looking at the achievement stats, I'm not the only one struggling with finding extended joy in this game, but if you keep at it, you can definitely get your money's worth here, as long as you keep in mind that the story requires, well, [i]some[/i] assembly from admittedly-very-atmospheric parts, yet still all in your own mind. Replayability is limited despite all the options and branches, since those are mostly "doing X will lock you out of Y", with Y being of dubious value to begin with, and after 3 playthroughs you pretty much [i]know[/i] what's going to happen. DLC might have fixed a bunch of the issues around this, but apparently the publisher has already given up on the series, so, yeah... Discount-bin-only, I guess?

[h1]The gist of it:[/h1]
I usually do not buy sequels to games I did not enjoy, especially when the first Outer Worlds game completely disappointed me, but my instinct sorta kicked in watching some footage, and I decided, why the hell not? I'm glad I did, because Outer Worlds 2 is far superior in every single way than its predecessor, and anyone claiming otherwise is full of bs. 

👍 [u]The good parts:[/u]
+Great atmosphere/visuals
+The dialogue system
+Level design

👎[u]The bad parts:[/u]
-Some of its writing is overblown
-No memorable companion

[h1]Story[/h1]
There's a lot of debate between people about which story is better, the first or the second, and while it's up to each one's preference, I will say, its main story won't blow your mind or remain memorable. Obsidian never made bombastic, amazing stories despite having good writers, and it's still the case here with OW2, however the majority of the lore here and dialogue is at times excellent and funny, and other times a bit too much that it completely bores you. 

You play as an agent of the Earth Directorate, think of it as some sort of spy for the government, and you're placed between the battle of 3 major factions that are controlling most of the space in Arcadia. Each faction has its own unique identity, which was impressive. Auntie's Choice are the corporate slaves, obsessed with product placements, consumerism, and ads. The protectorate are basically the communists, though to an extreme degree, which includes brainwashing for control and very strict rules and governing. The last faction is the Order of the Ascendants, which are some very smart nutjobs obsessed with the probability of the universe and everything they do is based around that. As you try to uncover a solution to the destruction of space, due to rift technology, you are caught between said factions, and your choices will allow you to ally or go to war with one or the other. 

Not much else happens, you do things for whomever, like an errand boy, and you save the day. It's not super memorable, it's not even very heroic, but it's decent enough. My main gripe was that your companions were pretty boring after their initial introduction. There is also no romance. 

Story rating: 7.5/10

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
It's an RPG, you level up, you shoot stuff, you talk, repeat. You've seen it a  lot of times, not much is different here, however, and it's a big one. The dialogue system in Outer Worlds 2 really impressed me, and I don't think I've seen it in any other game, or at least not to this degree of depth, and it's crazy that not many talk about it. In a lot of good games, dialogue choices matter and may have an impact on future dialogues as well as some of your actions, that's been the standard in most rpg's, but Outer Worlds 2 takes it to another level. Even something that may seem small, such as reading an email or observing something, will show up as a dialogue choice alongside other information you may have found in the world or your own skills. I genuinely never thought I'd see the day that a random email from a computer device I randomly found would have a major impact on certain choices. 

But skills are obviously not used just for dialogue choices and whatnot, it's an rpg after all, which means exploration is a big aspect of it, and it's another thing that impressed me. Most locations, instead of having just one linear path, they're filled with a lot of optional ways that you may find or discover based on your skills. A lot of doors can be opened with either lockpicking or maybe with your engineering skill. There are a lot of choices, and how you distribute your skill points will matter a lot. 

At its core, though you will shoot a lot of times, it was important that it was fun to do so, and I'm glad to say, I enjoyed liquifying enemies or shocking them to death. There are a ton of weapons and unique ones to find. Everything is viable, even melee. I had a lot of fun exploring everything and shooting stuff. 

Gameplay rating: 8.5/10

[h1]Audio[/h1]
The voice acting is pretty good, and it's a big aspect of Outer Worlds 2. There are obviously some boring characters to listen to, some cringe ones, but there are also some very funny lines that cracked me up. None of them are super spectacular or memorable, but they're all good. 

The soundtrack is also decent, though I don't remember anything amazing, aside from the main menu one, as for the sound effects, they're all pretty good as well, from guns to environments. 

Audio rating: 8/10

[h1]Graphics, performance and tech analysis[/h1]
Surprisingly, a competent UE5 game that runs really well without constant stutters, hitches, or crashes. Visually, the game is a stunner, really impressive at times, the amount of details in its environments and vistas are really a sight to behold. What's even more impressive is how a lot of npc's react to your stupid actions at times, simple yet effective. 

The level design was also something that impressed me, while a lot of areas are in open space, once you're inside bases or main story locations, the level design turns into something straight out of Deus Ex, with multiple ways to reach your objective, such as vents and shafts or through dialogue. 

Graphics, performance, and tech analysis score: 8.5/10

[h1]Conclusion[/h1]
I'm certain a lot of people are dismissive of the game thanks to its Microslop price, because there's no way Obsidian would sell the game at a 70 euro price, which was cut from 80 before launch. This is clearly a 50 euro product. That being said, it is a good game that I enjoyed. I wish its main story had more oompfh to it, but alas, that is Obsidian for ya.

[h1]Final score: 8.1/10[/h1]

I really, really wanted to like The Outer Worlds 2. I went in completely blind, with an open mind, and quite excited…

Only to bounce off the narrative again and again and again. The story, characters, and setting all fell completely flat. The premise is interesting enough if a bit derivative, but it meanders and waits too long to raise the stakes. Almost everyone you meet is a thin caricature parroting a faction’s schtick, and that extends to the companions as well. The backdrop is a war between powerful factions, but each of them basically boils down to “<thing> bad” and they really only differ in set dressing.

It leans on the humour to carry the day, and that humour just didn’t land for me at all. Tone-wise it’s in a really weird place where it’s too silly to take seriously, but too dour to be fun. I never really cared about what was happening, or felt I had a good reason to care either in or out of universe. The characters aren’t likeable, the factions are unsympathetic, I don’t understand what problem we’re trying to solve let alone my part in it, [i]why am I even here[/i]?

The gameplay is at best competent if unremarkable, at worst tedious and frustrating. I’m not a fan of hard skill checks over soft ones, the look-but-don’t-touch way they’re presented, or the balancing between skill check difficulty and skill points granted, but that’s mostly down to preference. I do feel the bullet-spongey enemies do hurt the feel of the combat, which otherwise is decent with a few neat tricks.

Eighteen hours in I finally admitted I was forcing myself to play a game I wasn’t enjoying, giving it “second” chance after “second” chance in the vain hope that it would eventually click. It might mesh for you in a way that it just never did for me. There was clearly a ton of work and a lot of love put into this game, and at times it does shine through. But at the end of the day, it’s a middling-to-good AA game at the same price point as some truly excellent AAA titles, and that’s not something I can recommend.

Just finished my first playthrough, and I enjoyed it for the most part. I'll address the elephant in the room first: No, this is not Space: New Vegas and do not go in expecting it to be that. I would honestly compare it more to something like Mass Effect but with first person combat and more emphasis on RPG mechanics from Mass Effect 1. If you take it for what it is, it's more than serviceable, though I wouldn't place it at the pinnacle of the genre. In terms of story and setting, its setup is interesting, at least on paper. Much like the first Outer Worlds, it takes place in a relatively secluded corner of space across multiple hub worlds with a heavy emphasis on corporate jargon and imagery. I'd say that Outer Worlds 2 is a biting satire of capitalism and consumerism, but it's honestly not. On the one hand, it has ambitions to tackle other topics such as religion in authority and how totalitarian regimes control their subjects. And on the other, it seems less interested in condemning the systems of capitalism and more in how people in positions of power exploit those without it (this has been used by the developers in interviews describing the game's themes). When you clap those two hands together, they don't make nearly as much of an impact as either should. When you boil it down, The Outer Worlds 2 doesn't actually have that much to say about any of the issues it tackles other than shrugging its shoulders and saying, "Wow, wouldn't that be crazy?" Yes, Outer Worlds 2. Living in a consumerist, plutocratic dystopia would and does indeed suck. But if you're looking for a game with a sharp ideological identity (I will never not use Disco Elysium as the ultimate example), you will more than likely find this comes up a little short. While disappointing in that regard, I did find that it did shine much more brightly when it came to the side stories and quirky characters you meet along the way. This is where a Fallout comparison is most apt, as I found it easy to get sidetracked by these little micro-adventures more so than the main plot. There's a great degree of freedom allowed in how you resolve any given situation, and almost any encounter, including the final boss, can be circumvented if your skill checks are successful. Obsidian has made a strong rebuke to lax respeccing that is often found in other games, and here, that's a success. How you allocate your skill and perk points and specialize your build will likely have you pausing to consider the ramifications at every level-up, and it makes it easy to plan your progression around specific perks. Of course, there is also the Flaw system returning from the first game, and I found the way they dynamically pop up throughout the game, even in its final hours, to be fun and another interesting choice to make. I unfortunately accepted the Easily Distracted flaw, which effectively eliminates specialization with the benefit of giving you an extra skill point every time you level up, without fully considering its later consequences, but I actually appreciate how the game treated me like a grown-up and made me sleep in the bed I had unwittingly made. The combat is also a marked improvement from its predecessor, with more weapon variety and more satisfying gunplay overall. I wouldn't go so far as to say it feels as good as the most polished shooters on the market, but it doesn't have to. It just needs to be fun. A couple things to note before purchasing: 1.) The version of the game I beat wasn't the most stable technically. There were quests that failed to complete and dole out XP and I encountered multiple crashes. I've attached my specs to this review for reference, but it is worth noting that in the time since I beat it a rather large patch has been released, so hopefully those issues are mostly taken care of. 2.) No, you cannot romance any of the companions, and yes, that is on purpose. While Josh Sawyer did not directly work on this game to my knowledge, his influence is definitely felt here in that regard. I didn't mind this too much, but if you really want to kiss the cultist or the space fascist, you cannot. This review has been particularly rambly and I still have more to say about this game, so I'll wrap it up by saying that The Outer Worlds 2 is a serviceable game that feels like a throwback to the Xbox 360 era of RPGs, for better and worse. I do feel bad that every game Obsidian releases ultimately ends up getting compared to New Vegas, so I would say come at this one with an open mind and the knowledge that the social commentary offered is relatively toothless while expecting some fun sidequests along the way. And I think it's a real shame that its commercial underperformance has apparently stopped any of this universe's interesting ideas to be expanded upon.

The outer worlds 2 is everything I loved about the first game, but more. From graphics, to enemy ai, animations, location variation, story telling, and more the second entry delivers. I took my time really reading the logs and exploring all major planetary locations. Visually the graphics are much cleaner in game than comes through on a video. It is hard to explain but the fidelity of the art style is very nice. Gameplay provides a really solid rpg, with lots of build diversity, and differing playstyles with choices that matter. 

This franchise is all about choices. It does not just boil down to one or two options. Every story you take on has paths that change the outcome. The ending doesn't drastically change in direction but there is variance that would require multiple playthroughs to really experience. 

My favorite part of The Outer Worlds games and the 2nd game in general is that choices were not black and white. Many times you sit there at the dialogue or in an action and think "was that the right choice?" "I think I made the good guy choice here" just to realize that there was some moral side effect of your choice you had to live with the weight of. 

My only complaint is the game takes too long to open up. What started as a very nice first planet turned into a slog to explore all 4 planets before all game mechanics and space POI opened up. I would liked to see some of those things sooner. I won't elaborate due to spoilers.

Fallout in Space 2 comes with a solid story, fun banter, and enough choices that it will make you want to play through a second time just to experience parts of the story you didn't before; however, it is a love / hate affair. As enjoyable as Outer Worlds 2 was, it had several elements that dragged it down.

Like other skill based [semi]open world games, Outer Worlds 2 encourages you to experiment. You'll find doors that need a bit of Engineering skill to get through, dialog paths locked behind Science and Medicine skill levels, the ability to sneak past enemies to achieve your goals, and more features that invite you to play around and find out, but you are damned if you do, because Outer Worlds 2 actively punishes you for such experimentation. The XP for your level ups come from three main sources: lock picking doors (small), completing quests (moderate), and killing things (well over half). Even though sneaking around and even sparing enemies may seem like a smart idea, if you do this, you will get less XP and may not hit the level max. Because there is a level max, there is a finite amount of skill points, and because combat is so important to the gameplay loop, you now must maximize your skill choices around combat, rather than dabbling in other skills to experiment and get dialog options. Compounding this is the skill level requirements for Perks, which are mostly all or nothing - requiring a small skill level or the maximum, rather than a gradient. Where all skills have some combat effect, you are ultimately disadvantaged by not going all in. A gameplay loop that gave greater emphasis to and rewards from more skills would show that non-combat options are not just an afterthought. The worst part of this system is that instead of encouraging organic character growth, you instead feel you need to plan out your character all the way to the max level and not deviate from the plan. 

The aesthetic aspects of Outer World 2 have a presentation problem. Faces, particularly those for the PC, easily dip into the ghoulish aspects of the uncanny valley. These blend jaggedly with janky animations, which look like they were lifted from Fortnite's leavings. If this were not distracting enough, the audio is poorly balanced. The voices are often drowned out by music and sound effects. This is most noticeable with audiologs, which play so quietly that if you don't have subtitles on you will not know what they are saying. All of this needlessly detracts from the overall experience.

Again, Outer Worlds 2 has a lot going for it; though, the majority of that is on the story side. It starts in character creation when you choose a background, which will give you a plethora of added dialog options, some of which even meaningfully impact conversations. It continues on through the choices you make throughout. Although the general endpoint is the same, the way you get there can vary, and that is always something great to see.

At this point, I've been playing for  only a few hours of this game. I don't like rating a game too early,  but I feel like this game really doesn't pull me in and I want to warn others about my experiences. I'm an avid fan of open world, first person games like fallout and the elder scrolls. Obsidian is well known for making Fallout: New Vegas, many years ago at this point. Ever since the studio has been acquired by XBOX games studios, the quality of these styles of games has dropped. 

   For all its flaws, I enjoyed the original Outer Worlds and felt like much of its criticism was unwarranted. 
I enjoyed it, although I felt it was definitely was not competitive with titles like Skyrim, Fallout 3, or their former Fallout: New Vegas. I used to think that Obsidian, as a studio, was a creative gem. I was excited to experience Avowed to experience the full world of Pillars of Eternity - before being  disappointed. At the very least, I saw Avowed through, and chalked it up to a rushed game. The writing and character design in Avowed was abysmal, even if the combat was relatively smooth. Avowed suffered from a lack of interactivity in its world common to other open world games over the past several decades. The reason I bring up Avowed is that Outer Worlds II suffers the same problems. In terms of game design, there are a few real improvements from the first game. The combat feels fluid and can be challenging even on normal difficulty. There are options for weapons and armor, and some interesting enemies with more animation and abilities than the first game. However, there are many, many regressions. Without a build to lockpick, hack, or engineer your way through the early hours of the game, you are simply locked out of content and loot for veritable hours. Even only a few hours in, I found myself backtracking after a level up just to complete an uninteresting quest I stumbled upon earlier with a faction that just wasn't compelling. If you level up and focus on these pathways to get this content/complete quests/obtain loot you kneecap your combat abilities. With there only being a few in game areas for combat on the first map - but several over-leveled enemies in that area, you can easily stumble upon a fight that will easily kill you if you don't play the game in a certain way - yet combat is the primary form of getting experience in the game. The game does not have enough ways to obtain XP considering the draconian nature of its checks. Many checks are pass/fail based on a required skill, and have nothing at all to do with mini-games with difficulty sliders, like in previous open world games. This means that unless a character is geared for combat, they will have a frustrating experience. If a character is geared for combat, they lose out on loot. The checks can be quite high even early in the game - there simply isn't enough content on the first map to warrant that level of frustration. I could better understand if a character specialized over time - but right out the gate? Locked out of content and loot in the tutorial? The world feels very, very closed.

The map design is strange, the color palette headache inducing. The two earliest factions you meet are hyper late stage capitalist or the level of  authoritarian communist your parents and grandparents warned you about. Both are propagandized to hell, and  both feel quite bad to interact with. It isn't a world I would want to live in. It hits rather close to home considered the highly politicized environment of today. It also isn't lost on the me the irony of a big corporation like Microsoft having its two cents in what makes capitalism good or bad. You can't even help the communists for the most part, other than converting them into capitalists. It may as well be propaganda the game (TM). It's gone from a fun little dystopian world to a very sad reminder of the world I live in. Choose between  an authoritarian nightmare or wage-slavery. Why not both? After disappointment, I felt real existential dread.

    The story is uninteresting, the characters flat, the writing all over the place. Unless you just want to shoot cool guns or aren't bothered by the politicized undertones (Often in favor of big corpo), I would steer clear of this mess. I used to like Obsidian as a studio, but after this negative experience and the disappointment of Avowed, I've had to re-evaluate my level of respect. It's a pattern now. I think the Obsidian I once knew is dead and gone - I won't be picking up another game from Obsidian.

As a huge fan of the first game, Outer Worlds 2 was an unexpected arrival, albeit the fact that the original was a relative success should have seen the obvious prediction that a sequel would be made. This is a case where a sequel wasn't really needed, but was interestingly enough and welcome enough to worth having a shot at. On sale, down from £59.99 at only £36, I felt it was worth a pop, even although the reviews weren't massively popular or positive on it. When there is a mostly positive or mixed rating, one tends to hope that the game will just click for yourself, even if it didn't necessarily click for everybody else. Unfortunately, in the case of the Outer Worlds 2, it just doesn't really work. The problem here is that the original was a slightly cartoony and funny game. It had a sense of humour and a real charm to it. Quite simply, it was a lot of fun. By going for the realistic route for the visuals, especially the NPCs and your own character, it's turned something that was a bit of a cartoony comedy into something that is a hybrid of realism mixed with that cartoony comedy, and it doesn't really work. It's not half as funny, and the introduction section is frankly a bit boring and confusing. The problem here just simply seems to be that this feels like cookie cutter of the original. It's kind of like a fan game of the original. It looks quite nice, for sure, but it doesn't fit the vibe that the first game built. Unfortunately, it has to go down as a not recommended. I enjoyed and loved the first game, but the same cannot apply to the second one.

The game heavily punishes you for investing in more than 3 or 4 skills. You won't be able to keep up with the skill checks if you invest in more than that. And worse, you have no way of knowing this until it is too late, unless you do a bunch of reading about the game online. If you walk into the game blind and start investing in the skills that seem interesting to you, you will be behind for the rest of the game because there are also no monster respawns. You will be stuck leveling up in the next area and then going back to past worlds all the time to catch up the things you missed. 

Another related gripe I have is the limit on the number of markers you can put on the map. If you made the mistake I did and invested in 1 too many skills, then the hope is to put markers on all the things you want to go back to, but the game limits the number of markers you can place so I had to consign myself to wandering around old empty maps that have nothing to do except re-locate the chests and doors I couldn't open the first time I was there.

Overall the game doesn't seem replayable enough to me to want to do multiple playthroughs, so I just ended up frustrated that there is so much content that I don't get to interact with. I get what the developers were trying to do, but the world design and main plotline don't seem aligned with that goal, and they need to really emphasize the importance of limiting your scope from the beginning. Investing in more than 4 skills shouldn't even be an option because it becomes an unfun gotcha very quickly.

It's fun. An rpg, narrative-driven adventure with satisfying combat.
The open world is interesting and exploration iis rewarding. It's also quite pretty, colorful, clearly a decent artistic vision behind it.
The gunplay is satisfying and there are multiple interesting weapons to play with.
Narrative choices matter, mechanically in a way similar to fallout, but we have less agency over the world events.
As for the worldbuilding, the way the factions are presented is noteworthy. Especially the protectorate feeling of oppressive dictatorship, with its propaganda radio's marching songs and permanent surveillance culture.
The others - a caricature of greedy corporation and exaggerated out of touch academia - are more akin to borderlands humour. It conveys those comedic parts effectively, but the writing stumbles when trying to be serious, coming off as childishly stereotypical or naive. It's not New Vegas.
Speeaking of stereotypes, the companions are just that. Very little personality shownig through the cliches they're written to portray.
For those asking if the writing  is "woke" - absolutely. It's literally matriarchy in space (bar a cartoon villain man), which is ironic given the dystopian setting.
All in all the game is a net positive, though there's a lot of unrealised potential.

Outer Worlds 2 is definitely worth picking up if you can find it for under $40. The story is genuinely entertaining, and it does a great job pulling you into its world—especially with how it handles politics and faction dynamics. It feels like you’re actively part of the chaos rather than just observing it.

The gameplay is smooth, and the combat feels responsive and satisfying. While there are a few bugs here and there, they don’t really take away from the overall experience. Visually, the game stands out with its colorful, vibrant style, and at times it honestly feels like you’re living inside a chaotic parody of Star Trek, which adds a lot of charm.

That said, I can understand why it struggled at launch. At full price, it’s harder to recommend—mainly because it feels short compared to other RPGs like Fallout. If you’re expecting a massive, long-form experience, you might be a bit disappointed.

Overall, it’s a fun and unique ride—as long as you go in with the right expectations and grab it at a good price.

I found myself at best ambivalent to their goals, which were obviously counter to quality of life and just the pursuit of greed.  The communist hellscape of The Protectorate was just as bad.  The Order at least didn't have constant audio broadcasts with slogans and thinly veiled threats, but that was a low bar by the time the story engaged with them.

Some of the companions I really liked.  Two of the companions were so awful that I ignored them entirely.  I think that lead to a hollowness in parts of the overall story, but I just couldn't bear tolerating them or their faction BS.  It was all so over the top annoying.

I still found my way to the end and did the only thing I could to save myself: [spoiler]volunteered to the drive the ship[/spoiler].

Not like the trailers at all, Not a good sequel cause nothing to do with the previous game except for retuning auntie cleo but no spacers choice. Would have preferred something similar to the first outer worlds with some more updated graphics and better gun play but instead this was a left turn when they should have kept going straight. Companions were well done though, thought out really kept you tied to their stories but they complete garbage in combat.

Visuals and art direction are great, A big improvement over the first game. Story and characters are just ok, a bit disappointing overall.
