Pretty good, significantly improved from the first game, feels like a game that could have come from old Obsidian. Gameplay wise feels like a nice middle ground between New Vegas and Kotor2. Not open world proper but instead large levels for each planet/moon and so mostly avoids that feeling of pointless emptiness. Gunplay is a little mushy but makes up for it with a good variety of very different feeling guns, with some that are Ratchet and Clank-tier whacky fun. I recommend switching difficulty to hard after you finish the first planets main storyline or starting on hard, once your build starts coming together you can get crazy overpowered, in a largely fun way. Huge variety in builds too, since the game gives tons of opportunities to use different skills to get around, avoid, hack, or even talk your way through many encounters. Plus lots of cases of getting to use your character's traits, skills, perks, and flaws in conversations. The flaws system itself is very cool, since they are gained based on how you actually engage with the game. My favorite example is one that popped up that gives me a damage bonus with guns as long as I never have to reload them from empty, or else it wears off plus a penalty for a few minutes, and it popped up from my habit of always keeping all my guns reloaded.
Story and characters are solid, certainly not Kotor2 or alpha protocol tier, but like a lesser new Vegas. Somehow avoids being politically preachy in any of the main story or companion quest lines, most of what I did come across was lines my character could say based on having the Professor background trait. All of those writers must have been busy on Avowed thankfully.
Only real complaint is it feels a bit short for this kind of game, another full planet would have rounded out the amount of content nicely.
Overall a solid game, get it if you're a fan of 2000s bioware or obsidian RPGs.

Honestly its just a temu Fallout 4 with better graphics. 

The conversations in this game will damage your brain. Every story based conversation you'll have will bombard you with 10+ conversation options, most of them pointless, you'll constantly get garbage like the below.

1. "Yeah, I hate that guy".
2. "No, that guys alright, hes funny"
3. "I like that guy he dresses funny"
4. "No, he stole my hat"

These conversation branches happen 5-10 times every story-based cutscene, if your lucky you MAY pick an option thats referenced later in the game but a large portion of them are just pointless time wasters.. In the end I just mashed enter on the first option just to get out of the brainrot. 

To make matters worse you also have a silent protagonist in a game thats mostly conversations for some reason, why? How is it that Fallout 4 had a playable character with a VOICE and toned down conversation options when it released over 10 years ago!?

Also, unlike Fallout 4, the side characters in this game are horribly bland and uninteresting. Something Fallout 4 did better again.

Just go play Fallout 4 with a texture pack.

I would recommend this..... barely, and with many qualifiers. It does a ton of stuff right, I liked finding all the stories to be a part of throughout the worlds, a lot of outcomes really depended on previous choices and your mood at the moment. very well done and the nerrator when you first turn on the game giving a little quip about what you are up too is a fantastic little treat.

But you get to deal with them however you want so that's pretty neat. 

You will find a lot of conversations and notes to read, but either I'm getting dumber in my old age or whoever wrote this stuff needs to tone down the two-bit words just a bit. Its a chore reading and listening to a lot of these monologues because you expect a  light reading but are given a dissertation.

I used the same equipment pretty much the whole game because nothing is super better than anything else, so the equipment kinda falls flat, and there is ammo and junk just everywhere. There is no concern I felt ever about money or things.

I mean, I just cannot recommend this game to the average player. Even if you do all the quests in order on Eden, the boss fight will be absurdly hard and you will run out of all your ammo and grenades before he is even at half health. The adds alone have absurd health and kill you in like three hits. You can't leave and come back, either. Maybe the psychos who play games like Dark Souls will enjoy this but the most annoying part is that it's such a sharp incline in difficulty seemingly out of nowhere. I have very high sneak for the level I'm at and I still get caught rather easily in the area. I never increased my difficulty. 

This isn't just about this one part, either. This game is such a massive down step in gameplay from the previous Outer Worlds. The pathing of NPCs is bizarre, weapons don't seem to be any better as you progress (I constantly get the same drops), and the experience seems to change very suddenly with your skills never seeming to be high enough for anything. I am playing on Normal difficulty, too. I guess, if all this sounds appealing to you, maybe you'll enjoy it but Fallout fans like me will be left disappointed. It felt like the game went from normal mode to the Fallout equivalent of Survival mode.

It's a fun game with beautiful locations and creatures, wonderful humor, and interesting storytelling - if you are willing to actually read and listen to everything.

I did enjoyed the first game a lot, despite it being an incomplete vision of what it could have been. The second game I would say fufills everything I wanted to have in the first game. From what I have seen across the internet amung those who do like the game, this in not an uncommon opinion. Here is what I found enjoyable about the game, and what I didn't. While I did receive the game for free, I wanted to go in depth for what this game does genuienly good, and not so good. I find too many people aren't articulate with how or what makes a game "mid" to them so this is a long read.

Positives: 
- Interesting Main Story
- A baller soundtrack that you can listen to in game like the Pipboy radio. All of the factions have their own songs themed around what they stand for and their ideologies. I listen to it when I am just driving or in the gym.
- Faction Reputation is a thing finally. While it's reactivity is a little basic, it does track across the whole game in what you do for those factions which I enjoyed. I hope they expand on this in future DLC's.
- Good Perk & Flaw selection with lots to choose from to boost and nerf your character in fun ways
- Combat is actually a lot of fun and I found myself 'locking in' at a lot of points on Hardcore Difficulty in the early to mid game when it mattered. 
- LOTS of unique weapons and armours (like seriously more that I ever thought I would find). It was a highlight to find a new unique weapon or armour that had a reactive affect or did something cool. 
- Good player player choice (albiet these choices are only segmented & recognized in each region / planet in the game)  

Negatives
- Lack of Proper optimization
(Not the first UE5 game I need to install engine opt. mods for. My first character was played on 1.0.6.0 which was great. Now, with the game on 1.1.0.1, while the game still runs fine, it does run rougher than it used to. Even after driver updates. In my first playthrough, DLSS worked wonders to get high frames at minimal lose of visual quality, but after 1.1.0.1 my game does not play like it used to even without DLSS or Upscaling. Everything looks like its being deep fried and now black flashes appear on objects and foliage at a distance (even on medium draw distance). 
- Semi Forgetable NPC's & their Quests. Unfortunately they get shoehorned into the last third of the game (Although Inez's story was the most interesting to me). 
- Companions no longer have perma death on Hardcore (why?) - The first game had this.
- Some of the games dialogue has convuluted languague that make my eyes and ears gloss over. The first game somewhat had this problem, but it's still here. The last thing that kills a cool quest is boring dialogue to converse the info for said quest. 
- The game even on Hardcore is too easy. I got my ass stomped in the beginning like I wanted, but by the time I got about two thirds the way through the game, I could buldoze anything that had a healthbar)
- The Proctecorate (the objectively bad faction) was cut from the game as a joinable faction, and you can definitly notice that, especially after having Tristan as a follower. After completeting the game, it's obvious in how many different quests could have been in the game to affect your choices if the Protectorate was icluded. - Again, I hope they expand on this in future DLC's.

In conclusion, this is a hard game to reccomend. I received the ultimate edition of the game for free, I love the universe and the I.P, and I had a great first playthrough and the ways they improved from the first is monumental. By all metrics I am very biased and I do love this game. But I have to be honest. I would have not paid $100 CAD / 70 USD on it. If the game was priced at around like the first game with WAY better optimization I would say absolutely go for it, the game is fun. But unfotunetaly Obsidian needs to do more for the game for me to tell you it's worth it at full price. Get the game on a sale OR when they update it further with optimization fixes & DLC content.

A witty, choice-driven RPG that builds on the original with sharper writing, deeper systems, and vibrant worlds.

Combat still lacks depth, but the humor, freedom, and storytelling carry the experience.

The combat is much better than the first but the world and storytelling fall flat and choices feel meaningless. Given that the world and storytelling are the main draw for this type of game it just doesn't deserve the price point its offered at. If you can find on sale for 10-20 dollars I would give it a try otherwise avoid.

Thoroughly enjoyed my time with the Outer Worlds 2. Having done most of the side content and finishing the main quest I was satisfied with the game. I like the fact that most of the quests were unique and I wasn't bombarded with too many things to do at a time. Each area I took my time to do most things available in the level and finally completed the game at 37 hours. This is a one and done game for me. You could replay the game with different builds but it isn't neccessary at all. One playthrough is enough to provide you with all the lore and gameplay you'll need.

I had a lot of fun with this game, much like the first. My major complaints with the first game was the lack of variety in weapons (The weapons you find at the beginning of the game are the exact same as the end) and the over-abundance of TTD related perks, an ability I seldom used in both games. Fortunately both problems were rectified in droves. The variety of weapons was almost overwhelming and the perks, while most being stat increases, did allow for more nuanced gameplay. The flaw system is back from the original and the amount of flaws in just my 1 play through that I was offered is probably 3x what the first game had in its entirety. The companions in this game were much more interesting and compelling than in the first game and their abilities were much more interesting, though I missed their stats enhancing my own. The level up and skill point allocation system is much more streamlined in a better way; all guns are affected by the "guns" skill rather than focusing on pistols, rifles, etc. I only had 3 hard crashes and most of them were related to the first planet. I really enjoyed my time on Arcadia but the future of this series is depressing at best. If you liked the first game, this is that, but 2. which is twice as good.

Another title I really wanted to enjoy. Like many gamers, I've been an Obsidian fan since Fallout: New Vegas. I like the vintage science fiction artistic style...

Two weeks later, I can't remember the names of a single NPC or planet in this game or the first. I just remember getting to the second planet, picking up several quests and hitting brick walls on all of them. Sure, I could have gone to the oracles of YouTube and Google to seek answers, but the lack of personal investment makes investing those five minutes seem wasteful.

I think that it's a technical improvement on the first game, but it doesn't feel as fun

I am happy to give the thumbs up to this game. I finished it today on Very Hard and it was great challenge and fun.

What I liked:
The planets are diverse and unique
Buildcrafting is fun
The loot is expansive and allows for multiple play styles - melee, ranged and even explosives - and from that, do you go guns, heavy weapons, sniping, big hammer, rockets, lobbing explisives? It is up to you
Good exploration that enourages return to planets you have completed
Allows for multiple playthroughs by making different decisions and seeing how the story unfolds - even better if using a completely different build
Encourages 100%ing

What I disliked:
30 levels is restrictive, allowing you to max 3 skills + 2 or 4 points in other skills
Why? The devs created the flaw Easily distracted and realized if we had several more levels, we would have 5 skills at 20 each instead of the intended 3
Some flaws are useless
Ranged is clearly the preferred style of play (but as with The Outer Worlds 1, you can make melee work)
Game forces you to play multiple times over to see everything (could be a good thing if you don't mind replaying)

All in all, a great game to sink your teeth into. I grabbed it on sale for its original price with dlcs and all included, and I think it was a good buy, allowing you to double and triple the price in game hours - or more if you really like it.

This is a very solid 7/10 in my book.

This loose sequel to the first game, featuring all the great gameplay mechanics, is a pleasant surprise.

This Fallout—which isn’t a Fallout from the creators of the best Fallout games (1, 2, and New Vegas)—once again follows in the footsteps of the creators of the original successful titles.

The soundtrack builds on the music of its predecessor.

Unfortunately, the game lacks gameplay elements from previous titles, such as loot weight. The Hardcore mode from Fallout: New Vegas is completely absent.

Nevertheless, the game offers a great experience, with a game world that changes based on your decisions, spiced up by excellent dialogue—the creators’ use of this variety is a great element of the game’s replayability.

Good game.  This game is clearly a successor to Fallout 4. This fact is both good and bad.   Good, because I like  Fallout 4 and its game mechanics. Bad, because the plot is too sarcastic.  
The idea itself is very clever. Take 3 main social formations. And show their interaction.  But their hyper-exaggeration makes social interaction quite stupid and then boring. So, for me, this game is perfect for only one time.

I was so looking forward to this game, so it maybe my own fault for having high expectations that simply were not met. But the gameplay itself and the graphics, despite needing a much beefier rig to run at decent settings compared to the first, have not really evolved significantly where this feels less like a true sequel more than a 1.5. I would personally be okay with that, as really did like the first one and even minor improvements to with a new fresh story and characters would have still been a treat. Sadly the story itself and the characters are not that interesting - while also being too complex and convoluted - to the point where after about 40 hours of play I just do not have an interest in continuing with this game any longer. I can see that if you were to like this game there is some replay value, the class you choose has some impact on bits of story you discover or some choices you can make. Normally that would be great for me, if it were worth even a single play through to begin with. But sadly it is not.

I don't know how to feel about this game. If I didn't hear them say "It's bigger and better since we had more time to work on it" I feel like I would be more positive. 

I was expecting it to be bigger and better but I got was a universe that I didn't really want to be in. It felt small and contained. It's the most linear open world game I think I have ever played. At times it feels like "oh i spec'd into this, I can do this a new way" but most of the time it feels like you are not rewarded or encouraged to do things differently. I was just waiting for more the entire game. 

Last game I got to the end thinking it was the half way point. This game, I got to the end hoping it was the end point. 

Keeping spoiler free, I love the idea that the rifts are causing these issues and there is going to be a grand unification of idea and it's going to feel so big. But it ends up feeling like the exact opposite. It feels like "One person" this and "one person" that. 

I still recommend the game as it's still a good adventure but it's not going to stick with me at ALL. It feels like this game is building you up to play the game for a hundred hours but that's not the case.
